caption: The pathological examination of the right breast tumor revealed an infiltrating carcinoma, confirmed by E-cadherin staining, measuring in its greatest dimension, with focal extension into superior, inferior, and medial surgical margins and approaching anterior and lateral margins. Additionally, the lateral surgical margin in the subsequent breast tissue samples was found to be positive for carcinoma. The axillary contents disclosed metastatic involvement in four of twenty-two lymph nodes, with two showing subcapsular metastases larger than 0.2 mm, another with micrometastasis under 2 mm, and one displaying isolated positive cells. Both right sentinel lymph nodes were positive for metastasis; the first exhibited multiple foci of metastatic breast cancer, while the second had subcapsular sinus deposits. The tumor's staging is classified as T2 N2a MX, corresponding to stage IIIA. The presence of malignancy at critical surgical margins and the involvement of multiple lymph nodes suggest an aggressive pathology, necessitating further oncologic treatment and close follow-up.

The histopathological examination of the resected tongue lesion confirms a diagnosis of poorly differentiated (G3) planoepithelial carcinoma without keratosis. The tumor was found within a resected tongue specimen featuring clearly grossed normal tissue margins, with the closest margin at the base measuring 0.2 cm and the side margin measuring 0.5 cm. No lymph node involvement is noted, and the tumor is characterized by its aggressive nature. Other relevant pathological findings include the absence of keratosis, aligning with the histopathological diagnosis.

The final diagnosis of the right upper lobe lobectomy reveals a moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. The tumor is unifocal and exhibits no visceral pleura invasion. Notably, all surgical margins, including bronchial, vascular, and parenchymal margins, are negative for malignancy, indicating complete resection. Lymphovascular invasion is present. Pathological staging classifies the primary tumor as pT2a with no regional lymph node involvement (pN0); all 25 lymph nodes examined, including those from levels 3, 7, 10, 11, 2R, and 4R, show no evidence of malignancy. Additional findings include sclerosing granuloma in one lymph node, consistent with histoplasmosis, confirmed by GMS special stain. Overall, there is no evidence of distant metastasis (pMX).

The pathological examination of the resected right frontal brain tumor reveals a diffuse astrocytoma, classified as WHO Grade II. The tumor is characterized by moderate hypercellularity with mildly pleomorphic, hyperchromatic nuclei. No mitotic figures, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis are observed. Immunohistochemical analysis shows diffuse positivity for glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) and p53, while MIB-1 labeling index is focally elevated at 20%. The tumor is negative for IDH-1 mutation, suggesting a wild-type status. The PCR-based MGMT methylation assay indicates that gene methylation is not detected, with a score of 1.35, categorizing it as unmethylated. These findings outline a moderately aggressive tumor with a typical appearance of a Grade II astrocytoma, without evidence of aggressive features such as necrosis or high mitotic activity, and an unmethylated MGMT promoter, which may influence therapeutic sensitivity.

The pathological analysis reveals a primary diagnosis of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the left upper lobe of the lung, characterized by unifocal involvement with additional encroachment upon the visceral pleura, lobar bronchus, and lymphatic, venous, and arterial structures. Suspicion for perineural invasion is noted. Importantly, the surgical margins, including pleural, bronchial, and vascular, are positive, indicating residual tumor presence. In situ carcinoma is also present within the specimen. Lymph node evaluation disclosed ten out of fourteen nodes positive for metastatic disease, including stations 5 and 10 showing significant nodal involvement, while nodes from stations R4, 7, and 9 remain negative for malignancy. Cancer staging is assessed as pT2b pN2, with metastasis status undetermined at this stage. Additional molecular and cytogenetic studies for specific gene mutations are pending, which may influence future clinical management. The collective findings emphasize an advanced stage of disease with extensive local and regional spread, warranting further clinical review for comprehensive treatment planning.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the left lower lobe of the lung. The tumor exhibits associated necrosis but lacks convincing evidence of lymphovascular invasion. Importantly, the surgical margins, including the visceral pleura, bronchial, and vascular margins, are free of tumor involvement, affirming a negative margin status. Examination of the lymph nodes from levels 5, 7, 9, and 11, as well as multiple peribronchial lymph nodes, reveals no evidence of malignancy, indicating negative lymph node involvement. The TNM staging is determined as pT1N0Mx, signifying a primary tumor without regional lymph node metastasis and an indeterminate distant metastasis status. Other notable findings include the absence of visceral or parietal pleural invasion and no extension into other structures, confirming confinement of the carcinoma. Overall, these findings highlight a well-circumscribed neoplasm with no metastatic spread or local invasion.

The left temporal tumor resection has been diagnosed as a diffuse astrocytoma, WHO Grade II. Microscopic examination reveals foci of increased cellularity within both gray and white matter, characterized by infiltrative borders composed of cells with irregularly shaped nuclei and moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm. Frequent pleomorphic and hyperchromatic cells, along with occasional multinucleated cells, are present, but there are no significant mitotic figures, necrosis, or vascular proliferation. Immunohistochemical staining shows the neoplastic cells are positive for GFAP but negative for synaptophysin and NeuN. The tumor margins are not clearly defined due to the infiltrative nature of the astrocytoma, and there are no specific additional pathological findings reported. Lymph node involvement is not addressed as it is not typically relevant in primary brain tumors. Overall, the pathological findings support the diagnosis of a diffuse astrocytoma, emphasizing its infiltrative characteristics and WHO Grade II classification.

The pathological evaluation of the lumpectomy specimen from the left breast, specifically the upper inner quadrant, reveals an infiltrating ductal carcinoma that is moderately differentiated. The lesion is grossly evident upon examination. The surgical margins are free of tumor involvement, indicating complete excision. There is no evidence of metastasis in any of the ten examined axillary lymph nodes. Due to incomplete information, the total Nottingham Score could not be determined. Overall, key pathological findings include a moderately differentiated infiltrating ductal carcinoma with no lymph node metastasis and clear surgical margins.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma, Grade II, confined to the lower lobe of the left lung. The tumor, measuring 1.5 cm in diameter, is localized within the lung parenchyma and does not extend to the pleura or other structures. Importantly, the tumor extends to within 2.2 cm of the closest parenchymal margin, which is noted to be clear of malignancy. Examination of the mediastinal lymph nodes (9L and upper 9L), comprising a total of three nodes, shows no evidence of metastatic involvement. Additionally, there were no signs of venous or arterial invasion observed. The pathological stage is determined as pT1a N0 MX, indicating a small, localized tumor with no regional lymph node metastasis.

The final diagnosis reveals an invasive, moderately differentiated squamous carcinoma of the right lower lobe of the lung. The tumor demonstrates extensive necrosis and a desmoplastic stromal reaction, measuring 4 cm in its greatest dimension. It is well-circumscribed, extending close to but not invading the visceral pleura, and is 2.5 cm from the bronchial margin, which is confirmed to be negative for tumor involvement. The non-neoplastic lung parenchyma shows moderate emphysematous changes without evidence of lymphovascular invasion. All surgical margins, including bronchial, parenchymal, and vascular, are free of tumor.

Lymph node analysis includes a right lower paratracheal node that is negative for tumor and multiple negative fragments from the subcarinal region. However, one lymph node from the posterior right tracheobronchial angle contains poorly differentiated squamous carcinoma. The pathological stage is defined as pT2a N1 MX, indicating that there is local lymph node involvement but no distant metastasis has been assessed. Additional intralobar lymph nodes discovered upon further dissection are also negative for tumor.

The pathological examination confirms the diagnosis of an oligodendroglioma, Grade 2, within the cerebrum. The tumor shows typical histologic features of oligodendroglioma with no evidence of discrete nodules upon gross inspection. Margins could not be assessed from the biopsy specimens provided. Fluorescence in-situ hybridization (FISH) analysis indicated deletions of chromosomes 1p and 19q, findings that are associated with a favorable prognosis. No familial syndromes or prior tumor history are noted in this case. Ancillary studies are pending to further validate the diagnosis. Therefore, the initial diagnostic assessment remains unchanged following external consultation.

The pathological evaluation of the left axillary sentinel node and left breast specimen reveals a well-differentiated infiltrating ductal carcinoma, corresponding to an Elston SBR grade 1. Microscopically, the tumor exhibits low mitotic and nuclear scores, and it measures 2.8 cm, classifying it as pT2. Importantly, the surgical margins, including the skin and nipple, are negative for tumor involvement, indicating a complete resection. There is no evidence of vessel invasion, skin, or chest wall involvement. The single lymph node examined from the left axillary sentinel node is negative for metastatic disease (pNO). Additionally, the non-tumorous breast tissue shows fibrocystic changes, including cysts, apocrine metaplasia, ductal hyperplasia, intraductal papillomatosis, and rare foci of atypical ductal and lobular hyperplasia. A 1 cm fibroadenoma is also identified. There are no features suggestive of Paget’s disease or distant metastasis (pM cannot be assessed). Prognostic markers were previously determined, completing the comprehensive assessment of the pathological findings.

The pathological examination of the left breast segmental mastectomy reveals an invasive ductal carcinoma, Nottingham Grade 2, arising from a solid papillary carcinoma. The invasive tumor exhibits intermediate tubule formation, nuclear grade, and mitotic activity, with the invasive component comprising a notable portion of the tumor. Extensive ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), nuclear grade 2, presents with cribriform, papillary, and micropapillary patterns, constituting 60% of the tumor volume and distributed both admixed with and separate from the invasive carcinoma. Importantly, no lymphovascular invasion is identified. The tumor margins show the invasive carcinoma is relatively close to the anterior margin. Additionally, the specimen includes atypical ductal hyperplasia, atypical papilloma, multiple intraductal papillomas, fibrocystic changes, columnar cell changes, sclerosing adenosis, and microcalcifications, alongside biopsy site changes. The invasive tumor tests positive for estrogen (ER) and progesterone (PR) receptors, while HER-2/neu status is equivocal by immunohistochemistry (IHC) but negative by fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH). Further segmental resections reveal DCIS extending to the medial margin in the lateral section and DCIS approaching resection margins in the superior section, accompanied by flat epithelial atypia, papillomas, and fibrocystic changes. The deep margin shows features of atypical ductal hyperplasia, numerous papillomas, radial scar, and sclerosing adenosis. Examination of two axillary sentinel lymph nodes reveals no metastatic carcinoma.

The pathology findings reveal a well-differentiated invasive squamous cell carcinoma with verrucous features located in the floor of the mouth and anterior mandible. The tumor exhibits vascular invasion but has free mucosal and soft tissue margins, indicating no residual tumor at the resection edges. A detailed examination of lymph nodes from both the left and right supraomohyoid neck dissections shows no evidence of metastatic involvement, with all nodes being tumor-free. Additionally, the submental gland in both dissections appears normal without any pathological abnormalities. Analysis of a segment of the mental nerve and associated fibrovascular tissue also reveals no tumor presence. Furthermore, the left posterior ridge displays marked chronic inflammation and focal parakeratosis with acanthosis but lacks any neoplastic changes. The specimen from the previous biopsy site shows ulceration, granulation tissue, and significant acute and chronic inflammation. Finally, the teeth were examined grossly without further pathological findings.

A segmental resection of the right colon revealed an invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma, moderately differentiated, associated with an adenoma. The tumor invades focally into but does not penetrate beyond the muscularis propria, and there is no evidence of lymphovascular invasion. Examination of eight regional lymph nodes showed no metastatic involvement, and the surgical margins are negative for the presence of tumor cells. The overall pathological stage according to the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) is T2 N0 Mx, indicating tumor invasion into the muscularis propria without lymph node metastasis or distant spread. The serosal surface adjacent to the tumor appears unremarkable, and no appendix was identified in the specimen. Additionally, there was a possible scar present on the serosal surface of the cecum.

The pathological analysis of the right mastectomy specimen indicates the presence of invasive lobular carcinoma, focally exhibiting pleomorphic features, accompanied by extensive ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of intermediate nuclear grade, solid type with comedo necrosis. The tumor, graded as Nottingham histologic grade II with a score of 7, demonstrates minimal tubule formation, moderate nuclear pleomorphism, and a mitotic count indicative of moderate proliferation. Notably, there is no evidence of lymphatic invasion. The deep surgical margin is clear of tumor involvement, while additional findings include vessel wall calcifications, intraductal papilloma, radial scar, and fibrocystic changes with sclerosing adenosis. The skin and nipple are histologically unremarkable. Corresponding axillary lymph node dissection reveals metastatic carcinoma in two out of thirteen nodes without extranodal extension, designating a pathologic stage of pT2N1aMx under AJCC classification. Immunohistochemical profiling shows negative HER-2-NEU oncoprotein expression. However, both estrogen and progesterone receptors are strongly positive, with high percentages of nuclei staining, suggesting a hormone receptor-positive status. These comprehensive pathological findings guide the therapeutic approach and prognostic assessment for the patient.

The pathological examination reveals an anaplastic astrocytoma (WHO grade III), fibrillary type, located in the right frontal lobe of the brain. Immunohistochemical analysis demonstrates p53 overexpression in the majority of the tumor cells, indicating potential genomic instability. The proliferation marker Ki67/MIB1 shows a low to moderate proliferative index, suggesting a variable rate of tumor cell division. Additionally, the tumor is positive for the IDH1(R132H) mutation, which is commonly associated with a better prognosis in gliomas. Margins were not explicitly mentioned in the findings, and lymph node involvement is typically not applicable for primary brain tumors. No other significant pathological abnormalities were observed in the examined sections.

The pathological evaluation reveals an invasive high-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma involving the bladder, prostate, seminal vesicles, and vas deferens. The carcinoma exhibits poorly differentiated pleomorphic areas with focal glandular differentiation and invades the outer half of the muscularis propria, presenting as a multifocal distribution on the right lateral and posterior walls. All examined margins, including ureteral, distal urethral, and soft tissue margins, are negative for tumor involvement. Lymph node dissections from both the right and left pelvic regions reveal no metastatic disease, with a total of 15 lymph nodes evaluated. Additionally, the prostate shows an adenocarcinoma with a Gleason score of 3+3=6, perineural invasion, and multifocal high-grade prostatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PIN). There is no evidence of seminal vesicle invasion or extraprostatic extension, and all surgical margins are clear of tumor. The final pathological staging is determined to be pT2b, N0, MX for the urothelial carcinoma and MX for the prostate adenocarcinoma.

The tissue from the left upper lobe lung revealed an invasive grade 3 squamous cell carcinoma. This carcinoma formed a nodule within the lung tissue, however, the pleura remained uninvolved. Importantly, the tumor was located a sufficient distance from the bronchial margin, which, along with the bronchial resection margin and several intrapulmonary peribronchial lymph nodes, showed no evidence of tumor involvement. A thorough examination of various lymph nodes, including those from superior and inferior mediastinal regions, as well as aortic and regional N1 nodes, indicated no metastatic spread of the tumor, confirming these nodes were negative for cancer. Overall, the findings suggest a localized but aggressive carcinoma with no current evidence of nodal metastasis.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated Grade 3 transitional cell carcinoma (TCC) of the bladder, exhibiting a mixed papillary and solid growth pattern. Microscopic examination demonstrates significant stromal invasion, extending into the muscularis propria, consistent with a pathological stage of pT2. Importantly, no vascular channel invasion is observed. The surrounding urothelium remains non-dysplastic, providing a normal tissue context. Margin status is unspecified, and lymph node involvement is not addressed within the current findings. These features collectively indicate a high-grade, invasive bladder carcinoma requiring comprehensive clinical management.

The pathological examination of the left partial glossectomy specimen reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated invasive squamous cell carcinoma with an infiltrative border. Additionally, both perineural and lymphatic invasion are present. The tumor margins are confirmed to be free of cancerous cells, as evidenced by the analysis of deep, anterior, dorsal, ventral, and posterior margins. The lymph node dissection shows metastatic squamous cell carcinoma in one out of twenty-nine regional lymph nodes examined, specifically in the specimens from levels 2 through 4 of the left neck. The submandibular gland is unremarkable with no significant abnormalities. Immunohistochemical staining indicates a negative result for p16 and HPV in situ hybridization, with an EGFR score of 2+. The final pathologic stage is classified as pT1 N1.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of well-differentiated astrocytoma, WHO Grade II, in the left temporal region. The tumor consists of moderately hypercellular glial neoplasm predominantly composed of fibrillary and protoplasmic astrocytes, with prominent microcystic changes. The neoplastic cells are consistently immunoreactive for p53, confirming their astrocytic origin. Although scattered MIB-1 reactive cells are present throughout the specimen with a labeling index averaging 3.6%, one area exhibits higher proliferative activity with a labeling index of 7.1%, warranting close follow-up despite not meeting criteria for anaplastic classification. There is notable mild atypia with only a few moderately atypical nuclei observed; no mitoses, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis are evident. Additionally, less than 10% of the specimen shows infiltrating low-grade glioma, without definitive oligodendroglioma differentiation. The majority of the tissue consists of acute hematoma and surgical packing material, and no specific measurements for tumor margins or lymph node involvement are provided in this summary.

The pathological examination reveals an invasive adenocarcinoma of the colon, characterized by poor differentiation with partial neuroendocrine differentiation, penetrating all wall layers and showing clear vascular infiltration. The tumor is classified as pT3, L1, V1, G3, ICDO-DA M8140/3. Margins are free of tumor involvement, with the proximal margin showing ileum-type mucosa and the distal margin showing colon-type mucosa. The ligature region contains local fatty connective tissue with proper vascular sections, and the appendix exhibits chronic recurrent and partially scarring appendicitis with pronounced lymph follicle activation. Lymph node assessment indicates chronic resorptive lymphadenitis with active lymph follicles, but no metastatic involvement is observed (0 out of 12 nodes, pN0), consistent with an R0 resection status.

The pathological evaluation indicates a diagnosis of anaplastic gemistocytic astrocytoma, WHO grade III, located in the left temporal region. The tumor is characterized by hypercellularity and significant infiltration into the cortical grey matter. The neoplastic cells exhibit moderate to marked cytologic atypia, with many showing prominent nuclear atypia and pleomorphism. Additionally, the identification of gemistocytic differentiation is evident, with neoplastic cells displaying abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and nuclear p53 positivity. Perivascular lymphocytes are occasionally observed around some blood vessels. There is an absence of significant mitotic activity, microvascular proliferation, and necrosis. The MIB-1 labeling index is relatively low at 2.8%. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not mentioned in this description, indicating that further clinical correlation may be required to assess these aspects. Overall, the pathological features are consistent with a high-grade astrocytic neoplasm with distinctive gemistocytic differentiation.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast. The tumor exhibits histologic features of grade 1 invasive carcinoma, including an architectural score of 2, nuclear score of 2, and mitotic score of 1. Microscopic analysis indicates a focal presence of ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of cribriform pattern, with nuclear grades 1-2, and absence of central necrosis. The primary invasive carcinoma is not involved with the skin, nipple, or chest wall and does not exhibit vascular invasion. Margins of the specimen are clear of tumor involvement, ensuring that the carcinoma does not extend to these edges. The non-tumorous tissue surrounding the mass shows fibrocystic changes with no additional lesions identified. Immunohistochemistry using keratin (AE1/AE3) immunostains confirms that the sentinel lymph nodes are negative for malignancy. Therefore, no lymph node metastasis is present, and the sentinel nodes on both levels were benign. This comprehensive assessment concludes with a clear margin status and absence of lymph node involvement, providing an essential basis for therapeutic decision-making.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive, moderately well-differentiated squamous carcinoma identified in the total glossectomy specimen, with three distinct foci demonstrating perineural invasion. The tumor is present with a diffuse invasion pattern. Importantly, all margins of resection are free of tumor cells, suggesting successful surgical removal of the carcinoma. Examination of the right facial lymph node, right hypoglossal node, and left hypoglossal nerve shows no evidence of metastatic disease. Both the left gingival nodule and the left gingival node are also free of tumor, with the former showing some fibrosis. Additionally, fibrosis is present in the left parapharyngeal space and anterior tonsillar pillar, with no tumor detected. The absence of tumor in the examined lymph nodes and other specified tissues suggests a lack of regional metastasis at this stage.

The left temporal tumor resection has been diagnosed as a diffuse astrocytoma, WHO Grade II. Microscopic examination reveals foci of increased cellularity within both gray and white matter, characterized by infiltrative borders composed of cells with irregularly shaped nuclei and moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm. Frequent pleomorphic and hyperchromatic cells, along with occasional multinucleated cells, are present, but there are no significant mitotic figures, necrosis, or vascular proliferation. Immunohistochemical staining shows the neoplastic cells are positive for GFAP but negative for synaptophysin and NeuN. The tumor margins are not clearly defined due to the infiltrative nature of the astrocytoma, and there are no specific additional pathological findings reported. Lymph node involvement is not addressed as it is not typically relevant in primary brain tumors. Overall, the pathological findings support the diagnosis of a diffuse astrocytoma, emphasizing its infiltrative characteristics and WHO Grade II classification.

The pathological analysis confirms a diagnosis of high-grade urothelial carcinoma of the bladder, demonstrating extensive invasion into the lamina propria and the muscularis mucosae. Notably, there is no definitive evidence of tumor invasion into the muscularis propria. The tumor exhibits high-grade malignancy features, characteristic of grade 3 urothelial carcinoma. No additional pathological findings, such as margin status or lymph node involvement, are mentioned in the provided details. The clinical history supports a background of bladder cancer, correlating with the invasive nature of the detected carcinoma.

The pathological examination of the left breast lumpectomy specimen reveals an infiltrative ductal carcinoma measuring 4.5 cm, classified as Grade III on the Scarff-Bloom-Richardson scale. The tumor displays multifocal angiolymphatic invasion and was found to be very close to the superior margin. Micrometastases were detected in sentinel lymph nodes 2, 3, and 4, while other sentinel nodes and additional surgical margins were free of tumor involvement. The axillary contents comprised six benign reactive lymph nodes. The tumor exhibited epithelioid features with focal mucinous differentiation, along with infiltrating and pushing borders. Immunohistochemical staining showed negative results for both estrogen and progesterone receptors. The overall pTNM classification is T2, N1mi, MX, corresponding to Stage Group IIB, and a breast cancer prognostic profile will be included in a subsequent report.

The pathological examination reveals a poorly differentiated solid carcinoma (G3) in the ascending colon, with significant necrosis and ulceration of the tumor's inner surface. The tumor is infiltrating all layers of the colon wall, extending into the extramuscular subserous fatty connective tissue, and is associated with peritumorous chronic recurrent secondary inflammation, alongside acute inflammatory activity. Additionally, there is evidence of carcinomatous lymphangitis. Importantly, all twelve examined lymph nodes are tumor-free. Other sections from the preparation also show no evidence of tumor involvement, indicating clear margins. Furthermore, there is no indication of serous carcinosis. Overall, the tumor is staged as pT3, pNO (0/12), MX, L1, and resected with negative margins (R0), classified under ICDO-DA M-8140/3, G3.

The pathological examination of the resected right frontal brain mass reveals a diagnosis of low-grade oligoastrocytoma, WHO grade II. Immunohistochemical analysis shows tumor cells positive for IDH1 (R132H) mutation, with rare p53 protein positivity and a low Ki-67 (MIB-1) labeling index, reinforcing the low-grade nature of the neoplasm. The margins of the resected specimen are not explicitly detailed, and there is no mention of lymph node involvement, as typically not applicable in primary central nervous system tumors. Additional fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) studies for 1p19q deletion have been ordered to further characterize the tumor. The gross examination describes multiple representative sections from the right frontal brain mass, supporting thorough sampling and analysis. These findings collectively support the diagnosis and guide further management and potential prognostic implications.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of infiltrative ductal carcinoma in the left breast. The tumor, which was identified as the primary lesion, was procured via radical mastectomy. Histologically, the tumor is characterized as a grade 2 malignancy. The pathological staging indicates a T2 stage, denoting a moderately sized tumor, combined with an N2 stage, confirming significant but not extensive lymph node involvement. No evidence of distant metastasis is identified, as indicated by an M0 stage. The surgical margins are clear, ensuring no residual tumor at the resection edges. No treatment had been initiated prior to the surgical procurement. Additionally, a normal sample was obtained from the patient's blood for comparative analysis, collected through a blood draw and preserved in a frozen state.

The pathology findings reveal a diagnosis of adenocarcinoma in the lateral segment of the left upper lobe, characterized as moderately differentiated. The tumor is unifocal and demonstrates visceral pleural invasion but no lymphovascular space invasion. Marginal analysis indicates the tumor is notably close to the bronchial and parenchymal resection margins and involves the pleura. The primary tumor has been staged as T2. Lymph node assessment at levels VI and IX shows no malignancy. These findings, combined with the clinical history of smoking and COPD, suggest a significant malignancy that requires further molecular studies to guide potential treatment options.

The histopathological examination revealed an invasive ductal carcinoma (Carcinoma ductale invasivum NHG3) in the left breast's outer upper quadrant. The neoplastic cells demonstrated estrogen and progesterone receptor positivity in over 75% of their nuclei, while HER2 protein expression scored 2+ on immunohistochemistry but without HER2 gene amplification on FISH analysis. The tumor was extensively infiltrative, with a visual area exhibiting 19 mitoses per 10 high-power fields. Pathologically, the lesion measured 3.0 cm at its largest dimension. Microscopic evaluation indicated that the carcinoma had reached but not breached the base margin, which was noted to be less than 0.05 cm. Other margins were clear but relatively close, specifically the front skin surface, which was 0.2 cm. Additionally, metastatic involvement was confirmed in two out of thirteen axillary lymph nodes, with carcinomatous infiltration extending to the capsule and perinodal adipose tissue. Based on these findings, the pathological staging was determined to be pT2, pN1a.

The pathology analysis of the left breast, following a total mastectomy for a pre-operative diagnosis of breast cancer, reveals invasive ductal carcinoma occurring in two distinct foci. The largest invasive focus is graded as a high histologic grade (Grade 3 of 3) and measures 2.8 cm in its greatest dimension. Additionally, extensive high-grade ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) is present, encompassing approximately 5 cm and showing central necrosis. The surgical margins are clear of invasive carcinoma with the closest margin being 4 mm deep, and the margins for DCIS are also clear but only 1 mm deep. The axillary dissection sampled four lymph nodes, one of which shows metastatic involvement with the largest metastatic deposit being 7 mm. Notably, no extranodal extension is identified. The tumor is staged as pT2 for the primary tumor and pN1a for regional lymph node involvement. Ancillary studies indicate that the tumor is positive for estrogen receptor (100% of cells, strong intensity), weakly positive for progesterone receptor (2% of cells, weak intensity), and HER2 positive (Score 3+), which is significant for treatment planning. These findings collectively provide a comprehensive overview of the extent and nature of the breast carcinoma and the involvement of regional lymph nodes, guiding further clinical management.

The histopathological examination reveals a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma (NHG1) and ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of the right breast. The tumor is located in the upper outer quadrant, exhibiting positive estrogen and progesterone receptor status in over 75% of neoplastic cell nuclei and a negative HER2 status (Score = 1+). Microscopically, the invasive carcinoma shows low nuclear grade, a low mitotic rate of 10 per 10 high-power fields, along with foci of cribriform-type DCIS with medium nuclear atypia and calcifications, accounting for 5% of the tumor. There are microcalcifications in the glandular tissue and benign changes such as fibrocystic changes and fibroadenoma. Importantly, metastases are identified in one of ten axillary lymph nodes, with evidence of capsular and perinodal tissue infiltration. Margins are noted with the tumor being very close to the skin. The pathological stage is pT2, pN1a, underscoring the presence of lymph node involvement.

The pathological examination of the hypopharynx revealed a moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. The surgical specimen from the total pharyngolaryngectomy, partial esophagectomy, and bilateral neck dissections confirmed the presence of carcinoma in the hypopharynx, with involvement noted in the right vocal cord and the esophagus. The thyroid gland showed an incidental finding of follicular papillary carcinoma, histological grade 2, with uninvolved surgical margins. Importantly, lymphovascular invasion was not identified. Lymph node analysis revealed metastatic carcinoma in two out of nine nodes from the left recorrential dissection, and three out of fifteen nodes from the right neck dissection, all without capsular involvement. In contrast, four lymph nodes from the left neck dissection showed no neoplastic involvement. Surgical margins were generally uninvolved by neoplasia, though the right-posterior and right-lateral margins were noted to be exiguous. Other structures including the epiglottis, left vocal cord, thyroid, cricoid, arytenoid cartilages, and hyoid bone were uninvolved by neoplasia. Overall, the findings indicated a moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the hypopharynx with regional lymph node involvement but without evidence of distant metastatic spread.

The pathological examination of the colon revealed a diagnosis of colon adenocarcinoma located in the cecum. The tumor exhibits characteristics consistent with a T3 stage, indicating that it has extended through the muscularis propria into the pericolorectal tissues, but has not invaded any adjacent organs or structures. Notably, the surgical margin status is clear, with no cancer cells observed at the resection borders, suggesting that the tumor was completely excised. Importantly, there is no evidence of lymph node involvement, as all examined nodes are free of metastatic disease (N0). Additionally, there is no indication of distant metastasis (M0). These findings correspond to an overall stage of IIA, suggesting a localized disease with no regional or distant spread. Other relevant pathological assessments did not reveal additional significant abnormalities.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of Oligoastrocytoma WHO grade II, characterized by both astrocytic and oligodendroglial differentiation. Notably, no mitotic activity is observed, and the proliferation index is low at 2%. The tumor exhibits features consistent with a low-grade, diffusely infiltrative glioma. Margin status is not specified, indicating the potential need for further clinical correlation or additional imaging to assess the completeness of surgical resection. Lymph node involvement is not mentioned, suggesting that lymphatic spread is unlikely in this central nervous system tumor type. Overall, the findings suggest a relatively indolent behavior with a favorable prognosis, though careful clinical monitoring is warranted due to the infiltrative nature of the tumor.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of invasive urothelial carcinoma involving the bladder and prostatic urethra, characterized as high grade and poorly differentiated. The tumor exhibits multicentricity and has extended into the perivesical fat and prostatic stroma, including the apex of the prostate, with extravesical and perineural invasion present. The associated epithelial lesions include non-invasive high-grade urothelial carcinoma and carcinoma in situ. Lymph node analysis shows metastatic spread with one out of eleven left pelvic nodes and two out of fifteen right pelvic nodes positive for metastatic high-grade urothelial carcinoma. In total, three out of thirty-one evaluated lymph nodes are found to be positive for metastasis. Importantly, the surgical margins, including the urethral, left and right ureter, and radial margins, are reported negative for tumor involvement. The prostate is involved by the tumor, whereas the seminal vesicles remain uninvolved. The overall pathologic staging is pT4a, pN2, translating to an AJCC Stage IV under the 2010 guidelines. Further, biopsies of the left and right distal ureters are benign with no evidence of tumor, and the true margins of the left and right ureters are also confirmed to be tumor-free.

The pathological examination reveals an infiltrative ductal carcinoma in the primary tumor located in the left breast of a female patient. The tumor is categorized as Grade 2, indicating moderately differentiated cells. It is classified as T2, indicating its size and extent. There is no evidence of regional lymph node involvement (N0) or distant metastasis (M0) at this time. Surgical margins are not mentioned, suggesting they are either clear or not assessed in this context. No adjuvant treatment has been administered yet. A normal blood sample procured via a blood draw serves as a control for comparison. The overall findings suggest a localized breast cancer with no current lymphatic or hematogenous spread.

The pathological findings reveal an infiltrating well-differentiated adenocarcinoma located in the right lower lobe of the lung, which extends through the visceral pleura. The tumor has spread to one of the six examined lymph nodes, indicating metastatic involvement. Importantly, the bronchial and vascular margins are clear of tumor infiltration. Additionally, a wedge resection of the right upper lobe uncovers a cavitary fibrotic cyst with colonization by branching hyphal fungal forms consistent with Aspergillus, with no evidence of malignancy in this specific section. Another area in the right upper lobe also contains a small focus of infiltrating well-differentiated adenocarcinoma. Overall, these findings are indicative of multifocal adenocarcinoma with local metastasis and a concurrent aspergillosis infection.

The pathologic findings indicate a diagnosis of poorly differentiated non-small cell carcinoma with squamous features located in the right upper lobe following lobectomy. The primary tumor is classified as pT2, and all 13 regional lymph nodes examined were negative for metastatic involvement (pN0). The resection margins were free of invasive carcinoma, suggesting complete removal of the tumor. No evidence of distant metastasis was observed; however, distant metastatic status remains indeterminate (pMx). Additionally, there was no evidence of venous or arterial large vessel invasion, and lymphatic invasion could not be conclusively determined. Multiple excised lymph nodes, including those from regions R10, R4, high R4, R11, and station 7, were all negative for tumor involvement, further supporting the absence of regional spread. The tumor was noted to be adjacent to the pleura but did not involve the excision margins significantly, confirming successful surgical excision with clear margins.

The pathological findings confirm a diagnosis of poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma located in the cecal pouch. The tumor measures 3.0 x 2.8 x 0.7 cm and extends through the muscularis propria into the subserosal adipose tissue. There is evidence of lymphovascular space invasion, but no perforation of the visceral peritoneum or mesenteric deposits. The tumor does not involve the proximal, distal, or radial margins, with the closest margin being within 1 mm of the serosal surface. Seventeen lymph nodes were examined, and one lymph node contained metastatic carcinoma. The appendix shows fibrous obliteration of the lumen without evidence of obstruction by the tumor. The cancer is staged as pT3N1a. The final diagnosis and findings have been reviewed and concurred upon by two pathologists.

Histopathological examination of multiple organ resection from a female patient revealed an invasive ductal carcinoma (carcinoma ductale invasivum) in the left breast, classified as NHG3 based on the grading system (3 + 2 + 3) with a high mitotic rate of 35 mitoses per 10 high power fields. The tumor was found at the border of the upper quadrants, with notable skin involvement suggested by the retracted nipple and peau d'orange appearance. The invasive carcinoma exhibited fibrocystic changes within the glandular tissue but no lesions in the nipple. Metastatic carcinoma was identified in one of twenty-one examined axillary lymph nodes, with evidence of capsular and perinodal tissue infiltration. Immunohistochemical analysis showed estrogen receptors present in 10-75% and progesterone receptors in over 75% of the neoplastic cell nuclei. HER2 protein staining was negative despite a high score, confirming the absence of HER2 overexpression. The margin status indicates close proximity to the skin but without specific mention of infiltration. Overall, the findings point to an advanced, aggressive tumor with regional lymph node involvement.

The pathological examination of the colectomy specimen from the colon reveals a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. The tumor extends into the pericolonic tissues, indicating a locally advanced disease. Notably, all twelve intraabdominal lymph nodes examined are positive for metastatic involvement, demonstrating extensive nodal metastasis. Importantly, the surgical margins are uninvolved, suggesting complete resection of the primary tumor. These findings collectively indicate a significant tumor burden with extensive lymph node metastasis, although the clear margins are a favorable prognostic indicator.

The patient, a female, has been diagnosed with invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast. The tumor exhibits variegated cut sections and, upon microscopic examination, it presents with solid nests, cords, comedo, and tubular patterns. The malignant cells are large with moderately pleomorphic, hyperchromatic nuclei and amphophilic cytoplasm. Desmoplastic stroma containing similar tumor cells and lymphocytes is observed, with focal areas of necrosis. The invasive carcinoma is 5mm from the closest deep resection margin. Overall, the findings indicate a malignant neoplasm with specific histological characteristics, including significant stromal reaction and necrosis, providing a clear diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma.

The pathological evaluation revealed a diagnosis of moderately-differentiated, non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma in the left upper lobe of the lung. The tumor was unifocal, with invasion into the inked visceral pleural surface (PL2). Importantly, the bronchovascular surgical margins were found to be free of tumor involvement. Lymph node analysis showed metastatic carcinoma in one out of four perihilar lymph nodes and one AP window lymph node, while the remaining perihilar and left lung lymph nodes were free of the tumor. The overall AJCC pathological stage is classified as pT2a pN2. Throughout, the specimen remained intact, and no additional masses or lesions were identified in the remainder of the lung.

A biopsy from a right craniotomy revealed the presence of an anaplastic astrocytoma, classified as WHO Grade III. The tumor exhibited significant nuclear and cellular pleomorphism, increased mitotic figures, and scattered blood vessels without definitive microvascular proliferation. Individual cell necrosis was present, although there were no pseudopalisading or geographic necrosis areas. Immunohistochemical analysis showed the tumor cells were positive for glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) and mutated isocitrate dehydrogenase 1 (IDH-1). Additionally, a notable portion of the tumor cells (approximately 70%) demonstrated p53 immunoreactivity. Proliferation markers indicated a high mitotic activity, with PHH3 highlighting up to 25 mitoses per 10 high power fields and Ki-67 reflecting a proliferation index reaching 30%. Importantly, 1p/19q testing showed no deletions in the tumor tissue. These pathological findings consolidate the diagnosis of an anaplastic astrocytoma, offering critical insights into the tumor's aggressive behavior and aiding in the guiding of treatment strategies.

The pathological evaluation confirms a diagnosis of poorly differentiated non-small cell carcinoma involving the right lower lobe of the lung. The tumor is confined to the lung and is classified as pT2b. Importantly, all surgical margins, including bronchial and vascular margins, are negative for tumor invasion. The tumor shows evidence of lymphatic and venous invasion but no arterial invasion. Regarding lymph node involvement, eight peribronchial and perihilar lymph nodes are free of metastatic carcinoma, while one out of four mediastinal (level 7) lymph nodes contains metastatic adenocarcinoma. The 4R and 2R lymph nodes are negative for metastatic disease, corresponding to a pN2 classification. Additional findings include post-obstructive pneumonitis, pulmonary edema, and a benign mediastinal cyst. No histopathological abnormalities are noted in the superior vein stump. The presence of lymphovascular invasion and metastatic involvement in a mediastinal lymph node highlights the locally advanced nature of the disease.

The pathological findings describe a low-grade astrocytoma, classified as Grade II of IV according to the WHO scale. The tumor exhibits mildly pleomorphic and infiltrative characteristics with astrocytic cell proliferation. There is no evidence of endothelial proliferation or necrosis, and mitotic activity is very rare, with only one definite mitosis observed. Immunohistochemical analysis using the proliferation marker Ki67 revealed a proliferation index ranging between 3.2% and 5.7%, averaging 4.2%, which is slightly above the expected upper limit for a Grade II astrocytoma. This minimal increase in proliferation index suggests the need for close clinical monitoring. No anaplastic features were identified in frozen sections, and there was no lymph node involvement to consider. The surgical margins were not explicitly detailed in the provided information, but comprehensive sampling was performed for both frozen and permanent sections. These findings collectively underscore the diagnosis of a low-grade astrocytoma with characteristics warranting careful follow-up.

The pathological examination of the right breast, following a wide local excision, reveals an invasive ductal carcinoma diagnosed as Nottingham grade 2. The carcinoma is associated with ductal carcinoma in situ of a solid type, exhibiting high nuclear grade without necrosis. Angiovascular invasion is present. Re-excision of the inferior and deep margins of the tumor confirms all margins are negative by a minimum of 1.0 cm. The skin ellipse shows no abnormalities. Examination of the right axillary sentinel lymph nodes reveals metastatic involvement in 2 out of 5 nodes, categorizing it as AJCC pN1. Additionally, all seven non-sentinel right axillary lymph nodes are free of tumor. Estrogen/progesterone receptor and Her2/Neu status are pending analysis from paraffin-embedded tissue samples.

The pathology findings reveal a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of colorectal type located in the colon. The tumor demonstrates infiltration into the tunica muscularis propria, indicating an advanced local invasion. Margins of the resected colon, however, are free of tumor involvement, suggesting complete surgical excision. The regional lymph nodes are free of metastatic disease, with none of the 21 examined nodes containing tumor cells. The pathological staging is pT2 pN0 pMX, denoting a local tumor with no regional lymph node metastasis and indeterminate distant metastasis status. Additionally, the tumor is graded G2, consistent with moderate differentiation, and shows no signs of lymphovascular invasion (L0, V0). The resection margins are negative for residual tumor (R0), confirming a complete excision of the neoplasm.

The pathological examination revealed an infiltrating, poorly differentiated ductal carcinoma of the right breast, characterized by high nuclear grade. The invasive focus measures 1.7 cm at its greatest dimension and exhibits positivity for estrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR), while HER2 immunohistochemistry is negative. HER-2 chromogenic in situ hybridization (CISH) testing is still pending. Surgical margins appear uninvolved by invasive carcinoma and ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), with the closest margin being 1 mm anteriorly. The carcinoma was accompanied by lymphovascular invasion, though the three sentinel lymph nodes sampled demonstrated no carcinoma presence, as confirmed by negative keratin immunostains. The excised breast tissue also included mammary parenchyma with stromal fibrosis and calcifications. The tumor has been staged as pT1c N0, indicating its localized nature without regional lymph node involvement. The non-involvement of dermal tissue and the absence of skeletal muscle in the specimen were noted. The overall impression supports a diagnosis of high-grade invasive ductal carcinoma with comprehensive excision and clear margins.

The pathological evaluation of the right breast and axillary contents from a modified radical mastectomy reveals an infiltrating lobular carcinoma, with a nuclear grade 2 of 3. The tumor is centrally located and measures 5.0 x 5.0 x 2.8 cm, showing extensive multifocal and multicentric disease throughout all quadrants of the breast. There is no evidence of lymphatic or vascular invasion, and an in situ carcinoma is absent. The nipple and skin are free of tumor involvement, and the non-neoplastic breast tissue exhibits benign fibrocystic changes. Surgical margins are negative, indicating that no residual cancer was identified at the inked specimen margins. A total of twenty-eight axillary lymph nodes were examined, with no evidence of malignancy detected in any of them (0/28). Estrogen and progesterone receptors are both positive, with values indicating receptor activity. A more detailed analysis will be provided in an addendum. The extensive nature of the multifocal infiltrating lobular carcinoma in the specimen underscores the complexity of the disease.

The pathological findings from a right frontal brain mass biopsy reveal a diagnosis of oligodendroglioma, WHO Grade 2. The tumor is characterized as a low-intermediate grade infiltrating glioma. The histological examination confirms the diagnosis, with a gray-white junction observed in the majority of the specimen, although some areas presented with an obscure differentiation. Real-time PCR analysis indicates a loss at chromosomal arms 1p and 19q, which is consistent with an oligodendroglial lineage and suggests a potentially more favorable response to adjuvant therapy. The specimens examined consisted of pieces of cerebral cortex and underlying white matter, submitted for a combination of histologic examination and frozen section analysis. The detailed examination did not find any focal lesions in the smaller fragments submitted. Margin status and lymph node involvement were not mentioned, indicating the primary focus on the molecular and histological characterization of the tumor.

The submitted pathology samples from the left parietal brain lesion reveal a highly cellular tumor characterized by enlarged pleomorphic ovoid and angulated cells with hyperchromatic nuclei. The presence of some gemistocytic tumor cells, microcystic changes, and prominent calcification are notable features. The tumor exhibits a fine vascular pattern without evidence of necrosis or endothelial proliferation. The proliferation index (Ki67) is 20%, and p53 staining is 15%, with IDH1 immunohistochemical staining being negative. Mitoses are observed at a rate of 7 per 10 high-power fields (HPF) on H&E staining, which increases to as many as 24 per 10 HPF on PHH3 stain. These histological characteristics are suggestive of an anaplastic astrocytoma, WHO grade III. However, features reminiscent of an oligodendroglioma necessitate further molecular pathology testing, including FISH for 1p/19q codeletion and EGFR amplification, as well as IDH1/IDH2 sequencing, all pending patient consent. The margins of resection are not explicitly discussed in the report, and lymph node involvement is not applicable or mentioned given the central nervous system origin of the tumor. The consistent findings align most closely with an anaplastic astrocytoma but require molecular confirmation to exclude an anaplastic oligodendroglioma.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma involving the right upper and lower lobes of the lung as well as the pleura. Histological examination confirmed the presence of a similar adenocarcinoma in all four specimens, characterized by a primary lung adenocarcinoma phenotype, substantiated by immunohistochemical staining. The tumor cells were positive for TTF-1, CK7, Napsin-A, and MOC-31, consistent with lung adenocarcinoma, while negative for markers such as calretinin, CDX2, CK20, and p63. The adenocarcinoma in the right upper lobe wedge excision was noted to be grossly close to the surgical margin. No specific lymph node involvement was mentioned in the findings. The presence of adenocarcinoma in the pleural biopsies suggests possible pleural involvement or metastasis. Overall, the findings indicate a comprehensive and consistent pattern of lung adenocarcinoma, with detailed immunohistochemical profiling corroborating the diagnosis.

The pathology findings indicate a diagnosis of low-grade oligoastrocytoma. The tumor exhibits a mildly to moderately hypercellular glial neoplasm that diffusely infiltrates both the gray and white matter. Tumor cells display monotonous round to oval nuclei with minimal atypia and prominent, often fibrillary cytoplasm, including microgemistocytic and occasional gemistocytic forms. The overall cell morphology is consistent with a combination of astrocytoma and oligodendroglioma features. The tumor has a low mitotic rate, with only two mitoses observed in 25 high power fields, and lacks microvascular proliferation or necrosis. Margins are not specified, but the presence of scattered MIB-1 reactive cells with a low labeling index of 1.7% suggests a minimally proliferative nature. No lymph node involvement is mentioned as it often does not apply to primary brain tumors such as this one. The amalgamation of these characteristics justifies the classification of the neoplasm as an oligoastrocytoma, a low-grade mixed glial tumor.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of poorly differentiated keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma originating in the nasopharynx. The primary tumor invades the nasopharynx, classifying it as pT4 according to the TNM staging system. The tumor was also found to have perineural invasion, though no venous or lymphatic invasion was noted. The excisional margins are uninvolved by carcinoma, with the closest margin located 1 mm from invasive carcinoma in the soft tissue. Lymph node analysis shows metastatic carcinoma in 6 out of 51 lymph nodes, with extranodal extension present. Specifically, positive lymph nodes were detected in the right neck (2/11), right neck level IV (1/8), levels II and III (1/7), and left neck level IV (2/17). Some of these nodes exhibited extracapsular extension, including soft tissue extension forming a nodule of carcinoma. Remaining sampled sites, including the left tongue base, right posterior pharyngeal wall, and various nasopharyngeal and soft palate excisions, were negative for tumor, although focal high-grade dysplasia was identified on the soft palate uvula. There was chronic inflammation in some regions, notably in the 2nd nasopharyngeal margin and additional nasopharyngeal site. Overall, the findings indicate a pN2c classification with bilateral or contralateral lymph node metastasis, without distant metastasis assessment (oMx).

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of Squamous Cell Carcinoma, not otherwise specified, located in the left lower lobe of the lung. The tumor exhibits aggressive characteristics, including infiltration of the visceral pleura, and is identified as a high-grade lesion (G3). Staging reveals it is at pT2b, indicating significant local invasion. Lymph node involvement is noted with two out of thirty-three lymph nodes positive for metastasis, classifying it as pN1. Metastatic status to distant sites cannot be assessed based on the available data (pMx). The findings underscore the importance of comprehensive clinical correlation and potential further evaluation for metastatic disease.

The pathological examination of the left breast mastectomy specimen revealed a moderately differentiated (Grade II/III) infiltrating lobular carcinoma with a Nottingham score of 6 out of 9, characterized by high tubule formation, moderate nuclear pleomorphism, and low mitotic activity. The tumor is firm and poorly defined, showing focal microcalcifications associated with invasive carcinoma and benign ducts, and lacks necrosis or venous/lymphatic invasion. The surgical margins are negative, with the closest margin being 0.5 cm from the deep tissue edge. There is an intraductal component of lobular carcinoma in situ, but no nipple or skin involvement is present. One sentinel lymph node examined previously was negative for metastasis. Estrogen receptors are highly positive, while progesterone receptors are negative. HER2/neu testing shows weak positivity by immunohistochemistry but is negative by FISH. Additional pathological findings include florid usual ductal hyperplasia, fibroadenomatoid changes, and fibrocystic changes with stromal fibrosis, cyst formation, apocrine metaplasia, and prior biopsy site changes. The absence of E-cadherin and the presence of 34betaE12 staining support lobular differentiation. The overall pathologic stage is pT2 N0 Mx.

The pathological findings pertain to a diagnosis of well-differentiated astrocytoma. The tumor is characterized by mildly hypercellular gray and white matter, with the hypercellularity more prominent in permanent sections compared to frozen sections. The tumor comprises glial cells with round to oval nuclei, displaying a small number of apoptotic figures but lacking perinuclear halos and other classic glioma features. Reactive astrocytes are present, but the infiltrating nature of the glioma is evident. No mitotic figures are observed. The presence of scattered MIB-1 reactive cells with a labeling index of 1.7% suggests a modestly proliferative neoplasm, consistent with low-grade histologic features. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not detailed in this pathology description. Overall, the findings are indicative of a moderately proliferative, well-differentiated astrocytoma.

The excised tissue from the sigmoid colon reveals a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma characterized by an exophytic, polypoid growth pattern. The tumor invades up to the muscularis propria without extending further. Importantly, the surgical margins are uninvolved, indicating that the tumor has been completely excised. Additionally, none of the five examined regional lymph nodes show any evidence of metastatic disease, suggesting a lack of lymphatic spread. These findings collectively support a localized malignancy with no regional lymphatic involvement and clear resection margins.

The pathological findings reveal a primary glial neoplasm located in the right parietal region, displaying oligodendrocytic features with a significantly elevated proliferation index. The tumor exhibits infiltrative and moderately pleomorphic glial cells, with relatively round nuclei and occasional perinuclear halos. Although features consistent with an oligodendroglioma are present, the classic tightly packed, honeycomb-like architecture with numerous perinuclear halos is not observed, likely due to tissue freezing artifacts, making a definitive diagnosis challenging. Regardless of this, the high proliferation index suggests a more aggressive behavior than typical grade II oligodendrogliomas. Scattered mitoses are present across the specimen; however, there is no evidence of endothelial proliferation or necrosis. Immunohistochemical analysis indicates a proliferation index consistently above standard thresholds, underscoring the tumor's aggressive nature. The final classification remains contingent on pending genetic analyses for the 1p, 19q co-deletion, which will further delineate its prognostic implications. Margins and lymph node involvement were not specified, indicating focus was maintained on primary tumor characteristics and cellular behavior.

The histopathological examination of the right breast, following multiple organ resection, reveals an invasive ductal carcinoma with features consistent with NHG1 grading. The tumor is located in the upper outer quadrant of the breast and demonstrates classic histological characteristics of ductal carcinoma, including low mitotic activity. Additional glandular tissue exhibits fibrocystic changes, simple ductal hyperplasia, and sclerosing adenosis. Notably, metastases are present in the axillary lymph nodes, with involvement in the lymph node capsule and perinodal tissue, along with vascular emboli. Out of the 22 axillary lymph nodes examined, two contain metastatic carcinoma. Immunohistochemical staining indicates the presence of estrogen receptors in a significant proportion of neoplastic cells (10-75%) and progesterone receptors in over 75% of neoplastic cells. HER2 protein testing yields a negative result, scoring 1+. Margins were not specifically commented on in the pathological description. The overall pathology indicates a primary diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast with lymphatic metastasis and vascular invasion, aligning with staging classifications pT1c and pN1a.

Histopathological examination of the partial resection of the transverse colon reveals a diagnosis of tubular and partially mucinous adenocarcinoma with a high-grade (G3) classification. The macroscopic assessment identified a cauliflower-shaped tumor that completely encircles the circumference of the intestine. This neoplasm extensively invades the muscularis propria and pericolonic adipose tissue. Importantly, the surgical excision margins are free of neoplastic involvement, indicating no residual tumor at the resection lines. Additionally, a separate polyp exhibiting tubular adenoma with mild dysplasia was identified outside the primary tumor. Microscopic examination of the omentum showed no evidence of metastatic disease. However, metastatic carcinoma was present in one out of eleven examined lymph nodes, indicating lymph node involvement (pN1a). These findings, consistent with a diagnosis of pT3, pN1a, place the patient in the Duke's C (Astler-Coller C2) stage, denoting a more advanced disease state with regional lymphatic spread but clear surgical margins.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of invasive high-grade urothelial carcinoma of the bladder and prostatic adenocarcinoma. The bladder carcinoma, measuring the greatest dimension beneath an ulcerated area, involves the left lateral wall and invades through the detrusor muscle into the perivesical soft tissue. Lymphovascular and perineural invasion are absent. Importantly, all surgical margins are clear of invasive carcinoma, and there is no tumor identified in the examined lymph nodes. Additionally, extensive therapy-related changes are noted alongside areas of flat urothelial carcinoma in situ. The pathological stage of the bladder tumor is pT3a NO MX. 

The prostatic adenocarcinoma, with a Gleason score of 3+4=7, involves a small portion of the right lobe and is accompanied by high-grade prostatic intraepithelial neoplasia. Similar to the bladder findings, surgical margins are free of tumor, and none of the examined lymph nodes contain carcinoma. The pathological stage of the prostate cancer is pT2a NO MX. 

Overall, the radical cystoprostatectomy specimen shows no evidence of metastasis, and all evaluated lymph nodes from both the right and left pelvic regions are benign. The pathological stage for both the bladder and prostate tumors denotes localized disease without nodal involvement or distant metastasis.

The histopathological examination of the resected sigmoid colon and upper rectum reveals an 8.5 cm moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma located at the rectosigmoid junction. The tumor extends through the muscularis propria into the subserosal adipose tissue but does not breach the serosal surface. All surgical margins, including proximal, distal, and circumferential, are uninvolved by the tumor. The adenocarcinoma is 4 mm away from the closest serosal margin. No macroscopic tumor perforation, lymph-vascular invasion, or perineural invasion is identified. Additionally, there is evidence of tumor deposits. Pathologic staging indicates a primary tumor classification of pT3, with regional lymph node involvement (pN2b) evidenced by metastasis in 8 out of 15 examined lymph nodes. No distant metastasis is applicable. Other noteworthy findings include the presence of a tubular adenoma and serosal adhesions. The anastomotic rings show no pathological abnormalities.

The pathological evaluation of the resection material from the rectum reveals the presence of non-inflamed diverticula in the proximal section and clear tumor-free oral and aboral resection margins. A locally advanced, ulcerated, moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma was identified, which extends deeply into the perimuscular fatty tissue and approaches within 2 mm of the perirectal resection margin. Microscopic examination shows the adenocarcinoma with lymphovascular invasion, indicating the presence of tumor cells in both lymphatic and blood vessels. Additionally, there is significant regional lymph node involvement, with metastases detected in numerous nodes. The tumor is classified as moderately differentiated (G2), staged as pT3 indicating deep tissue infiltration, and exhibits lymphatic (L1) and vascular (V1) invasion, but the resection margins are clear of tumor cells (R0). The lymph node status is pN2, with metastatic disease present in 20 out of 34 examined lymph nodes.

The pathology findings indicate a final diagnosis of mucinous adenocarcinoma in the left lower lobe of the lung. Histologically, the tumor is categorized as well to moderately differentiated. Pathologic staging reveals tumor involvement without penetration through the visceral pleura and no evidence of large vessel invasion; however, there is noted lymphatic invasion. Margins of resection, including bronchial and vascular margins, are negative for carcinoma. Metastatic carcinoma is identified in a subset of examined lymph nodes—particularly one hilar lymph node and two proximal bronchial lymph nodes. Specifically, no metastasis is found in the inferior pulmonary ligament or subcarinal lymph nodes, while one focus of metastatic carcinoma is seen in another hilar lymph node. Additional pathological findings include multifocal nodular areas of bronchiolar pneumonitis exhibiting a BOOP-like pattern, centered around non-neoplastic bronchioles. Immunohistochemical analysis shows the neoplastic cells are positive for CK7 and TTF-1 and negative for CK20, supporting the diagnosis of primary lung carcinoma.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of oligoastrocytoma, WHO grade II. Microscopically, the tumor is characterized by marked hypercellularity with diffuse parenchymal infiltration. The predominant cellular phenotype resembles astrocytoma, while a notable proportion of cells demonstrate features consistent with oligodendroglioma, including poorly formed paranuclear halos and a microgemistocytic phenotype. Cellular atypia is generally mild, though there is a presence of many moderately atypical nuclei. Mitotic figures are rare, and no microvascular proliferation is observed. The MIB-labeling index, which indicates proliferative activity, varies within the tumor and peaks at 6.1%. This intermediate level of proliferation raises concerns about potential aggressive behavior, although it does not meet the criteria for anaplastic (grade III) classification. Margins and lymph node involvement are not applicable due to the infiltrative nature of the tumor within the central nervous system.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of anaplastic oligodendroglioma, WHO grade III, situated in the left frontal lobe of the brain, with an enhancing nodule. The tumor is characterized by a proliferation of neoplastic cells resembling oligodendrocytes, displaying prominent perinuclear halos, rounded hyperchromatic nuclei, and few cellular processes. The tumor exhibits moderate to marked cytologic atypia, arranged in sheets, and infiltrative behavior into both gray and white matter. Features such as microvascular proliferation, early necrosis, and isolated discrete necrosis are evident, with the latter potentially indicative of a previous biopsy site. Numerous mitotic figures (up to eight per 10 high power fields) and visible microgemicytes are noted. Additionally, microcalcifications and branching thin-walled vessels, hallmark features of oligodendrogliomas, are present. The calculated MIB-1 labeling index stands at 24.4%, highlighting the tumor’s proliferative activity. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not addressed in this specific examination, indicating a need for further investigation to ascertain comprehensive staging. The pathological characteristics confirm the aggressive nature of this anaplastic oligodendroglioma.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of an anaplastic astrocytoma, grade 3, located in the left temporal region of the brain. Histological examination reveals a diffusely infiltrative astrocytic neoplasm characterized by mild to moderate hypercellularity and atypia. The tumor cells resemble fibrillary astrocytes, with occasional perinuclear halos suggested but not definitively identified. A rare mitotic figure is observed, but there is no evidence of microvascular proliferation or necrosis. Immunohistochemically, the neoplastic cells are negative for IDH1, with a minority of cells showing focal positivity for p53. Areas of MIB-1 reactivity are present, and the most proliferative regions exhibit a MIB-1 labeling index of 13.3%, indicative of a high-grade astrocytoma. Further details regarding the resection margins and lymph node involvement are not provided.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive squamous cell carcinoma, originating from the left oral mucosa. The excised left buccal mucosa displayed signs of acute and chronic inflammation, ulceration, and mild atypia, but showed no evidence of high-grade dysplasia or malignancy. The well-differentiated, keratinizing carcinoma had a significant depth of invasion and bone invasion, but without lymphovascular space invasion. Perineural invasion was identified, along with areas of carcinoma in situ. Notably, the mucosal, soft tissue, and bone surgical margins were free of carcinoma. Lymph node dissection revealed a total of 44 nodes examined, with one node from level 2 containing metastatic carcinoma, but without extracapsular extension. Other biopsied lymph nodes, including those from levels 1 and the submandibular triangle, were benign. The left submandibular gland and anterior mandibular marrow margin showed no evidence of malignancy, and the anterior mandibular margin re-excision was also negative for malignancy. The proximal left inferior alveolar nerve margin contained benign nerve and vascular tissue. The overall pTNM staging was determined to be pT4aN1.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of poorly differentiated urothelial carcinoma with extensive infiltration, including involvement of the entire bladder wall, perivesical fatty tissue, right seminal vesicle, and right ureter. There is multifocal invasion of lymphatic and blood vessels, and dysplasia in preserved urothelial sections. Tumor margin status is positive (R1), indicating infiltration up to the surgical resection margin, including the pelvic wall and right ureter. Lymph node evaluation indicates carcinoma infiltration in four out of seven lymph nodes on the right side, with the left side showing four tumor-free nodes out of four examined. Additionally, the prostate exhibits poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma confined to the organ, predominantly in the right peripheral area, with a Gleason score of 4+5=9. The urothelial carcinoma has been classified as pT4b, L1, V1, R1, pN2 (4/11). Notably, carcinoma in situ was previously identified at the right ureter resection margin, though follow-up resection material showed no residual tumor or dysplasia.

The pathological evaluation of the sigmoid colon reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated colonic adenocarcinoma. The tumor is classified as grade 2 out of 3 and exhibits a napkin ring-like circumferential growth pattern. Notably, the carcinoma invades through the muscularis propria and extends into the pericolonic fat. Surgical margins of resection are free of malignant involvement, with the nearest margin being 6 cm away from the tumor. There is a prominent host response, marked by acute and chronic inflammation, along with the presence of a pericolonic abscess likely associated with a diverticulum. Importantly, no vascular invasion is observed. Thirty pericolonic lymph nodes were examined and found to be free of malignancy, confirming no lymph node involvement. The proximal margin biopsy of the sigmoid colon is also negative for malignancy. Therefore, the tumor staging is determined to be pT3 N0, indicating tumor invasion into pericolonic tissues without regional lymph node metastasis.

The excised lung tissue revealed a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma located in the right upper lobe. The tumor extends to the visceral pleura. Lymph node analysis indicates metastasis, with 1 out of 1 intrathoracic lymph node found to be positive. Immunohistochemical staining demonstrated positivity for CD15, CK7, TTF1, and CEA, while being negative for CK20 and CDX2. Margin status, lymphatic invasion, venous invasion, and evidence of neo-adjuvant treatment were not specified in the findings. Other tumor nodules were also not specified. The overall diagnosis is a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma with metastatic spread to at least one intrathoracic lymph node.

The pathological assessment reveals a diagnosis of high grade papillary urothelial carcinoma located on the posterior wall of the urinary bladder. The tumor infiltrates the outer one-third of the muscularis propria, indicating a TNM stage of pT2b. There is evidence of squamous differentiation, while glandular differentiation is absent. Associated urothelial dysplasia is observed, and lymphatic invasion is considered probably present. Despite this, all twelve examined lymph nodes, including those from the right and left iliac and obturator regions, are negative for tumor involvement, confirming a pNO staging. Additionally, the prostate shows benign prostatic hyperplasia and high-grade prostatic intraepithelial neoplasia, but no invasive tumor. Examination of the seminal vesicles, as well as resection margins of the prostate, urethra, ureters, and surrounding soft tissue, reveal no tumor presence, suggesting clear surgical margins.

The pathological evaluation revealed multifocal, multicentric infiltrating mixed ductal and lobular carcinoma of the left breast. The modified radical mastectomy specimen displayed angiolymphatic invasion and a significant component of lobular carcinoma in situ, which accounted for 10% of the tumor. Tumor involvement extended to the nipple with focal invasive carcinoma in the deep stromal tissue. The margins were clear of both invasive and in situ carcinoma. The nipple and breast skin exhibited duct ectasia and chronic inflammation. Hormone receptor status was positive for estrogen and progesterone receptors, while HER-2/neu was negative. There were significant biopsy site changes and associated fibrocystic changes with ductal epithelial hyperplasia, columnar cell changes, and a microscopic peripheral ductal papilloma. Notably, the carcinoma metastasized to numerous lymph nodes; 27 out of 27 examined lymph nodes contained metastases, including the sentinel lymph node, both from the axillary tail and level 3 lymph nodes, with some exhibiting extracapsular spread. The pectoralis minor muscle was free of tumor. Based on these findings, the tumor was staged as pT3, and the Nottingham score for the invasive component graded the carcinoma at 6, indicative of a moderate grade 2 neoplasm.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma located in the apical posterior segment of the left upper lobe of the lung. The primary tumor, confined to the lung and measuring less than 3 cm, shows no direct extension or large vessel invasion. However, there is lymphatic invasion present. Metastatic carcinoma is identified in 7 of the 11 excised lymph nodes, including one node from L-11, six nodes from the regional nine lymph nodes, and one node from Station 6. The margins are uninvolved by invasive carcinoma, and there is no evidence of pleural involvement. Immunostaining results indicate that the neoplastic cells are positive for P63 and negative for TTF-1 and Napsin-A, with mucicarmine stain being negative and elastin stain confirming the pleural integrity. Additionally, significant tumor necrosis is noted, with neoplastic cells present in multiple lymphatic channels separate from the primary tumor mass. The findings support a staging of pTl with pN2 regional lymph node involvement and pMx distant metastasis status being undetermined.
