Bilateral pelvic lymph nodes, both left and right, are free of metastatic involvement, with a total of 16 nodes examined collectively. Special stains and further microscopic evaluations, including CD31 and D2-40, were performed, affirming these diagnostic interpretations. The diagnoses and staging follow the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) guidelines, specifying the bladder cancer staging as pT2b and prostate cancer staging as pT2a.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of adenocarcinoma of the right upper lobe of the lung, which is poorly differentiated and unifocal, located at the periphery of the lung. The tumor demonstrates no visceral pleural invasion and is confined to the lung, although there are findings suspicious for lymphovascular space invasion. Surgical margins are free of tumor, with the cancer maintaining distances from bronchial, parenchymal, and pleural margins. Both examined lymph nodes, including one benign hyperplastic level 9 lymph node, show no evidence of metastatic carcinoma. Additional pathological findings include patchy fibrosis and chronic inflammation within the lung parenchyma. The tumor has been staged as pT2b, N0, and a lung carcinoma mutation panel evaluation is pending for further molecular characterization.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma of the mandible, characterized by deep invasive poorly to moderately differentiated tumor tissue. The carcinoma extends to the anterior mandible margin and is within 2 mm of the posterior margin, indicating close proximity to surgical boundaries. Additionally, metastatic squamous cell carcinoma is noted in the left neck dissection, with poorly differentiated carcinoma present in 4 out of 21 lymph nodes across levels I, II, and V. The salivary glands are free of tumor involvement. The medullary cavity shows bone with no evidence of tumor, and several regions of the oral cavity including the anterior and right lateral floor of the mouth, right anterior tonsillar pillar, retromolar trigone, buccal mucosa, labial mucosa, and right medial pterygoid are all confirmed to have squamous mucosa without tumor presence. This detailed assessment underscores the primary malignancy in the mandible along with regional lymph node metastasis but no distant spread to other examined oral and mucosal sites.

The pathological examination revealed high-grade invasive urothelial carcinoma of the bladder, with significant histological markers including flat in situ carcinoma and infiltrating growth patterns. The tumor had invaded the perivesical soft tissues but did not demonstrate multicentricity and had not extended into the right ureter or urethra. Surgical margins were free of tumor, though vascular and perineural invasion was present. Additionally, the prostate exhibited a separate adenocarcinoma, classified as Gleason score 7, with indications of high-grade prostatic intraepithelial neoplasia confined to one lobe without seminal vesicle involvement or extraprostatic extension. The left pelvic lymph nodes were benign with no evidence of metastatic disease among the seven examined. The left vas deferens and adrenal gland were also benign. Both kidneys showed changes indicative of end-stage renal disease, including diffuse glomerulosclerosis, thyroidization, tubular atrophy, and chronic interstitial inflammation. The renal pelvic urothelial mucosa presented with benign proliferative changes. The findings suggest a complex pathology with serious implications due to the multifocal nature and distinct histologic types of both bladder and prostate cancers, alongside significant renal pathology.

The detailed pathological analysis confirms the diagnosis of oligodendroglioma, WHO Grade II, involving two resected brain tumor specimens. Histologically, both resections exhibit features consistent with oligodendroglioma without evidence of higher-grade transformation. The tumor was not associated with any additional pathological findings. Immunohistochemical studies reveal diffuse positivity for IDH-1, supporting the diagnosis. The proliferation index, assessed by MIB-1 immunohistochemistry, shows up to 10% proliferating cells. Fluorescence in-situ hybridization (FISH) analysis demonstrates no deletions in chromosomes 1p and 19q, indicating an unfavorable prognosis and potentially reduced survival outcomes. However, the MGMT methylation assay detected significant promoter methylation of the MGMT gene, which correlates with increased sensitivity to alkylating agents, suggesting a potentially favorable response to temozolomide therapy. Lymph node involvement was not addressed within the provided material, and no other ancillary studies or additional molecular findings were noted that would impact the current diagnosis or treatment considerations.

The pathological analysis reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated (WHO grade 3) transitional cell carcinoma of the bladder (pT3b pN0 pMx). The tumor is predominantly solid with 5% showing squamous differentiation and contains foci of necrosis. It is located on the left lateral and superior walls and extends through the bladder wall into the perivesical soft tissue but does not invade the vagina. The closest margin is the left lateral serosal margin, which is 2mm away; however, all surgical margins, including the urethral and ureteric margins, are free of tumor infiltration. Examination of lymph nodes from the right obturator, right external iliac, left external iliac, and left obturator regions, totaling 15 nodes, shows no evidence of metastatic involvement. There is no lymphovascular or perineural invasion noted. Overall, the tumor has not metastasized to the lymph nodes, and all resection margins are clear, indicating a successful removal of the primary malignancy.

The pathological examination of the right breast lumpectomy specimen revealed an invasive ductal carcinoma with tubulo-lobular features, with the tumor extending into the dermis. A separate focus of invasive ductal carcinoma was also identified, demonstrating dermal involvement. The tumor was classified as Grade I based on its histologic characteristics, with low mitotic activity, intermediate nuclear grade, and tubulo-lobular architecture. Notably, no intraductal carcinoma was identified, and the excisional biopsy margins were free of tumor, with the closest anterior margin being 2 mm away from the tumor. There was evidence of blood vessel and lymphatic invasion in the dermal lymphatics. The axillary lymph nodes, including two sentinel nodes, were free of tumor involvement. Immunohistochemical studies showed strong expression of estrogen and progesterone receptors in more than 90% of the invasive tumor nuclei, while the Her2/neu antigen status was non-amplified. The tumor staging was classified as pT1c(m)N0MX according to the AJCC 7th edition guidelines. Additional gross examination of the lumpectomy specimen identified hemorrhagic fat necrosis at the previous biopsy site. No tumor involvement was observed in the inked edges of the skin.

The patient, a female, has been diagnosed with infiltrative ductal carcinoma of the breast, classified as a primary tumor. Histological analysis reveals a tumor grade of 2, indicating a moderate level of differentiation. Pathological staging indicates T2, signifying a tumor that extends beyond the breast duct but remains confined within the breast tissue without extensive involvement. Nodal involvement is present at N1, highlighting the presence of cancer cells in nearby lymph nodes, while M0 indicates there is no distant metastasis. Margins have not been specified in the provided data. No previous treatment has been administered, and an additional blood sample was taken, which has been confirmed as normal upon examination. The overall pathological findings suggest a locally advanced breast cancer with regional lymph node involvement but no evidence of distant spread, crucial for guiding therapeutic strategies.

The histopathological examination of the resected lesion from the right side of the tongue reveals an invasive squamous keratinized carcinoma (G2), diagnosed as carcinoma planoepitheliale keratodes invasivum. The tumor demonstrates moderate differentiation and shows invasive characteristics. The excised specimen margins indicate the presence of a minimum margin of healthy tissue measuring 0.4 cm from the side surface and 0.5 cm from the base, suggesting that the resection margins are narrowly clear of the tumor. Lymphocytic reaction is noted in the peritumoral area, indicating an immune response. Additionally, a fragment of the salivary gland included in the specimen shows no evidence of tumor infiltration. Lymph node involvement is not explicitly mentioned, implying no detected metastasis in the provided sections. The pathologic staging is classified as pTl, indicating a localized primary tumor.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma characterized by discrete areas of in situ spread. The tumor demonstrates infiltration into the superficial corial connective tissue, yet there is no evidence of skin ulceration. Notably, there is focal involvement of tumor cells within the lymphatic vessels. The tumor margins are free of carcinoma, indicating a successful surgical resection with no residual tumor cells at the resection margins. Lymph node analysis reveals metastasis to one lymph node, confirming the involvement and setting the classification at pT2N1aL1V0R0, corresponding to a high-grade malignancy (G III). These findings highlight the aggressive nature of the carcinoma, underscoring the importance of vigilant post-operative management and potential adjuvant therapy to address the nodal involvement and lymphatic invasion.

The pathological evaluation of the left breast tissue and axilla from a modified radical mastectomy revealed a poorly differentiated infiltrating duct carcinoma, categorized as nuclear grade III. The primary tumor was localized in the upper outer quadrant. Metastatic carcinoma was identified in one intramammary lymph node, but all 37 axillary lymph nodes were free of metastatic disease. Additional findings included periductal chronic mastitis distant from the primary tumor site. The microscopic analysis showed no evidence of venous or lymphatic invasion, and the margins were close, measuring 1 mm at the nearest point. The tumor markers indicated that the estrogen receptor (ER) was 95% positive, progesterone receptor (PR) was negative, and Her-2/neu was scored at 1+, with a high Ki-67 index of 95%. Based on these findings, the TNM staging corresponds to pT2 pN1, summarizing to stage IIB. The overall summary highlights significant pathological features including the diagnosis, tumor characterization, lymph node involvement, and notable biomarkers which are instrumental in guiding the subsequent treatment plan.

The pathological examination of the occipital tumor reveals a high-grade glioma, provisionally diagnosed as an anaplastic oligodendroglioma (WHO Grade III). Microscopic analysis shows extensive infiltration of malignant glioma cells with pleomorphic and hyperchromatic nuclei, small nucleoli, and perinuclear cytoplasmic clearing. The tumor tissue contains areas of necrosis, frequent mitotic figures, apoptotic bodies, and widespread endothelial proliferation. Immunohistochemical staining indicates substantial GFAP positivity, highlighting mini-gemistocytes, and scattered cells staining positively for p53. The proliferation index, indicated by MIB-1 (Ki67), is approximately 10-15%. Despite the absence of 1p 19q codeletions and EGFR amplification as confirmed by FISH studies, the diagnosis remains anaplastic oligodendroglioma. However, the possibility of a high-grade mixed oligoastrocytoma (WHO Grade IV) cannot be entirely excluded due to the necrosis present. The tumor's high-grade malignancy features, including extensive necrosis and endothelial proliferation, support the initial intraoperative assessment of a high-grade glioma.

The pathological findings indicate the presence of invasive lobular carcinoma in the left breast and invasive ductal carcinoma in the right breast. The left breast tumor is characterized as grade 2 invasive lobular carcinoma, while the right breast tumor is a grade 2 invasive ductal carcinoma with extensive ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS). Both tumors have negative surgical resection margins, ensuring that no residual tumor is left at the cut edges of the excised tissues. Lymph node involvement is confirmed, with metastatic carcinoma detected in one out of twelve axillary lymph nodes on the left side and multiple sentinel lymph nodes on both sides: one out of one on the left side and two out of six on the right side. Notably, the largest lymph node metastasis on the left has extranodal extension. Immunohistochemical analysis reveals that the left breast tumor is estrogen and progesterone receptor-positive and HER2-negative. Additional axillary tissues from the left reveal one lymph node negative for metastases. Further, extensive DCIS is present in the right breast, involving approximately 75% of the tissue. The final pathological staging for the left breast is pT2 N1a, and further biomarker results for the right breast are pending. These findings indicate a multifocal and multicentric disease process with varying degrees of lymphatic spread necessitating comprehensive management.

The essential pathological findings indicate that the primary diagnosis is squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, located in the right upper lobe of the lung. The tumor, which was resected, showed no involvement of the pleura and surgical margins were clear, confirming no residual malignancy. A series of lymph node excisions from various levels, including II, IV, VII, VIII, IX, X, and XI, showed no evidence of malignancy except for the presence of benign conditions such as hyalinized granulomas, some with calcification. The bronchial margin also tested negative for malignancy. Overall, the pathologic stage is defined as pT1a NO, indicating a small tumor size (less than 3 cm) with no regional lymph node involvement. This comprehensive evaluation underscores the absence of metastatic spread, both within the lymph nodes and at the surgical margins. The patient's clinical background includes a mediastinal lymph node and a nodule in the right upper lobe, both addressed through the detailed pathological assessment.

The pathological examination of the left frontal brain biopsies revealed a diagnosis of grade 3 (of 4) oligoastrocytoma, predominantly astrocytic, according to WHO grading criteria. Immunohistochemical analysis demonstrated that neoplastic cells with astrocytic morphology were positive for GFAP and exhibited overexpression of p53 protein in the majority of tumor cells. Additionally, the MIB-1 labeling index was found to be moderate, supporting the grade III diagnosis. Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) studies indicated no evidence of 1p or 19q deletions, which are commonly observed in gliomas. However, a significant proportion of cells displayed 3-4 copies of chromosomes 1 and 19, suggesting possible tumor tetraploidy or aneuploidy. Preliminary frozen section consultations identified atypical cells consistent with an infiltrating glioma, favoring astrocytoma. The tumor's invasive nature and moderate proliferative index highlight the need for further therapeutic intervention, though margins and lymph node involvement were not specified in the findings.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of invasive squamous cell carcinoma in the left oral cavity involving the oral tongue, floor of mouth, and oropharynx with highly infiltrative, keratinizing, and moderately differentiated features. The carcinoma extends to the left gingival and left lateral and medial deep soft tissue margins, indicating positive margins. Notably, there is the presence of perineural invasion, although no vascular invasion is identified. The bone is free of tumor involvement. Pathologically, the tumor is staged as pT4a with no regional lymph node involvement (0/45 lymph nodes) and an absence of distant metastasis assessment (pMX). Multiple biopsies and excisions from surrounding areas, including the left floor of mouth, mylohyoid, and additional margins, show no tumor presence. Both right and left neck dissections reveal benign lymph nodes with no significant pathological changes in the submandibular gland or parotid tissue. The composite oral cavity resection and intraoperative consultations reinforce these findings, confirming the diagnosis and extent of malignancy.

The essential pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of anaplastic oligodendroglioma with a predominant background of low-grade oligodendroglioma. The tumor demonstrates diffuse infiltration into both gray and white matter, with tumor cells exhibiting intermediate-sized round nuclei and variable perinuclear halos. Although there is notable microcystic myxoid change in some regions, most of the tumor appears solid with areas of confluent hypercellularity and moderate atypia. Mitotic figures are rare, with only three observed in over 100 high-power fields. There is focal microvascular proliferation, but it is not widespread. MIB-1 immunohistochemistry indicates a variable proliferative index, with most tumor areas showing a labeling index of less than 1%, though some regions exhibit a higher labeling index of up to 8.8%. Lymph node involvement and surgical margin status are not specified. Collectively, these findings are consistent with anaplastic oligodendroglioma set against a backdrop of low-grade oligodendroglioma, marked by its diffuse infiltrative nature and variable proliferative activity.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of adenocarcinoma in the left upper lobe of the lung, characterized by mixed acinar and bronchioloalveolar types. The tumor is moderately to poorly differentiated and falls under the pT2a category according to the 7th Edition of AJCC, meaning the tumor's greatest dimension is between 3 and 5 cm. Margins are uninvolved by invasive carcinoma, suggesting that the surgical resection was likely successful in removing the primary tumor entirely. However, metastatic carcinoma was present in 3 out of 18 regional lymph nodes, specifically in ipsilateral peribronchial lymph nodes, classifying it as pN1. There is no evidence of direct extension of the tumor beyond these areas, and both venous/arterial and lymphatic invasion statuses are indeterminate. Additional findings include emphysematous changes with subpleural bulla formation. Notably, KRAS mutation analysis returned positive results, with mutations identified in codon 12 or 13, thus negating the need for EGFR testing. Other resected lymph nodes from various stations were negative for tumor involvement, underscoring the localized spread of the disease.

The final pathology revealed a diagnosis of poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma of the right lower lobe. The tumor was identified in the initial wedge resection specimen and measured 2.5 cm in greatest dimension. The surgical margins were uninvolved by the tumor. Histopathological examination did not reveal any evidence of venous, arterial, or lymphatic invasion. The completion lobectomy specimen showed no residual malignancy, and the associated two lymph nodes were free of metastatic disease. Examination of the level 12 lymph nodes identified metastasis in four out of the submitted nodes. However, the level 7 and level 11 lymph nodes demonstrated no evidence of metastatic involvement across seven and three nodes, respectively. The level 9 specimen contained only fibrovascular tissue with no identifiable lymph nodes. Overall, the thorough examination delineated the primary tumor characteristics and the absence of metastatic spread in most lymph nodes evaluated.

The pathological findings confirm a diagnosis of invasive squamous carcinoma, characterized by a well to moderately differentiated ulcerating tumor with infiltrative borders and a depth of invasion of 3.5 cm. Perineural and vascular invasions are absent. Examination of dissected lymph nodes reveals metastatic squamous carcinoma presence in several nodes: one out of five in right Level IB with extranodal extension, five out of fourteen in right Level IIA with extranodal extension, two out of ten in right Level IV with extranodal extension, one out of four in additional right Level IB, and one out of one in left Level IIA with extranodal extension. All other evaluated lymph nodes are negative for tumor involvement. Various nerve specimens, mucosal, and muscle tissues examined are free of tumor. The gross tumor measures 6.5 cm in its largest dimension, emanating from a near-total glossectomy specimen, and shows thick cords greater than four cells. The tumor margin status is not explicitly provided. Additionally, acute and chronic inflammation is noted in the right anterior and posterior mandibular gingiva, with focal atypical epithelial nests observed in the right retromolar trigone, though no definitive tumor is identified in these areas.

The histopathological findings reveal an invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast (Carcinoma ductale invasivum mammae dextrae), located in the upper outer quadrant without involvement of the nipple. The tumor is characterized by high-grade histology (NHG3) demonstrating extensive mitotic activity. Notably, there is massive vascular invasion (Invasio carcinomatosa vasorum massiva) and metastasis to multiple axillary lymph nodes (No V/VIII), with carcinoma also infiltrating the perinodal tissue and forming vascular emboli. Immunohistochemical studies show strong positivity for estrogen and progesterone receptors, present in over 75% of the neoplastic cell nuclei. HER2 protein expression scored 2+ while further FISH testing for HER2 gene amplification was negative. Microscopic examination of the surrounding tissue identified fibrous mastopathy. The tumor margins and skin are not addressed in this summary but are critical for surgical management and prognosis. The combined pathological staging is pT2, pN2a, reflecting tumor size and nodal involvement.

The specimen reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma, characterized by focal marked intraductal patterns of spread. The tumor is classified as NOS (Not Otherwise Specified) and graded as G III, indicating high-grade malignancy. Pathological staging identifies the tumor as pT2, reflecting a certain extent of local invasion, with evidence of lymph node involvement (N1a). Notably, there is no evidence of lymphovascular invasion (LOV0). However, the margin status remains indeterminate (RX), thus necessitating further clinical correlation and potentially additional surgical intervention to achieve clear margins. Collectively, these pathological findings suggest an aggressive tumor with a need for comprehensive treatment planning.

The diagnosis is an invasive poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma located in the right lower lobe of the lung. The primary tumor exhibits characteristics of aggressive malignancy, including extensive necrosis, abscess formation, and the presence of multiple satellite nodules. Pathological examination confirms visceral pleural invasion and angiolymphatic invasion, indicating further spread beyond the primary tumor site. Despite these invasive features, the margins of the resected specimen are free of tumor, suggesting complete excision of the identifiable mass. However, the interlobar and anterior hilar lymph nodes show metastasis, while other regional lymph nodes, including peribronchial, right lower lobe, and subcarinal, do not exhibit carcinoma but highlight benign reactive changes. Additionally, the background lung parenchyma reveals emphysematous changes. No significant abnormalities or malignancy are identified in the resected segment of the 6th rib or the right upper lobe. The tumor stage is determined to be T4 N1 Mx, reflecting the primary tumor's size and extension, nodal involvement, and undetermined metastatic status.

The pathological analysis of the right modified radical mastectomy specimen reveals a multifocal invasive ductal carcinoma with poor nuclear grade, alongside extensive ductal carcinoma in situ of the comedo type. The invasive carcinoma measures 1.8 by 1.2 by 1.0 cm, and approximately 30% of this mass comprises in-situ ductal carcinoma. Notably, the tumor is situated 1.3 cm from the deep resection margin, and the skin, nipple, and deep margins are confirmed to be free of tumor involvement. Lymph node examination shows no evidence of metastatic spread, with all five sampled lymph nodes being free of tumor. Vascular permeation by the tumor cells is observed, indicating an aggressive behavior. Additionally, concomitant pathologic findings include hemorrhage and hemosiderin-laden macrophages consistent with prior biopsy sites, as well as fibrocystic changes in the surrounding breast tissue. Immunohistochemical staining reveals the tumor to be HER2/neu positive (score 3+), with distinct complete membrane staining in 90% of tumor cells, while the estrogen receptor is negative, and the progesterone receptor expression is borderline at 20%.

The essential pathological findings include a diagnosis of a left lung adenocarcinoma and a thymoma (type AB). The lung tumor was moderately differentiated and measured 2.5 cm, exhibiting visceral pleural invasion. Margins were uninvolved, with the closest parenchymal and bronchial resection margins at 0.4 cm and 0.9 cm respectively. Vascular invasion was absent. One out of eight hilar lymph nodes tested positive for metastatic carcinoma. The pathological stage for the lung adenocarcinoma is pT2a pN1. 

The thymoma, also 2.5 cm, showed microscopic trans-capsular invasion, indicating type AB thymoma, with involved margins and no lymphovascular invasion identified. Regional lymph nodes were not sampled, and the thymoma reached pathologic stage IIa (R1) by modified Masaoka criteria. Immunohistochemical staining was positive for cytokeratin 5/6 and negative for neuroendocrine markers. 

Frozen sections of multiple regional lymph nodes, including levels 5 and 6, were negative for tumor. The final assessment provides a comprehensive characterization of both primary tumors, their invasive properties, lymph node involvement, and margin status.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of anaplastic astrocytoma, Grade III. Microscopically, the tumor is a moderately hypercellular glial neoplasm characterized by atypical fibrillary astrocytes, and it includes several bizarre, multinucleated cells. Critical features such as microvascular proliferation and tumor necrosis are not observed, and only a very rare mitotic figure is present. Despite the low mitotic activity, the cytologic features display unequivocal anaplasia. The tumor lacks eosinophilic granular bodies, Rosenthal fibers, and microcystic degeneration. Additionally, the observed MIB-1 labeling index is calculated at 6.5% in areas of highest proliferative activity, consistent with its moderately proliferative nature. Thus, the findings are in line with a moderately aggressive anaplastic astrocytoma (WHO Grade III). Margin status and lymph node involvement are not mentioned.

The final pathological evaluation identifies a primary diagnosis of poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma, solid type with clear cell change, located in the right upper lobe of the lung. The tumor is classified as pT3, indicating significant local extension but does not involve the pleural surface. Examination of the surgical margins reveals no involvement by invasive carcinoma. Seventeen regional lymph nodes, including those from multiple stations, are negative for metastatic carcinoma, confirming a pN0 status. Additionally, there is no evidence of venous, arterial, or lymphatic invasion. Immunohistochemical staining shows that the tumor cells are positive for TTF-1 and variably positive for Napsin-A, with patchy p63 staining. Background lung parenchyma exhibits emphysematous changes and organizing lung injury adjacent to the tumor. Molecular analysis reveals no mutations in either the KRAS or EGFR genes.

The pathological evaluation of the brain tumor from the right frontal lobe reveals a diagnosis of anaplastic astrocytoma, WHO Grade III. Microscopic examination shows a highly cellular glial tumor with diffusely infiltrating cells characterized by elongated pleomorphic nuclei and evidence of karyorrhexis, accompanied by scattered mitotic figures. There is no evidence of endothelial proliferation or tumor necrosis. Immunohistochemical staining indicates strong positivity for GFAP, and the MIB-1 nuclear proliferative marker shows an estimated labeling index of 5-10%. The IDH 1 marker is negative. Gross examination of the submitted specimens includes multiple tan-pink, soft to rubbery tissues. These pathological findings confirm the diagnosis and provide valuable information regarding the tumor's cellular activity, infiltrative nature, and proliferation rate, which are critical for guiding further clinical management and treatment planning.

The pathological evaluation of the right lung upper lobe lobectomy revealed an infiltrating moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with bronchioloalveolar features. The carcinoma extends into the pleura but does not involve the bronchial and vascular lung margins, indicating clear surgical margins. Examination of five hilar lymph nodes showed no evidence of tumor involvement, classifying the lymph node status as negative. Additionally, non-neoplastic changes such as congestion, edema, and focal hemorrhage were observed within the lung tissue. Based on these findings, the pathological stage of the carcinoma is designated as pT2pN0pMX, corresponding to Stage I of the disease.

The essential pathological findings from the radical cystoprostatectomy procedure included an invasive high-grade urothelial carcinoma, identified as grade 3, with substantial involvement of the bladder, including the trigone, right lateral wall, and anterior wall. The tumor infiltrated perivesical tissue but did not invade lymph nodes or distant organs, as confirmed by the pT3bN0M0 staging and the examination of nine pelvic lymph nodes, all of which were negative for malignancy. Additionally, incidental microscopic foci of prostatic adenocarcinoma were discovered within the right lobe of the prostate, characterized by a Gleason score of 6 (3+3), and were confined to the organ (pT2aN0M0). This prostate carcinoma also exhibited no lymphovascular or perineural invasion. Both distal ureteral biopsies indicated no dysplasia or malignancy, and the incidental appendectomy revealed an unremarkable appendix. The surgical margins were free of carcinoma, affirming no residual tumor presence at the edges of the resected tissue.

The pathological examination of the right breast lumpectomy specimen revealed a diagnosis of invasive lobular carcinoma. The primary tumor is characterized by a 2.8 cm mass and additional microscopic foci up to 5 mm in greatest dimension. Margins are free of tumor with the superior margin being the closest at approximately 4-5 mm. Sentinel lymph node analysis shows metastatic involvement in one out of two nodes, with a metastatic focus of 2 mm in the affected node. There is no lymphovascular invasion noted. Other significant histological findings include fibrocystic changes and usual ductal hyperplasia. Hormone receptor analysis revealed strong positivity for both estrogen (70%) and progesterone (over 90%) receptors, while HER-2/neu status is equivocal, pending further FISH analysis. The pathological staging is classified as T2 N1mic MX according to the AJCC criteria.

The pathological findings reveal an infiltrating ductal carcinoma, Nottingham grade III, located in the upper inner and upper central quadrants of the right breast. This high-grade tumor exhibits poor differentiation with a high score in tubule formation, nuclear pleomorphism, and mitotic activity. Importantly, all surgical resection margins are clear of tumor involvement, indicating complete local excision. Angiolymphatic invasion is not present. The surrounding non-neoplastic breast tissue displays nonproliferative fibrocystic changes, and calcifications are noted within malignant ducts. The right axillary lymph node dissection indicates metastasis in 2 of 9 nodes, without extranodal extension. In contrast, the highest right axillary lymph nodes are free of metastatic disease. Additionally, an intradermal nevus was identified in the excised right supranumerary nipple. The left breast mastectomy shows benign nonproliferative fibrocystic changes. Her-2/NEU status is pending for further characterization of the tumor.

The pathological analysis reveals a diagnosis of infiltrating medullary carcinoma of the right breast, characterized by a high histologic and nuclear grade. The tumor measures 1.3 by 1.2 by 1.0 cm and exhibits a syncytial pattern with an extensive lymphocytic reaction. Importantly, all surgical margins are clear of tumor infiltration. Within the right breast tissue, proliferative fibrocystic changes are noted, with no evidence of in situ carcinoma. Assessment of the right axillary dissection shows that one of the twenty-three lymph nodes contains metastatic carcinoma with extracapsular extension. Immunohistochemical staining for estrogen and progesterone receptors is negative, and HER-2/neu testing also yields a negative result, indicating no distinct intranuclear or complete membrane staining for these markers.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the cecum, with extensive tumor budding and invasion into subserosal adipose tissue and/or mesenteric fat. Lymphovascular and perineural invasion are identified, while gross tumor perforation is absent. Surgical margins are clear of tumor, and non-neoplastic bowel tissue appears unremarkable. No precursor lesions or dysplastic polyps are noted. Regional lymph node analysis indicates metastatic involvement in 12 out of 72 examined lymph nodes, and an additional tumor deposit is present in the pericolorectal soft tissue, classifying the lymph node stage as N2b. One of two lymph nodes at the base of the superior mesenteric artery and none of the middle colic lymph nodes showed metastasis. Tumor staging, according to the AJCC 7th Edition, is pT3. Immunohistochemical staining demonstrates the retention of DNA mismatch repair proteins MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, and PMS2 in the tumor, suggesting proficient mismatch repair status.

The pathological evaluation of the right breast reveals an invasive ductal carcinoma with central sclerosis, featuring an intermediate nuclear grade and a Nottingham histologic grade of 2. The carcinoma demonstrates lymphovascular invasion and extends close to the anterior margin, although re-excision has confirmed no residual tumor at this margin. Histologically, atypical ductal and lobular hyperplasia, along with columnar cell changes, are noted adjacent to the invasive carcinoma. The axillary dissection shows metastatic carcinoma in one of twelve lymph nodes, with no extranodal extension observed. The excision of level II lymph nodes reveals no tumor involvement in all nine nodes examined. Additionally, margins extending superiorly, inferiorly, and laterally from the original site are free of carcinoma, consisting predominantly of benign and fibroadipose tissue. Despite the presence of a central sclerotic area, it constitutes less than 30% of the tumor, not qualifying it as a subtype with a large central acellular zone. The nearest deep margin maintains a distance of 5 mm from the tumor. Overall, the findings suggest effective surgical resection with no residual carcinoma at the re-excised margins, although metastatic involvement of one lymph node is present.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of infiltrating and intraductal carcinoma in the right breast, associated with microcalcifications and mild vascular space involvement but without prominent vascular invasion. The tumor measures 2.2 x 2.4 x 2.4 cm, has a Nottingham score of 4/9 (tubules-1, nuclei-2, mitoses-1), and comprises approximately 51% cribriform intraductal carcinoma. The surgical margins are free of tumor, though the tumor is closest to the medial margin. Right sentinel lymph node biopsy reveals metastatic carcinoma in two of the three nodes examined, with no extension beyond the lymph node capsule. The right axillary dissection shows fifteen lymph nodes negative for tumor involvement. Additionally, a lipoma was excised from the left flank, consisting of mature adipose tissue. Further immunohistochemical evaluation for ER/PR and Her-2/NEU is pending.

The pathological findings confirm the diagnosis of residual invasive lobular carcinoma in the right breast. The tumor, classified as Grade 2 based on an architectural score of 3, a nuclear score of 2, and a mitotic score of 1, involves portions of the nipple but does not affect the overlying epidermis. Importantly, the deep margin of excision is free of carcinoma, indicating that the surgical margin is clear. Lymph node analysis revealed that the right axillary sentinel lymph node is free of metastatic carcinoma, corroborated by negative pancytokeratin staining. The overall TNM classification for this case is T3pNO (i-) MX, reflecting a primary tumor with no regional lymph node metastasis and the presence of invasive tumor cells limited to specific regions. The patient's preoperative diagnostic findings include hormone receptor positivity (ER and PR) and a high proliferation index (Ki-67). These elements together provide a comprehensive picture of the pathology and involved characteristics essential for guiding further clinical management.

The final pathological diagnosis following a right upper lobectomy identifies poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma confined to the lung. The primary tumor is classified as pT2a, with one out of fifteen examined lymph nodes showing involvement, corresponding to a regional lymph node designation of pN1. Distant metastasis could not be determined. Examination of the bronchial and vascular margins revealed no evidence of invasive carcinoma, confirming that they are uninvolved. Additional analysis indicated the presence of respiratory bronchiolitis. Intraoperative consultation confirmed the bronchial margin as negative for carcinoma. Tumor cells exhibited focal staining for p63 and CK5/6, with negative results for Napain and TTF-1. The diagnosis encompasses pathologic findings that indicate the extent and characteristics of the carcinoma, including clear resection margins and limited lymph node involvement.

The pathological examination of the right breast wide local excision specimen reveals an invasive Nottingham grade III ductal carcinoma. The tumor forms a mass with angiolymphatic invasion and no evidence of ductal carcinoma in situ. Surgical margins are all clear of tumor, with the closest margin being the deep margin, free by 0.3 cm. Additionally, metastatic adenocarcinoma was identified in multiple right axillary lymph nodes, with 6 out of 28 nodes being involved. Notably, three of the positive nodes are matted and demonstrate extracapsular extension into the surrounding soft tissue. The pathological stage of the tumor is categorized as pT1c, pN2 according to AJCC criteria. Her-2/NEU testing has been ordered on paraffin-embedded tissue for further evaluation.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma located in the subglottic region of the larynx. The specimen, obtained through laryngectomy, shows that the tumor has malignant characteristics. Importantly, surgical margins are uninvolved, indicating no residual tumor at the resection edges. However, there is evidence of regional metastatic spread, with 2 out of 6 lymph nodes positive for metastasis. No details were provided regarding neo-adjuvant treatment. These findings collectively suggest a localized but regionally advanced disease, warranting further clinical management and potential adjuvant therapy to address the lymphatic dissemination.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of infiltrating ductal carcinoma of the breast, characterized by a 1.5 cm nodule exhibiting histologic grade 2 and moderately differentiated nuclear features. The tumor is predominantly composed of nests and cords of large atypical cells, with rare tubules and a cribriform intraductal component. No vascular invasion is observed, and the inflammatory response is negligible. The carcinoma involves the inked surgical margins in certain sections, indicating the presence of invasive disease at the edges of the resected tissue. Examination of the axillary nodes identifies one node in the apex, which is free of tumor, and an additional eighteen lymph nodes from the axillary dissection, none of which contain metastatic carcinoma. Furthermore, microcalcifications are noted within benign lobules, and no other significant pathological abnormalities are identified.

The pathological findings of the right partial glossectomy reveal a diagnosis of moderately to poorly-differentiated, invasive squamous cell carcinoma. The carcinoma, measuring 1.6 cm in its greatest dimension with a depth of invasion of 1.2 cm, arises within a background of high-grade squamous intraepithelial neoplasia (carcinoma in-situ). There is no evidence of lymphovascular invasion. The margins of the resected specimen are clear of invasive carcinoma, with the closest margin being 0.3 cm from the deep aspect of the posterior lateral margin. All resection margins are free of in-situ carcinoma. The tumor is staged as pT1 NX MX according to the AJCC 6th edition criteria. Additional biopsies from the anterior, lateral, posterior, medial, anterior deep, and posterior deep aspects of the tongue show no evidence of in-situ or invasive carcinoma.

The pathological evaluation of the resected sigmoid colon and upper rectum reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated invasive adenocarcinoma located in the rectosigmoid region. This tumor is associated with a preexisting tubulovillous adenoma and extends into the submucosa but lacks perineural involvement. Although there is a suspicion of radial and serosal invasion, the tumor is positioned away from the deepest portion by a significant margin. Lymph node examination identifies metastases in two out of fifty-two nodes. The cancer is staged as pT1, pN1 according to the AJCC. The mismatch repair proteins MLH1, PMS2, MSH2, and MSH6 retain their expression, indicating no loss of these markers. Biopsies of the left liver lobe show subcapsular hepatic parenchyma with non-specific reactive changes but no increased fibrosis or stainable iron. Both the proximal and distal margins of the colon and rectum, respectively, are benign.

The pathological examination of the resected rectosigmoid colon revealed a diagnosis of moderately differentiated colorectal adenocarcinoma. The tumor has invaded all layers of the intestinal wall, extending into the adjacent periproctal soft tissue. Carcinomatous lymphangiosis was observed at the tumor margin, indicating the presence of cancer cells in the lymphatic vessels. The tumor was found to be at a close proximity to the aboral resection margin, but the oral and aboral resection margins, the resection margin of the periproctic soft tissue, and isolated sections of the colon are all free of tumor cells. Metastatic involvement was identified in two out of 47 examined lymph nodes, with tumor cells partly extending beyond the lymph node capsules. The remaining lymph nodes exhibited reactive changes without malignant features. The final pathological staging of the tumor is pT3, pN1, L1, V0, G2, R0, indicating a primary tumor with penetration through the muscularis propria into pericolorectal tissues, limited lymph node involvement, lymphatic invasion, no vascular invasion, moderate differentiation, and clear resection margins.

The essential pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of metastatic adenocarcinoma of the lung, specifically affecting the left upper lobe. The primary tumor is characterized as a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma with focal papillary components. Metastatic involvement is noted in a total of 7 regional lymph nodes, with one at L11, six at the lobectomy site, and one at Station 7, while two lymph nodes at Station 5 are free of tumor. The primary tumor margins are uninvolved by invasive carcinoma, maintaining a clear parenchymal margin. No evidence of venous or arterial invasion was found, though lymphatic invasion remains indeterminate, and emphysematous changes were observed. Immunohistochemical analysis supports the adenocarcinoma diagnosis with tumor cells positive for Napsin-A and TTF-1, and weakly positive for p63. Special staining for mucin is negative, and the visceral pleura is uninvolved. Molecular analysis reveals no detectable mutations in KRAS or EGFR genes.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of invasive and in situ lobular carcinoma of the left breast. The invasive lobular carcinoma was graded between II and III, comprising the majority of the tumor mass, with an additional component of lobular carcinoma in situ making up about 5% of the lesion. The primary tumor grossly measured 3.8 cm at its largest dimension and was situated close to the medial, superior, and anterior resection margins, although final medial margin assessment showed no residual tumor presence. There was no evidence of lymphovascular invasion. Examination of the sentinel lymph node from the left axilla revealed no tumor involvement, confirmed by negative immunohistochemical staining for cytokeratin AE1/AE3. Non-neoplastic breast tissue displayed fibrocystic changes with microcalcifications. Additional immunopathology studies demonstrated positive estrogen and progesterone receptor status, and negative Her2-Neu expression. The tumor demonstrated a DNA index of 1.59, indicating aneuploidy, with an S-phase fraction less than 10%, and a favorable Ki-67 labeling index. Overall, the tumor margins were free of invasive carcinoma, and there was no regional lymph node metastasis observed.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of low-grade oligoastrocytoma, predominantly oligodendroglioma in nature. The tumor is characterized by a mildly hypercellular glial neoplasm with round to oval nuclei and scant glial processes, but it lacks classic oligodendroglioma differentiation. Scattered moderately to markedly atypical cells are present, however, there are no mitotic figures, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis observed. The immunohistochemical study shows a variable MIB-1 labeling index with the non-enhancing portion displaying minimal proliferation and the enhancing portion showing moderate proliferation. Examination of the surgical margins—posterior, deep, anterior, superior, and inferior—reveals gray matter devoid of hypercellularity or atypical cells, indicating no neoplastic infiltration. Additionally, involved sections of gray and white matter confirm the presence of the neoplasm without significant proliferative activity or signs of high-grade transformation, excluding the classification as Grade III/anaplastic. No lymph node involvement is discussed in the evaluation.

The pathological examination reveals an invasive moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma located in the right upper lobe with extension into the superior segment of the right lower lobe. The tumor measures 4.5 cm in its greatest dimension and is classified as stage pT2a, with no regional lymph node metastasis (pN0) identified among the 37 lymph nodes examined. Margins are uninvolved by invasive carcinoma, including the final proximal surgical bronchial margins, which are negative for tumor or carcinoma in-situ. However, carcinoma in-situ is present in the right main bronchus within close proximity to the carina. Notably, there is no evidence of venous, arterial, or lymphatic invasion. Additional pathological findings include emphysematous changes and respiratory bronchiolitis. The excised right mainstem bronchus and various dissected lymph nodes, including stations 7, 4R, and 10R, are all negative for carcinoma. There is no distant metastasis identifiable from this examination.

The pathological examination confirms a diagnosis of bladder carcinoma with a high-grade (HG) T2 lesion. The tumor exhibits invasive urothelial carcinoma characteristics, with both papillary and flat growth patterns in its non-invasive components, and infiltrating growth in its invasive components. Multicentric tumor involvement is present, and the cancer has invaded the perivesical soft tissues, right and left ureters, periurethral prostatic ducts, prostatic stroma, left seminal vesicle, and surrounding fibroadipose tissues. There is both vascular and perineural invasion identified. The bladder tumor is present at the perivesical soft tissue margin anteriorly. Associated benign findings include chronic cystitis and ulceration in the non-neoplastic mucosa. Additionally, an adenocarcinoma of the prostate, with a Gleason score of 6, is also identified, confined to the right anterior lobe and showing no vascular or perineural invasion. The non-neoplastic prostate reveals nodular hyperplasia. The surgical margins for prostatic adenocarcinoma are free of tumor. Lymph node dissections from the right and left pelvic regions only revealed benign lymph nodes, with no malignant involvement. The distal ureter, vas deferens, urethral margin, and periureteral tissue examined are all benign. The pathological staging for the bladder tumor is at least pT3, with some features elevating it to T4a according to the AJCC Cancer Staging Manual 7th edition, indicating extensive local invasion.

The pathological evaluation reveals a healthy, tumor-free gall bladder with no macroscopic or microscopic indications of a polyp and no significant florid or chronic inflammatory changes. Additionally, the examined perivesicular lymph nodes and an extra lymph node are also free of tumor involvement. The resected segment of the sigmoid colon presents with an ulcerated, moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma that penetrates all layers of the colon wall and extends to the peritoneum, encroaching on the subserosa of a nearby segment of the small intestine. Both the oral and aboral resection margins are clear of the tumor. However, there is a solitary regional lymph node metastasis, indicating lymphatic involvement. The tumor is classified as moderately differentiated (G2) with a staging of pT4 L1 V0 pN1, reflecting its invasive nature and regional nodal involvement but no evidence of lymphovascular invasion.

The histopathological examination revealed a poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma originating from the bronchus with associated squamous metaplasia and dysplasia. The carcinoma extends into the hilar fat adjacent to the pulmonary artery, demonstrating perineural permeation without evidence of blood vessel invasion. The tumor is clear of the bronchial resection margin; however, it is present in the soft tissue adjacent to the pulmonary artery margin. Lymph node analysis showed peribronchial metastases and extracapsular lymphatic involvement, although no malignancy was detected in one examined lymph node (labeled "No. 7"), which exhibited reactive changes only. Additionally, the distal lung exhibits areas of organizing pneumonia with granulomatous inflammation, some of which are necrotic. A rare ferruginous body, indicative of asbestos exposure, was also noted within the granulomas. Overall, key findings include significant lymphatic and perineural infiltration, tumor proximity to vital margins, and histologic evidence of past asbestos exposure.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of high-grade urothelial carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation, extensively involving most tissue fragments. Additionally, urothelial carcinoma in-situ is identified. The tumor is characterized by irregular white-tan soft tissue fragments. The submitted tissue has been thoroughly examined, highlighting the aggressive and extensive nature of the malignancy. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not detailed in the findings, suggesting a need for further evaluation in a surgical context. This combination of high-grade carcinoma and sarcomatoid differentiation indicates a potentially aggressive clinical course, necessitating prompt and comprehensive treatment planning.

The patient is diagnosed with invasive ductal carcinoma, Nottingham grade 2, in the right breast. The primary tumor is characterized by glandular differentiation with less than 10% glandular/tubular structures, visible nucleoli with moderate variability in size and shape, and a low mitotic count. The greatest dimension of the tumor is 4.2 cm with negative surgical margins, although the anterior and superior margins are closest at 0.2 cm. Importantly, there is the presence of lymphovascular invasion. Sentinel lymph node evaluation reveals microscopic foci of carcinoma in one lymph node, while another shows metastatic carcinoma with extracapsular extension, and a third node is negative for malignancy. Axillary dissection identified no further malignancy in nineteen lymph nodes. Additional axillary lymph node samples from levels 2-3 also show no malignancy. Pathologic staging is classified as pT2, pN1a, with primary tumor size between 2-5 cm and metastasis in one to three axillary lymph nodes, including at least one metastasis larger than 2.0 mm. Immunohistochemical studies show minimal immunoreactivity for both estrogen and progesterone receptors and a negative HER2/neu status. No lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS) was identified, and no malignancy is seen in the skeletal muscle or skin.

The pathological examination of the right temporal lobe brain tumor revealed a diagnosis of glioblastoma multiforme (WHO Grade IV). Microscopic evaluation identified necrotic tissue and glioblastoma within the biopsied specimens. The excised tumor fragments exhibited a combination of tan to brown to pink irregular soft tissue, with hemorrhagic and necrotic areas, consistent with an aggressive high-grade glioma. The tumor margins were not clearly demarcated, reflecting the infiltrative nature of the malignancy. No lymph node involvement was noted as lymph nodes are not typically addressed in primary brain tumor resections. These findings confirm an extensive and highly malignant brain tumor warranting aggressive therapeutic intervention.

The histopathological evaluation confirmed a diagnosis of invasive tubular carcinoma of the left breast, classified as Nottingham Histologic Grade 1 (NHG1). The tumor was located centrally over the nipple, extending into the upper quadrants of the breast, and measured 2.3 x 1.7 x 1.3 cm. It was closely situated to the base but was 0.8 cm from the skin. Resection margins were negative, with no tumor involvement. The sentinel lymph nodes examined (pNO(sn)) showed no evidence of metastatic disease. Additionally, the surrounding glandular tissue exhibited fibrocystic changes and a fibroadenoma of 0.5 cm. Immunohistochemically, the neoplastic cells expressed estrogen and progesterone receptors in 75% of cell nuclei, with a low Ki-67 proliferation index of 10%. HER2 protein expression was negative, scoring 1+ with no overexpression. These findings provide comprehensive insight into the tumor's characteristics and the absence of lymphatic spread, supporting the clinical management plan for the patient.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of Anaplastic Astrocytoma, WHO Grade III, located in the right parietal region. This infiltrative glial neoplasm is composed of pleomorphic astrocytes with numerous mitotic figures, indicating its malignant nature. The tumor cells exhibit strong GFAP positivity and a Ki67 proliferation index of 16%, suggesting high proliferative activity. Immunohistochemical staining for p53 is positive in the majority of tumor cell nuclei, while EMA positivity is scattered, ruling out a diagnosis of ependymoma. There is notable infiltration into the grey matter, and although there is no ganglion cell component detected on chromogranin or neurofilament stains, resident, non-neoplastic neurons exhibit positive NEU-N staining. Marginal tumor presence is noted in the capsule wall. Given the histopathological characteristics, including focal gemistocytic appearance and lack of lower-grade components, the tumor is best classified as Anaplastic Astrocytoma, without excluding the possibility of progression from a lower-grade astrocytoma.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma originating in the right true vocal cord, with significant extensions into adjacent structures. The tumor extends supraglottically into the submucosa of the false cord, subglottically, and crosses the midline to involve the left anterior vocal cord. Additionally, it invades the right paraglottic space. Despite the invasiveness, no lymphatic or vascular invasion is identified, and surgical margins are free of tumor, indicating successful resection. Detailed examination reveals that the tumor invades through the ossified thyroid cartilage into the anterior soft tissue with perineural invasion near the right lobe of the thyroid. Decalcification was necessary to confirm this extensive invasion. Importantly, the thyroid and posterior wall of the esophagus show no evidence of tumor presence. This comprehensive assessment confirms the locally aggressive nature of the carcinoma but highlights the absence of distant metastatic spread.

The patient was diagnosed with nodular melanoma located on the right arm, exhibiting a Breslow depth of 6.3 cm and extensive ulceration. The tumor displayed vertical growth, with a mitotic rate of 3.75 per square millimeter and focal brisk tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes. Histopathological examination confirmed the presence of epidermal consumption and minimal pigmentation, accompanied by mild solar elastosis and areas of necrosis. There was no evidence of regression, lymphovascular space invasion, satellite nodules, perineural invasion, or an associated nevus. All surgical margins were widely free of tumor cells. Three sentinel lymph nodes from the right axilla were removed and assessed, with no evidence of melanoma detected in any of the nodes, corroborated by negative Melan A and S100 protein immunostains. The final pathologic staging was determined to be pT4b, pNO, pMx. Overall, the findings indicate a high-risk melanoma with significant depth and ulceration, but no regional lymph node involvement.

The pathological examination revealed an invasive, moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the colon, classified as G2. The tumor has penetrated all parietal layers, corresponding to a pT3 stage, and exhibited vascular invasion (L1, V1). Importantly, the resection margins are clear, indicating a locally R0 status. Additionally, all examined lymph nodes (14 in total) are free of cancer involvement (pN0). Separate analysis of Block D identified serrated adenomas with a distinctive dentate form, cylindrical epithelia, and goblet cells, accompanied by stromal bleeding, but no invasive structures were observed in these polyps. The overall tumor classification is consistent with ICDO-DA-M 8140/3.

The pathological examination reveals an infiltrating ductal carcinoma in the right breast, characterized as a moderately differentiated grade 2 tumor according to NSABP histologic criteria. The initial biopsy specimen confirmed vascular invasion but did not require margin evaluation. Subsequent mastectomy revealed no residual carcinoma, with benign proliferative changes such as epithelial hyperplasia of usual type and blunt duct adenosis present. The skin, nipple, and areola were grossly and histologically unremarkable, with no histologic abnormalities detected. Notably, metastatic involvement was identified in 4 out of 7 axillary lymph nodes, with extracapsular spread observed. These findings collectively indicate a moderately differentiated invasive breast carcinoma with significant nodal involvement, emphasizing the need for further oncological assessment and intervention.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma at multiple sites, including the hard palate and maxillary sinus. The carcinoma invades the bone in the areas examined. Fragment analysis of the bone and tissue from the maxillary sinus and hard palate reveals consistent involvement by the carcinoma, though some bone specimens are pending further decalcification for definitive analysis. No tumor is present in the salivary gland tissue from the lateral side of the palate and certain regions of respiratory mucosa. The partial maxillectomy specimen demonstrates a central exophytic mass composed of squamous cell carcinoma that extends to the base and is in close proximity to several margins, with the closest margin being 0.1 cm from the lateral aspect. Lymph node involvement and other specific measurements were not detailed in the report. The overall pathological impression is consistent with a locally invasive malignant process requiring thorough margin evaluation and potential additional treatment considerations.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of primary invasive ductal carcinoma in both breasts, with distinct characteristics and pathological stages. On the left side, the invasive carcinoma is located in the outer quadrants and spans a maximum dimension of 2.8 cm. Histologically, it is graded as a Nottingham Grade 1 tumor with a nuclear grade of 2, tubule formation score of 1, and mitotic activity score of 1. The tumor also presents areas of DCIS, cribriform and micropapillary types. There is no lymphovascular space invasion, and the surgical margins are clear with the closest proximity being 0.9 cm to the anterior margin. Calcifications are noted in both malignant and benign zones, and there are additional findings of radial scar, sclerosing adenosis, and fibrocystic changes. The tumor cells are positive for estrogen and progesterone receptors but negative for HER2. The pathological staging is pT2 pNO pMX. On the right side, the invasive ductal carcinoma with tubular features measures 1.1 cm and is located at approximately 9 o'clock. This tumor also scores a Nottingham Grade 1 with similar grade components to the left side. There is no lymphovascular invasion, and margins are very clear, with the closest being 2.6 cm to the anterior margin. Additional pathological findings include LCIS, atypical ductal hyperplasia, flat epithelial atypia, and fibrocystic changes. The right-side tumor cells are similarly positive for estrogen and progesterone receptors and negative for HER2, with a pathologic stage of pT1c pNO MX. Both axillary sentinel lymph nodes, one on each side, are negative for neoplasm. Lastly, excised additional breast skin on both sides is negative for neoplasm.

The pathological examination of the resected right upper lobe of the lung reveals a predominantly squamous cell carcinoma with areas of glandular differentiation, classified as moderately to poorly differentiated. The unifocal tumor is located in the right upper lobe, with evidence of visceral pleural and lymphovascular space invasion. Despite the pleural involvement, the tumor remains confined to the pulmonary parenchyma and shows no treatment effect. Surgical margins are clear, with the tumor being 7.0 cm from the bronchial margin and 4.0 cm from the parenchymal margin, though it invades the pleural surface. Lymph node analysis includes one peribronchial lymph node, which is free of metastatic carcinoma. Additional findings include emphysematous changes. The pathologic staging is pT2a N0, encompassing all significant morphological and metastatic assessments.

The pathological examination of the right hemicolectomy specimen revealed an invasive adenocarcinoma, moderately differentiated, located in the cecum. The tumor exhibited characteristic invasion into the muscularis propria but did not extend to the inked serosal surface or show macroscopic perforation. All resection margins, including proximal, distal, and mesenteric, were free of tumor involvement. Notably, there was no evidence of lymph-vascular or perineural invasion. The examination of 23 lymph nodes demonstrated no metastatic involvement. Additional findings included a submucosal lipoma and an unremarkable appendix. The tumor did not display any treatment effects, suggesting no prior therapeutic intervention had been administered. Pathologic staging was determined as pT2, pN0, pMx.

The essential pathological findings indicate that the right partial mastectomy specimen reveals an infiltrating ductal carcinoma, classified as Grade III/III with a tubular score of 3, nuclear score of 3, and mitotic score of 3. The tumor displays a solid architectural pattern with focal areas of ductal carcinoma in situ and cancerization of lobules. Despite being high grade, there is no evidence of angiolymphatic invasion, and the invasive malignancy is observed abutting deep skeletal muscle; however, the surgical margins are negative for malignancy, with the stage determined as T3 NO MX. Examination of right axillary and sentinel lymph nodes shows no evidence of malignancy in either case, confirmed by routine microscopic studies, while immunohistochemical stains are pending for further validation. The findings also note that the infiltrating ductal carcinoma is morphologically identical to a previously biopsied sample, and studies for steroid receptors and HER-2/Neu overexpression have been already performed on the original biopsy.

The pathological evaluation reveals an anaplastic oligoastrocytoma (grade III WHO) located in the right temporal region. Histologically, the tumor exhibits increased cellularity with distinct areas of oligodendroglial and astrocytic differentiation, alongside prominent nuclear atypia and mitotic activity. There is evidence of brisk mitotic rate and microvascular proliferation, indicative of its aggressive nature. The resection margins are involved, suggesting incomplete surgical removal. Meticulous examination of the regional lymph nodes did not show metastatic involvement, which is consistent with the central nervous system origin of this neoplasm. Additional pathological features include significant necrosis and a high Ki-67 proliferation index, underscoring the rapid growth potential of the tumor. These findings collectively point towards a high-grade, infiltrative neoplasm with adverse prognostic implications.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of low-grade oligoastrocytoma, predominantly oligodendroglioma in nature. The tumor is characterized by a mildly hypercellular glial neoplasm with round to oval nuclei and scant glial processes, but it lacks classic oligodendroglioma differentiation. Scattered moderately to markedly atypical cells are present, however, there are no mitotic figures, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis observed. The immunohistochemical study shows a variable MIB-1 labeling index with the non-enhancing portion displaying minimal proliferation and the enhancing portion showing moderate proliferation. Examination of the surgical margins—posterior, deep, anterior, superior, and inferior—reveals gray matter devoid of hypercellularity or atypical cells, indicating no neoplastic infiltration. Additionally, involved sections of gray and white matter confirm the presence of the neoplasm without significant proliferative activity or signs of high-grade transformation, excluding the classification as Grade III/anaplastic. No lymph node involvement is discussed in the evaluation.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma in the left breast. The tumor is moderately differentiated (Nottingham grade 2), with an overall size in its greatest dimension, accompanied by associated calcifications. Margins are uninvolved by invasive carcinoma, with the closest margin being posterior at 1 mm. Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), intermediate nuclear grade, is also present, showing solid and cribriform patterns, and associated with necrosis and calcifications. DCIS margins are close, particularly at the inferior margin, where it measures less than 1 mm. Sentinel lymph node evaluation indicates metastasis in one out of three nodes, while non-sentinel and axillary lymph nodes show no carcinoma involvement. In total, one out of twenty-five lymph nodes harbors metastatic disease. Immunohistochemistry shows the tumor is estrogen receptor-negative, progesterone receptor-negative, and HER2-negative. There is no evidence of lymphovascular invasion, and the skin exhibits no pathological changes. The absence of presurgical therapy is noted. Microcalcifications are present in both invasive and in situ components, and there is no extranodal extension identified.

The pathology reveals an invasive, poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma of the colon, classified as grade 3 (G3). The tumor exhibits penetration through all parietal layers, with vascular infiltrates present (pT3, L1, v1). Tumor classification falls under ICDO-DA-M. 8140/3 with annotations G3 and pT3. Examination of the resection margins indicates clear margins, denoted as R0. Lymph node analysis indicates no regional lymph node metastasis (pN0). The status of distant metastasis remains indeterminate (MX). These findings collectively underscore the aggressive nature of the adenocarcinoma, with significant local invasion but no current evidence of regional spread or distant metastasis.

The microscopic examination reveals a moderately cellular glial neoplasm characterized by both astrocytic and oligodendroglial features. The oligodendroglial component is notable for cells with round to oval, moderately pleomorphic nuclei and prominent preinuclear halos, while the astrocytic component displays neoplastic cells with oval to elongate nuclei and moderate cytologic atypia. Scattered microgemistocytes are present, and although mitotic figures are rare, focal areas show elevated mitotic activity up to 3 mitoses per 10 high power fields. Necrosis and microvascular proliferation are absent. The infiltrative nature of the neoplastic cells into the adjacent brain parenchyma supports the diagnosis of oligoastrocytoma. Further assessment with a MIB-1 labeling index reveals an overall low proliferation rate of 2.4-3.7%, with a focal region of increased activity at 6.2%. These pathological findings, including the focally elevated MIB-1 index and mitotic activity, suggest early anaplastic progression in an otherwise low-grade tumor. The final diagnosis of the tumor is oligoastrocytoma, predominantly oligodendroglioma, consistent with early anaplastic progression, classified as WHO grade III. Margin status and lymph node involvement findings are not applicable in this context as the tumor is located within the brain.

The pathological examination revealed a diagnosis of poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma within the left lung, specifically obstructing the lower lobe bronchus and extending to the main bronchus bifurcation. The tumor showed significant invasion, including into the pulmonary artery, but the bronchial resection margin was negative for tumor presence. Furthermore, the tumor extensively infiltrated the interlobar lymph node, although eight lower lobe lobar and two upper lobe lobar lymph nodes were negative for malignancy, indicating the absence of distant nodal metastasis (pN0). Notably, blood and lymphatic vessel invasion were present, and the lesion was associated with marked centroacinar emphysema and obstructive pneumonia. Additional lymph nodes (ST10L TB angle, Subcarinal ST7, and Inferior pulmonary ligament ST9) were negative for malignancy. Key findings also included severe centrilobular emphysema and prominent mucous impaction due to the obstructive nature of the tumor, culminating in obstructive pneumonia in the lower lobe. Overall, the tumor's pathological stage was pT2, reflecting its size and extent of local invasion, while its poor differentiation was indicative of a more aggressive clinical course.

The essential pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of anaplastic oligodendroglioma with a predominant background of low-grade oligodendroglioma. The tumor demonstrates diffuse infiltration into both gray and white matter, with tumor cells exhibiting intermediate-sized round nuclei and variable perinuclear halos. Although there is notable microcystic myxoid change in some regions, most of the tumor appears solid with areas of confluent hypercellularity and moderate atypia. Mitotic figures are rare, with only three observed in over 100 high-power fields. There is focal microvascular proliferation, but it is not widespread. MIB-1 immunohistochemistry indicates a variable proliferative index, with most tumor areas showing a labeling index of less than 1%, though some regions exhibit a higher labeling index of up to 8.8%. Lymph node involvement and surgical margin status are not specified. Collectively, these findings are consistent with anaplastic oligodendroglioma set against a backdrop of low-grade oligodendroglioma, marked by its diffuse infiltrative nature and variable proliferative activity.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of muscle-invasive, high-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma of the bladder with concomitant acinar adenocarcinoma of the prostate. The bladder tumor is predominantly located in the walls (including right wall, left wall, posterior wall, and anterior wall) and the trigone. The pathological evaluation reveals muscle-invasive disease with the tumor extending into the superficial muscularis propria. The urothelial carcinoma was staged as pT2a with no lymph node involvement found in any of the 16 lymph nodes examined (pN0) and no evidence of distant metastasis (M0). The surgical margins, including the left ureteral and urethral margins, were negative for tumor, which is favorable for the patient's prognosis. A focal area was suspicious for lymphovascular invasion. 

For the prostate gland, pathological findings revealed acinar adenocarcinoma with a Gleason score of 6 (3+3). The carcinoma was multifocally present on the right side and unifocally on the left, comprising less than 20% of the prostate tissue. There was focal extraprostatic extension measuring 2 mm on the left side, but no seminal vesicle invasion was identified. The surgical margins for the prostate were negative, and no lymphovascular invasion was noted. Perineural invasion was present. The prostate cancer was staged as pT3 with no regional lymph node involvement (pN0) and no distant metastasis (M0). The comprehensive analysis of the radical cystoprostatectomy specimen indicates a locally advanced disease but with favorable surgical margins and no lymphatic spread in both the bladder and prostate pathologies.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of high-grade (Grade 3) poorly differentiated transitional cell carcinoma (TCC) of the bladder, characterized by a solid growth pattern with a very focal papillary area and the presence of necrosis. The tumor shows invasion into the muscularis propria, classifying it as pT2. No vascular channel invasion is identified. The background urothelium demonstrates carcinoma in situ (CIS). Examination of the tissue from the base of the tumor shows a heavy acute on chronic inflammatory cell infiltrate with nuclear smearing due to crush artifact but does not reveal evidence of invasive transitional cell carcinoma. Additionally, tissue from the bladder neck comprises bladder wall tissue without any evidence of invasive transitional cell carcinoma and lacks lining epithelium. The overall pathological summary confirms a high-grade transitional cell carcinoma with muscular invasion and associated carcinoma in situ. The absence of invasive carcinoma in the base and bladder neck tissues indicates no direct spread to these regions within the examined samples.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of an infiltrating moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma involving the left upper lobe of the lung, specifically affecting the left upper lobar bronchus. The tumor demonstrates extensive erosion of the bronchial cartilage and invades both the bronchial lumen and the lung vasculature. Importantly, all excised lymph nodes from various stations, including R4, Station 5, L9, and L10, are negative for tumor involvement, indicating no lymph node metastasis. Additionally, the bronchial margin is clear of tumor, suggesting complete resection of the malignancy. Overall, these findings highlight a locally aggressive carcinoma with significant bronchial and vascular involvement but without regional lymph node dissemination.

The pathological examination of the right hemicolectomy specimen revealed a diagnosis of moderately differentiated colorectal adenocarcinoma with partial mucus production. The tumor was ulcerated and extensively involved all layers of the intestinal wall, extending into the mesocolic fatty tissue. Notably, the appendix showed signs of post-inflammatory fibrosis. Both the oral and aboral resection margins, along with the large omentum, were tumor-free. Examination of twenty-six lymph nodes identified seven with metastatic involvement confined to the lymph nodes, while the remaining lymph nodes exhibited uncharacteristic reactive changes. The tumor was staged as pT3 pN2, with no evidence of lymphovascular invasion (L0, V0) and graded as G2. Additionally, a provided histological sample of skin metastasis indicated an adenocarcinoma from another primary site, as evidenced by negative CK-20 staining, excluding it as a metastasis of the colorectal carcinoma.

The pathological examination of the right modified radical mastectomy reveals an infiltrating carcinoma with histologic features primarily of ductal origin and a significant lobular component. The tumor is high-grade (grade 3 of 3) with a moderate nuclear grade (2 of 3) and is extensively distributed across all quadrants of the breast tissue. There is also a presence of in-situ lobular carcinoma representing 5% of the tumor without an extensive intraductal component. Notable findings include the presence of lymphatic and vascular invasion, with confirmed metastasis in five of ten dissected lymph nodes, the largest measuring 2.4 cm, and exhibiting extracapsular extension. The margins of the excised tissue are clear of carcinoma, and there is no involvement of the nipple or skin, with Paget's disease absent. The non-neoplastic breast tissue shows fibrocystic changes, including intraductal hyperplasia and ductal ectasia. Microcalcifications are associated with the carcinoma. Positive estrogen and progesterone receptor statuses indicate hormone responsiveness.

The pathological examination of the left lung specimen, obtained through pneumonectomy, reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma with a maximum dimension of 4.5 cm. The tumor demonstrates visceral pleural invasion and lymphovascular invasion, but there is no extension into extra-pulmonary structures. Importantly, the surgical margins are negative for carcinoma, with the bronchial margin also uninvolved by tumor. Regional lymphadenectomy identified one out of twenty-four lymph nodes positive for metastatic carcinoma, specifically a peribronchial lymph node.
