This finding assigns the tumor a pathologic stage of IIA (pT2aN1M0). Additionally, microscopic analysis identified focal necrotizing granulomatous inflammation with rare fungal organisms consistent with Histoplasma species, as well as areas of atypical adenomatous hyperplasia and emphysematous changes in the lung parenchyma. Special stains confirmed visceral pleural invasion and the presence of fungal organisms, while stains for acid-fast bacilli were negative. These pathological findings substantiate the diagnosis and provide a comprehensive understanding of the tumor characteristics and spread.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of malignant melanoma located on the skin of the shoulder. The tumor is observed to be pigmented and exhibits ulceration. Microscopic evaluation confirms invasion up to the papillary-reticular dermal interface, corresponding to Clark's level III, with a significant Breslow thickness of 10 mm. Notably, the excised specimen shows no evidence of venous invasion. Importantly, the surgical margins are clear of tumor involvement. Additionally, there is a presence of perifocal lymphoid infiltration, suggesting an immune response in the surrounding tissue. Lymph node involvement has not been documented. Overall, the findings indicate an aggressive form of melanoma with deep dermal invasion but successful excision with uninvolved margins.

The pathological evaluation of the lung specimen from the right upper lobe, along with a portion of the superior right lower lobe, confirmed a diagnosis of poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma. The tumor, which did not infiltrate the visceral pleura, presented a unifocal growth pattern. Margins evaluation revealed a positive status in the peribronchial soft tissue, though bronchial and vascular walls were free of tumor involvement. Despite this, the positive peribronchial soft tissue implies clinical significance akin to positive bronchovascular margins. No lymphovascular or perineural invasion was identified. All examined lymph nodes (0/6) were benign, and there was no evidence of distant metastasis, placing the regional lymph node staging as pN0. The overall pathological stage is classified as IB (pT2a pN0 pMX). Immunohistochemical staining was positive for TTF-1, Napsin-A, and CK7, confirming primary lung adenocarcinoma, with negative staining for CK20, ER, and CDX-2.

The pathological examination of the right axilla and right lumpectomy specimens revealed critical findings consistent with invasive poorly differentiated ductal carcinoma. In the right axilla, micrometastatic carcinoma was identified in one of six lymph nodes, with a metastatic deposit size of 1.25 mm, detected using immunohistochemistry for keratin. The lumpectomy specimen contained a high nuclear grade invasive ductal carcinoma with lymphovascular space invasion. The invasive carcinoma was found to be present within 1 mm of the inferior margin, and ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) was also identified within 1 mm of the lateral and inferior margins. No invasive carcinoma breached the dermis or epidermis, nor was skeletal muscle identified in the specimen. The tumor was a single focus invasive carcinoma exhibiting a combined histologic grade of 2, with high nuclear pleomorphism and a low mitotic count. Margins were largely uninvolved by invasive carcinoma, although the nearest margin to invasive cancer was less than 1 mm at the inferior edge. The sentinel lymph node evaluation indicated one node with micrometastases and no macrometastatic involvement or extranodal extension. Ancillary studies identified the tumor cells to be negative for ER, PR, Her-2, and E-cadherin, suggesting a basal subtype of mammary carcinoma. No presurgical neoadjuvant therapy effects were observed. The pathologic staging was classified as pT2, pN1mi, pM0, indicating a localized tumor with limited regional lymph node micrometastasis. These findings underscore the aggressive nature of the carcinoma and highlight critical considerations for further therapeutic intervention.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of diffuse astrocytoma Grade III, located in the left frontal lobe. The tumor presents with both gray and white matter involvement, with a soft texture and an indistinct brownish structure grossly. Microscopically, the astrocytoma shows diffuse infiltration with a Ki-67 proliferative index of less than 10%, indicative of moderate cellular activity. The resection margins are not specified, implying potential consideration for further evaluation. The lymph node involvement is not applicable in this intracranial pathology. Overall, the findings are consistent with a high-grade, locally invasive tumor, necessitating further clinical management.

The resection of the left lower lobe of the lung revealed an invasive grade 3 squamous cell carcinoma forming a mass that obliterates the proximal bronchus, without any pleural involvement. Notably, the bronchial margins are free of neoplastic cells. Examination of multiple lymph nodes from various stations, including left lower paratracheal, subaortic, subcarinal, pulmonary ligament, pulmonary artery, and intrapulmonary peribronchial regions, showed no evidence of tumor involvement. This suggests that the malignancy is confined to the primary tumor site with no lymphatic spread detected in the sampled nodes. These findings collectively indicate a localized advanced squamous cell carcinoma.

The pathological evaluation reveals an infiltrating adenocarcinoma of the left breast, histologically typified as invasive ductal carcinoma. It is graded between 2 and 3 on the N.S.A.B.P. scale with a nuclear grade of 2. The tumor, inclusive of the invasive component, predominantly spans an area of 1.6 cm, and 10% of the tumor displays in situ carcinoma of the solid type. Notably, there is no extensive intraductal component. The specimen is marked by substantial lymphatic and vascular invasion. Although the carcinoma approaches the superior margin closely and multifocally, all surgical margins remain technically free of malignancy. Adjacent non-neoplastic breast tissue exhibits features of duct ectasia, intraductal papillomatosis, and apocrine metaplasia. In the axillary dissection, three out of twenty-one lymph nodes tested positive for metastatic carcinoma, with one lymph node demonstrating extracapsular extension. Additionally, estrogen receptor assays returned positive, while progesterone receptor activity was negative.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma, Nottingham Grade 1, in the right breast. The tumor is located in the upper outer quadrant and is free of carcinoma at the margins. Additionally, ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of the cribriform type, with microcalcifications and nuclear grade 1, is present but does not involve the margins. Assessment of the nipple shows no tumor involvement. Examination of the lymph nodes reveals two sentinel lymph nodes and nine additional axillary lymph nodes, all of which are negative for carcinoma. The invasive carcinoma is confirmed as estrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR) positive, while HER2 is negative by FISH. Pathological staging is pT1c N0, according to the AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 7th Edition. Non-neoplastic areas of the breast include radial scars, with no evidence of lobular neoplasia or vascular/lymphatic invasion. The DCIS constitutes approximately 15% of the tissue and is found in association with the invasive tumor but does not exhibit necrosis. Overall, the pathological findings indicate a localized tumor with no nodal involvement, favorable hormone receptor status, and clear surgical margins.

The pathological examination reveals an oligodendroglioma, Grade II (WHO) involving the posterior part of the right frontal brain, anterior portion of the right frontal brain, and the cingulate gyrus of the right frontal lobe. Each lesion exhibits characteristics of a low-grade glioma consistent with oligodendroglioma. Gross examination of the specimens identified infiltrating neoplastic growth in both white and gray matter, submitted in their entirety for histopathological evaluation. Importantly, fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) testing demonstrated a combined loss of chromosomal arms 1p and 19q, consistent with an oligodendroglial lineage. This genetic alteration is significant as it correlates with a potentially more favorable prognosis and responsiveness to adjuvant therapy. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not addressed in the provided sections. These findings are critical for informing the therapeutic approach and prognostic outlook for the patient.

The histopathological examination of the left breast resected from a female patient revealed an invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC) with an accompanying ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS). The IDC is classified as grade 3 (NHG3) based on its significant mitotic activity (35 mitoses per 10 high-power fields) and was found to exhibit high nuclear atypia. The DCIS component, comprising 10% of the tumor, displayed solid-type growth with high nuclear atypia and comedo necrosis. Peritumoral lymphocytic reaction was noted, suggesting an immune response to the tumor. The overall glandular tissue demonstrated parenchymal atrophy without lesions in the nipple-areolar complex. Crucially, the surgical margins appear to be clear. Examination of the axillary lymph nodes indicated reactive lymphonodulitis but no metastatic involvement was detected (pT2, pN0). No evidence of lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS) was found. The final pathological diagnosis is invasive ductal carcinoma and ductal carcinoma in situ of the left breast.

The pathology findings indicate a diagnosis of high-grade invasive urothelial carcinoma. The primary tumor invades the full thickness of the bladder wall, extending into adjacent adipose tissue and the prostatic stroma, classified as pT4. The tumor is present at the posterior soft tissue margin and the prostatic urethral margin. Additionally, there is invasion into the prostatic stroma from the prostatic urethra, with focal carcinoma in situ and high-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma. Lymph node involvement is confirmed with 2 out of 11 regional lymph nodes positive for metastatic carcinoma (pN2), including 1 left pelvic lymph node showing extracapsular extension of the tumor. Notable pathological findings also include vascular and perineural invasion and involvement of the left vas deferens. The right and left distal ureters show focal urothelial atypia with inflammation, and the bladder's surrounding tissue has areas of diverticulosis with diverticulitis and a benign adenomatous polyp in the sigmoid colon. The appendix reveals no significant histopathologic abnormalities.

The pathological evaluation reveals an invasive ductal carcinoma, SBR grade II, located in the left breast, measuring 1.8 cm, accompanied by ductal carcinoma-in-situ (DCIS), nuclear grade 2/3, solid type. The invasive carcinoma exhibits lymphovascular invasion and is positioned less than 0.1 cm from the anterior margin, necessitating close clinical follow-up. The DCIS is associated with the invasive component and comprises approximately 5% of the tumor mass, with margins not involved by DCIS. Comprehensive analysis of the three sentinel lymph nodes from the left axilla reveals no evidence of metastatic carcinoma, indicative of negative lymph node involvement (0/3). The tumor is estrogen receptor-positive, progesterone receptor-positive, and HER2-negative. Additionally, Oncotype DX testing yielded a low recurrence score of 7. These findings collectively stage the tumor at pT1c N0, guiding therapeutic planning and prognostic assessment.

The specimen consists of lung tissue obtained via lobectomy, revealing a diagnosis of well-differentiated papillary adenocarcinoma located in the left upper lung. The tumor is characterized by papillary glands forming elongated finger-like processes lined by moderately stratified cylindrical or cuboidal cells supported by fibrovascular connective tissue cores, arranged back-to-back. The tumor cells exhibit irregular, large, hyperchromatic nuclei with prominent nucleoli, and numerous mitotic figures are present. Invasion into the alveolar structures is noted, accompanied by inflammatory cell infiltration in the stroma. The tumor extends into the visceral pleura but does not show evidence of other tumor nodules. Importantly, the solitary mediastinal lymph node sampled is negative for metastatic involvement. Margin status, lymphatic invasion, venous invasion, and evidence of neo-adjuvant treatment are not specified in the findings.

The pathological findings reveal infiltrating ductal and lobular carcinoma in the breast tissue obtained from a radical mastectomy specimen. The lesion is grossly evident, indicating a palpable and visible tumor in the examined tissue. Microscopic evaluation confirms the presence of malignant cells with infiltrative growth patterns characteristic of both ductal and lobular types of carcinoma. Notably, the surgical margins appear free of tumor invasion, suggesting that the resection was complete. Additionally, none of the eight regional lymph nodes examined show signs of metastatic involvement. These findings collectively support a diagnosis of infiltrating ductal and lobular carcinoma without regional lymph node metastasis, indicating a localized malignancy with no current evidence of spread to nearby lymphatic structures.

The pathological examination of a prophylactic simple mastectomy of the left breast revealed focal atypical ductal hyperplasia, columnar cell hyperplasia/changes, complex sclerosing lesions, usual ductal hyperplasia, small papillomatosis, sclerosing adenosis with associated microcalcifications, and apocrine metaplasia, with no evidence of malignancy. The skin and nipple were also negative for any malignant changes. In contrast, the right breast simple mastectomy presented an invasive lobular carcinoma of mixed classical and pleomorphic/solid types, with unifocal involvement primarily affecting the upper inner quadrant and retroareolar area. The carcinoma had nuclear grades ranging from I-II for the classical type and III for the pleomorphic type. Margins were negative, with the nearest invasive lobular carcinoma tissue located at least 1 cm away from margins, and there was a focal area suspicious for lymphovascular invasion. Associated in-situ lobular carcinoma was also present. The right axillary dissection yielded 18 lymph nodes, of which two were positive for metastatic carcinoma without extranodal extension. The staging was consistent with Stage IIB, assuming “cM0” status, classified as pT2, pN1a, cT2, cN1, cM0. Additional findings included benign microcalcifications and prior biopsy site changes, along with focal usual ductal hyperplasia, columnar cell changes, microcysts, and apocrine metaplasia. The nipple and skin were notably free of tumor involvement.

The pathological findings revealed a diagnosis of left-sided invasive ductal carcinoma, moderately differentiated. The tumor measured 55 mm and demonstrated extensive invasion of the lymph vessels, but no intraductal tumor component was present. Surrounding tissue exhibited fibrocystic and proliferating mastopathy, ductal hyperplasia, and sclerotic intraductal papillomas. The epidermis, including the nipple, and isolated samples of the pectoral muscle were free of tumor involvement. The margins were clear, with the closest margin being 3 mm at the basal aspect, and the margin at 12 o'clock measuring 6 mm. One of the thirteen examined lymph nodes was positive for metastatic disease, resulting in a classification of pN1a. The overall pathological stage was determined to be stage III A with clear resection margins (R0). The tumor was classified as M-8500/3, Grade 2, with additional designations of pT3, pL1, and pMx.

The final pathological diagnosis reveals key findings related to both bladder and associated structures. The most significant finding is an invasive high-grade urothelial carcinoma with predominant small cell features in the bladder, particularly involving the left lateral wall and extending through the detrusor muscle into the perivesical soft tissue. High-grade carcinoma in situ (CIS) is also identified. The carcinoma exhibits a solid/nodule configuration and substantial necrosis, with neoplastic cells demonstrating a high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio and prominent nuclear molding. Immunohistochemical staining confirms neuroendocrine differentiation of the small cell component, with positive markers for synaptophysin, NSE, and CD56, and negative for chromogranin, pankeratin, and thrombomodulin. Importantly, the surgical margins are uninvolved by invasive carcinoma, and there is an absence of lymphovascular or perineural invasion. Examined lymph nodes from both pelvic regions are benign, and no lymphatic or venous involvement is noted. Additional findings include focal high-grade prostatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PIN) and benign prostatic hyperplasia in the prostate. Pathological staging is pT3a, pN0, indicating tumor invasion into the perivesical tissue but no regional lymph node metastasis. Thus, the findings highlight a high-grade, aggressive bladder carcinoma with involvement of adjacent structures yet clear margins and no nodal metastasis.

The pathological analysis reveals a diagnosis of anaplastic astrocytoma, classified as a Grade III tumor. The samples, obtained from the right parietal lobe, display characteristic features of this malignancy. The gross description noted grayish-pink, translucent pieces of brain tissue. Microscopically, the tissue shows increased cellularity, nuclear atypia, and mitotic activity consistent with anaplastic astrocytoma. The tumor is infiltrative, and margin status indicates that the neoplasm extends to the edges, implying the potential presence of residual disease. Additionally, no lymph nodes were involved, as they are not a primary site of metastasis for this type of brain tumor. The findings collectively suggest an aggressive neoplasm with significant potential for recurrence, requiring further clinical management and possibly adjunctive therapy.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of malignant melanoma located on the skin of the trunk. The tumor is characterized by ulceration and pigmentation. Histologically, the melanoma demonstrates a notable Breslow's depth, indicative of significant vertical growth. Importantly, there is no evidence of venous invasion. The surgical margins are clear of tumor involvement, suggesting that the excision has successfully encompassed the malignant growth. No lymph node involvement is described, which typically bodes well for prognosis. This summary highlights crucial aspects of the melanoma, including its aggressive features and favorable resection status, informing the clinical approach for further management.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive poorly differentiated mucinous signet ring cell adenocarcinoma of the transverse colon. The tumor extends through the wall of the colon into the adjacent omentum. The proximal, distal, and radial margins of resection are confirmed to be free of tumor involvement. However, the tumor demonstrates vascular invasion and has metastasized to thirteen of the forty-one examined mesenteric lymph nodes, inclusive of the marked splenic flexure lymph node. Notably, there is extracapsular extension of the tumor present in multiple lymph nodes, with the largest metastatic lesion measuring 1.6 cm. Additionally, a non-lymph node pericolonic tumor presence is noted. Examination of the appendix reveals no significant histopathologic abnormalities, and an incidental adenomatous polyp is identified. The presence of distant metastasis could not be assessed from this evaluation.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma of the larynx, histologically graded as 2 with a keratinizing pattern. The tumor demonstrates an extensive invasion, affecting the mucosa, cartilage, and skeletal muscle, and presents with ulceration. The lymphatic system shows signs of invasion, although vascular invasion is not detected. Neural invasion within the tumor is minimal. Desmoplastic reaction is discrete, and there is a discrete lymphocytic infiltrate within the tumor. Metastasis is identified in three lymph nodes; one of these nodes shows capsular involvement and desmoplasia, while two others do not exhibit capsular involvement. Six additional lymph nodes are examined, with no evidence of neoplasia. The thyroid gland remains uninvolved by the neoplastic process but displays lymphocytic chronic thyroiditis. Margin status and other pertinent details have been carefully evaluated to support the comprehensive assessment of the malignancy.

The pathology findings reveal a diagnosis of multifocal multicentric invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC) in the right breast, classified as Nottingham Grade 1 with a combined Nottingham score of 5, indicating low mitotic activity and mild nuclear atypia. The carcinoma predominantly occupies the upper quadrants, with the largest tumor focus grossly measuring 6 cm and multiple microscopic foci present throughout all quadrants. The examination of margins reveals the tumor is within 0.1 cm from both anterior and posterior margins. Lymphovascular invasion is absent. The sentinel lymph node biopsy from the right axilla demonstrates metastatic carcinoma in four lymph nodes, though no extracapsular extension is identified. Additionally, non-sentinel and axillary dissections confirm metastatic involvement in all ten examined lymph nodes without extracapsular extension. The background breast tissue shows fibrocystic changes, columnar cell changes, and a radial scar in the left breast. The pathological stage is designated as T3 N3 MX, signifying locally advanced disease with extensive nodal involvement.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of a cerebellopontine (CP) angle/cerebellar tumor, specifically a well-differentiated astrocytoma, classified as Grade 2. Histologically, the neoplasm exhibits moderate cellularity with cells that resemble modestly atypical fibrillary astrocytes. The tumor demonstrates an expanded interface with the adjacent cerebellum, with scattered Rosenthal fibers predominantly at the periphery. It exhibits a solid growth pattern accompanied by vascular hyalinization and extensive microcalcifications, the latter sometimes being more prevalent than tumor cells. Importantly, there are no atypical ganglion cells or eosinophilic granular bodies present. Additionally, perivascular pseudorosettes are not observed. The findings suggest a relatively benign, low-grade tumor with slow growth characteristics, though its expanded interface with the surrounding cerebellum warrants careful monitoring. Margins and lymph node involvement are not specified, emphasizing the need for a comprehensive clinical context to assess potential spread and complete excision status.

The pathological evaluation revealed adenocarcinoma of the lung, characterized as a solid type and poorly differentiated (Grade 3). The tumor was localized within the lung, and there was no evidence of venous invasion. The surgical resection margins were clear of tumor involvement. Examination of seven hilar lymph nodes showed reactive changes, with no metastasis detected in any of the lymph nodes. Venous invasion was absent. There was no documented evidence of neo-adjuvant treatment effects or additional noteworthy pathological findings.

The pathological findings reveal an oligodendroglioma in the brain, diagnosed from excision biopsy specimens. The tumor exhibits a loose, microcystic background with moderate cellularity and mild nuclear pleomorphism. Notably, mitotic figures are absent, and there is no evidence of vascular hyperplasia or necrosis, suggesting a relatively indolent nature. The proliferation index, determined by MIB-1 staining, is low at 2%. The presence of numerous minigemistocytes is also observed. Margins and specific lymph node involvement are not detailed in the provided results. The macroscopic examination shows semi-firm, yellowish-grey, glistening fragments of tissue. Initial intra-operative consultation suggested a glioma, with a differential diagnosis that included astrocytoma.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of infiltrating ductal carcinoma with a component of metaplastic carcinoma and focal apocrine features in the left breast, following a segmental mastectomy procedure. The tumor exhibits a Nottingham score of 9/9, indicating high-grade malignancy with poor differentiation (tubules 3, nuclei 3, mitoses 3). Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), of less than 1% tumor volume, is present in both solid and cribriform patterns with high nuclear grade. Lymphovascular invasion is not observed, and all surgical resection margins are free of tumor, indicating complete excision. The pathology also demonstrates benign fibrocystic changes, sclerosing adenosis with microcalcifications, a small intraductal papilloma, and a calcified nodule with periductal inflammation. Hormone receptor status is positive for estrogen receptor (ER), and negative for progesterone receptor (PR) and HER-2/neu. Sentinel lymph node biopsies show no evidence of metastatic disease (0/3 examined). Additional non-neoplastic findings include fibrocystic changes and fibroadenomatous nodules with microcalcifications. The tumor is classified pathologically as pT2, pN0, pMx, indicating a primarily localized disease without regional lymph node metastasis or distant metastasis evaluated.

The patient is diagnosed with deeply invasive squamous cell carcinoma originating from the left pyriform sinus, accompanied by squamous mucosa dysplasia. The pathology from the right posterior neck dissection at levels 2, 3, and 4 shows no evidence of tumor involvement within the thirty-six lymph nodes examined. Following a total laryngopharyngectomy, bilateral neck dissection, and subtotal thyroidectomy, the findings revealed invasive, moderately to poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with areas of necrosis. Importantly, the surgical margins are clear of tumor involvement. The bilateral neck dissection identified metastatic squamous cell carcinoma in five out of forty-two lymph nodes, with specific positive findings in the left level II, III, and IV nodes. No tumor was detected in the right vocal cord, bilateral submandibular glands, or the left thyroid lobe. The closest left oropharyngeal margin shows squamous mucosa with dysplasia and underlying lymphoid aggregate, but no tumor is present. The carcinoma in the resected specimen invades deeply into surrounding soft tissue, salivary glands, and skeletal muscle, indicating probable extranodal extension and subsequent destruction of the lymph node architecture.

The pathological examination of the left upper lobe lung tissue following lobectomy reveals a Grade 3 adenocarcinoma forming a bronchocentric mass. The tumor exhibits angiolymphatic invasion but does not involve the pleura, and the bronchial margin is free of tumor, which is indicative of clear surgical margins. Metastatic adenocarcinoma of the same grade is present in a single left subaortic lymph node (station 5L) and two out of six intrapulmonary peribronchial lymph nodes. No metastatic involvement is detected in lymph nodes from left paraaortic (station 6L), subcarinal (station 7), left pulmonary ligament (station 9L), or left interlobar (station 11L) sites. These findings suggest a primary lung adenocarcinoma with regional lymphatic spread, necessitating consideration for adjunctive therapy.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of low-grade/grade II oligoastrocytoma, predominantly featuring oligodendroglioma characteristics. Microscopically, the tumor presents as a moderately hypercellular glial neoplasm that diffusely infiltrates both the gray and white matter. The tumor cells consist mainly of small, round nuclei with smooth contours, many of which display well-formed perinuclear halos, though a significant minority of the cells do not have such halos. Additionally, a small percentage of the nuclei exhibit elongate shapes with fibrillary processes. Importantly, there is no observed atypia, mitotic activity, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis, reinforcing the low-grade nature of the tumor. The MIB-1 labeling index is 3.1%, further supporting the classification as a low-grade neoplasm. The margins and lymph node involvement are not specifically discussed in the findings provided.

The colon resectate reveals a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of colorectal type, which infiltrates the pericolic fatty tissue and has metastasized to seven regional lymph nodes, including one at the mesenteric resection margin. Notably, both the small and large intestinal resection margins are clear of tumor involvement. The tumor is pathologically staged as pT3 pN2 pMX, with a grade of G2, exhibiting lymphatic invasion but no vascular invasion and is locally resected to clear margins (R0). Additionally, the ileocolon resectate shows a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of colorectal type arising from the base of a tubular adenoma with high-grade dysplasia located in the cecum, which infiltrates the tunica submucosa. Examination indicates that regional lymph nodes, small and large intestinal resection margins, mesenteric resection margin, appendix, and omental fat tissue are tumor-free. This tumor is staged as pT1 pN0 pMX, with a grade of G2, displaying no lymphatic or vascular invasion and is fully resected with clear margins (R0).

The pathological examination of the right breast mastectomy reveals an invasive lobular carcinoma of the classical type, graded as Nottingham Grade 2, characterized by moderate tubule formation, moderate nuclear pleomorphism, and low mitotic activity. The invasive carcinoma, which is located at the junction of the upper and lower outer quadrants, measures 4.0 cm in its largest dimension and is 1.0 cm from the nearest posterior margin, with all resection margins being negative for carcinoma. Additionally, lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS) and lymphovascular space invasion are present. The nipple and skin are free of tumor involvement. The non-neoplastic breast tissue exhibited sclerosing adenosis, fibroadenoma, fibrocystic changes, columnar cell changes, and pseudoangiomatous stromal hyperplasia. Immunohistochemical staining indicated that the tumor cells are positive for estrogen receptor and progesterone receptor with high scores, and negative for HER-2. Examination of the right axillary lymph nodes found metastatic carcinoma in two out of eighteen nodes, with the largest metastatic focus measuring 1.6 cm, but no extracapsular extension. The left breast mastectomy showed benign findings, including fibroadenoma, columnar cell changes, fibrocystic changes, and pseudoangiomatous stromal hyperplasia, with an unremarkable nipple and skin. The pathological staging is pT2 N1a for the right breast cancer.

The pathological findings indicate the presence of cribriform intraductal and infiltrating ductal carcinoma in the right breast biopsy, characterized by a moderately differentiated nuclear grade and histologic grade of two out of three. The largest dimension of the tumor is 2.0 cm. Examination of the right breast, including a previous biopsy site and axillary tail, revealed no residual carcinoma. Margins are not grossly involved, and all surgical margins appear free of tumor involvement. All fifteen axillary lymph nodes examined were negative for carcinoma, indicating no lymph node involvement. Other notable findings in the breast tissue include focal florid usual hyperplasia, interlobular fibrosis, papillary apocrine metaplasia, and cystically dilated ducts.

The excised temporal brain tumor is diagnosed as a glioblastoma (WHO Grade IV), initially characterized as an anaplastic astrocytoma (WHO Grade III) due to the presence of moderately high pleomorphism, scattered mitotic figures, and apoptotic bodies without evidence of microvascular proliferation or necrosis. Re-evaluation of additional sections revealed greater pleomorphism and focal microvascular proliferation, justifying the upgrade in diagnosis. Immunohistochemical staining shows strong and diffuse p53 positivity with a Ki-67 labeling index of approximately 20%, indicating a high proliferative rate. The minor oligodendroglial component observed was not sufficient to alter the overall diagnosis. Both Loss of Heterozygosity (LOH) at 1p and 19q were negative, signifying the absence of allelic loss on these chromosome arms. However, a methylated MGMT promoter was detected, which has therapeutic implications. Importantly, the tumor margins were not explicitly mentioned, and there was no indication provided regarding lymph node involvement, consistent with the primary location being the brain. The combination of histopathological and molecular findings supports the definitive diagnosis of glioblastoma.

The pathological examination revealed an adenocarcinoma in the rectosigmoid colon, characterized as moderately differentiated (grade 2/3), lacking lymphovascular space invasion, and having no evidence of visceral peritoneum perforation or mesenteric deposits. The tumor penetrated the muscularis propria into subserosal fat but did not nearly breach beyond that. According to the Astler-Coller classification, it was categorized as C1. Surgical margins, including proximal, distal, and radial, were all negative for tumor involvement, with the closest approach being at a radial distance of 2.2 cm. Of the 21 lymph nodes analyzed, none contained metastatic carcinoma. The tumor was staged as pT3b, N0, Mx. Additionally, the omental excision showed fat necrosis and fibrosis with scattered calcifications, without malignancy. The right colon exhibited tubular adenomas with focal high-grade dysplasia but no invasive malignancy. Eight lymph nodes examined were negative for metastatic disease, and mucosal fragments showed congestion.

The pathological examination of the left upper lobe lobectomy specimen revealed an adenosquamous carcinoma with extensive vascular and lymphatic invasion. The tumor predominantly consists of squamous cell carcinoma with interspersed glandular formations and signet ring cells, making up approximately 5-10% of the mass. Notably, the tumor invades through the visceral pleura and is present at the pleural adhesion margins, while the bronchial margin is negative for neoplasia. The pulmonary arterial margin exhibits tumor involvement within the lymphovascular spaces but spares the fibroadipose tissue. Metastatic adenosquamous carcinoma was detected in the peribronchial lymph nodes, and a microscopic focus of metastatic non-small cell carcinoma was observed in a level 10 lymph node. All other examined lymph nodes, including those from levels 5, 6, 7, 9, and 11, were negative for neoplasm. Additionally, the lung parenchyma showed evidence of centrilobular emphysema. Immunohistochemical staining for TTF-1 and p-63 confirmed the diagnosis. EGFR mutation analysis revealed no alterations, indicating a wild-type genotype. The tumor is staged pathologically as pT4 N1 MX, corresponding to stage IIIA according to the AJCC 7th edition.

The pathological analysis reveals a diagnosis of colon adenocarcinoma located in the ascending colon. The tumor has invaded through the muscularis propria into the subserosa without involving adjacent structures, classifying it as T3. No metastatic involvement is observed in the regional lymph nodes (N0) or distant sites (M0), resulting in an overall stage of IIA. The resection margins are clear of tumor involvement, indicating complete excision of the malignancy. Other relevant pathological findings include the absence of lymphovascular invasion and perineural invasion, contributing to the overall favorable prognostic outlook.

The histopathological examination of the left breast and axillary tissues from a male patient revealed an invasive ductal carcinoma with partially micropapillary features, classified as NHG2. The tumor predominantly occupied the outer quadrants and was closely situated to both the lower boundary and base of the resected tissue. Immunohistochemical analysis demonstrated a strong positivity for estrogen and progesterone receptors in over 75% of the neoplastic cell nuclei, and a HER2 protein overexpression with a HercepTest score of 3+. Microscopic sections also identified foci of ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) exhibiting micropapillary and cribriform patterns with moderate nuclear atypia. Additionally, glandular tissue showed signs of gynecomastia. Examination of the axillary lymph nodes indicated significant metastatic involvement, with metastases and micrometastases observed in 25 out of 28 sampled lymph nodes, including infiltration into the lymph node capsule and perinodal tissue. The pathological findings support a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma with micropapillary components, accompanied by extensive axillary lymph node metastases, staged as pT2 and pN3a.

The pathology findings indicate a diagnosis of low-grade oligoastrocytoma. The tumor exhibits a mildly to moderately hypercellular glial neoplasm that diffusely infiltrates both the gray and white matter. Tumor cells display monotonous round to oval nuclei with minimal atypia and prominent, often fibrillary cytoplasm, including microgemistocytic and occasional gemistocytic forms. The overall cell morphology is consistent with a combination of astrocytoma and oligodendroglioma features. The tumor has a low mitotic rate, with only two mitoses observed in 25 high power fields, and lacks microvascular proliferation or necrosis. Margins are not specified, but the presence of scattered MIB-1 reactive cells with a low labeling index of 1.7% suggests a minimally proliferative nature. No lymph node involvement is mentioned as it often does not apply to primary brain tumors such as this one. The amalgamation of these characteristics justifies the classification of the neoplasm as an oligoastrocytoma, a low-grade mixed glial tumor.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of low-grade oligoastrocytoma, predominantly oligodendroglioma in nature. The tumor is characterized by a mildly hypercellular glial neoplasm with round to oval nuclei and scant glial processes, but it lacks classic oligodendroglioma differentiation. Scattered moderately to markedly atypical cells are present, however, there are no mitotic figures, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis observed. The immunohistochemical study shows a variable MIB-1 labeling index with the non-enhancing portion displaying minimal proliferation and the enhancing portion showing moderate proliferation. Examination of the surgical margins—posterior, deep, anterior, superior, and inferior—reveals gray matter devoid of hypercellularity or atypical cells, indicating no neoplastic infiltration. Additionally, involved sections of gray and white matter confirm the presence of the neoplasm without significant proliferative activity or signs of high-grade transformation, excluding the classification as Grade III/anaplastic. No lymph node involvement is discussed in the evaluation.

The final pathological diagnosis reveals invasive adenocarcinoma in the right upper and lower lobes of the lung, characterized by two foci in the right upper lobe and a single focus in the right lower lobe. Tumor margins, including the bronchial and vascular margins, are negative for carcinoma, and the parenchymal margin is reassessed to be free of tumor. A total of 20 lymph nodes across various stations were examined, all of which were negative for metastatic tumor. Pathologic staging is classified as pT2b, pN0, pMX. Additional findings include emphysematous changes noted in the right upper lobe. The gross and microscopic examinations corroborate the presence of tumor masses in the right upper and lower lobes, with all other submitted specimens, including lymph nodes and margins, remaining free of neoplastic involvement.

The essential pathological findings reveal a highly differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma of the breast with circumscribed intraductal components. Importantly, the dorsal resection margin is free of tumor involvement, indicating complete surgical excision in that area. There is no evidence of lymph node involvement in the provided specimen, and no lymphovascular or perineural invasion is observed. Based on the tumor characteristics, this carcinoma is classified as Grade I (G I), indicating it is well-differentiated. The tumor is categorized as pT3 based on size, signifies no regional lymph node metastasis (N0), and there are no other significant findings noted. These findings collectively provide a comprehensive overview of the pathological diagnosis and features of the carcinoma.

The pathological evaluation of the left breast radical mastectomy reveals an infiltrating ductal carcinoma, classified as Nottingham grade III, suggestive of high-grade tumor characteristics. The primary mass is located centrally adjacent to the biopsy cavity. Additionally, two separate microscopic foci of invasive carcinoma are identified in the subareolar tissue, associated with high-grade ductal carcinoma in situ. Evidence of angiolymphatic invasion is present. Notably, all surgical resection margins are negative for tumor involvement. The axillary lymphadenectomy discloses metastatic adenocarcinoma in four out of sixteen axillary lymph nodes, with the largest involved node exhibiting extracapsular tumor extension. Both the fallopian tubes and ovaries from the salpingo-oophorectomy exhibit no diagnostic abnormalities. Estrogen and progesterone receptor analysis as well as HER2/neu status testing are pending.

The pathology findings reveal a diagnosis of invasive adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon, exhibiting both moderately and poorly differentiated histology. The exophytic (polypoid) tumor measures 3.9 cm in its greatest dimension and invades into the subserosa. Grossly, the tumor is located 3.5 cm from the closest surgical margin. Margins (proximal, distal, and radial) are all negative for tumor involvement, indicating a complete resection (R0). There is no perineural or venous/lymphatic invasion detected, although there is suspicion of angiolymphatic invasion. The carcinoma is associated with a tubular adenoma. Of the twenty-four lymph nodes examined, two exhibit metastatic carcinoma, with evidence of extranodal extension. The proximal and distal donuts from the colon show no evidence of malignancy. Immunohistochemistry reveals the absence of PMS2 expression, while MLH1, MSH2, and MSH6 are positive. Further testing for microsatellite instability is pending. The pathological staging is pT3 N1 Mx, indicating the primary tumor invades through the muscularis propria into pericolorectal tissues (subserosa), with metastasis to one-to-three regional lymph nodes and presence of extranodal extension.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of an anaplastic astrocytoma, specifically of the gemistocytic type. The tumor is characterized by moderate hypercellularity and the presence of atypical gemistocytes demonstrating a range from mild to moderate atypia, along with occasional highly atypical nuclei. There is an observed mitotic activity of up to 2 mitoses per 10 high power fields, including some atypical mitoses, although the overall MIB-1 labeling index is relatively low at 6.6%. Notably, there is no evidence of microvascular proliferation or necrosis. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not specified in the findings, and no additional pathological abnormalities are reported.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of mucinous type colonic adenocarcinoma, grade 2 out of 3, located in the rectosigmoid colon. The tumor exhibits characteristics such as invasion through the muscularis propria into the pericolonic soft tissue, and the presence of some signet ring cells. Importantly, all resection margins are negative for malignancy, with the nearest margin being 3 cm away from the tumor. Examination of 32 pericolonic lymph nodes showed no evidence of malignancy, indicating no lymph node involvement. Additionally, the specimen included several hyperplastic polyps, none of which appear malignant. Vascular invasion is absent, and there is only mild chronic inflammation noted as a host response. Moreover, special stains for acid-fast bacteria and fungi on a necrotizing granuloma in one lymph node were negative. The final pTNM stage is T3 N0, and the probability of microsatellite instability (MSI) is low at 3%. Furthermore, a biopsy of a peritoneal implant showed no evidence of malignancy.

The pathological examination revealed a diagnosis of high-grade invasive urothelial carcinoma of the urinary bladder, predominantly involving the left lateral wall and trigone. The carcinoma infiltrates the superficial detrusor muscle but shows no definitive evidence of angiolymphatic invasion. Notably, the urinary bladder also exhibits focal high-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma and urothelial dysplasia extending to the carcinoma in situ in the right and left ureteral ostia as well as the anterior wall/dome. All examined surgical margins are free of urothelial neoplasia. The lymph nodes show no evidence of malignancy with all twenty-three examined nodes being negative, although there are instances of non-necrotizing granulomas in a few nodes. Additional pathological findings include granulomatous inflammation and biopsy site changes in the background bladder, related to a history of BCG therapy. The prostate is benign with multifocal necrotizing granulomas, nodular hyperplasia, glandular atrophy, and chronic inflammation, and the seminal vesicles are also benign. The pathological stage is designated as pT2a NO MX.

The pathological evaluation of the right hemicolectomy specimen, including the right and transverse colon, reveals an invasive adenocarcinoma with mucinous features, classified as moderately differentiated (G2). The primary tumor, located in the right (ascending) colon, measures 4.0 cm in its greatest dimension and extends into the subserosa. Importantly, the proximal, distal, and radial margins are free of tumor involvement, indicating a complete resection (R0) with no residual cancer at the margins. Angiolymphatic invasion is present, but no perineural invasion is identified. Associated adenomatous changes and tubular adenomas are observed away from the primary tumor site. The appendix shows no histopathological abnormalities. An examination of seventeen lymph nodes reveals no evidence of malignancy (0/17), confirming the absence of lymph node metastasis. The overall pathological staging for this cancer is pT3 N0 MX. No additional pathological findings were identified.

The pathological findings reveal a complex presentation with multiple diagnoses. First, the left breast shows fibrocystic mastopathy with ductal hyperplasia and extensive foci of lobular neoplasia, including carcinoma lobulare in situ (CLIS and LN2). Additionally, there is well-differentiated intraductal breast carcinoma (DCIS grade 1), partly with a papillary structure, which at two points transitions to invasive, well-differentiated ductal breast carcinoma (G1, pT1a(2) L0 V0 R0). Importantly, the left axillary lymph nodes are free of tumor involvement (pN0 0/13). Next, the resected section of the rectum shows a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma infiltrating the lamina muscularis propria with invasion into the lymph vessels but no local lymph node metastases (G2 pT2, L1 V0 R0 pN0 0/23 pM1 HEP). Additionally, the resected hepatic wedge shows no metastatic tumor deposits from the rectal carcinoma; however, it does reveal non-active cirrhosis with focal steatosis. Notably, the resection margins in both the rectal and hepatic specimens are tumor-free, underscoring the absence of residual disease at these sites.

The pathological analysis indicates a diagnosis of adenocarcinoma, mixed subtype, located in the right upper lobe of the lung. The tumor exhibits solid and micropapillary patterns and demonstrates poor differentiation with perineural and visceral pleural invasion. Notably, the resection margins are clear of carcinoma, although metastatic carcinoma was identified in one out of seven peribronchial lymph nodes, classifying the disease as pN1. Additionally, emphysematous changes are observed. Among the various excised lymph nodes, no metastatic carcinoma was detected except for fragmented tissue from the 12R lymph node, which revealed fragments of adenocarcinoma along with a minute fragment of lymphoid tissue. A skin lesion from the right posterior chest is identified as seborrheic keratosis. In summary, the lung adenocarcinoma is sizable with visceral pleural involvement, and regional lymph node involvement is confirmed with one positive node among several resected nodes.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of keratotic squamous cell carcinoma (Carcinoma planoepitheliale keratodes) of the lower lip, classified as Grade 2 (G2) and staged as pT1. Microscopically, the tumor demonstrated invasive characteristics with a keratotic appearance. The excision was successfully completed with clear margins, as there was healthy tissue surrounding the tumor. The minimal margin of normal tissue was 2 mm on the lateral side and 1.5 mm at the bottom, indicating that the tumor has been entirely resected. No lymph nodes were involved in the specimen provided, and there was no evidence of metastasis in the examined tissues. Overall, the tumor was completely excised with clear margins, although close monitoring is advised due to the invasive nature and moderate differentiation of the carcinoma.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of high-grade invasive urothelial carcinoma of the bladder, involving the anterior wall, dome, and posterior wall, with extension through the muscularis propria into the posterior perivesical fat. The surgical margins are clear of tumor involvement. In addition, the prostate contains a low-grade adenocarcinoma with a Gleason score of 3+3, affecting the left apex and right mid portions and involving 10% of the gland, without evidence of angiolymphatic or extracapsular invasion, and with clear surgical margins. Both bilateral seminal vesicles and vas deferens are free of carcinoma. Examination of the pelvic lymph nodes shows no metastatic carcinoma in any of the eleven nodes sampled. An additional finding includes a foreign object in the abdominal cavity associated with fat necrosis and peripheral fibrosis. AJCC staging classifies the bladder carcinoma as pT3a, pN0, and pMx, and the prostate adenocarcinoma as pT2c, pN0, and pMx.

The pathological findings indicate that a moderately differentiated, keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma was identified in the right hemiglossectomy specimen, likely originating from the right tongue. The tumor measured 2.0 cm at its greatest dimension with a depth of invasion of 1.6 cm into the muscle of the tongue. No angiolymphatic or perineural invasion was found. Carcinoma in situ was present, but all surgical margins were negative for malignancy. The lymph node analysis from various levels of the right neck revealed metastatic squamous cell carcinoma in one of the eight lymph nodes from Level 2, measuring 1.1 cm, without extracapsular extension, and no carcinoma was identified in the remaining lymph nodes (0/34). Additionally, benign tissues and lymph nodes showed no carcinoma involvement, and no significant findings were reported apart from the chronic inflammation and dystrophic calcification in the anterior buccal gingiva specimen. HPV and p16 immunostains were negative. The final AJCC pathologic stage was determined as pT1 pN1.

The pathological evaluation of the buccal mucosa reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma, characterized by ulceration and infiltration into the skeletal muscle tissue. The surgical margins, including the margin of the inferior alveolar nerve, are confirmed to be uninvolved by neoplasia. Lymphatic invasion is present, although perineural invasion is not identified. Lymph node analysis from various levels on the right side shows mixed involvement: 1 out of 8 lymph nodes at Level IIA contains metastatic squamous cell carcinoma without capsular involvement, while the remaining lymph nodes at Levels IIB, III, and IB are uninvolved by neoplasia. Additionally, the right inferior alveolar nerve and skeletal muscle tissue demonstrate no neoplastic involvement. The neoplasia infiltrates the osseous tissue by contiguity, but the osseous tissue margin remains uninvolved. These findings collectively outline the extent of metastatic spread and local tissue involvement.

The pathological findings indicate a primary diagnosis of high-grade urothelial carcinoma with mixed transitional cell and adenocarcinoma types, exhibiting deep invasion into the muscularis propria of the urinary bladder but no extension into the perivesical fat or adjacent organs. A small focal area of carcinoma in situ was also noted in the urinary outflow tract, alongside papillary urothelial hyperplasia in the trigone region. Importantly, all surgical margins, including the urethral, right and left ureteral, and perivesical margins, were free of tumor. Lymphatic or vascular invasion was not identified. Of the lymph nodes examined, metastatic carcinoma was found in one of the eleven left pelvic lymph nodes, resulting in an overall finding of metastatic involvement in one out of twenty-three nodes analyzed, without extranodal tumor growth. The prostatic tissue demonstrated no evidence of malignancy. The final AJCC/UICC staging for this case is pT2bN1Mx, indicating a tumor extending into the outer half of the muscularis propria and metastasis to a single lymph node.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of high-grade infiltrating ductal carcinoma in the left breast. The tumor demonstrates a grade of 3, characterized by an architectural score of 2, nuclear score of 3, and mitotic score of 3. No metastasis is found in the assessed left axillary sentinel lymph node, with one lymph node examined showing no evidence of metastatic disease. Similarly, no metastasis is identified in the left axillary contents, as all seven lymph nodes examined are free of metastatic involvement. The largest dimension of the primary tumor within the breast tissue is noted as significant, and although the surgical margins are negative, they are described as close, being less than 2 mm from the tumor edge in several areas. No evidence of vessel invasion, calcification, or ductal carcinoma in situ is observed. The non-tumorous breast tissue exhibits proliferative fibrocystic changes with intraductal papillomatosis. These findings collectively provide a comprehensive overview of the pathological status post-mastectomy and lymph node resection.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of grade II astrocytoma located in the left frontal lobe of the brain. The tumor exhibits a loose architecture, infiltrating the surrounding benign brain tissue. It is composed of groups of gemistocytes intermixed with spindle cells. The tumor cells have elongated and irregular hyperchromatic nuclei with small nucleoli, and mitotic figures are observed, indicating cellular proliferation. The background of the tumor is fibrillar, and there is hyperplasia of the vasculature. Notably, areas of necrosis, hemorrhage, and lymphocytic invasion are present. No comments on margins or lymph node involvement were provided. The tumor is moderately differentiated, reflecting its grade II status, which typically indicates a lower level of malignant potential compared to higher-grade astrocytomas.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of an infiltrating moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma involving the left upper lobe of the lung, specifically affecting the left upper lobar bronchus. The tumor demonstrates extensive erosion of the bronchial cartilage and invades both the bronchial lumen and the lung vasculature. Importantly, all excised lymph nodes from various stations, including R4, Station 5, L9, and L10, are negative for tumor involvement, indicating no lymph node metastasis. Additionally, the bronchial margin is clear of tumor, suggesting complete resection of the malignancy. Overall, these findings highlight a locally aggressive carcinoma with significant bronchial and vascular involvement but without regional lymph node dissemination.

The pathological evaluation of the right upper lobe and associated lymph nodes reveals a diagnosis of a moderately to poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma. The tumor is characterized by a firm cream nodule, which extends close to the pleural surface but is clear of the bronchial resection margin. The lung parenchyma exhibits emphysema, particularly towards the apical region, without other significant lesions. Notably, the tumor invades the visceral pleura on the medial aspect with focal involvement of the surface, but there is no evidence of lymphatic, vascular, or perineural invasion. Examination of the hilar and intrapulmonary lymph nodes shows no signs of malignancy. Additionally, no metastatic disease is present in lymph nodes numbered 3 and 4. The tumor histology includes the presence of a central fibroelastotic scar and a tumorlet within the bronchial wall. The pathological stage is determined as T2N0MX given the absence of lymph node metastases and the size and extent of the primary tumor.

The pathology examination revealed a diagnosis of moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma located at the oral cavity (tongue), which was substantively sized at 7 cm. Surgical margins were uninvolved by the tumor, with the tumor approaching within 1 mm of the posterior pharyngeal soft tissue. Lymph node analysis showed metastasis in multiple ipsilateral nodes, classified as pN2b, with extracapsular extension present. Additionally, perineural and intraneural invasion were identified, while venous and lymphatic invasion could not be determined. Other sampled tissues, including areas such as the deep right tongue base, left hard palate, deep skull base, left nasopharynx, and others, were negative for tumor presence. Examined lymph nodes from the left neck levels 1-3 revealed metastatic squamous cell carcinoma in some nodes, specifically one node at level 2 with extranodal extension and two nodes at level 3 without extracapsular extension. The lymph node from the left axilla, left neck level 2B, and right neck level 1B were negative for tumor. The status of distant metastasis (pMX) could not be assessed.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of Glioblastoma Multiforme (WHO Grade IV) involving the left and right frontal regions of the brain. Microscopic examination reveals several irregularly shaped, tan-pink to opaque white cerebriform soft tissue fragments, consistent with malignant neoplastic proliferation. The tumor is highly infiltrative, extending into the white matter, and involves bifrontal brain lesions. The left-side tissue samples show extensive infiltration and necrosis, characteristic of glioblastomas. The right frontal tumor exhibits a lateral margin with pathological involvement, indicating incomplete resection with positive tumor margins. No specific lymph node involvement is noted as per the provided pathology details, although such information might typically be obtained through separate examination. The aggressive nature of the glioblastoma is further evidenced by its multi-locational spread and the infiltrative growth pattern. Overall, the findings underscore a poor prognosis due to the high-grade nature and extensive involvement of critical brain structures.

The pathological evaluation of the right temporal brain tumor revealed an anaplastic oligoastrocytoma (WHO grade III). The tumor sections displayed significant hypercellularity with a mixed oligoastrocytic phenotype and notable mitotic activity, averaging seven or more mitoses per ten high-power fields. Importantly, no microvascular proliferation or necrosis was observed. Immunohistochemical staining showed GFAP positivity and p53 overexpression in neoplastic cells, with a Ki-67 index reaching approximately 8%, indicating a moderate proliferation rate. Genetic analysis did not show 1p/19q loss of heterozygosity, and the MGMT promoter was unmethylated. The presence of these molecular features suggests a poorer prognosis and potential resistance to alkylating therapy. Margins status and specific lymph node involvement were not discussed, as lymphatic spread is atypical for primary brain tumors. Overall, the findings are consistent with a high-grade glial tumor requiring aggressive management.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma (M-8500/3, G3) on the left side. The tumor exhibits focal lymphovascular invasion and extension to the base of the nipple and pectoral muscle, indicating a locally advanced stage. Importantly, the carcinoma reaches the excision margins but extends only into the healthy tissue of the basal epidermis, achieving clear surgical margins (R0). Lymph node analysis shows involvement in five of the thirteen examined nodes (pN2a), and no distant metastasis is assessed (pMX). This case is classified as stage IIIA (pT3, pN2a, pMX), with no evidence of Paget's disease or skin infiltration.

The pathological evaluation reveals a high-grade urothelial carcinoma with mixed histologic features, including urothelial carcinoma NOS, keratinizing squamous, and small cell/neuroendocrine components, primarily involving the bladder diverticulum. The tumor displays both papillary and flat non-invasive growth patterns and an infiltrating invasive component with evidence of local invasion into the perivesical soft tissues. Tumor multicentricity is identified. Importantly, surgical margins are free of tumor, and there is no involvement of the right or left ureters, urethra, or identified prostate or seminal vesicles. Vascular and perineural invasion are present, which may suggest an aggressive tumor behavior. Examination of the right pelvic lymph nodes shows no evidence of metastatic involvement, with all eight lymph nodes examined being benign. Additionally, the perivesical fat, perivesical tissue, periureteral tissue, and the omentum are free of malignancy, and a segment of the right vas deferens is benign. Chronic cystitis and mucosal denudation are observed in the non-neoplastic mucosa. The pathological stage, according to AJCC 2002 criteria, is pT3, indicating invasion into the perivesical soft tissue.

The pathological findings in this lung specimen indicate a diagnosis of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the left upper lobe. The tumor is characterized by a firm, rubbery nodule with minimal umbilication of the overlying pleura. Microscopically, the tumor displays focal visceral pleural invasion, although there is no definitive evidence of vascular invasion. Margins of resection are negative, with the lesion situated approximately 0.7 cm from the stapled margin. Regional lymph node involvement (pNX) and distant metastasis (pMX) cannot be assessed from this specimen. Additional notable findings include focal fibrosis in the lung tissue surrounding the nodule. The remaining lung parenchyma appears spongy and tan-pink without any focal lesions.

The pathological evaluation of the resected left upper lobe of the lung revealed a moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma exhibiting patchy necrosis and vascular invasion. The tumor measures 68 mm at its largest dimension and is devoid of pleural, lymphatic, or perineural invasion. Notably, the bronchial and pulmonary artery resection margins are free from malignancy. Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma was identified in the peribronchial lymph nodes, contributing to a pathological staging of T2 N1. Additionally, the lung tissue showed evidence of obstructive pneumonitis adjacent to the tumor. Other pathological findings include multiple separate foci of granulomatous inflammation with central necrosis, suggestive of an atypical Mycobacterial infection. Sections of the pulmonary artery, as well as various lymph nodes, demonstrated no involvement of metastatic carcinoma, though some presented with findings such as follicular hyperplasia, sinus histiocytosis, and silicotic nodules. These comprehensive findings indicate the presence of secondary infection and metastatic involvement of the primary lung carcinoma.

The patient presents with a diagnosis of poorly differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma characterized by a lack of cellular differentiation, indicative of high-grade malignancy. The tumor is classified as grade III, illustrating aggressive histological features. The lesion falls under the category of pT2, signifying its extent within the primary site. Margins are not explicitly described, suggesting additional evaluation may be warranted for complete surgical excision status. Notably, there is involvement of regional lymph nodes, classified as N1a, pointing to metastatic spread beyond the primary tumor site. Lymphovascular invasion and other regional organ involvement are noted, reinforcing the advanced and invasive nature of the carcinoma. These pathological findings depict a comprehensive overview of an aggressive breast cancer with significant metastatic potential, impacting both prognosis and management strategies.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma located in the rectosigmoid colon. The tumor exhibits invasion into the muscularis propria and extends through the bowel wall into the pericolonic fat, evoking a scirrhous reaction. It comes to within 1.5 cm of the distal margin and is present at the radial margin, indicating a minimally positive circumferential margin. Additionally, vascular invasion is observed with rare lymphatic and arterial involvement. Among 52 regional lymph nodes examined, 2 demonstrate metastatic disease, resulting in a pN1b classification. There is also evidence of non-lymph node pericolonic tumor involvement. The resected proximal colon and distal donut specimens show no significant pathology, with all identified lymph nodes in these areas testing negative for metastatic disease. Distant metastasis could not be assessed in this evaluation. The overall pathological findings indicate an advanced and locally invasive rectosigmoid adenocarcinoma with limited regional lymph node involvement and evidence of vascular invasion.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of anaplastic astrocytoma (WHO Grade III) originating from the right frontotemporal region of the brain. The tumor demonstrates high infiltration into adjacent brain tissues, including the cortex. Despite areas of low-grade histology, notable mitotic activity and elevated Ki-67 labeling indices up to 10% signify increased proliferative activity. Ancillary studies show strong p53 immunoreactivity (60%) and negative IDH-1 immunostaining, consistent with a more aggressive tumor phenotype. Immunohistochemical analysis indicates the presence of PHH3-positive cells, confirming mitotic figures. Chromosome 1p and 19q are non-deleted, suggesting a distinct genetic profile from oligodendrogliomas and potential therapeutic implications. No lymph node involvement is reported. The gross examination of specimens includes fragments of neoplastic tissue and infiltrated cortical regions, consistent with the invasive nature of the tumor.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of an anaplastic oligodendroglioma (WHO Grade III) within a predominant background of low-grade (Grade II) oligodendroglioma. The tumor is located in the temporal lobe, encompassing the amygdala, anterior and posterior temporal lobe, uncus, and hippocampus. Histologic examination shows a variably cellular and infiltrative diffuse glioma, characterized by round nuclei with perinuclear halos, with a majority displaying a microgemistocytic phenotype. Atypia is generally mild to focally moderate, although a few highly atypical nuclei are observed. Mitoses are uncommon in most sections, and there is no evidence of microvascular proliferation or necrosis. However, in the tissue designated as enhancing uncus, there is marked hypercellularity and increased atypia, with up to six mitotic figures per 10 high power fields, along with evidence of microvascular proliferation, although necrosis remains absent. The calculated MIB-1 labeling index is 12.5%, indicating the proliferative activity of the tumor. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not described, suggesting the focus is solely on the histopathologic attributes of the tumor.

The essential pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast, with a Nottingham score of 8/9, indicating high-grade features such as poor tubule formation, significant nuclear pleomorphism, and moderate mitotic activity. The tumor also contains a component of ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), specifically micropapillary and solid types with comedo necrosis, contributing to 5% of the tumor volume. Lymphovascular invasion is present, and the invasive carcinoma is close to the inferior surgical margin, although margins are overall free of the lesion. Additionally, microcalcifications, proliferative fibrocystic changes with atypical ductal epithelial hyperplasia and columnar cell changes, as well as microscopic peripheral papilloma and sclerosing adenosis, are noted in the surrounding tissue. Two out of three sentinel lymph nodes are positive for metastatic carcinoma, with no extracapsular extension observed. Skin involvement is absent. The pathological staging is pT2 pN1a pMX, and the tumor demonstrates positivity for both estrogen and progesterone receptors.

The pathological findings reveal an intermediate-grade infiltrating ductal carcinoma of the right breast, measuring 2.0 cm, accompanied by a focal area of intermediate-grade ductal carcinoma in situ with necrosis. The margins are free of tumor, including the deep margin, which encompasses skeletal muscle. No lymphovascular invasion is observed. Six lymph nodes were examined (three sentinel nodes, one internal mammary sentinel node, and two non-sentinel nodes), and all were negative for malignancy, indicating no lymph node involvement. The tumor extends to a pathologic stage of pT2N0M0 according to the AJCC classification. Additional non-neoplastic findings include fibrocystic changes and microcalcifications in the surrounding breast tissue. Immunohistochemical analysis reveals the tumor cells are estrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR) positive, but HER2-neu negative. Immunostains for keratin in the lymph nodes are also negative. These findings collectively support a diagnosis of intermediate-grade invasive ductal carcinoma with negative surgical margins and no regional lymph node involvement.

The histopathological examination of the resected lesion from the tongue reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma (G2), specifically described as carcinoma planoepitheliale keratodes. The macroscopic examination shows a surgical specimen comprising a fragment of the tongue and mucous membrane, with a cream-colored tumor occupying nearly the entire specimen. Microscopically, the carcinoma is partially keratinized, with no clear margin of normal tissues identified from the edge of the specimen. The tumor classified as pT2 indicates a moderately advanced stage but localized in the primary site of the tongue and floor of the mouth on the right side. The involvement of lymph nodes is not specified, and no additional relevant pathological findings were noted.

The diagnosed condition is pleomorphic carcinoma of the right lower lobe of the lung, consisting predominantly of moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma (90%) and a small component of poorly differentiated spindle cell carcinoma (10%). The tumor did not demonstrate any invasion into the visceral pleura or vascular structures and was fully excised with clear margins, the closest being over 1.5 centimeters from the tumor. Additionally, five lymph nodes (four lobar and one segmental) excised from the resection were all free of malignancy, though they did show non-necrotizing granulomatous inflammation. The lung tissue in both the wedge resection of the right middle lobe and the right lower lobectomy revealed a pattern of patchy interstitial chronic inflammation and fibrosis consistent with usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP). Furthermore, all six lymph nodes from various regions presenting non-necrotizing granulomatous inflammation showed no evidence of malignancy, though some displayed focal mixed dust nodules with central necrosis and hyalinization. Special stains for fungal elements and acid-fast organisms performed on the lymph node sections were negative. Overall, the pathology supports a primary lung carcinoma with associated interstitial lung disease and granulomatous inflammation, potentially indicative of a superimposed process such as infection, drug reaction, or sarcoidosis.

The pathological examination of the breast tissue from the radical mastectomy reveals the presence of infiltrating ductal and lobular carcinoma. The tumor is located in the left, upper, outer quadrant and is grossly evident. The status of the surgical margins is not specified, but it is crucial to note that 2 out of 11 axillary lymph nodes are positive for metastasis, indicating regional lymph node involvement. These essential findings suggest a need for further clinical correlation and potential adjuvant therapy. The Nottingham Score, which could provide additional prognostic information, cannot be determined from the available data.

The pathological examination of the right breast following a simple mastectomy reveals a diagnosis of mixed invasive ductal and lobular carcinoma, classified as Nottingham grade II with a score of 5 out of 9. The carcinoma forms a mass located in the superior/central region of the breast and exhibits associated changes from a previous biopsy. Additionally, ductal carcinoma in situ is present in the upper inner quadrant, along with lobular carcinoma in situ. Importantly, the tumor does not extend to the nipple, overlying skin, or underlying chest wall. Surgical resection margins are free of tumor involvement, with the smallest tumor-free margin being noted in the anterior/superior margin. Examination of seven right axillary sentinel lymph nodes reveals no evidence of metastatic carcinoma, which is confirmed by immunohistochemical cytokeratin staining. 

In the left breast, the simple mastectomy shows mild proliferative changes characterized by ductal hyperplasia without atypia, alongside a previous biopsy site. The nipple appears normal with no diagnostic abnormalities. Estrogen and progesterone receptor analysis, along with Her-2/NEU testing, is pending on the paraffin-embedded tissue. Overall, the essential pathological findings highlight the characteristics of the carcinoma, margin status, and lymph node involvement, providing a comprehensive overview of the disease.

The pathological examination of the lobectomy specimen from the right upper lung reveals a diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma, which is moderately differentiated in histologic grade. The tumor is located in the right-upper region of the lung. Importantly, the surgical margins are free of cancer, indicating that the tumor has been completely excised. Lymph node involvement was assessed, and no metastasis was detected in the examined lymph nodes. These findings highlight the localized nature of the moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma, which has been effectively removed with clear margins and without evidence of spread to the lymphatic system.

The pathological evaluation of the urinary bladder biopsy and transurethral resection of the bladder tumor (TURBT) reveals a diagnosis of invasive high-grade urothelial carcinoma with squamous differentiation. The tumor primarily invades the muscularis propria, an important clinical finding, although no associated epithelial lesions were identified. The histologic grade is classified as high grade according to WHO/ISUP criteria. The analysis did not detect lymphovascular invasion, and the tumor’s microscopic extent confirms invasion into the muscularis propria, which aligns with an AJCC staging of pT2. While the tumors exhibit varying proportions of tumor nuclei and necrosis, no additional pathological abnormalities were noted. The margins and lymph node status are indeterminate due to the nature of the specimen, and further staging information regarding metastasis (pM) remains not applicable at this point. Overall, the tumor characteristics, margin status, and absence of lymph node involvement collectively indicate an aggressive urothelial carcinoma necessitating comprehensive clinical management.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC) of the left breast, with modified Black's nuclear grades 1 and 2, presenting as three distinct masses. The invasive component of the tumor shows perineural invasion and is located exceedingly close to the medial margin, with minimal clearance from the deep and anterior margins. Additionally, lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS) is widely dispersed throughout the breast tissue, accompanied by fibrocystic changes. Metastatic adenocarcinoma is identified in the left axillary sentinel lymph node, with further involvement found in both a palpable lymph node and within the left axillary contents, showing extranodal extension in some of these nodes. Importantly, a second palpable lymph node was free of metastasis, and excised additional deep and new anterior margins did not reveal invasive carcinoma, although few foci of LCIS and columnar alterations were noted. The presence of occasional tubule formation and few signet ring cells in both in situ and invasive tumor components further characterizes the malignancy.

The pathological evaluation of the radical mastectomy specimen revealed an infiltrating ductal and lobular carcinoma of the breast. A grossly evident lesion was identified, evident on examination. Histologically, one out of nine axillary lymph nodes was positive for metastasis, indicating lymph node involvement. The status of the surgical margins was not specified in the findings. The Nottingham Histologic Score could not be determined, thus limiting a complete histopathologic grading. These findings suggest a diagnosis of infiltrating ductal and lobular carcinoma with lymph node metastasis and unspecified margin status, critical for guiding further clinical management.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma of the lung, classified as Grade 3, indicating a high degree of cellular abnormality and aggressiveness. The primary tumor exhibits characteristics typical of squamous cell carcinoma, with a significant proportion of tumor cells present. Notably, the tumor margins are clear of cancerous involvement, suggesting that the excision was complete. Additionally, there is no lymph node involvement, as evidenced by the TNM staging of T0 N0 M0, implying no regional or distant metastasis detected. The specimen analysis also included examination of normal lung tissue and blood samples, providing a comprehensive overview of the patient's pathology.
