The histological description supports the diagnosis, and the frozen blood tube analysis may offer further insights into the systemic implications of the disease. No direct cancer treatment information is provided in this summary.

The right upper lobe of the lung exhibits two foci of moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma, both showing visceral pleural and angiolymphatic invasion, with associated tumor necrosis exceeding 50%. The largest mass measures 4.5 cm, accompanied by a smaller satellite nodule. Metastatic disease is confirmed in one out of twelve N1 lymph nodes, while all four examined N2 lymph nodes are benign. Surgical resection margins are free of tumor involvement. Additionally, the background lung shows areas of organizing pneumonia and respiratory bronchiolitis, as well as changes consistent with emphysema and smoker’s bronchiolitis. The pathologic stage is identified as T4N1Mx. Ancillary studies including histochemical stains substantiate the outlined diagnosis. Biopsies from the diaphragm and Level 4 lymph nodes reveal benign findings with chronic inflammation and anthracosis, respectively.

The diagnosis of the male patient with invasive bladder cancer following cystectomy reveals a high-grade invasive urothelial carcinoma with focal glandular features, extending nodularly and invading into the perivesical soft tissues (pT3b). The carcinoma spans the maximal dimension and notably includes extensive in-situ carcinoma, which extends along the prostatic urethra, but without vascular invasion. Perineural invasion is identified. Margins examined during surgery are free of carcinoma, implicating a complete resection. The prostate exhibits nodular hyperplasia and focal high-grade prostatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PIN), while the seminal vesicles remain unremarkable. Chronic cystitis is observed in the non-neoplastic mucosa. Perivesical lymph nodes show no malignancy (0/3), but metastatic urothelial carcinoma is found in one of seven right pelvic lymph nodes and one of four right hypogastric lymph nodes, denoting lymph node involvement. Both the right and left vas deferens, along with the left pelvic lymph nodes, and distal ureters are free of malignancy. The gross examination supports the above findings, further confirming the diagnosis and extending the pathological details necessary for the patient's treatment planning.

The pathology report confirms a diagnosis of high-grade invasive urothelial carcinoma, NOS, identified in the bladder, bilateral tubes, ovaries, and uterus from a radical cystectomy, hysterectomy, and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy specimen. The non-invasive component exhibits both papillary and flat growth patterns, while the invasive component demonstrates infiltration into the superficial half of the muscularis propria. Tumor multicentricity is present, with involvement observed specifically in the left ureter but sparing the right ureter and urethra. No vascular or perineural invasion is detected, and surgical margins are free of tumor. Non-neoplastic bladder mucosa shows ulceration, a foreign body reaction, and proliferative cystitis. Female genital organs exhibit endometrial atrophy and benign findings such as focal nodular epithelial proliferation in the right fallopian tube and a simple serous cyst in the left ovary. Perivesical lymph nodes are uninvolved, and the pathological stage is designated as pT2a. Further immunohistochemical evaluation suggests that a nodule in the fallopian tube is consistent with a primary Müllerian-derived glandular lesion, rather than metastatic urothelial carcinoma, supported by low Ki-67 proliferative index and the expression of specific markers. The resected pelvic lymph nodes from both sides, round ligaments, perivesical tissue, and distal segments of left and right ureters are all benign.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive and intraductal mammary duct carcinoma of the left breast, with the primary lesion being unifocal and located in the subareolar region. The invasive component measures 1.6 cm, with an additional incidental microinvasion of less than 1 mm. The ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) component is extensive, with scattered foci spanning 11 cm. The invasive carcinoma is of the apocrine type and is graded as nuclear grade III and histologic grade III based on the scoring system used (tubules score 3, nuclear grade score 3, and mitoses score 3). The DCIS exhibits variable histologies, including comedo with grade 3 necrosis, cribriform non-apocrine with comedonecrosis grade 2-3, and cribriform apocrine without comedonecrosis grade 2. All 14 axillary lymph nodes assessed, including sentinel and palpable nodes, were negative for metastatic involvement. The margins of the resected specimen were negative, with the closest margins being 3 cm away at the anterior and posterior locations, respectively. Additionally, there was focal lymphovascular invasion noted, and microcalcifications associated with comedo-DCIS were present. The nipple and skin were negative for malignancy but had numerous pigmented seborrheic keratoses and simple lentigines. The pathological staging was determined to be stage I, with pTNM classification of pT1c and pN0, and clinical staging as cT1, cN0, and cM0. The specimen's integrity and orientation were intact, and no skeletal muscle was present. Other notable findings included two core biopsy sites, focal microcysts, mammary duct stasis/ectasia, and focal usual duct hyperplasia.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of anaplastic oligodendroglioma, WHO grade III, situated in the left frontal lobe of the brain, with an enhancing nodule. The tumor is characterized by a proliferation of neoplastic cells resembling oligodendrocytes, displaying prominent perinuclear halos, rounded hyperchromatic nuclei, and few cellular processes. The tumor exhibits moderate to marked cytologic atypia, arranged in sheets, and infiltrative behavior into both gray and white matter. Features such as microvascular proliferation, early necrosis, and isolated discrete necrosis are evident, with the latter potentially indicative of a previous biopsy site. Numerous mitotic figures (up to eight per 10 high power fields) and visible microgemicytes are noted. Additionally, microcalcifications and branching thin-walled vessels, hallmark features of oligodendrogliomas, are present. The calculated MIB-1 labeling index stands at 24.4%, highlighting the tumor’s proliferative activity. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not addressed in this specific examination, indicating a need for further investigation to ascertain comprehensive staging. The pathological characteristics confirm the aggressive nature of this anaplastic oligodendroglioma.

The pathological examination revealed hyalinized inactive granulomas in the excised lymph node from the aortopulmonary (AP) window, with no evidence of tumor presence. A segment of the rib excision showed unremarkable bone tissue. Lymph nodes from stations 5 and 7, as well as the periaortic tissue and lymph node L11, were all negative for tumor involvement, displaying only lymphoid tissue and associated adipose or fibrous connective tissue without any pathological abnormalities. Therefore, no evidence of malignancy was detected in any of the excised lymph nodes or other tissues, and all margin statuses were clear, indicating an absence of tumor infiltration. The findings point towards non-neoplastic pathology with no apparent metastatic involvement or primary tumor within the sampled specimens.

The pathological evaluation reveals an anaplastic oligoastrocytoma, predominantly astrocytoma, classified as WHO grade III. The tumor is characterized by moderate cellularity with diverse cellular components, including protoplasmic astrocytes, gemistocytes, microgemistocytes, and oligodendroglioma cells. The cellular atypia is predominantly mild but shows focal areas of moderate atypia. There are few scattered mitotic figures, indicating a relatively low mitotic activity. Additionally, the presence of microvascular proliferation is noted, though necrosis is absent. The tumor exhibits focal hypercellular clustering and shows prominent subpial accumulation with invasion into the subarachnoid space. The margin status and lymph node involvement were not specified in this evaluation.

The pathological findings indicate an ulcerated malignant melanoma of the left upper back, identified as a spindle cell type with notable features such as Clark's Level V and a Breslow depth exceeding 14 mm. The excised tumor was free at the surgical margins, indicating complete removal with no residual melanoma noted in additional medial or lateral margins, although solar elastosis and solar lentigo were present. Lymph node dissection of left axillary contents revealed metastatic melanoma in two of the twenty-five examined lymph nodes, with extracapsular extension signifying advanced disease spread. Mitotic activity was recorded at 7.98/mm^2, and a brisk infiltrate of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes was evident, but no angiolymphatic invasion, regression, or satellite lesions were observed. The overall pathological stage is pT4b N2b Mx, highlighting the severity and spread of the melanoma, with uninvolved margins ensuring no residual malignant tissue in resected areas.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of invasive squamous cell carcinoma with prominent basaloid features, moderately differentiated, located in the right lower lung lobe. The primary tumor measures 4.5 cm in its greatest dimension and is well-circumscribed. The surgical margins are negative for tumor involvement, indicating clear resection margins. There is no evidence of tumor extension into adjacent structures. Additionally, there is no venous, arterial, or lymphatic invasion observed. Lymph node analysis includes three negative middle lobe lymph node fragments, one negative hilar lymph node, and eight negative level 7 lymph node fragments, indicating the absence of regional lymph node involvement. Background lung parenchyma demonstrates respiratory bronchiolitis. Immunohistochemical staining results indicate that the neoplastic cells exhibit strong reactivity with CK5/6 and focal reactivity with CD56, while being non-reactive with TTF-1, synaptophysin, and chromogranin.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of invasive moderately differentiated keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma originating from the left mandible. The primary tumor exhibits angiolymphatic, perineural, and bone invasion, with a maximum size indicative of significant involvement. Carcinoma in situ is also present. The surgical soft tissue margins are negative for carcinoma, although the closest margin, located at the lateral posterior soft tissue, is notably close. The anterior and posterior mandible bone margins are negative for tumor involvement. Lymph node assessment shows metastatic squamous cell carcinoma in 3 out of 8 lymph nodes from the left hemimandibulectomy specimen and additional metastases in isolated regional lymph nodes, with the largest metastasis measuring 1.8 cm and no evidence of extracapsular extension. Additional lymph node examinations from various cervical levels show variable involvement; specifically, one out of twenty nodes at level 2A, one out of two nodes at right level 2, and three out of eight nodes at right level 1 exhibit metastasis, while other examined nodes show no tumor invasion. The high-risk HPV and p16 immunohistochemistry statuses are negative. The AJCC pathologic staging is pT4a pN2c, based on current findings, pending further clinical review. Additional findings include benign submandibular gland tissue and fragments of benign bone and soft tissue from tooth extractions.

The pathological findings encompass a diagnosis of multifocal invasive lobular carcinoma of the left breast, graded as Nottingham grade 2. The excised specimen reveals several foci of invasive carcinoma distributed over a lumpectomy area, with the largest focus measuring 3.5 cm. The carcinoma is notably present at the superior margin and in close proximity to the inferior margin. Additionally, lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS) is identified. Evaluation of sentinel lymph nodes from the left axilla reveals metastatic carcinoma in one lymph node with focal extranodal extension, while the remaining four lymph nodes show no evidence of carcinoma. The tumor demonstrates positive estrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR) status, but it is negative for HER2 by FISH. No necrosis or vascular/lymphatic invasion is identified. The pathological staging is determined as pT2 N1, based on the AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, indicating a larger primary tumor with limited regional lymph node involvement. Non-neoplastic findings include columnar cell change, and no ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) is present.

The pathological findings suggest a diagnosis of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma in the cecum, arising within a tubulovillous adenoma, and histopathologically classified as grade G2. The tumor exhibits necrosis and has infiltrated the wall layers of the colon, reaching the tunica muscularis. There is notable chronic granulomatous and recurrent peritumoral inflammation with an acute component, including partially purulent abscess formations and inflammatory adhesions involving the cecum, ascending colon, and omental fat tissue adjacent to the tumor. Additionally, chronic inflammation is present in the pelvic wall, alongside chronic lymphadenitis in the regional lymph nodes, featuring small focal epithelioid cell reactions and both structured and unstructured giant cells. Examination of resection margins shows they are free of tumor involvement. Associated findings include chronic recurrent appendicitis and chronic granulomatous periappendicitis. The tumor staging aligns with pT2, pN0, MX, R0, indicating localized tumor invasion into the muscular layer without lymph node metastasis, with no residual tumor at the resection margins and indeterminate distant metastasis.

The histopathological examination of the right breast specimen reveals an invasive ductal carcinoma, classified as grade 2 based on the Nottingham Histologic Score, which presents a solitary focal lesion. The tumor is located in the upper and lower inner quadrants. The invasive carcinoma extends perilously close to the deep margin, with a clearance of 0.4 mm, and also invades the dermal lymphatics without causing skin ulceration. No involvement of the nipple with ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) or lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS) is noted. The resected specimen includes the entire breast, the nipple, and overlying skin, with negative margins for invasive carcinoma. Lymph node assessment is not feasible as no lymph nodes were identified in the specimen. Estrogen receptor expression is noted in 10% of tumor cells with low-intensity staining, while progesterone receptor expression is absent. HER2/neu testing demonstrates positive immunohistochemical staining (3+) and gene amplification confirmed by FISH. Pathologic staging concludes with a primary tumor classification of pT2, with regional lymph nodes (pN) not assessable (pNX). The findings collectively characterize a moderately differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma with extension near the resection margins and dermal invasion but without evidence of nodal involvement or distant metastasis.

The pathological evaluation of the right parietal lobe brain tumor revealed a diagnosis of Glioblastoma Multiforme (WHO Grade IV). The biopsy and excision of the tumor confirmed the presence of this highly malignant neoplasm. Tumor samples obtained from multiple sites around the lesion, specifically at locations indicated as T=0 and T=+5, exhibited histopathological evidence consistent with glioblastoma multiforme, while no solid tumor was identified at the T=-5 site. Margin status was not explicitly delineated in the findings, albeit the characterization of the tumor's presence at multiple biopsied sites implies aggressive local infiltration. Lymph node involvement was not addressed, likely due to the primary intracerebral location of the tumor, which typically disseminates within the central nervous system rather than via the lymphatic system. Overall, the findings highlight a severe grade of malignant glioma, characterized by diffuse parenchymal invasion and significant pathological aggressiveness.

The pathological evaluation determined the liver nodule biopsy to exhibit minimally atypical epithelioid cell proliferation, negative for colorectal carcinoma, suggesting a benign process pending correlation with clinical findings. The right colon hemicolectomy revealed an invasive, moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with mucinous features located in the cecum. The tumor extends into the subserosal tissue without reaching the proximal, distal, or radial surgical margins, which remain tumor-free. An assessment of fifteen lymph nodes showed no carcinoma involvement. Additional findings include the absence of venous, lymphatic, and perineural invasion, with no significant pathology in the remaining ileal and colonic mucosa. The tumor's configuration was exophytic and infiltrative, indicating a complete resection with microscopically negative margins. Immunohistochemistry highlighted an absence of PMS2 expression, with positive results for MLH1, MSH2, and MSH6, pending further microsatellite instability testing.

The final pathological evaluation identifies a primary diagnosis of poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma, solid type with clear cell change, located in the right upper lobe of the lung. The tumor is classified as pT3, indicating significant local extension but does not involve the pleural surface. Examination of the surgical margins reveals no involvement by invasive carcinoma. Seventeen regional lymph nodes, including those from multiple stations, are negative for metastatic carcinoma, confirming a pN0 status. Additionally, there is no evidence of venous, arterial, or lymphatic invasion. Immunohistochemical staining shows that the tumor cells are positive for TTF-1 and variably positive for Napsin-A, with patchy p63 staining. Background lung parenchyma exhibits emphysematous changes and organizing lung injury adjacent to the tumor. Molecular analysis reveals no mutations in either the KRAS or EGFR genes.

The pathological examination of a right partial maxillectomy revealed a well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma located in the hard palate and maxilla. The tumor presented as a single focus with an ulcerated appearance and extended into the underlying bone. There was no evidence of lymphovascular invasion, perineural invasion, or extranodal extension. The margins of excision were free of both invasive carcinoma and carcinoma in situ, indicating complete resection. Lymph node involvement could not be assessed in this case. The tumor's depth of invasion was moderate, classifying it as pT2 based on its size criteria. Examination of the extracted teeth showed no remarkable findings, analyzed grossly without microscopic examination.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of WHO grade III fibrillary and gemistocytic astrocytoma, also known as anaplastic astrocytoma, in both the left frontal and left temporal regions of the brain. The tumor is characterized by a mixture of fibrillary and gemistocytic cells, indicative of a high-grade, aggressive glioma. The presence of anaplasia and increased mitotic activity distinguishes it as a grade III neoplasm. The margin status is not specified in the summary, suggesting the need for further investigation to determine the extent of tumor infiltration and the completeness of resection. Lymph node involvement is not mentioned, typically an indication that it is either not applicable or not assessed, given the primary brain tumor context. Additional relevant findings from the preliminary frozen section consultation confirm the presence of glioma in the left frontal mass, with a recommendation to classify and grade upon further analysis. These collective findings underscore a high-grade astrocytic tumor with a challenging prognosis requiring comprehensive therapeutic intervention.

The pathological examination reveals an anaplastic mixed glioma with a predominance of Grade II oligodendroglioma in the left temporal-parietal region of the brain. The tumor displays diffuse infiltration characterized by uniform, round nuclei, perinuclear halos, and thin-walled capillaries arranged in a chicken-wire pattern, consistent with oligodendroglioma. Mitotic figures are identified at a rate of up to 4 per high-power field in these areas, alongside focal vascular endothelial proliferation. Additionally, regions of astrocytoma with higher mitotic activity are present. Immunohistochemical staining indicates variable GFAP positivity, IDH1 positivity, and an overall MIB-1 labeling index of approximately 5%. Molecular testing confirms a co-deletion of 1p19q chromosomes. No specific information on margin status or lymph node involvement is provided in this examination. The gross examination includes multiple brain tissue fragments showing similar pathological features with occasional necrotic areas. The findings suggest a complex glioma with both oligodendroglial and astrocytic components necessitating a combined therapeutic approach.

The essential pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of well-differentiated invasive squamous carcinoma involving multiple anatomical sites. The carcinoma exhibits bone invasion in the left nasal intima, left infraorbital nerve, pterygoid, and other associated specimens, except for the remnants of the mandible, which appear grossly unremarkable. Notably, the tumor infiltrates the perineural spaces of the infraorbital nerve. Margin status indicates that the tumor is less than 0.1 mm from the surgical margin in the pterygoid specimen and within 0.1 mm of the superior and posterior soft tissue margins in the hemimandibulectomy specimen, while all other surgical margins are free of tumor involvement. Lymph node assessment and lymphatic involvement are not addressed in the findings presented. Additional observations include the presence of sinonasal mucosa with focal granulation and fibrosis without tumor involvement in the infraorbital tissue. Specimens from the pterygoid plate are pending decalcification for further assessment. The comprehensive examination consolidates the pathological intricacies of the well-differentiated squamous carcinoma invasion, critical margin assessments, and absence of tumor in specific tissues.

The pathological examination of the left breast mass has confirmed a diagnosis of poorly differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma, classified as grade III per the modified Bloom-Richardson grading system, accompanied by extensive necrosis. The tumor measures approximately 3.0 cm in diameter and presents with minimal high-grade in-situ components. Lymphovascular space invasion is present, highlighting the aggressive nature of the malignancy. Notably, the tumor extends to within 1 mm of the deep surgical margin, indicating a close proximity to critical tissue boundaries, which might necessitate further surgical intervention. Immunohistochemically, the tumor is negative for estrogen receptors and shows focal positivity for progesterone receptors, while HER2/neu expression is negative (0+). Additionally, a hyalinized fibroadenoma is identified within the specimen. The gross examination describes a firm, well-circumscribed mass with tan, gritty, and focally hemorrhagic features against a predominantly fatty and unremarkable background. These findings collectively provide a comprehensive insight into the tumor's aggressive and invasive pathology, guiding potential therapeutic and surgical approaches.

The essential pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma in situ located predominantly in the right posterior buccal margin, right anterior lip margin, and various margins of the tongue including the left and right bases. Additionally, invasive poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with basaloid features was identified in the anterior floor of the mouth, mandible, displaying a significant depth of invasion and identified local soft tissue metastasis in a minor salivary gland. There is focal lymphovascular invasion, but no perineural invasion. Examination of numerous lymph nodes from various dissections, including those from levels IIB, III, IV, and the central compartment, demonstrated no tumor involvement in all sampled nodes. Margins of resected specimens are noted to be free of tumor, despite sections showing bone invasion by the squamous carcinoma. Furthermore, corresponding peripheral nerve biopsies and bone marrow touch preps remained clear of tumor presence. The decalcification process confirmed bone invasion without compromising the margin status, which remains clear of tumor cells.

The pathological examination of the right upper lobe following a lobectomy for lung carcinoma reveals a poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with extensive central necrosis and cavitation. The tumor does not invade the pleural surface and is clear of the hilar resection margin by a significant distance. Although no lymphatic invasion is evident, metastatic carcinoma is detected in one peribronchial lymph node, confirming lymph node involvement. Vascular invasion is present, adding to the tumor's aggressive characteristics. The bronchial resection margin is free of malignancy but shows a benign mucous gland adenoma. The adjacent lung parenchyma displays organizing pneumonia, and there is emphysema in the distant lung tissue. A separate pleural nodule is identified as a silicotic nodule within a pleural lymph node. Additional sampling of the hilar lymph node and mediastinal lymph node shows no evidence of metastatic carcinoma, although pigmented macrophages are present. The partial bronchus specimen shows squamous metaplasia with severe dysplasia, falling short of carcinoma in situ, and no invasive malignancy. The final pathological staging is T2 N1, indicative of the tumor size and lymph node metastasis.

The pathological examination of the right temporal lobe mass reveals a diagnosis of anaplastic oligoastrocytoma, classified as WHO Grade III. The tumor exhibits high cellularity, significant mitotic activity, and a mixture of oligodendroglial and astrocytic differentiation. Notably, there is pronounced microvascular proliferation and the presence of multiple areas of coagulative necrosis, which lack the characteristic peripheral palisading of tumor cells. Immunohistochemical analysis for the IDH1 mutation is negative, and fluorescence in situ hybridization confirms the absence of deletions in chromosomes 1p and 19q. The intraoperative consultation suggested a diagnosis of glioblastoma based on touch imprint and microscopic evaluation, but the final diagnosis based on comprehensive analysis is anaplastic oligoastrocytoma. The pathological findings underscore the aggressive nature of the tumor, requiring further clinical correlation and management.

The pathological analysis reveals a diagnosis of infiltrating ductal carcinoma with focal lobular features in the right breast, graded as Nottingham grade 2. The tumor exhibits a moderate mitotic index and significant size. Examination of the deep margin and the skin overlying the tumor reveals no malignancy, indicating clear resection margins. However, carcinoma presence is confirmed in lymphatics in the nipple. Metastatic involvement is identified in the lymph nodes, with 2 out of 2 sentinel lymph nodes in the right axilla and 1 out of 9 axillary lymph nodes being positive for metastatic carcinoma. The total lymph node count includes 11 nodes, and the largest metastasis measures up to 3.5 mm without extranodal extension or matted lymph nodes. The left breast tissue is negative for malignancy. Pathologically, the tumor is staged as pTNM T3 pN1a.

The final pathological diagnosis indicates an invasive poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma located in the left upper lobe of the lung. The tumor displays significant necrosis and hemorrhage, and although it invades an adjacent bronchus and surrounds large vessels, it does not breach the visceral pleura. Four sampled peribronchiolar lymph nodes and additional lymph nodes from levels 10 and 11, as well as the AP window, are free of neoplastic involvement. Margins, including bronchial, vascular, parenchymal, and pleural, are all negative for malignancy. Notably, there is no evidence of vascular or lymphatic invasion. The presence of centrilobular emphysema is also noted in the surrounding lung parenchyma. Immunohistochemical staining reveals strong positivity for p63 and negativity for TTF-1, affirming the squamous cell carcinoma diagnosis. The pathologic staging is determined to be pT2aN0, corresponding to Stage IB.

The pathological examination of the resected sigmoid colon reveals a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, characterized by the infiltration of the perimuscular fatty tissue. Importantly, the surgical resection margins, both oral and aboral, are free of tumor involvement, indicating successful removal of the primary malignancy (R0). No regional lymph node metastases are observed, with all examined lymph nodes (0 out of 37) showing no evidence of tumor (pN0). While vascular invasion is absent (V0), lymphatic invasion is noted (L1), suggesting potential microscopic spread through the lymphatic channels. Additionally, the pericolic lymph nodes exhibit an unusual appearance upon microscopic examination, raising a clinical suspicion of underlying lymphatic leukemia. In the absence of a clinical diagnosis of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), further consultation and investigation via the Lymph Node Registry are recommended to conclusively explore this possibility and ensure comprehensive pathological assessment.

The pathology findings correspond to a well-differentiated, infiltrating squamous cell carcinoma of the right tonsil, identified following a right radical tonsillectomy and partial glossectomy. The tumor extends to the inked deep surgical margin in the posterior lateral aspect and approaches within less than 1 mm of the anterior medial aspect, while the remaining margins, including the anterior tongue margin, are clear. There is no evidence of overt lymphovascular invasion. Lymph node involvement includes four out of eight positive right level 2A lymph nodes, with all right level 2B and level 3 lymph nodes testing negative. The pathologic stage is determined to be T2, N2b under the AJCC classification. These comprehensive findings support the diagnosis and provide critical information on tumor spread and margin involvement, essential for guiding subsequent management and treatment planning.

The pathological examination reveals the presence of glioblastoma multiforme, classified as WHO grade IV, in the right frontal brain tissue. The neoplastic astrocytes display characteristic "naked" infiltration nuclei and cell patterns, alongside typical microcalcifications. The tumor exhibits significant cortical infiltration with perineuronal satellitosis and numerous mitotic figures, pseudopalisading, and regional necrosis, though it lacks definite vascular endothelial proliferation. Gross examination highlights a hemorrhagic, soft, gray-red lesion with areas of yellow-orange discoloration, distinguished from adjacent normal brain architecture. Lymph node involvement is not applicable to the CNS region, and the margin status is inherently tied to the infiltrative nature of the tumor. A tissue block from the specimen will undergo further analysis to provide additional insights.

The pathological examination reveals an invasive moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma in the sigmoid colon, extending through the muscularis propria into the perirectal fat, without evidence of angiolymphatic or perineural invasion. The surgical resection margins are confirmed to be free of tumor, and all three examined pericolic lymph nodes are negative for tumor involvement, staging the local disease at pT3 N0 M0. However, a separate liver biopsy indicates the presence of metastatic moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, thereby designating a pathologic stage of pT3 N0 M1. Additionally, hyperplastic polyps were found in the colon. The primary tumor exhibits an ulcerating configuration and a low histologic grade. There is a mild to moderate tumoral lymphocytic response noted. The comprehensive evaluation of the resected specimen confirms complete clearance at the proximal, distal, and circumferential surgical margins.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of gynecomastia with no evidence of malignancy in the left breast following a prophylactic mastectomy. In contrast, the right breast modified radical mastectomy showed invasive ductal carcinoma, Nottingham grade 3, measuring 4.7 cm, with focal features of invasive micropapillary carcinoma. The tumor also revealed high-grade ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) with cribriform and papillary architecture, comedo necrosis, and calcifications present, involving adjacent lactiferous ducts and the upper inner quadrant. The surgical margins are free of tumor, with the carcinoma located 0.2 cm from the deep margin and at least 2 cm from all other margins. Metastatic carcinoma was identified in 3 of 17 axillary lymph nodes, with the largest metastatic focus measuring 1.3 cm, though no extranodal extension was observed. The presence of lymphovascular invasion supports the advanced staging, and the disease is staged as pT2 pN1a pM1, indicating stage IV due to distant metastasis to the iliac bone. Ancillary studies showed 100% estrogen receptor positivity with strong intensity, negative progesterone receptor status, HER2 IHC score of 1+, and a Ki-67 proliferation index of 15%.

The pathology findings reveal a high-grade, non-papillary urothelial carcinoma of the bladder with focal squamous differentiation, which invades the bladder wall transmurally and extends into the perivesical fat. The serosal surface is free of tumor, and no lymphovascular invasion is identified. The resection margins are also free of tumor. In the prostate, the urothelial carcinoma extends to the periurethral prostatic ducts and exhibits superficial invasion of the periductal stroma. The prostatic tissue shows glandular atrophy and peri-basilar cell hyperplasia with atypia, consistent with treatment effect, and is characterized by chronic and focal acute inflammation. There is no residual prostatic carcinoma identified. Importantly, the lymph node examination, including left and right external iliac, obturator, and common iliac lymph nodes, shows no evidence of tumoral involvement. Additionally, the final margins of both the left and right ureters display unremarkable urothelium with no tumor presence. These findings collectively suggest a comprehensive evaluation of the resected tissues, with key insights into the extent and margins of the urothelial carcinoma and associated pathological changes.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of moderately differentiated invasive squamous carcinoma of the alveolar ridge, involving the alveolar bone. The anterior and posterior mandibular margins of resection are free of tumor, ensuring clear surgical margins in these areas. Examination of lymph nodes revealed no presence of metastatic disease, with one left middle jugular lymph node and thirteen cervical lymph nodes (comprising submental-submaxillary, subdigastric, midjugular, and upper posterior cervical nodes), all devoid of tumor. Additionally, the submaxillary salivary gland showed no evidence of malignancy. The carcinoma grossly involved the anterior and medial soft tissue margins; however, the final mucosal and soft tissue margins of resection are confirmed to be free of tumor, as indicated in the supplemental report. There was also the evaluation of four teeth included in the specimen. Overall, these findings suggest effective surgical resection with no residual tumor in the critical resection margins or lymph nodes.

The histopathological examination of the total organ resection from the right breast with axillary tissues in a female patient revealed an invasive ductal carcinoma (NHG3), characterized by high mitotic activity and significant nuclear atypia. Additionally, numerous foci of ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) were present within the tumor, specifically of the solid type with comedo necrosis and calcifications, contributing to 20% of the tumor volume. The carcinoma demonstrated extensive vascular invasion and carcinoma emboli within the nipple area. Marginally, in situ lesions were found close to the base. Lymph node analysis showed extensive metastatic involvement in all sampled axillary lymph nodes, with perinodal tissue infiltration and vascular embolization. Immunohistochemically, estrogen receptors were present in over 75% of the neoplastic cell nuclei, while progesterone receptors were found in 10-75% of the neoplastic cells. HER2 protein expression yielded a score of 2+, indicating a recommendation for further FISH verification. The overall diagnosis is invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast with extensive axillary lymph node metastases, demonstrating a high-grade tumor with considerable vascular invasion.

The essential pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of moderately differentiated invasive squamous cell carcinoma located in the right lower lobe of the lung. The primary tumor is classified as stage pT2a, with no evidence of regional lymph node involvement (pN0) as all twenty-five lymph nodes examined were free of tumor. The surgical margins are clear, denoted as R0, indicating no residual tumor at the resection margins. Additionally, there is no evidence of lymphovascular or perineural invasion. The pathologic stage is designated as IB, with no involvement of distant metastasis assessed (pMX). Notably, there is no visceral pleural invasion or extension into extra-pulmonary structures. The margin evaluation confirms that the bronchial margin is negative at a safe distance from the tumor. Other findings include an uninvolved lung exhibiting features of organizing pneumonia, with no significant pathological deviations in the sampled lymph nodes. The overall clinical and histopathological features support a favorable prognosis post right lobectomy and regional lymph node dissection.

A pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of gliosarcoma, which is glioblastoma multiforme with areas demonstrating focal spindle cell differentiation, classified as WHO Grade IV. The tumor exhibits coagulative necrosis prominently, accompanied by mild microvascular proliferation and significant leptomeningeal invasion, consistent with an aggressive nature. Reticulin histochemistry along with immunohistochemical staining for neurofilaments and endothelial antigens corroborate the diagnosis. Additionally, biopsy of the dura mater indicates the presence of adherent malignant glioma. These findings collectively underscore the malignant and invasive characteristics of the gliosarcoma, particularly with its extension into the leptomeninges and involving the dura mater.

The pathological examination reveals an infiltrating ductal carcinoma of the right breast, specifically located in the lower inner quadrant. The tumor exhibits moderately differentiated (Grade 2) histologic features. Radical mastectomy was performed, and the tumor was classified as T2, indicating a size greater than 2 cm but not exceeding 5 cm. Importantly, there is no evidence of venous invasion, and the surgical margins are clear of the tumor. Of the ten axillary lymph nodes examined, none demonstrated metastatic involvement, though they showed sinus histiocytosis. These findings confirm the absence of metastatic spread to the lymph nodes, suggesting a localized disease at this stage.

The patient's pathological assessment revealed a moderately differentiated, keratinizing type of invasive squamous cell carcinoma originating in the floor of the mouth and extending from the ventral aspect of the tongue to the anterior surface of the gums. The tumor was characterized by a single exophytic mass without additional focal tumors. Marginal analysis confirmed all the margins were free of invasive carcinoma, with the closest margins being within 2 to 3 mm of the tumor. Lymphovascular invasion was identified, though there was no evidence of perineural invasion. Pathological staging identified the primary tumor as pT3, indicating a tumor size exceeding 4 cm in its greatest dimension. Lymph node evaluation indicated metastatic involvement of a single ipsilateral lymph node out of 64 examined. All examined margins of the resected tissue (ventral surface of the tongue, lower gingiva, and anterior floor of mouth) were uninvolved by carcinoma. Examination of the submandibular gland and various level neck dissections from both sides (levels 2-4) showed no evidence of malignancy with lymph nodes being benign. The patient's pre/post-operative diagnosis was squamous cell carcinoma of the mandible, with no distant metastasis detected.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of well-differentiated astrocytoma, WHO Grade II, in the left temporal region. The tumor consists of moderately hypercellular glial neoplasm predominantly composed of fibrillary and protoplasmic astrocytes, with prominent microcystic changes. The neoplastic cells are consistently immunoreactive for p53, confirming their astrocytic origin. Although scattered MIB-1 reactive cells are present throughout the specimen with a labeling index averaging 3.6%, one area exhibits higher proliferative activity with a labeling index of 7.1%, warranting close follow-up despite not meeting criteria for anaplastic classification. There is notable mild atypia with only a few moderately atypical nuclei observed; no mitoses, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis are evident. Additionally, less than 10% of the specimen shows infiltrating low-grade glioma, without definitive oligodendroglioma differentiation. The majority of the tissue consists of acute hematoma and surgical packing material, and no specific measurements for tumor margins or lymph node involvement are provided in this summary.

The pathological findings indicate a colon resection specimen containing a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of colorectal origin. The tumor demonstrates infiltration into the pericolic fatty tissue and is associated with metastatic involvement of two regional lymph nodes out of a total of thirteen examined. Importantly, the resection margins of the colon are free of tumor cells. The tumor is classified as stage pT3 pN1, reflecting its invasive nature and limited lymph node spread, with a histologic grade of G2, suggesting moderate differentiation. Lymphatic invasion is present (L1), though vascular invasion is absent (V0). Clinically, the patient has evidence of liver metastases, corresponding to stage pM1 in the TNM staging system, and the overall assessment denotes residual disease (R2), indicating that the surgical resection was not completely curative due to the presence of distant metastasis.

The pathological evaluation of the resected colon sample, particularly from the rectosigmoid region, revealed an ulcerated, moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. The carcinoma demonstrated infiltration into the perimuscular fatty tissue but did not exhibit metastasis in the local lymph nodes. Critical examination confirmed that both oral and aboral resection margins were free of tumor involvement, ensuring clean surgical margins. The cancer was classified as moderately differentiated (G2) and staged as pT3, indicating significant depth of invasion without vascular invasion (V0), and absence of residual tumor post-surgery (RO). The examination of 29 lymph nodes revealed no metastatic involvement (pN0, 0/29), suggesting no spread beyond the primary tumor site.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of low-grade oligoastrocytoma, classified as WHO Grade II. The tumor is characterized by a diffusely infiltrating glioma involving both gray and white matter, exhibiting mild to moderate hypercellularity. The cells display round to somewhat irregular nuclei with scant cytoplasmic processes and frequent perinuclear halos. Mitotic activity is rare, and there are no signs of microvascular proliferation or necrosis. The MIB-1 labeling index, which measures the proliferative activity, is 6.1% in the most active areas, indicating an intermediate level of proliferation. Importantly, this degree of proliferation does not qualify the tumor for reclassification as anaplastic. Details on margin status and lymph node involvement are not applicable in the context of this brain tumor.

The patient was diagnosed with moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the left lung. The primary tumor, located in the left mainstem bronchus, measured the greatest dimension noted as 2.7 cm and did not invade the visceral pleura. Importantly, bronchial and vascular margins were negative for carcinoma, indicating that the surgical excision was successful in removing the cancerous tissue without leaving residual disease. Additionally, no lymph-vascular invasion was identified, and there were no signs of squamous cell carcinoma in situ at the bronchial margin. The excised lymph nodes, including hilar, mediastinal, and peribronchial nodes, showed no metastatic involvement in any of the sampled nodes. Specifically, a total of 22 lymph nodes were examined, and all were free of carcinoma. The findings collectively indicate that the cancer was localized without evidence of spread to regional lymph nodes or distant sites, as reflected by the pT1b and pN0 staging. Other findings included focal bronchioloectasis adjacent to the tumor. The comprehensive pathological evaluation suggests a favorable surgical outcome with no residual tumor detected at the surgical margins or within the lymphatic system.

The histopathological examination reveals a diagnosis of oligoastrocytoma, classified as a grade II tumor according to the World Health Organization (WHO) grading system. The tumor predominantly consists of a mixed population of oligodendroglial and astrocytic cells, characterized by moderate cellular density and cytological atypia, but lacks significant mitotic activity, necrosis, or endothelial proliferation, which is consistent with its grade II designation. The surgical resection margins are clear, indicating no residual tumor cells at the edges of the excised tissue. Additionally, there is no evidence of lymph node involvement or metastasis, which is typical for this type of primary central nervous system tumor. Immunohistochemical analysis further supports the diagnosis, identifying positive staining for relevant markers that differentiate between the oligodendroglial and astrocytic components. Overall, the findings are consistent with a moderately aggressive neoplasm with a favorable margin status and no indications of regional or distant spread.

The pathological evaluation reveals an anaplastic oligoastrocytoma, classified as Grade III. The tumor is described as grayish and soft-elastic, involving the right occipital and parietal lobes. Microscopically, the brain tissue shows characteristics consistent with anaplastic oligoastrocytoma, indicating a high-grade malignancy. The tumor exhibits features such as increased cellularity, significant pleomorphism, and mitotic activity. Margin status remains a critical factor, though specific details about its involvement are not described. The assessment does not mention lymph node involvement, which is less commonly addressed in primary brain tumors. Overall, the findings underscore a high-grade, aggressive neoplasm necessitating a thorough treatment approach.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of adenocarcinoma in the left lower lobe lung. The tumor is well-differentiated with focal mucin production and is confined to a single site within the left lower lobe. Importantly, there is no invasion into the visceral pleura or lymphovascular spaces, and the tumor has not extended beyond its local region. Margins are clear with the tumor proximate but not encroaching upon the bronchial and parenchymal resection margins. No malignancy is detected in the examined level 5 and level 15 lymph nodes. Molecular analysis indicates that mutations commonly associated with targeted therapies, such as EGFR and KRAS, are not present, and no ALK gene rearrangement is observed, although an incidental gain of the ALK region is noted with uncertain clinical relevance. Immunohistochemical staining for TTF1 supports a primary lung origin. The tumor is classified as pT1b. Further ancillary studies and additional genomic analysis are pending to provide more comprehensive characterization and potential therapeutic targets.

The pathologic findings indicate a diagnosis of poorly differentiated non-small cell carcinoma with squamous features located in the right upper lobe following lobectomy. The primary tumor is classified as pT2, and all 13 regional lymph nodes examined were negative for metastatic involvement (pN0). The resection margins were free of invasive carcinoma, suggesting complete removal of the tumor. No evidence of distant metastasis was observed; however, distant metastatic status remains indeterminate (pMx). Additionally, there was no evidence of venous or arterial large vessel invasion, and lymphatic invasion could not be conclusively determined. Multiple excised lymph nodes, including those from regions R10, R4, high R4, R11, and station 7, were all negative for tumor involvement, further supporting the absence of regional spread. The tumor was noted to be adjacent to the pleura but did not involve the excision margins significantly, confirming successful surgical excision with clear margins.

The pathological findings from the right colon hemicolectomy reveal an intramucosal well-differentiated adenocarcinoma arising from a tubulovillous adenoma, located in the cecum. The tumor exhibits an exophytic (polypoid) configuration and microscopically extends into the lamina propria without evidence of further invasion. The surgical resection margins, including proximal, distal, and radial, are free of tumor involvement. Examination of the appendix shows no tumor presence. Additionally, all 24 dissected lymph nodes are negative for metastatic disease, with no signs of venous, lymphatic, or perineural invasion. The complete resection was achieved with grossly and microscopically negative margins, classified as an R0 resection. The pathological staging is designated as pTis N0 MX, indicating an in situ carcinoma with no regional lymph node metastasis, according to the AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 7th Edition.

The pathological evaluation of the left upper lobe lobectomy reveals an adenocarcinoma with solid, acinar, and bronchioloalveolar components, accompanied by centrilobular emphysema and pulmonary hemosiderosis. The tumor extends into the visceral pleura but does not exhibit vascular invasion, while lymphatic invasion status is pending. The bronchial and vascular margins are negative for tumor involvement, although tumor cells are present within lymphatic spaces at the bronchial margin. Metastatic adenocarcinoma is identified in the peribronchial, 11L, and #5 lymph nodes, whereas the 10L and 12L lymph nodes are free from neoplastic involvement. Necrotizing granulomas are also noted in the peribronchial lymph nodes. Immunohistochemical analysis shows diffuse TTF-1 and focal p-63 positivity in tumor cells, with negative results for fungal organisms and acid-fast bacilli on special stains. Molecular testing indicates no EGFR mutations, confirming a wild-type genotype. The pathological staging is determined as pT2a N2 MX, corresponding to stage IIIA.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of Glioblastoma Multiforme (WHO Grade IV) involving the left parietal lobe of the brain. The tumor exhibits classic characteristics of glioblastoma, such as heterogeneous regions of necrosis and cellular proliferation. Margin status from the biopsy (specimen A) is not explicitly clear, but specimen B shows relatively normal-appearing neural parenchyma infiltrated by scattered glioma cells, suggesting tumor infiltration into adjacent brain tissue. This finding underscores the aggressive and infiltrative nature of the tumor. Additionally, there is no mention of lymph node involvement, consistent with the primary central nervous system localization of glioblastomas, which typically do not metastasize to lymph nodes. The findings collectively highlight the invasive and highly malignant behavior of this tumor, necessitating a complex treatment approach and thorough clinical management.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of adenocarcinoma in the right upper lobe of the lung, characterized as moderately to poorly differentiated. The primary tumor measures 3.0 cm at its greatest dimension and does not demonstrate bronchial or visceral pleural involvement. Margins of resection are negative, indicating clear surgical margins with no residual tumor left behind. There is no evidence of direct extension of the tumor, venous or arterial invasion, and no angiolymphatic spread. Evaluation of regional lymph nodes shows none definitively identified. Additionally, the upper lobe resection reveals emphysematous changes, intra-alveolar hemorrhage, and calcified nodules. The right middle lobe wedge resection contains two fibrotic pleural-based nodules without malignancy. Examination of the 2R fat pad and 4R submission reveals benign fibroadipose and fibrous tissues, respectively, with no definitive lymph nodes identified and no malignancy evident. The right lower lobe wedge resection shows a calcified fibrotic nodule with no malignant cells present.

The pathological analysis of various tissue biopsies and excisions from the tongue and neck reveals significant findings. A well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma was identified in the right posterior portion of the tongue, with a primary tumor size noted. Importantly, there was no evidence of angiolymphatic invasion, although perineural invasion was present. The surgical margins from the excised tissue were clear of tumor cells, signifying negative margin status. Lymph node analysis from the right neck revealed no malignancy in most nodes, except for one positive lymph node in Level 2, which contained metastatic squamous cell carcinoma without extranodal extension. The pathological staging of the disease was determined to be pT2 pN1 based on AJCC criteria. Biopsies from various margins of the tongue, including medial, posterior base, lateral deep, lateral mucosal, and anterior areas, all showed no evidence of malignancy but did indicate varying degrees of acute and chronic inflammation in some samples. This comprehensive evaluation aids in the understanding of the tumor's extent and provides crucial information for further clinical management.

The pathological examination of the right breast tissue reveals an infiltrative ductal carcinoma, which is the primary tumor. This high-grade (Grade 3) neoplasm was obtained through a radical mastectomy procedure. The tumor is staged as T2, indicating a moderate size with considerable invasion into surrounding tissue, but no regional lymph node involvement (N0) or distant metastasis (M0) was detected. The margins of resection appear clear, implying no residual tumor at the edges of the excised tissue. The nuclear morphology confirms the aggressive nature of the tumor. Additionally, a normal blood sample was procured and preserved without abnormalities. No adjuvant treatment has been administered prior to surgery.

The pathology findings indicate a diagnosis of high-grade invasive urothelial carcinoma. The primary tumor invades the full thickness of the bladder wall, extending into adjacent adipose tissue and the prostatic stroma, classified as pT4. The tumor is present at the posterior soft tissue margin and the prostatic urethral margin. Additionally, there is invasion into the prostatic stroma from the prostatic urethra, with focal carcinoma in situ and high-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma. Lymph node involvement is confirmed with 2 out of 11 regional lymph nodes positive for metastatic carcinoma (pN2), including 1 left pelvic lymph node showing extracapsular extension of the tumor. Notable pathological findings also include vascular and perineural invasion and involvement of the left vas deferens. The right and left distal ureters show focal urothelial atypia with inflammation, and the bladder's surrounding tissue has areas of diverticulosis with diverticulitis and a benign adenomatous polyp in the sigmoid colon. The appendix reveals no significant histopathologic abnormalities.

The pathology findings indicate a diagnosis of high-grade invasive urothelial carcinoma, primarily involving the urinary bladder and prostate. The radical cystoprostatectomy and extended pelvic lymph node dissection revealed a 7 cm mass with extensive carcinoma in situ (CIS) affecting the prostate, including its ducts and acini, as well as the ejaculatory duct and seminal vesicles. The bladder shows multifocal lymphovascular invasion and invasion into the inner half of the muscular propria, but importantly, all surgical margins across various organs and structures are free of tumor involvement. Examination of the lymph nodes identified metastatic carcinoma in three out of twenty-two nodes (one left pelvic and two right pelvic lymph nodes). The specimen was staged as pT4a, N2, M0, grouping it as Stage IV. No prostate adenocarcinoma was detected, and no lymphovascular invasion was noted within the prostate-specific examination. Additional findings include focal low-grade dysplasia in the right distal ureter and reactive atypia in the apical urethral surgical margin. There were no diagnostic alterations in a discarded segment of the small bowel.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of high-grade urothelial carcinoma with extensive small cell differentiation located on the left lateral wall of the bladder. The tumor exhibits invasive characteristics, penetrating into the muscularis propria. Additionally, there is evidence of lymphovascular invasion, indicating a higher risk for potential metastasis. The presence of these aggressive features underscores the severity of the disease and highlights the need for a comprehensive therapeutic approach. Margin status and lymph node involvement were not explicitly mentioned in the findings, leaving a gap in the complete staging of the pathology.

The pathological findings reveal a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the colorectal type. The tumor infiltrates the tunica muscularis propria and extends up to the border of the pericolic fatty tissue. No lymph node involvement is observed, with all 27 examined lymph nodes free of metastasis. The tumor is characterized as stage pT2, pNO, pMX, and grade 2 (G2), indicating a lack of lymphovascular invasion (L0, V0) and complete surgical resection with negative margins (R0). These findings are crucial for guiding further management and potential adjuvant therapy for the patient.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of benign vaginal squamous mucosa and submucosal stroma in the vaginal biopsy, with no carcinoma identified. Both the left and right ovaries, removed via oophorectomy, contain benign cysts, also devoid of carcinoma. The distal sigmoid colon and rectum resection reveal a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma penetrating into the submucosa, with the muscularis propria uninvolved. There is no evidence of lymphatic, venous, or perineural invasion. Surgical margins, including proximal, distal, and circumferential, are negative, ensuring complete excision with the nearest margin being 2.0 cm (distal). Four examined regional lymph nodes show no metastasis, leading to an AJCC stage of pT1 pN0 pMx for the colorectal adenocarcinoma. Additionally, resection of the terminal ileum and proximal right colon confirmed the presence of four adenomatous polyps (tubular adenomas) without invasive carcinoma, and none of the eight pericolic lymph nodes showed metastatic carcinoma. Overall, no further pathological findings were identified to suggest additional malignancies, complementing the clinical history of a rectal moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma and a tubulovillous adenoma in the cecum.

The pathological analysis of the left lower lobectomy specimen revealed a diagnosis of adenocarcinoma with a lepidic pattern. The tumor is described as minimally invasive, non-mucinous, well-differentiated, and unifocal, without evidence of visceral pleura invasion or significant extension beyond the subpleural region. Surgical margins are clear, with the invasive carcinoma being well-distanced from the closest bronchial margin. Examination revealed no lymph-vascular invasion. Regional lymph nodes were assessed, with none of the three lymph nodes from the left inferior pulmonary ligament showing malignancy, and only one out of seventeen lymph nodes showing involvement, classifying the regional lymphatic spread as pN1. The primary tumor is staged as pT1b with no identifiable distant metastases (pMX). Overall, the absence of involvement at the bronchial, vascular, and parenchymal margins along with the well-differentiated histologic grade suggests a favorable prognosis post-surgery.

The pathological examination revealed hyalinized inactive granulomas in the excised lymph node from the aortopulmonary (AP) window, with no evidence of tumor presence. A segment of the rib excision showed unremarkable bone tissue. Lymph nodes from stations 5 and 7, as well as the periaortic tissue and lymph node L11, were all negative for tumor involvement, displaying only lymphoid tissue and associated adipose or fibrous connective tissue without any pathological abnormalities. Therefore, no evidence of malignancy was detected in any of the excised lymph nodes or other tissues, and all margin statuses were clear, indicating an absence of tumor infiltration. The findings point towards non-neoplastic pathology with no apparent metastatic involvement or primary tumor within the sampled specimens.

The pathological examination reveals a high-grade invasive urothelial carcinoma predominantly involving the posterior aspect of the urinary bladder, with extension to the right and left lateral walls and involvement of the posterior inked surgical margin. The carcinoma also demonstrated angiolymphatic invasion and extended into the perivesical fat. Lymph node dissection showed metastatic carcinoma consistent with a urothelial primary in lymph nodes from the right external iliac region (2/5) and the right common iliac region (1/3), while other excised lymph nodes (left distal ureter, right distal ureter, left external iliac, left obturator and deep obturator, left common iliac, left para-aortic, presacral, right obturator and deep obturator, and right paracaval) were negative for tumor involvement. The pathological staging identifies the primary tumor as pT3a and regional lymph nodes as pN3. In addition, the prostate gland harbored multifocal well-differentiated prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason grade 6 (3+3), involving 2% of the prostate tissue without extraprostatic extension and negative margins. The periprostatic and seminal vesicle tissues were not invaded. Overall, the metastatic involvement in the lymph nodes and the invasive nature of the urothelial carcinoma suggest an advanced disease state requiring comprehensive therapeutic planning.

The pathological examination reveals an anaplastic oligoastrocytoma, predominantly displaying astrocytoma characteristics. This markedly hypercellular glial neoplasm is composed of atypical cells resembling astrocytes, with a significant oligodendroglial component. The neoplastic cells exhibit pleomorphic and often bizarre nuclei, along with a notable presence of gemistocytes in some regions. The tumor diffusely infiltrates adjacent gray and white matter, with scattered mitotic figures evident throughout the sample. Histological analysis also identifies occasional foci of early spontaneous necrosis and microvascular proliferation. The MIB-1 labeling index reaches up to 10.2% in the most proliferative areas of the tumor, indicating a high proliferation rate. These findings are consistent with the diagnosis of an anaplastic oligoastrocytoma, favoring astrocytoma predominance. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not addressed in this summary.

The pathological findings reveal the presence of invasive ductal carcinoma with mucinous features in the left breast. The tumor is classified as SBR Grade 2 and has a multifocal distribution, predominantly involving the upper outer quadrant. The invasive carcinoma measures at least 3 cm in its largest focus, with an overall span of approximately 7 cm. Importantly, the surgical margins are free of tumor involvement. Additionally, ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of micropapillary, papillary, cribriform, and solid types, nuclear grade 2 with necrosis, is identified and associated with the invasive tumor, comprising about 35% of the tumor volume. The skin, nipple, and skeletal muscle associated with the left breast show no signs of tumor infiltration. Both sentinel lymph nodes from the left axilla are negative for carcinoma, implying no lymph node involvement. Pathological staging is determined as pT2N0. Estrogen and progesterone receptors are positive, while HER2 is negative. The Oncotype DX Breast Cancer Assay yields a recurrence score of 21, correlating with a 14% distant recurrence rate at 10 years. The right breast tissue exhibits benign changes, including apocrine metaplasia and stromal fibrosis, with unremarkable skin and no malignancy.

The pathological examination of the left breast wide local excision reveals an infiltrating ductal carcinoma, Nottingham grade II, forming a multilobulated mass adjoining the prior core biopsy site, classified as AJCC pT1c. Additionally, ductal carcinoma in situ of the solid type with high nuclear grade is noted outside the primary tumor mass. No evidence of angiolymphatic invasion is present. Skin examination shows no diagnostic abnormalities. All surgical resection margins are negative for the tumor following the re-excision of the deep margin involving skeletal muscle. However, multiple left axillary lymph nodes (3 out of 25) are positive for metastatic carcinoma, with evidence of extranodal extension, and two of these positive nodes are matted together, which corresponds to AJCC pN1. Testing for Her-2/NEU on paraffin-embedded tissue has been ordered for further evaluation.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon, displaying a variable histologic grade ranging from well-differentiated to undifferentiated cancerous cells. The tumor exhibits a complex pattern, including atypical glandular structures with hyperchromatic nuclei and poorly differentiated areas where cells form solid nests and cords. The carcinoma extensively invades the bowel wall, reaching into the pericolonic adipose tissue and is accompanied by a desmoplastic reaction. Importantly, sixteen examined regional lymph nodes are free from metastatic involvement. The resection margins, encompassing proximal, distal, and radial aspects, are clear of tumor, indicating successful surgical excision. Chronic inflammatory changes are observed on the serosal surfaces of the bowel and appendix. No lymphatic or venous invasion is detected. Overall, the findings indicate a TT3, NO, MX stage tumor with no involvement of lymph nodes or margins, and a significant presence of chronic serosal inflammation.

The pathological analysis reveals a diagnosis of moderately well-differentiated adenocarcinoma of the left lung, located in the lower lobe. The tumor exhibits focal bronchioloalveolar features and closely approaches but does not invade the pleura. Examination of the resected tissue confirms that all surgical margins are clear of tumor involvement. A total of sixteen lymph nodes were examined, with none showing evidence of metastatic carcinoma. Specifically, lymph nodes from levels 5, 7, and 9 were all negative for tumor cells. Thus, there is no evidence of lymph node metastasis in the sampled nodes, and the margins are free of tumor, highlighting a contained neoplastic process without regional dissemination.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC) with tubular and cribriform features in the left breast. The invasive carcinoma exhibits a Nottingham Grade of 1, characterized by a total score of 4/9, incorporating low scores for tubule formation, nuclear pleomorphism, and mitotic activity. A notable component of ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) is also identified, comprising 20% of the total tumor mass, and is classified as nuclear grade 2 with cribriform and micropapillary types. Both the invasive and in situ components are observed to lie close to the posterior margin, yet no carcinoma is present at the resection margins, ensuring the surgical margins are clear. No lymphovascular space invasion is detected, and sentinel lymph nodes from the left axilla are found to be free of metastatic carcinoma. Histological examination also notes atypical ductal hyperplasia, sclerosing adenosis, and fibrocystic changes. Furthermore, the invasive tumor cells are positive for both estrogen and progesterone receptors, while HER-2/neu status is negative by FISH. The pathological staging is recorded as pT1c, pN0, and pMx.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of malignant melanoma located on the trunk, with notable superficial ulceration and a Breslow's depth of 6 mm. The histologic analysis confirms the malignancy as melanoma, showing no evidence of venous invasion. Importantly, the surgical excision margins are clear, indicating uninvolved margins. However, information on lymph node involvement, lymphatic invasion, and histologic grade is not provided. These findings underscore the aggressive nature of the tumor and its potential for deep tissue infiltration, necessitating further clinical correlation and possible additional management steps to address any undetected metastatic spread.

The pathological findings reveal invasive well-differentiated squamous carcinoma following a resection of a fibulograft and left partial glossectomy. The primary tumor, which involves the tongue and the floor of the mouth, extends focally to the left anterolateral floor of mouth mucosal margin. The bone margins of the resection are free of the tumor, as are the other margins, ensuring no residual malignancy in those areas. Perineural invasion is noted, indicating a potential for the tumor to invade nerve spaces, although no clear evidence of lymphatic or vascular invasion is observed. The specimen includes multiple teeth and fragments, assessed grossly without additional pathologic findings. Further decalcification studies are pending to provide additional insights into the bone involvement.

The pathological findings pertain to a diagnosis of well-differentiated astrocytoma. The tumor is characterized by mildly hypercellular gray and white matter, with the hypercellularity more prominent in permanent sections compared to frozen sections. The tumor comprises glial cells with round to oval nuclei, displaying a small number of apoptotic figures but lacking perinuclear halos and other classic glioma features. Reactive astrocytes are present, but the infiltrating nature of the glioma is evident. No mitotic figures are observed. The presence of scattered MIB-1 reactive cells with a labeling index of 1.7% suggests a modestly proliferative neoplasm, consistent with low-grade histologic features. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not detailed in this pathology description. Overall, the findings are indicative of a moderately proliferative, well-differentiated astrocytoma.

The pathological examination of the left frontal brain tumor biopsy revealed the presence of an oligodendroglioma, WHO grade II/IV, with focal areas of anaplasia elevating part of the tumor to WHO grade III/IV. While the majority of the neoplastic cells demonstrated uniform round nuclei with perinuclear haloes and slight nuclear pleomorphism, a focal area exhibited increased cellular proliferation and necrosis, indicative of anaplastic transformation. Mitotic activity was low, and no significant microvascular proliferation or necrosis was noted except in the anaplastic regions. Immunohistochemical staining revealed sparse gliofibrillary background with GFAP, approximately 20% of cells overexpressing p53 protein, and a proliferation index of around 12% in active tumor areas. The meager glial fibrillary acidic protein expression and absence of MGMT promoter methylation were noted. The overall neoplasm is described as a grade II oligodendroglioma with a small zone of elevated anaplasia, corresponding to grade III features, which has implications for prognosis and therapeutic strategies.

The pathological analysis of the right breast lumpectomy revealed an invasive ductal carcinoma, which was moderately differentiated with a Nottingham score of 6/9, indicating a Grade 2 tumor. There was no evidence of tumor necrosis or microcalcifications, and both venous and lymphatic invasions were absent. The invasive carcinoma was found 1.2 cm from the anterior margin, while ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), cribiform type with low nuclear grade, was present 0.5 cm from the inferior margin. Estrogen receptors were positive, whereas progesterone receptors and HER2/neu were negative. The pathologic stage was designated as pT1c N2a Mx. Additionally, benign fibrocystic changes were noted. The corresponding right axillary node dissection identified metastatic carcinoma in five out of sixteen lymph nodes, confirming lymph node involvement. Skin involvement and multicentricity were absent. The gross examination described a well-circumscribed mass with proximity to surgical margins, predominantly surrounded by adipose tissue with benign characteristics. These findings collectively support the diagnosis and provide crucial information for prognosis and potential further treatment planning.

The pathological evaluation of the breast specimens reveals a bilateral diagnosis of carcinoma. In the right breast, poorly-differentiated adenocarcinoma was identified, although the medial margin biopsy contained benign breast tissue. The left breast contained an invasive and intraductal adenocarcinoma, classified as NSABP histologic grade 3 with nuclear grade 3. The tumor was composed of a non-comedo type intraductal component making up 5% of the tumor and showed evidence of vascular space invasion. Notably, the margins of resection for the left breast tumor were free of carcinoma, but the superior margin was within close proximity at merely 1 mm. Microcalcifications and other benign changes such as apocrine metaplasia and microcyst formation were also noted in the remaining breast tissue. For the right axillary dissection, the invasive and intraductal carcinoma extended near the resection margins and was closely aligned within 100 microns, though there was an absence of vascular space invasion. Both the right and left axillary dissections showed no lymph node involvement, with 10 and 8 nodes examined respectively, all devoid of tumor. However, an area of cautery artifact was present, which focally extended to the tumor.

The pathological examination diagnosed a poorly differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma, classified as Not Otherwise Specified (NOS), Grade III. The tumor exhibits focal intraductal components. The pathological staging indicated a primary tumor (pT2) with one positive lymph node (N1a), suggesting regional lymph node involvement. Tumor margins were evaluated, and findings indicate clear surgical margins, affirming the absence of residual tumor at the resection edges. Additional histopathological features include significant nuclear atypia and high mitotic activity, characteristics typical of higher-grade malignancies. No evidence of vascular or perineural invasion was identified. These findings collectively underscore an aggressive tumor biology, necessitating a comprehensive therapeutic approach.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of high-grade invasive urothelial carcinoma with squamous differentiation and extensive necrosis. The tumor involves the bladder, prostate, and left seminal vesicles, penetrating the detrusor muscle and extending into the perivesical fat. Margins are positive for invasive carcinoma at both the prostatic resection and the distal urethral resection sites. Perineural invasion and extensive angiolymphatic invasion are present. The primary tumor is staged at pT4a, with no regional lymph node involvement (pN0) among the 24 lymph nodes examined, and the metastasis status remains uncertain (pMx). In addition, there is evidence of benign and reactive changes in the ureteral margins, with low-grade urothelial dysplasia observed in the right ureteral tissue. These findings highlight the aggressive nature of the carcinoma and the extent of local invasion, emphasizing the need for comprehensive management and potential adjuvant therapies.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the oral cavity. The primary tumor exhibits a greatest dimension of 5.5 cm and a thickness of 2.5 cm, with evidence of perineural invasion but no lymphovascular invasion. Margins are generally negative for the tumor; however, the tumor is noted to be close to the deep tongue and posterior soft tissue margins. Lymph node involvement is significant, with a total of eight out of fifty-nine examined lymph nodes showing metastatic carcinoma, specifically six on the left neck (in levels I, II, and III) and two on the right neck (in levels II and III). Notably, extranodal invasion is present on the left side but absent on the right. The involved lymph nodes have a maximum size observed at 3.1 cm. The submandibular gland and surgical waste samples exhibit no pathologic changes. All additional margin samples (posterior, anterior, lateral mucosa, and deep muscle) are free of tumor. Pathologic staging reveals pT3 classification based on the tumor size and pN2c due to bilateral lymph node involvement without nodes exceeding 6 cm. There is no assessment of distant metastasis. These findings collectively provide a comprehensive overview of the tumor characteristics, margin status, and extent of lymph node involvement.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of primary lung adenocarcinoma. The tumor exhibits typical histological features of adenocarcinoma without mention of specific cellular or architectural patterns. The tumor grade is moderately differentiated, and the assessment of the tumor, node, and metastasis (TNM) staging classifies it as T2N2M0, indicating a tumor size that has invaded the lymph nodes but with no distant metastasis. Margin status indicates that the resection borders are free of tumor cells, suggesting a complete surgical removal of the tumor. Examination of the regional lymph nodes shows metastatic involvement, further supporting the classification of N2. The tissue specimen was appropriately identified and processed, including both OCT block preparation and blood samples for additional diagnostic and research purposes.
