The percentage of tumor cells present in the examined specimen is 74%, which may have implications for prognosis and treatment strategies. No abnormalities were observed in the blood drawn alongside the tissue sample.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma in the right lung, characterized by moderate infiltration and invasion into the bronchus. Notably, the pleural, bronchial, and vascular margins are free of tumor involvement. However, metastatic carcinoma is present in three out of nine examined hilar lymph nodes. Additional findings include pleural scarring. A separate analysis of lymph nodes from different regions (R4 and R9) reveals no evidence of tumor involvement in the two lymph nodes and the associated adipose tissue from R4, as well as in the single lymph node and associated adipose tissue from R9.

The histopathological examination of the multiple organ resection from the left breast confirms a diagnosis of mixed ductal and papillary invasive carcinoma. The tumor, located in the outer lower quadrant of the left breast, demonstrates significant estrogen and progesterone receptor positivity, with over 75% of neoplastic cell nuclei expressing these receptors. HER2 protein expression was equivocal, with a HercepTest score of 2+; however, subsequent FISH analysis did not reveal HER2 gene amplification. The carcinoma is classified as NHG1 with moderate differentiation and notable mitotic activity. The microscopic analysis also identified fibrocystic changes and usual ductal hyperplasia in the surrounding glandular tissue. Importantly, invasive tumor lesions extend to the base, but the axillary lymph nodes show chronic inflammation without evidence of metastatic cancer, indicating a negative margin status for carcinoma. Overall, the pathological findings detail a localized but aggressive neoplasm with favorable hormone receptor status and no detected lymph node involvement.

The pathological assessment revealed bilateral invasive ductal carcinoma. On the right breast, the wide local excision showed a 2.4 cm invasive ductal carcinoma, SBR grade 2, with ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) presenting central necrosis and microcalcifications. The invasive carcinoma was located within 1 mm of the superior and medial surgical resection margins, with lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS) also noted. An additional margin excision confirmed involvement of invasive ductal carcinoma at the superior margin. The right tumor was ER and PR positive, HER2 negative, with a pathological staging of pT2Nx. The left breast excision revealed a 1.8 cm invasive ductal carcinoma, SBR grade 2, with negative margins and LCIS. Similarly, this tumor was ER and PR positive, HER2 negative, and staged as pT1cNx. Both specimens exhibited fibrosis with biopsy site changes. No lymph node involvement was assessed as the axillae were clinically negative. The patient is anticipated to undergo radiation and endocrine therapy.

The patient was diagnosed with supraclavicular laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma and underwent a total laryngectomy with bilateral neck dissection. Pathological evaluation revealed an invasive, moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma located in the right supraglottis and pyriform sinus, extending into the thyroid cartilage. Perineural and angiolymphatic invasions were present. The surgical margins were free of tumor, confirming complete resection. Pathologic staging was determined as pT4A, N2A, MX. Metastasis was identified in one out of fifty-six lymph nodes, specifically in a right level 3 lymph node, without extracapsular spread. The thyroid parenchyma was unremarkable, and the specimen included a normocellular parathyroid gland. Additional findings noted chronic inflammation and fibrosis at the tracheostomy site, a saccular cyst of the left vocal cord, and a nodule on the right vocal cord. The bone marrow was hypercellular with trilineage hematopoiesis. This comprehensive pathology evaluation highlights the aggressive nature of the carcinoma and its metastatic potential, despite clear surgical margins.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of ulcerated malignant melanoma located on the scalp. The tumor is characterized as a nodular type with a significant depth of invasion that is partially obscured by ulceration, suggesting a severe and advanced stage (pT4b). Importantly, the surgical margins, both lateral and deep, are free of invasive melanoma, indicating that the excision was complete. In addition, fourteen regional lymph nodes were examined, and all were free of metastatic involvement, including sentinel nodes from the left occipital, left posterior cervical chain, left supraclavicular, and right posterior cervical regions. No satellite nodules were observed in the surrounding tissues. Immunostains for S-100 and HMB-45 on these lymph nodes confirmed the absence of metastatic melanoma. Despite the significant tumor thickness, the complete excision and absence of lymph node metastasis are notable aspects of this case.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma located in the cecum of the right colon, presenting as an ulcerative polypoid mass. The tumor invades into, but does not penetrate, the muscularis propria. No tumor involvement is detected at the proximal, distal, or radial resection margins. There is an absence of intramural lymphovascular and perineural invasion, with only a mild peritumoral inflammatory response noted. The specimen also shows a presence of multiple hyperplastic and adenomatous polyps and a lipoma at the ileocecal valve. Noteworthy is the identification of fat necrosis with dystrophic calcifications in the peritoneal mass. Importantly, all 13 regional lymph nodes examined are negative for tumor involvement, classified as pT2 N0 Mx based on the AJCC 6th Edition criteria. The presence of a residual adenoma and additional benign findings such as hyperplastic polyps and a lipoma are noted, and representative sections have been submitted for further studies.

The pathological evaluation of the right frontal tumor revealed an anaplastic oligodendroglioma, WHO grade III. Histologically, the tumor is characterized by a sheet-like proliferation of small, round, uniform cells with central nuclei and a perinuclear halo. Areas of increased cellular density exhibit larger nuclei with open chromatin and conspicuous nucleoli, and the presence of minigemistocytes is notable. The tumor diffusely infiltrates the cerebral parenchyma and aggregates beneath the pia mater, with delicate vasculature but no microvascular endothelial proliferation. Mitotic activity is significant, quantified at 9 per 10 high power fields, with a proliferation index estimated at 10% by MIB1 staining. Importantly, no definite necrosis is identified, and p53 staining is weak but varying in intensity among cells. GFAP highlights the minigemistocytes, and FISH studies indicate 1p and 19q co-deletion, while EGFR amplification is absent. The findings support the diagnosis of an anaplastic oligodendroglioma with favorable genetic features of 1p 19q co-deletion.

The pathological analysis reveals a diagnosis of invasive poorly differentiated urothelial carcinoma of the bladder, characterized by invasion through the muscular wall and involvement of the perivesical adipose tissue. The tumor exhibits glandular and squamous differentiation and is classified as high-grade according to the WHO/ISUP (1998) criteria. Notably, the surgical margins are free of invasive cancer, and there is no evidence of venous or lymphatic invasion. Examination of the left and right distal ureters shows no presence of neoplasm. The radical cystoprostatectomy specimen includes the bladder and prostate, with the latter showing glandular hyperplasia and chronic inflammation but no neoplasm in the seminal vesicles. Lymph node involvement was systematically assessed, with ten lymph nodes from both the right and left pelvic regions being negative for metastasis. The pathological staging indicates the primary tumor (pT3b) has macroscopic invasion into the perivesical fat, with no regional lymph node metastasis (pN0) and distant metastasis status undetermined (pMX).

The pathological examination revealed a diagnosis of invasive squamous cell carcinoma of the right tongue and floor of mouth, well to moderately differentiated. The tumor involved the deep muscles of the tongue, floor of the mouth, marrow space of the mandible, and extended to the subcutaneous tissue of the submental skin. Significant features included perineural invasion and angiolymphatic invasion. Importantly, all surgical margins were free of carcinoma. Lymph node analysis showed metastasis in two out of twenty-six lymph nodes from the right neck, specifically in level II nodes, with extracapsular spread noted. No tumor was present in the seventeen lymph nodes from the left neck or the three lymph nodes associated with the primary tumor resection. Additionally, both submandibular glands exhibited mild chronic sialadenitis. The pathological stage was determined as pT4a, pN2b, MX, indicating a locally advanced tumor with regional lymph node involvement but no distant metastasis assessed.

The specimen from a brain biopsy revealed an anaplastic gemistocytic astrocytoma, classified as WHO Grade III. Histological evaluation disclosed a glioma infiltrating brain tissue, characterized by cellular pleomorphism with prominent gemistocytes, along with perivascular lymphocytic inflammation. Scattered mitotic figures were observed without evidence of microvascular proliferation or necrosis. Immunohistochemistry confirmed the presence of tumor cells with GFAP positivity, a Ki-67 labeling index of 3%, and absence of macrophage infiltrate highlighted by HAM56 negativity. The tumor exhibited polysomy for chromosomes 7 and EGFR, loss of 9p21 and chromosome 9 centromere in substantial fractions of cells, while chromosomes 10 and PTEN remained intact. Immunohistochemical analysis demonstrated widespread expression of PDGFR-A and PDGFR-B, alongside positive staining for EGFR wt, S6, AKT, and MAPK. However, there was no detection of EGFR vIII, carbonic anhydrase IX, or VEGF. Additionally, MGMT promoter methylation was not detected, indicating potential implications for therapeutic response. The absence of vascular, necrotic alterations coupled with molecular markers aligns with the diagnosis of anaplastic gemistocytic astrocytoma, recurring within a complex genomic landscape.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of high-grade urothelial carcinoma of the bladder with extensive squamous differentiation, constituting over 50% of the tumor. The neoplasm shows invasion into the muscularis propria and is positive for lymphovascular invasion, indicating a more aggressive disease. The margins of resection were not mentioned, which leaves the status of completeness of excision unclear. In the prostatic urethra, no evidence of malignancy is observed; however, there is chronic inflammation and reactive changes. Additionally, benign prostatic glands and fibromuscular stroma are present. Lymph node involvement is not addressed in the findings, which may require further investigation for comprehensive staging of the disease. Overall, these findings suggest a high-grade bladder carcinoma with significant invasive and squamous features, alongside benign prostatic changes.

The pathological examination revealed the presence of a high-grade infiltrating urothelial carcinoma in the bladder, characterized by poorly differentiated transitional cell carcinoma. The tumor was necrotic, with cells demonstrating hyperplasia and arranged in sheets, nests, groups, or cords. Tumor cells displayed moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm and enlarged nuclei with variabilities in shape and size, irregular nuclear membranes, nucleoli, and abnormal chromatin patterns, alongside frequent mitoses. Microscopic analysis confirmed that the tumor invades the bladder wall and extends into the perivesical fat tissue. Notably, the surgical margins were uninvolved, suggesting clear margins. Additionally, the stroma showed lymphocytic invasion, but there was no evidence of neo-adjuvant treatment. Hence, the diagnosis is a high-grade infiltrating urothelial carcinoma of the bladder extending to the perivesical tissue.

The pathological examination of the brain tumor reveals a well-differentiated astrocytoma, consistent with WHO Grade II. The tumor exhibits a prominent microcystic component with readily observed microcalcifications, and the presence of round, regular nuclei, some with perinuclear haloes, and mini gemistocytes. There are also focal areas of increased cellularity with elongated, hyperchromatic nuclei indicative of astrocytic differentiation, although some features suggest oligodendroglioma. No unequivocal vascular proliferation, necrosis, or definitive mitotic figures are identified. Immunohistochemically, the Ki-67 proliferation index approaches 5% in focal areas. The tumor's margins and lymph node involvement are not described in the report; however, the presence of hypercellular areas and a mildly elevated MIB-1 labeling index suggests a potential for more aggressive behavior, warranting close clinical follow-up.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of an epithelioid malignant tumor, consistent with metastatic melanoma. Microscopically, the tumor occupies approximately 95 percent of the examined tissue, demonstrating a sheet-like growth pattern. The tumor cells exhibit round to oval nuclei with granular chromatin and a high nucleo/cytoplasmic ratio. The presence of apoptotic cells and a mitotic count of 8 mitoses per square millimeter indicate high proliferative activity. Additionally, the tumor cells feature medium-sized pigmented cytoplasm. Notably, there are lymphocytic infiltrates constituting about 3 percent of the tissue cells, as well as the presence of melanophages. The pathological findings corroborate the diagnosis of metastatic melanoma, though margin status and lymph node involvement are not specified in the available data.

The histopathological examination of the partial resection from the right breast's inner upper quadrant revealed an invasive ductal carcinoma (Carcinoma ductale invasivum, NHG3). The tumor is characterized by high nuclear grade and a high mitotic rate, indicative of aggressive behavior. DCIS (ductal carcinoma in situ) foci with solid type and high nuclear atypia, including comedo necrosis, were observed at the tumor edges, accounting for 5% of the overall tumor. The tumor demonstrates a positive expression of estrogen receptors in over 75% of the neoplastic cell nuclei, and progesterone receptors are present in 10-75% of the neoplastic cells, suggesting potential responsiveness to hormone therapy. HER2 protein staining was negative (Score = 1+). No lymph node involvement was identified (pNo, SN), and the resection margins are within acceptable distances to various anatomical landmarks. The overall pathological stage is pT2, indicating the tumor size and local extension without lymph node metastasis.

The pathology findings indicate a diagnosis of infiltrative ductal carcinoma of the breast in a female patient. The tumor is located at the primary breast site and is characterized as a high-grade (Grade 3) malignancy. The tumor is classified as T2 stage, indicating a significant, though not the largest, volume of tumor presence without direct invasion to adjacent structures. No lymph node involvement has been observed (N0), and there is no evidence of distant metastasis (M0). The margin status, which is a critical determinant for surgical excision, shows clear margins, suggesting that the tumor has been entirely removed with no residual detectable malignancy at the edges of the excised tissue. Relevant pathological findings confirm the primary nature of the tumor, and no treatment has been administered to the patient at this time. Additionally, a normal blood sample was obtained and stored frozen in a tube for potential comparative or future diagnostic purposes.

The pathological evaluation of the right colon specimen reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma. The tumor is located in the ileocecal valve/cecal region and is characterized by its invasive nature, penetrating the full thickness of the bowel wall and extending into the surrounding adipose tissue. The tumor mass is not in proximity to the radial margin. Assessment of both the proximal and distal resection margins indicates that they are clear of malignancy. A comprehensive examination of thirty-three lymph nodes shows no evidence of malignant involvement. Additionally, there is no identified pericolonic tumor beyond lymph nodes, and distant metastasis cannot be determined from the provided data. The appendix, included in the specimen, is grossly and microscopically unremarkable, showing no specific diagnostic abnormalities. Overall, the specimen's margins are free of malignancy, and there is no vascular invasion identified.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of well-differentiated Grade II astrocytoma, characterized by infiltration of both gray and white matter by tumor cells resembling protoplasmic and fibrillary astrocytes. The tumor exhibits low overall cellularity with mild atypia, although certain regions show moderate hypercellularity and highly atypical nuclei. A singular mitotic figure is identified, and there is an absence of microvascular proliferation or necrosis, which are indicative of a less aggressive neoplasm. Additionally, microcystic changes are noted focally. The calculated MIB-1 labeling index is 2.9%, reflecting a modest level of proliferative activity, supporting the non-anaplastic nature of the tumor. Margins and lymph node involvement are not addressed in this histopathological assessment, which centers on the intrinsic properties of the astrocytoma.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma, SBR grade 3, with focal squamous features and necrosis in the right breast. Additional findings include ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of solid type, nuclear grade 3, with necrosis and microcalcifications involving lobules. The invasive tumor is 2.7 cm in size and located at the 12:00 position of the breast, with all margins free of tumor. Sentinel lymph node biopsies from the right axilla (four nodes in total) showed no evidence of metastatic carcinoma. The skin and nipple are clear of tumor, and there is no vascular or lymphatic invasion identified. The additional right axillary tail excision revealed fibroadipose tissue without any tumor or lymph nodes. There is also a scalp lesion, diagnosed as a pilar cyst. The hormonal receptor status is positive for estrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR), and HER2 is positive by FISH. Pathological staging corresponds to pT2N0.

The pathological examination of the excised tissues from a patient with right lung cancer reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the non-mucinous bronchioloalveolar subtype. The tumor is located in the right upper lobe, characterized by an ill-defined, firm, stellate mass, invading into the visceral pleura. Importantly, there is evidence of vascular invasion; however, both the bronchial and vascular margins are uninvolved by the invasive carcinoma. The comprehensive lymph node evaluation, which includes a total of 84 regional lymph nodes from various levels, shows no evidence of metastasis (0/84). Consequently, the tumor staging is classified as pT2 N0 Mx.

The pathological evaluation of the left posterior temporal brain lesion and corresponding CUSA aspirant has led to a diagnosis of glioblastoma multiforme (WHO Grade IV), a highly aggressive and infiltrative primary brain tumor. The submitted specimens consisted of multiple irregular fragments of soft cerebriform tissue, ranging in coloration from light tan to red-brown. The tumor shows hallmark features consistent with glioblastoma, including cellular density, pleomorphism, and areas suggestive of necrosis and endothelial proliferation, characteristic of high-grade gliomas. Margins could not be fully assessed due to the fragmented nature of the specimens. No lymph node tissue was included in the submission for evaluation. Overall, the findings support a diagnosis of glioblastoma multiforme, a grade IV astrocytoma with an aggressive clinical course.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma located in the lung, specifically in the left upper lobe, following a lobectomy. The tumor exhibits moderate differentiation. Information on the extent of the tumor or presence of additional tumor nodules is not provided. Lymph node involvement, lymphatic invasion, and venous invasion have not been specified in the findings. Margin status and evidence of neo-adjuvant treatment are also not detailed in the available information. There are no additional pathological findings or comments noted.

The pathological evaluation reveals no tumor involvement in the excised lymph nodes from stations 5, R11, and L8, all of which consist of one lymph node and surrounding fibroadipose tissue. The lobectomy specimen from the left upper lobe of the lung has been assessed, though specific characteristics of the tumor within the lung tissue are not described in the provided diagnosis. Importantly, there is no evidence of metastatic spread to the evaluated lymph nodes, suggesting localized disease within the primary lung tissue. All margins and other relevant pathological features must be considered in the context of the complete pathology report to determine the overall prognosis and next steps for treatment.

The pathological findings reveal a poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma in the left lung, with the largest dimension being 6 cm. The tumor exhibits large vessel and pleural invasion and is categorized under AJCC staging as pTX N2 MX. The bronchial margin and chest wall margins are free of tumor, indicating no malignancy at these surgical margins. However, metastatic carcinoma is identified in multiple lymph nodes at Station 5 (three out of five nodes examined). Additionally, no malignancy is observed in preoperative biopsies from the left upper lobe, left secondary carina, and several mediastinal lymph nodes (including Stations 4L and 9). The non-tumorous lung tissue displays post-obstructive pneumonia. The intraoperative consultation confirms the absence of malignancy in the left upper lobe biopsy, secondary carina, and Station 4L mediastinal lymph node. Additional evaluation of bone and soft tissue from the left chest area indicates fibrosis and chronic inflammation without evidence of carcinoma, and bone fragments show no malignancy. The overall diagnosis points towards invasive primary lung cancer with significant implications for vascular and pleural involvement and metastatic spread to mediastinal lymph nodes.

The pathological examination revealed a diagnosis of poorly differentiated transitional cell carcinoma (TCC) of the bladder with both papillary and flat growth patterns, demonstrating invasion into the perivesical soft tissues but not extending beyond the bladder. Importantly, there was no evidence of vascular or perineural invasion, and all surgical margins were free of tumor. Additional findings included granulomatous cystitis in the non-neoplastic mucosa and prostatitis, with unremarkable seminal vesicles. Both ureters and vasa deferentia were benign. Extensive lymph node evaluation including right and left pelvic lymph nodes, common iliac lymph nodes, and right and left vasa deferentia biopsies, did not reveal any metastatic involvement, confirming the lymph nodes as benign. These comprehensive findings suggest that while the primary bladder tumor is invasive, it has not metastasized to the examined lymph nodes or adjacent structures, and the surgical resection achieved clear margins.

The pathological analysis reveals significant findings in both breasts, with the most critical diagnosis being invasive ductal carcinoma with lobular features in the right breast. This carcinoma measures 3.2 cm in maximum dimension and is classified as Nottingham grade 2/3, with a score reflecting moderate tubule formation and nuclear atypia, and low mitotic activity. The tumor exhibits extensive lymphovascular invasion, but the surgical margins are free, with the closest margin being within 0.5 cm. Despite a thorough examination, no definitive ductal carcinoma in situ is identified. In addition to the invasive carcinoma, other changes consistent with atypical ductal epithelial hyperplasia, fibrocystic alterations, and an intraductal papilloma are noted. Importantly, one sentinel lymph node displays metastatic involvement with extracapsular extension measuring 1.2 cm, emphasizing the extent of nodal metastasis. The left breast biopsy shows a variety of benign and atypical changes, including atypical ductal epithelial hyperplasia, complex papillary proliferative lesions, and sclerosing adenosis, without any signs of invasive carcinoma. Immunohistochemical analysis confirms positive estrogen and progesterone receptor status, and HER2/neu expression is low. These findings collectively stage the right breast cancer as pT2 pN1 pMX, indicating a primary tumor with regional lymph node involvement.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma in the right lung, characterized by moderate infiltration and invasion into the bronchus. Notably, the pleural, bronchial, and vascular margins are free of tumor involvement. However, metastatic carcinoma is present in three out of nine examined hilar lymph nodes. Additional findings include pleural scarring. A separate analysis of lymph nodes from different regions (R4 and R9) reveals no evidence of tumor involvement in the two lymph nodes and the associated adipose tissue from R4, as well as in the single lymph node and associated adipose tissue from R9.

The pathological examination of the resected left superior pulmonary lobe revealed a diagnosis of poorly differentiated, nonkeratinizing squamous cell carcinoma. The tumor exhibited significant invasion, extending into the mediastinum with infiltration of the lipomatous thymus tissue. Notably, the bronchial resection margins and the adjacent pericardial lobe were found to be free of tumor involvement. Microscopic evaluation confirmed the presence of tumor-free lymph nodes, although these exhibited anthracotic pigmentation and varying degrees of sinus histiocytosis and lymphofollicular hyperplasia. The peritumoral area showed marked xanthomatous retention pneumonia, likely attributable to nicotine abuse, and severe pulmonary emphysema with structural changes suggestive of sclerosis. Additional findings included fibrosis and fibroelastosis in the upper lobe, alongside alveolar septal inflammation in emphysematous regions. Importantly, the outer resection margins were clear of malignant cells, indicating complete resection of the carcinoma. The tumor was classified as M-8070/3, G3, with a pathologic stage of pT4, pN0, pMX, corresponding to stage IIIB, and a final margin status of R0.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of invasive well-differentiated squamous carcinoma following a right partial glossectomy. The tumor displays ulceration with an infiltrative border characterized by thick cords of more than four cells and a focal presence of perineural invasion, though vascular invasion is absent. All surgical margins, including the dorsal tongue, anterior tongue, ventral tongue, anterior and posterior floor of the mouth, posterior tongue, and deep tongue, are free of tumor involvement. Furthermore, a comprehensive neck dissection encompassing levels I through IV shows that all examined lymph nodes are negative for metastatic involvement, totaling 53 nodes. Additional findings include benign salivary gland tissue and evidence of multiple dental extractions. Importantly, in situ hybridization for high-risk human papillomavirus (HPV) genotypes has returned negative results, thereby excluding the presence of HPV-related oncogenic activity in this tumor.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of low-grade oligoastrocytoma, predominantly oligodendroglioma in nature. The tumor is characterized by a mildly hypercellular glial neoplasm with round to oval nuclei and scant glial processes, but it lacks classic oligodendroglioma differentiation. Scattered moderately to markedly atypical cells are present, however, there are no mitotic figures, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis observed. The immunohistochemical study shows a variable MIB-1 labeling index with the non-enhancing portion displaying minimal proliferation and the enhancing portion showing moderate proliferation. Examination of the surgical margins—posterior, deep, anterior, superior, and inferior—reveals gray matter devoid of hypercellularity or atypical cells, indicating no neoplastic infiltration. Additionally, involved sections of gray and white matter confirm the presence of the neoplasm without significant proliferative activity or signs of high-grade transformation, excluding the classification as Grade III/anaplastic. No lymph node involvement is discussed in the evaluation.

The pathological examination reveals an oligodendroglioma, Grade II (WHO) involving the posterior part of the right frontal brain, anterior portion of the right frontal brain, and the cingulate gyrus of the right frontal lobe. Each lesion exhibits characteristics of a low-grade glioma consistent with oligodendroglioma. Gross examination of the specimens identified infiltrating neoplastic growth in both white and gray matter, submitted in their entirety for histopathological evaluation. Importantly, fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) testing demonstrated a combined loss of chromosomal arms 1p and 19q, consistent with an oligodendroglial lineage. This genetic alteration is significant as it correlates with a potentially more favorable prognosis and responsiveness to adjuvant therapy. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not addressed in the provided sections. These findings are critical for informing the therapeutic approach and prognostic outlook for the patient.

The pathological examination of the left upper lung tissue following lobectomy revealed a diagnosis of bronchiolo-alveolar adenocarcinoma, characterized as moderately differentiated. The tumor was located within the lung tissue and was accompanied by clear and uninvolved surgical margins, indicating that it was entirely excised. The lymph nodes examined from the specimen were free of metastatic disease, suggesting no regional lymph node involvement. Overall, the findings indicate a localized tumor with moderated histological features and clear resection margins, without evidence of lymphatic spread.

The pathological findings are indicative of a diffuse astrocytoma, classified as World Health Organization (WHO) grade II. Histomorphologically, the tumor exhibits characteristics typical of diffuse astrocytoma, including increased nuclear pleomorphism. The proliferation index is relatively low, measured at 2%, which aligns with the grade II classification. Key tumor characteristics include the diffuse infiltration of surrounding brain tissue and the absence of significant mitotic activity. The assessment of surgical margins and lymph node involvement is not typically relevant in this context, given the primary site of origin being the central nervous system. Overall, the pathological evaluation confirms the diagnosis of a WHO grade II diffuse astrocytoma with noted nuclear atypia and a low proliferation rate.

A pathological examination of the terminal ileum, appendix, and ascending colon from a right hemicolectomy reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma in the cecum. The tumor, which originated from a pre-existing tubulovillous adenoma at the carcinoma site, shows invasion into the muscularis propria but does not extend to the serosa. Importantly, there is evidence of vascular invasion, though perineural invasion is absent. Surgical margins were found to be free of tumor, indicating clear resection boundaries. Additionally, no polyps were detected away from the carcinoma site, and the non-neoplastic bowel tissues were unremarkable. Lymph node analysis, involving twenty-eight nodes, showed no metastatic involvement with all nodes being benign (0/28). Based on the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) criteria, the pathological stage is classified as pT2, pN0, reflecting tumor invasion into the muscularis propria without nodal metastasis. The specimen’s gross examination corroborates the microscopic findings, supporting the comprehensive staging and diagnosis.

The pathological examination revealed a moderately differentiated keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma in the right upper lobe of the lung, measuring less than 5 cm in its greatest dimension, with tumor necrosis present. Importantly, there is no evidence of malignancy in any of the examined mediastinal lymph nodes, including levels 2R, 4, 7, 10R, and 11. Additionally, the pleura and all surgical margins are free of tumor infiltration. The tumor shows no lymphatic, venous, or arterial invasion. Non-neoplastic lung tissue demonstrates anthracosis and granulomatous inflammation with caseating granulomas; subsequent staining identified yeast forms consistent with Histoplasma species, while AFB stains were negative. The overall pathological stage is provisionally designated as pT2a/NO/MX, indicating a primary tumor greater than 3 cm but less than or equal to 5 cm, no regional lymph node metastasis, and distant metastasis undetermined.

The pathological examination reveals a right frontal lobe brain tumor diagnosed as an anaplastic astrocytoma (grade III). Microscopically, the tumor is markedly hypercellular, demonstrating diffuse infiltration by neoplastic cells resembling fibrillary astrocytes with moderate atypia. There are frequent mitoses, with five mitotic figures identified in the first ten high power fields, and scattered MIB-1 reactive cells, resulting in a labeling index of 9.1%. Immunohistochemistry shows diffuse positivity for P53, whereas IDH1 testing is negative. Notably, no microvascular proliferation or confluent tumor necrosis is observed; however, an area of infarct-like necrosis is present. Evidence of oligodendroglioma differentiation is also identified. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not applicable to this central nervous system tumor.

The pathology evaluation reveals a diagnosis of invasive moderately differentiated keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma of the tongue, primarily involving the ventral and dorsal surfaces. Upon examination, the tumor is extensive, ulcerated, and presents as a single focus. While the deep left lateral margin of the specimen is involved by the carcinoma, all other resection margins, including the anterior, posterior, right lateral, and superior surfaces of the tongue, as well as the floor of the mouth and surrounding structures, are free of invasive malignancy. No perineural or lymph-vascular invasion is identified. Comprehensive neck dissections, encompassing both right and left sides, evaluated a total of seventy-one lymph nodes, none of which were found to contain metastatic disease. Pathologic staging classifies the primary tumor as pT3 due to its size, and regional lymph nodes as pN0, indicating no regional lymph node metastasis. The integrity of the specimen is intact, and additional intraoperative consultations consistently reported no tumor presence at various examined sites, further supporting the absence of metastatic spread. The overall findings are significant for a localized squamous cell carcinoma with clean surgical margins except for the involved deep left lateral margin, requiring focused clinical management and potential additional therapeutic interventions.

The pathological evaluation revealed a diagnosis of acinar cell adenocarcinoma in the right upper lobe, characterized by poorly differentiated tumor cells. The surgical margins were assessed and found to be clear of tumor infiltration, indicating no residual malignancy at the resection edges. Lymph node examination showed metastasis in 3 out of 13 hilar lymph nodes, suggesting regional spread of the disease. No additional pathological findings were identified. This comprehensive assessment provides critical information for planning further treatment and determining prognosis.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of infiltrative urothelial cell carcinoma of the bladder, presenting as a papillary tumor with giant cells and sarcomatoid aspects, and graded as poorly differentiated (G3, high-grade). The tumor has invaded extensively, affecting the entire bladder wall, prostate, and seminal vesicles, classifying it as stage stpT4. Notable features include intramural tumor emboli, necrosis, and ulceration. Lymph node involvement is significant, with metastases detected in the right iliac intern, extern, and obturator nodes, as well as the right para-rectal and left iliac nodes (pN3). Venous and lymphatic invasion are also present, heightening concern for systemic spread. Adjacent structures such as the right ureter are free of malignancy, while the left kidney displays chronic non-specific nephritis and associated changes in the renal pelvis and ureter, suggestive of ongoing inflammatory processes. These combined findings underscore an advanced stage of the disease (stage IV, pMx).

The pathological evaluation reveals an invasive ductal carcinoma (NOS) located in the left breast, characterized by a high-grade (SBR Grade III) tumor with areas of necrosis. The tumor is predominantly situated in fatty breast tissue with focal areas of columnar cell change and post-biopsy site changes. Importantly, the margins of resection are negative for tumor, including the inferior, lateral, medial, and posterior margins. Additionally, a satellite nodule was identified in the inferior margin. The excised specimen included sentinel lymph nodes from the left axilla, all of which were negative for carcinoma. Pathological staging is pT2N0, indicating no lymph node metastasis (0/8). The tumor tested negative for estrogen and progesterone receptors (ER/PR) with an Allred score of 0, and HER-2 was also negative with an intensity of 1+ in 8% of tumor cells. There was no evidence of ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) or lobular neoplasia associated with this tumor. Vascular or lymphatic invasion was indeterminate. These findings are consistent with a high-grade hormone receptor-negative breast carcinoma without lymph node involvement.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma, Nottingham grade II, in the left breast, characterized by the presence of multiple tumor masses. The largest mass is located in the upper outer quadrant. Additionally, smaller nodules are situated inferior and just superior to the main mass. Notably, there is an approximate 10% component of ductal carcinoma in situ of the cribriform type, displaying an intermediate nuclear grade. The skin, nipple, and deep margin show no signs of neoplastic involvement. Examination of the left axillary lymph nodes reveals that six out of thirty nodes are positive for metastatic carcinoma, including two matted nodes. Further HER2/neu testing has been ordered on paraffin-embedded tissues to provide additional diagnostic information. This detailed assessment underscores the aggressive nature of the malignancy and aids in determining the appropriate clinical management.

The pathological findings indicate a poorly differentiated transitional cell carcinoma with glandular differentiation in the bladder, exhibiting nuclear grade 4 out of 4 and invasive characteristics, penetrating the full thickness of the muscularis propria into the surrounding fibrofatty tissue. Notably, all inked margins and the peritoneal surface are clear of the tumor, and there is no evidence of lymphovascular space invasion. There is also the presence of carcinoma in situ. The remaining bladder tissue shows marked chronic cystitis, cystitis glandularis, and a granulomatous reaction consistent with BCG therapy. The ureters and urethral margins are free of carcinoma in situ. The prostate demonstrates bilateral prostatic adenocarcinoma with a Gleason score of 6, with focal invasion into but not through the capsule, and all inked radial margins are free of tumor, yet again revealing no lymphovascular invasion. There is associated granulomatous prostatitis in the prostate consistent with BCG therapy. A thorough examination of multiple lymph node groups, including para-aortic, paracaval, common iliac, external iliac, hypogastric/obturator, presciatic, and presacral nodes shows no metastatic carcinoma in any of the nodes examined. Overall, the diagnosis highlights organ-confined prostate cancer (pT2cN0MX) and advanced bladder cancer without nodal involvement (pT3bN0MX).

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of an epithelioid malignant tumor, consistent with metastatic melanoma. Microscopically, the tumor occupies approximately 95 percent of the examined tissue, demonstrating a sheet-like growth pattern. The tumor cells exhibit round to oval nuclei with granular chromatin and a high nucleo/cytoplasmic ratio. The presence of apoptotic cells and a mitotic count of 8 mitoses per square millimeter indicate high proliferative activity. Additionally, the tumor cells feature medium-sized pigmented cytoplasm. Notably, there are lymphocytic infiltrates constituting about 3 percent of the tissue cells, as well as the presence of melanophages. The pathological findings corroborate the diagnosis of metastatic melanoma, though margin status and lymph node involvement are not specified in the available data.

The right frontal tumor is diagnosed as a WHO Grade III mixed oligoastrocytoma, exhibiting features consistent with an anaplastic oligodendroglioma. Microscopically, the tumor demonstrates variable cytoarchitectural patterns, including sparsely cellular microcystic areas and hypercellular foci with neoplastic cells showing pleomorphic and hyperchromatic nuclei. These findings are accompanied by a proliferation index that is elevated in hypercellular areas, confirmed by MIB 1 immunohistochemical staining. Notably, no regions of vascular endothelial proliferation or palisading necrosis are identified, although mitotic activity exceeds five mitoses per ten high-power fields in the most cellular areas. Immunohistochemistry reveals synaptophysin positivity in several neoplastic cells, while p53 and NeuN markers are negative. Neurofilament highlights a non-neoplastic cellular population and background neuropil. FISH analysis indicates no deletions of chromosomes 1p and 19q, and EGFR amplification is absent. The tumor lacks overt features of anaplasia but meets the criteria for WHO Grade III classification due to significant mitotic activity and cellular atypia. Margins and lymph node involvement are not applicable in this central nervous system neoplasm. These pathological findings confirm the diagnosis of a high-grade glioma with oligodendroglial characteristics.

The essential pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of high-grade nonpapillary urothelial carcinoma of the bladder with significant transmural invasion extending into the perivesical adipose tissue. Additionally, there is focal urothelial carcinoma in situ in a background of diffuse low-grade intraurothelial neoplasia. Importantly, both the serosal and resection margins are free of tumor involvement, suggesting complete excision in these areas. Examination of the prostate shows microcystic adenomatous change but no evidence of malignancy. No tumor is detected in the segments of the left and right vas deferens or ureters. Furthermore, extensive lymph node analysis revealed no tumor involvement in the right common iliac, right external iliac, right obturator, left common iliac, left external iliac, and left obturator lymph nodes, indicating no lymphatic spread of the malignancy. These findings provide a comprehensive evaluation of the pathological status of the patient, indicating a localized, though invasive, urothelial carcinoma without regional lymph node metastasis.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of high-grade invasive urothelial carcinoma involving the bladder and prostate, with no evidence of involvement in the right and left seminal vesicles. The tumor infiltrates to the level of the muscularis propria, indicating a significant depth of invasion. Additional examinations confirmed these findings. Importantly, there was no evidence of sanguineous vascular, lymphatic vascular, or perineural invasion. Surgical margins, including the bladder wall margin and right ureteral margin, are free of neoplastic involvement, although the right and left ureteral margins were noted to be compromised by neoplasia. Extensive lymph node dissections on both the right (0/8) and left (0/14) sides, as well as an examined individual lymph node, show no metastatic involvement, indicating no lymph node involvement by carcinoma.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive moderately differentiated keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma originating from the tongue. The tumor extends deeply into the skeletal muscle of the left hemiglossectomy specimen and reaches far anteriorly, very close to the anterior tip. The carcinoma displays evidence of angiolymphatic and perineural invasion, but no bone invasion or carcinoma in situ is identified. All surgical margins are deemed negative for definite involvement by carcinoma, though the anterior tip margin is close but negative. Pathologic examination of lymph nodes reveals metastatic squamous cell carcinoma in 3 of 48 nodes, with extracapsular extension identified in 2 lymph nodes. The largest metastasis measures 1.0 cm. Other significant findings include the presence of benign salivary gland tissue in one of the lymph nodes. The tumor's pathological stage is determined as pT4a pN2b according to the AJCC staging system. Overall, the findings confirm the presence of a deeply invasive squamous cell carcinoma with significant metastatic spread to regional lymph nodes.

The pathological examination of the left breast following skin-sparing prophylactic total mastectomy revealed fibrocystic changes, columnar cell changes, sclerosing adenosis, pseudoangiomatous stromal hyperplasia (PASH), and ductal epithelial hyperplasia, along with the presence of nipple and skin tissue. The right breast, subjected to skin-sparing total mastectomy, exhibited a more complex pathology with the presence of invasive lobular carcinoma, classical type, in two distinct foci, classified as Nottingham Grade 2/3 based on a combined score accounting for tubule formation, nuclear atypia, and mitotic activity. Lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS) with comedo necrosis was also noted. Importantly, the carcinoma did not show lymphovascular invasion, and surgical margins were free of invasive carcinoma, although it was within less than 1 mm from the anterior margin. Additional findings in the right breast included fibrocystic changes, columnar cell changes and hyperplasia, fibroadenomatoid changes, PASH, a microscopic radial scar, and biopsy site changes. One sentinel lymph node from the right axilla was negative for metastatic carcinoma, as confirmed by cytokeratin immunostain. Skin excisions from both breasts showed benign breast parenchyma, skin, and subcutaneous tissue. The tumor expressed positive estrogen and progesterone receptors, while HER2/neu was not amplified. Overall, the diagnosis highlighted multifocal classical invasive lobular carcinoma in the right breast with a multifocal aggregate size of the invasive component defined, accompanied by LCIS and various benign alterations.

The pathological examination of the right temporal lobe revealed no evidence of tumor presence. In contrast, the excised specimens from the right temporal tumor identified in sections #1 and #2 were diagnosed as grade 3 (of 4) oligoastrocytoma, corresponding to WHO grade III. The tumor showed a deletion of the 19q chromosomal region, as indicated by a 19q to 19p probe ratio of 0.56 and relative loss of 19q in approximately 90% of cells with two copies of 19q and three to four copies of 19p. Additionally, 80% of cells demonstrated three to four copies of the 1p and 1q probes, which is consistent with aneuploidy commonly seen in gliomas. The margins of the excised tissue are not discussed in detail, and there is no mention of lymph node involvement, which is typically not applicable in primary brain tumors. The findings from the frozen section consultations were concordant with the final pathology, confirming the presence of a diffuse glioma, subsequently typed and graded as oligoastrocytoma.

Based on the pathological findings, the patient was diagnosed with an anaplastic oligodendroglioma, WHO grade III, located in the left temporal lobe. Histological analysis revealed an extensively infiltrating tumor involving the adjacent cortex, with numerous mitotic figures identified. Additionally, scattered foci of microvascular proliferation were present, which is consistent with the anaplastic nature of the tumor. Immunohistochemical studies showed that the tumor cells were strongly positive for IDH1 (R132H mutant form) and negative for p53. The MIB-1 proliferative index was determined to be approximately 5%. Further, fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) for 1p19q will be performed to aid in the diagnostic evaluation. The analysis included sections from three separate specimens labeled as 'tumor,' 'permanent = brain tumor,' and 'CUSA trap - permanent,' all of which consisted of soft, irregular tissue fragments. No information about margin status or lymph node involvement was provided in the findings.

The pathological evaluation of the lobectomy specimen from the right lower lobe of the lung reveals an invasive and in situ squamous cell carcinoma. The carcinoma is unifocal and moderately differentiated, without visceral pleura invasion. The parenchymal margin is free of invasive carcinoma, but the bronchial and vascular margins could not be assessed. There is no lymph-vascular invasion identified. Thirteen regional lymph nodes were examined across multiple levels (levels 7, 10, R4, R2), and all were negative for malignancy, indicating no regional lymph node metastasis (pN0). Additionally, the wedge resection of the right middle lobe shows emphysematous changes and alveolar hemorrhage, without evidence of malignancy. The immunohistochemical profile of the tumor shows negative TTF-1 and Napsin A, with positive p63, confirming the diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma. Thus, the final diagnosis is pT1a squamous cell carcinoma of the right lower lobe with no lymph node involvement and no evidence of visceral pleura or lymph-vascular invasion.

The pathological findings confirm a diagnosis of infiltrating lobular carcinoma, pleomorphic type, poorly differentiated, originating from the left breast, specifically between the 1-2 o’clock position. The tumor exhibits poor tubule formation, intermediate nuclear grade, and high mitotic activity, without an in situ component or definite lymphovascular invasion. The tumor size is 2.0 cm and infiltrates as sheets, single file, alveolar nests, and occasional larger nests with focal early necrosis alongside a desmoplastic stromal response. Resection margins are compromised at the posterior, lateral, and superior segments of the initial specimen, while six additional margin biopsies are benign. Immunocytochemistry reveals the tumor cells are negative for HER-2/neu and weakly positive for p53. Ki-67, a proliferation marker, is high at 40-50%. Estrogen and progesterone receptor tests are positive. The axillary sentinel lymph node and one other lymph node biopsy are negative for metastasis, confirming no lymph node involvement (0/2). Other findings include atypical lobular hyperplasia, radial scar, and fibrocystic changes, with no evidence of malignancy in additional segments and margin biopsies. The pathological stage is classified as pT1c NO (sn) (i-) MX. Molecular studies on block 3A indicate an aneuploid DNA index, with no reported S-phase fraction due to multiple aneuploid peaks. Overall, the tumor displays unfavorable prognostic markers, including high proliferative activity and compromised resection margins, requiring further clinical management.

The final pathological diagnosis indicates a well-differentiated infiltrating squamous cell carcinoma involving the left hemimandible, lip, facial skin, and left submental triangle, with extension into the left buccal mucosa, the mandible, and erosion through the skin. The tumor appears as a single focus with a greatest dimension of 6.6 cm. All surgical margins are free of tumor, with the closest margin being the inferior margin, which is 2 mm away from the tumor. No overt lymphovascular or perineural invasion is observed. However, there is significant lymph node involvement: metastatic squamous cell carcinoma is identified in 2 out of 5 right level 2 lymph nodes, 2 out of 11 right level 3 lymph nodes, and 3 out of 4 right level 1a lymph nodes. Additionally, right level 4 and right level 1b lymph nodes are negative for metastasis. Special immunoperoxidase stains for p16, a surrogate marker for HPV, are negative. The overall pathological stage according to AJCC is T4a, N2b.

The pathological examination of the right lower lobe lobectomy specimen reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated (G3) squamous cell carcinoma. The tumor is unifocal, measuring 3.1 cm, and is located subpleurally without invasion of the visceral pleura or adjacent structures. There is no evidence of tumor-associated atelectasis or obstructive pneumonitis. Furthermore, lymphovascular space invasion is absent. The surgical margins are free of carcinoma, with the nearest margin being the bronchial margin, situated 3 cm away from the tumor. Examination of 11 sampled peribronchial lymph nodes shows no evidence of malignancy, confirming benign lymph node status. The elastic stain utilized further supports the absence of visceral pleural invasion. According to the AJCC staging, the tumor is categorized as pT2a with no regional lymph node metastasis (pN0). No additional significant findings were noted in the examined lung parenchyma. The gross pathological examination shows a granular tan-gray mass with a cavitated center containing purulent material, and the pleural surface of the lung bears moderate anthracotic stippling.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma in the right upper lobe of the lung. The tumor is moderately differentiated (Grade II) and exhibits no evidence of pleural invasion or lymphovascular space invasion. Notably, all surgical margins are free of tumor, ensuring complete resection, with the tumor being 2 mm away from the pleural surface. The biopsy of one lymph node at level 10 and one lymph node at level 7 both show no evidence of neoplasm, indicating no lymph node involvement. The lobectomy specimen reveals emphysematous changes and hemorrhage from the prior excision but no residual neoplasm, further confirming the successful removal of the tumor. These findings collectively support a staging of pT1, N0, M0.

The pathological analysis reveals a diagnosis of anaplastic astrocytoma (WHO Grade 3), characterized by a markedly hypercellular astrocytic neoplasm infiltrating both gray and white matter. The tumor exhibits moderate to marked nuclear atypia, with mitotic figures ranging from rare to numerous across specimens. Enhanced portions of the tumor demonstrate the greatest degree of atypia and mitotic activity. Notably, no microvascular proliferation or tumor necrosis was identified. The assessment did not mention the status of surgical margins or lymph node involvement, suggesting that further examination may be required to complete the evaluation. Overall, the pathological findings indicate a high-grade glioma with aggressive features, necessitating comprehensive clinical correlation and therapeutic strategy.

The pathological findings pertain to a diagnosis of well-differentiated astrocytoma. The tumor is characterized by mildly hypercellular gray and white matter, with the hypercellularity more prominent in permanent sections compared to frozen sections. The tumor comprises glial cells with round to oval nuclei, displaying a small number of apoptotic figures but lacking perinuclear halos and other classic glioma features. Reactive astrocytes are present, but the infiltrating nature of the glioma is evident. No mitotic figures are observed. The presence of scattered MIB-1 reactive cells with a labeling index of 1.7% suggests a modestly proliferative neoplasm, consistent with low-grade histologic features. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not detailed in this pathology description. Overall, the findings are indicative of a moderately proliferative, well-differentiated astrocytoma.

The pathological findings reveal an excision of a skin lesion from the hip, diagnosed as malignant melanoma. The tumor exhibits significant pigmentation and superficial ulceration. Histologically, the tumor is classified as a malignant melanoma with a Breslow thickness of 1 cm, indicating a considerable depth of invasion. The lesion is ulcerated and extends more than 4 mm. No evidence of venous invasion is observed. Importantly, the surgical margins are free of tumor, suggesting that the excision likely achieved complete removal of the malignant tissue. There is no mention of lymph node involvement, which might have further implications for prognosis and treatment. Overall, the report underscores the aggressive characteristics of the melanoma, necessitating close follow-up and possibly additional therapy.

The pathological examination reveals a right hemicolectomy specimen that includes a diagnosis of colorectal double carcinoma, characterized as an ulcerated, moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma located in the ascending and transverse colon. Both carcinomas exhibit invasion into the lamina muscularis propria with evidence of lymphatic invasion. Importantly, the resection margins are free of tumor involvement. Additionally, there is the presence of three metastatic local lymph nodes among the twenty-one examined, corresponding to a pN1 stage. The pathological assessment also identifies several tubular colon adenomas with up to moderate dysplasia along with recent mucosal ulcerations, and a history of prior adenomas excised from the colon mucosa. The overall findings specify a G2 grade, pT2 stage with lymphatic involvement (L1) but no vascular invasion (V0) and clear resection margins (R0).

The pathological evaluation reveals an invasive, poorly differentiated rectal carcinoma with features consistent with a mucinous carcinoma, classified as Grade 3 (G3). The tumor has penetrated all layers of the rectal wall, corresponding to a pathological stage of pT3. Evidence of lymphatic vessel invasion (L1) is present, although all resection margins and the ligature area are free of tumor involvement. There is no lymph node metastasis, as all 12 examined lymph nodes are free of carcinoma (pN0). Additionally, the secondary specimen consists of fatty connective tissue containing lymphoid elements, which are also free of malignancy. Examination of the distal anastomosis ring reveals cryptal mucosa with focal moderate chronic inflammation and prominent vascular complexes. The final tumor classification is ICD-O-DA M-8140/3, with pathological staging parameters G3, pT3, L1, pN0.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of an epithelioid malignant tumor, consistent with metastatic melanoma. Microscopically, the tumor occupies approximately 95 percent of the examined tissue, demonstrating a sheet-like growth pattern. The tumor cells exhibit round to oval nuclei with granular chromatin and a high nucleo/cytoplasmic ratio. The presence of apoptotic cells and a mitotic count of 8 mitoses per square millimeter indicate high proliferative activity. Additionally, the tumor cells feature medium-sized pigmented cytoplasm. Notably, there are lymphocytic infiltrates constituting about 3 percent of the tissue cells, as well as the presence of melanophages. The pathological findings corroborate the diagnosis of metastatic melanoma, though margin status and lymph node involvement are not specified in the available data.

The pathological analysis reveals an anaplastic oligoastrocytoma, grade III (WHO classification), originating from the temporal lobe. The tumor exhibits a heterogeneous composition with both hypercellular and moderately cellular areas characterized by a predominantly myxoid stroma and frequent microcystic degeneration. Cellular morphology includes rounded cells with delicate cytoplasmic processes and clear perinuclear halos, displaying moderately atypical, hyperchromatic, and pleomorphic nuclei. Rare gangliocytic phenotype cells and occasional gemistocytes are also observed. The mitotic activity is notable with 7 mitoses per high-power field, including occasional atypical forms, and numerous apoptotic figures. Microvascular proliferation pockets are present, however, no areas of necrosis are identified. Immunohistochemistry results show positive staining for GFAP and Vimentin, with negative staining for NEU-C and Synaptophysin, and an elevated KI-67 index, supporting the malignant and proliferative nature of the tumor. The margin status and lymph node involvement are not specified in the provided material, focusing instead on the intrinsic characteristics of the neoplastic tissue.

The final diagnosis reveals a histologic type of adenocarcinoma with minor areas of squamous and bronchioloalveolar differentiation, graded as GII-III. The tumor extends to the pT2 stage, signifying invasion into the surrounding lung tissue but not beyond the visceral pleura. Importantly, the vascular and bronchial margins are clear of tumor, indicating successful resection of the primary mass. However, blood and lymphatic vessel invasion are present, suggesting aggressive behavior of the carcinoma. Metastatic carcinoma is identified in three of three hilar lymph nodes, indicating regional spread of the disease. Although distant metastasis could not be evaluated in this specimen, additional pathological findings include patchy chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) in the surrounding lung tissue. Overall, the pathological findings depict a moderately to poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma with significant regional lymph node involvement and vascular invasion, consistent with an aggressive tumor profile.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of carcinoma of the upper rectum, specifically an infiltrating adenocarcinoma that is moderately to poorly differentiated (pT3). The tumor extends through the bowel wall into the pericolonic fat, but both the proximal and distal margins are negative for tumor involvement. The tumor reaches within 2.7 cm of the distal margin and is also negative at the circumferential (radial) margin. Vascular invasion is not detected. Metastatic disease is present in 9 out of 18 regional lymph nodes (pN2), and there are two sites of non-lymph node pericolonic tumor. An adenomatous polyp is also noted near the proximal margin. The umbilical hernia sac contains adipose and fibromembranous tissue consistent with its origin from the hernia sac, with no tumor present. The appendix shows no significant pathological findings.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the lung. The tumor is characterized by a large parahilar mass with central necrosis and ulceration, demonstrating keratinization and an associated fibrous-inflammatory stroma. Microscopically, the neoplasm exhibits pleomorphic cells with keratinization and parakeratosis. The tumor extends towards the periphery, leading to retraction of the visceral pleura; however, no tumor infiltration is observed within the fibrocollagenous pleura itself. The adjacent pulmonary parenchyma shows infiltration by the squamous cell carcinoma. Importantly, the resection margins are free of tumor, indicating no residual malignancy at the resection borders. Examination of the lymph node Station XI shows no evidence of metastasis, as the single lymph node evaluated is negative for tumor involvement. Overall, these findings denote a localized, well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with clear resection margins and no lymph node metastasis.

The pathological examination of the breast tissue reveals a diagnosis of infiltrating ductal carcinoma. The tumor is located in the breast and is grossly evident. Histologically, it demonstrates characteristics consistent with infiltrating ductal carcinoma. The assessment of the surgical margins indicates whether or not the tumor has been completely excised, with no residual cancerous tissue at the edges of the resected specimen. Additionally, lymph node involvement is notable, with metastases detected in three out of five axillary lymph nodes examined. These findings are crucial for determining the prognosis and potential treatment strategies for the patient. There are no other significant pathological abnormalities noted in the specimen beyond the primary tumor and lymph node involvement.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of mixed anaplastic oligodendroglioma-astrocytoma located in the left temporal region of the brain. The tumor is characterized by a proliferation of malignant glial cells exhibiting round to oval hyperchromatic nuclei with a speckled chromatin pattern and punctate chromocenters, as well as elongated spindle cells with hyperchromatic spindle-shaped nuclei. Mitotic activity is observed in both cellular components of the tumor. Additional features include vascular proliferation and focal calcification in areas of tumor degeneration. Notably, there is no evidence of necrosis within the examined tissue. While the predominant tumor component aligns with characteristics of a grade III oligodendroglioma in certain grading systems, margin status and lymph node involvement were not specifically addressed in this examination. The absence of necrosis and the presence of both oligodendroglial and astrocytic elements are significant findings in the overall pathological assessment.

The pathological examination of the left temporal brain lesion reveals a diagnosis of diffuse astrocytoma. The tumor exhibits a WHO grade characteristic for diffuse gliomas, indicative of their infiltrative nature. Immunohistochemical analysis shows positive expression for IDH1 R132H mutation in the tumor cells, a common alteration in low-grade gliomas, and a proliferative index (MIB-1/Ki-67) ranging between 2-3%, with focal areas increasing to 5%, suggesting a relatively low proliferative activity. Margins and lymph node involvement are not explicitly assessed in the provided data; however, the lack of frank anaplasia suggests relatively lower grade pathology. 1p/19q co-deletion status is pending, which may refine the diagnosis and provide further prognostic information. Overall, the histopathological and molecular findings are consistent with a diffuse astrocytoma, supporting the clinical presentation of a large, heterogeneous lesion in the left hemisphere observed on MRI.

The pathological analysis of the patient's left frontal lesion reveals a high-grade glioma, most consistent with an anaplastic oligodendroglioma (WHO Grade III), identified in both biopsy and resection specimens. Histologically, the tumor is recognized as an anaplastic oligodendroglioma, characterized by high cellularity, nuclear atypia, and a high mitotic index. The tissue samples exhibit a pink-tan, focally hemorrhagic appearance with no discrete nodules observed upon gross inspection. The microscopic examination of both frozen and permanent paraffin sections confirms the high-grade nature of the glioma. Margins of the resected specimen cannot be assessed. Importantly, FISH analysis indicates deletions in both 1p and 19q chromosomal regions, which is associated with a favorable prognosis for this type of brain tumor. The evaluation of 1p/19q status corroborates the initial diagnosis and provides additional prognostic insight, emphasizing the potential for a more favorable clinical outcome. The need for a neuropathology consultation is noted for further evaluation and confirmation of these findings.

The histopathological examination of the right breast and axillary tissue specimen from a female patient revealed an invasive mucinous carcinoma. The tumor, located at the boundary of the upper quadrants of the breast, was noted to be invasive with a high-grade histologic classification (NHG2), presenting moderate differentiation. Importantly, the invasive lesions were found to be very close to the margins, particularly 0.1 cm from the base, indicating a concern for marginal clearance. The nipple was free of pathological lesions, and glandular tissue showed parenchyma atrophy. Metastatic involvement was evident in one of thirteen examined axillary lymph nodes, with the metastasis extending beyond the nodular capsule, further classifying this case as pT3, pN1a. These findings are critical for staging and management, highlighting the necessity for potential additional treatment to address the close surgical margins and lymph node involvement.

The pathological examination reveals an anaplastic astrocytoma, grade 3. The tumor is characterized by mildly to moderately hypercellular glial cells displaying moderate to marked atypia, including fibrillar and gemistocytic astrocytes. Prominent multifocal perivascular lymphocytic inflammation is present, but there is no evidence of oligodendroglioma or ganglion cell differentiation. Additionally, no microvascular proliferation or necrosis is identified, and mitotic figures are absent in the observed sections. Despite the lack of mitotic activity, the cellular atypia justifies the high-grade classification. Immunohistochemical staining for MIB-1 reveals a labeling index of 16.3%, which supports the diagnosis of a high-grade neoplasm. Margin status and lymph node involvement have not been specified in the provided findings. The overall pathological features are indicative of an aggressive astrocytic tumor with significant proliferative activity.

The pathological analysis reveals a diagnosis of low-grade oligodendroglioma (WHO Grade II). The tumor exhibits a variably cellular glial neoplasm that diffuses through the gray and white matter, with regions of mild hypercellularity interspersed with areas of confluent hypercellularity. The tumor cells display mildly atypical, enlarged, round nuclei with either perinuclear halos or microgemistocytic cytoplasm. Mitotic activity is extremely low, with only a single mitosis observed upon extensive examination. Importantly, no evidence of microvascular proliferation or necrosis is present. Immunohistochemical staining for MIB-1 shows scattered reactive cells in the most proliferative areas, with a calculated labeling index of 0.6%. Margin involvement and lymph node status are not applicable in this context given the nature of brain tumors, focusing our assessment on the intrinsic characteristics of the neoplasm.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of infiltrating lobular carcinoma of the right breast. Microscopic examination of the mastectomy specimen revealed an invasive carcinoma with a histologic grade of 2 based on the Elston SBR grading system. The tumor was ER-positive and PR-positive, showing moderate to strong staining, while HER-2/neu status was equivocal, necessitating further analysis via FISH. Sentinel lymph node biopsy of the right axilla showed metastatic lobular carcinoma in 1 of 3 lymph nodes, with the metastatic tumor measuring 8 mm at its greatest dimension and no evidence of extranodal extension, categorizing it as pN1a. The tumor in the breast tissue had a greatest dimension of 2.2 cm (pT2), and the resection margins were clear of invasive carcinoma with a distance much greater than 1 cm from the tumor. Notably, no vascular invasion, skin involvement, or ductal carcinoma in situ was present. However, lobular carcinoma in situ was prominent around the invasive tumor, accompanied by proliferative fibrocystic changes. Additionally, no evidence of Paget’s disease was identified at the nipple, and distant metastasis could not be evaluated (pMx). Prognostic marker studies confirmed the hormone receptor status and necessitated further examination for HER-2/neu amplification.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated non-small cell carcinoma, specifically consistent with poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma, located in the right lower lobe of the lung. The resected tumor exhibits a poorly differentiated histologic grade and is classified as pT1b. Importantly, the surgical margins are negative for malignancy, indicating that the tumor has not extended to the resection edges. Additionally, there is no evidence of direct tumor extension, pleural invasion, or vascular invasion. The hilar and peribronchial lymph nodes, as well as the lymph nodes from level 7 and level 11, are negative for tumor involvement, classifying the regional lymph nodes as pN0. No distant metastasis is applicable in this context. Other notable findings include scattered chronic inflammation and interstitial thickening in the lung tissue away from the tumor. No fungal elements are observed in the examined sections. The collective pathological findings suggest a localized primary lung carcinoma with no evidence of lymphatic spread or distant metastasis.

The pathological examination reveals crucial findings. Level 10, 11, and 4 lymph nodes exhibit benign hyperplasia with no signs of malignancy. A right upper lobe lobectomy specimen confirms the diagnosis of adenocarcinoma with mucinous and bronchoalveolar features, characterized as low-grade. The tumor is positioned peripherally, exhibiting unifocality and remaining confined within the lung tissue with no visceral pleural or lymphovascular space invasion noted. Additionally, there is no evidence of treatment effect. Importantly, the surgical margins are clear, with satisfactory distances from the bronchial, parenchymal, and pleural margins, although the tumor lies close to the pleural surface. The examination of received lymph nodes shows no metastatic carcinoma. The tumor's staging is designated as pT1, NO, confirming the absence of nodal metastasis. These findings collectively indicate a primary lung adenocarcinoma confined to the lung without regional lymph node involvement. The frozen section diagnosis of adenocarcinoma is corroborated by CK7 and TTF-positive staining and negative CK20 and CDX2, supporting the final diagnosis.

The pathological examination of the right hemicolectomy specimen revealed an ulcerated colon carcinoma classified as a moderately differentiated colorectal adenocarcinoma. This tumor is located close to Bauhin's valve and has invaded the outer layers of the muscularis propria. Histological analysis confirms that both oral and aboral resection margins, along with the large omentum, are free of tumor involvement. Additionally, twelve mesocolic and mesenteric lymph nodes were assessed, all of which were tumor-free but showed reactive changes. The tumor staging is pT2 pN0 (1/12), L0, V0, G2, indicating no lymphovascular or venous invasion and a moderately differentiated grade.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of low-grade/grade II oligoastrocytoma, predominantly featuring oligodendroglioma characteristics. Microscopically, the tumor presents as a moderately hypercellular glial neoplasm that diffusely infiltrates both the gray and white matter. The tumor cells consist mainly of small, round nuclei with smooth contours, many of which display well-formed perinuclear halos, though a significant minority of the cells do not have such halos. Additionally, a small percentage of the nuclei exhibit elongate shapes with fibrillary processes. Importantly, there is no observed atypia, mitotic activity, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis, reinforcing the low-grade nature of the tumor. The MIB-1 labeling index is 3.1%, further supporting the classification as a low-grade neoplasm. The margins and lymph node involvement are not specifically discussed in the findings provided.

The pathology findings reveal a diagnosis of poorly differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma with focal intraductal components and angioinvasion. The ablated left breast sample shows extensive invasive and angioinvasive tumor components, along with foci of intraductal tumor components in the cavity region of the excision. There is also a significant presence of subepidermal carcinomatous lymphangitis in the skin spindle, which is indicative but not definitive of an inflammatory carcinoma, given the lack of ulceration. The dorsal resection margin is free of the tumor. The final tumor classification is NOS, G III, pT3N3aL1V1R0, suggesting an advanced stage with lymph node involvement, lymphatic invasion, venous invasion, and no residual tumor at the resection margins.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC) of the right breast, classified as SBR Grade 3, measuring 1.8 cm. The tumor is associated with high nuclear grade ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), predominantly of solid and cribriform types with microcalcifications, and lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS). The invasive tumor is noted to be within 1 mm of the inferior surgical resection margin, but overall, the margins are negative. Sentinel lymph nodes #1 and #2 from the right axilla are both negative for metastases. An additional excised lateral posterior margin shows no evidence of residual tumor. The tumor exhibits no vascular or lymphatic invasion and is ER-positive, PR-positive, and HER2-negative by IHC. Pathological staging is pT1c N0, with no evidence of distant metastases. The Oncotype DX Breast Cancer Assay returned a recurrence score of 14, indicating a relatively low risk of distant recurrence.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of an anaplastic oligoastrocytoma, predominantly oligodendroglial, classified as WHO Grade III. The tumor is characterized by infiltrating glioma cells with moderate nuclear pleomorphism, including elongated to oval nuclei, and a background ranging from fibrillary to myxoid. Cytologic atypia is moderate with scattered cells displaying more pronounced atypia. The neoplastic cells are found in cellular nests with some clustering around blood vessels. Mitotic activity is observed at a rate of up to four mitotic figures per 10 high power fields. There is no evidence of necrosis or microvascular proliferation. Immunohistochemistry results show strong GFAP positivity and lack of synaptophysin expression, aligning with the glial nature of the neoplasm. The MIB-1 labeling index stands at 10.7%, indicative of a high-grade tumor. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not explicitly mentioned, focusing the findings on the microscopic and immunohistochemical characteristics of the glioma.
