Additionally, a tooth included for analysis was examined grossly without further pathological investigation.

The pathological evaluation of the right frontal brain tumor revealed a low-grade astrocytoma, classified as WHO Grade II. The tumor displayed modest hypercellularity, characteristic of low-grade glioma, and the Ki-67 proliferation index was noted to be 5%, indicating a low proliferative activity. Molecular studies were conducted to rule out oligodendroglioma, with no evidence of loss of heterozygosity (LOH) detected in 1p or 19q chromosomal regions, confirming the diagnosis as astrocytoma, WHO II/IV. The gross examination revealed multiple fragments of soft, pink-tan tissue, while the histopathological analysis confirmed the presence of hypercellular glial tissue consistent with the low-grade glioma. No margin status or lymph node involvement was noted, as is typical with intracranial tumors. The final diagnosis was confirmed by Dr. and a thorough report has been documented, including the findings and diagnostic process.

The pathological findings indicate the presence of an invasive squamous cell carcinoma in the right tonsil, extending to the base of the tongue, right soft palate, and lateral and medial pharyngeal regions. The carcinoma is keratinizing and moderately differentiated. Angiolymphatic invasion is observed, but notably, perineural invasion is absent. All surgical resection margins are free from carcinoma, which indicates complete excision of the primary tumor. The pathologic stage is classified as pT2N3MX, suggesting a primary tumor of a certain size with significant lymph node involvement but no distant metastasis assessed. In the right neck dissection, a metastatic squamous cell carcinoma is identified in one of four lymph nodes at Level II, which also shows extracapsular extension, indicating more aggressive disease. The submandibular gland appears unremarkable. Additional lymph nodes at Level V show no evidence of tumor. These findings highlight the locally advanced nature of the carcinoma with notable regional metastatic spread, emphasizing the complexity of the case.

The pathology report reveals a diagnosis of high-grade muscle-invasive urothelial carcinoma of the bladder with metastatic involvement. The primary tumor displays both papillary and flat non-invasive growth patterns as well as diffuse invasive characteristics. Key findings include the presence of invasive carcinoma at the urethral margin and carcinoma in situ at the right ureteral margin. There is significant lymph node involvement, with metastasis noted in both left and right pelvic lymph nodes, and multiple nodes involved in the left pelvic region. Additionally, the pathologic staging is pT3 and PR2, indicating advanced local invasion. Other noteworthy findings include chronic cystitis, a foreign body reaction within the bladder, proliferative cystitis, and nodular prostate hyperplasia. The urethral distal margin reveals benign fibromuscular tissue, while benign vas deferens and ureteral structures are noted in other specimens. Notably, invasive carcinoma involves periureteral left tissue and periurethral right tissue. Overall, this comprehensive examination underscores the extensive progression and significant metastatic spread of the urothelial carcinoma.

The pathological examination reveals a case of bilateral mastectomy with distinct findings for each breast. The right breast displays benign breast tissue with notable fibrocystic changes, including sclerosis, microcyst formation, usual ductal hyperplasia, duct ectasia, and focal microcalcification, but no evidence of malignant transformation. Conversely, the left breast harbors an invasive ductal carcinoma, graded as Nottingham III/III with a high score, indicating aggressive features such as the presence of tumor necrosis and venous/lymphatic invasion. Margins reveal the tumor approaching within close proximity, specifically to the superficial lower outer quadrant. Additionally, a multifocal high-grade intraductal component is noted, extending significantly beyond the invasive carcinoma. No nipple or skin involvement is detected. Lymph node assessment demonstrates one out of twenty-two lymph nodes positive for metastatic carcinoma, specifically a non-sentinel left axillary lymph node, placing the pathologic stage at pT2 N1 MX. Hormone receptor status indicates strong positivity for estrogen receptors but negative and weak for HER2/neu and progesterone receptors, respectively. Supplementary pathological changes include various fibrocystic alterations such as microcyst formation, focal duct ectasia, sclerosis, usual ductal hyperplasia, and apocrine metaplasia. The examination of the lymph nodes yielded one sentinel and twenty other left axillary lymph nodes negative for metastatic carcinoma, showing a guarded but possibly non-advanced stage of metastasis.

The pathological examination of the brain tumor, resected from the right frontal lobe, confirms a diagnosis of anaplastic astrocytoma, WHO grade III. The neoplasm exhibits diffuse infiltration into the cerebral cortex and underlying white matter. Histologically, the tumor is characterized by cells with angulated to irregular nuclei displaying moderate to marked nuclear pleomorphism, with a fibrillar to spindled cytoplasm and focal gemistocytic features. Occasional multinucleated tumor cells and scattered mitotic figures are observed, but there is no evidence of necrosis or vascular proliferation. Immunohistochemical studies reveal a high Ki-67 labeling index, reaching approximately 25% in certain areas, indicating high proliferative activity. The tumor cells are positive for mutant IDH-1. There is no mention of specific margin status or lymph node involvement, which is typical given the intracranial location of the tumor. Further molecular analyses, including MGMT gene methylation and 1p/19q chromosomal losses, have been sent for additional characterization. The overall histopathological and immunohistochemical profile suggests an aggressive glial tumor with potential for further progression, though it currently lacks the necrosis and vascular proliferation that would elevate it to a grade IV glioblastoma.

The pathological assessment reveals the presence of three distinct foci of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma in the left upper lobe of the lung. These are characterized by their respective patterns: a 20-mm acinar adenocarcinoma with extensive blood vessel invasion and probable lymphatic permeation; a 12-mm papillary adenocarcinoma; and a 5-mm bronchioloalveolar pattern adenocarcinoma. Both the acinar and papillary adenocarcinomas display separation by unremarkable lung tissue, with no evidence of perineural permeation, and are clear of the bronchial resection margin. Notably, the acinar-type carcinoma shows invasion into the visceral pleura, whereas the papillary carcinoma does not. Immunohistochemical staining confirms the pulmonary origin of these lesions, and they appear to represent synchronous primary tumors. There is no evidence of lymph node metastasis in the sampled hilar lymph nodes, which exhibit reactive changes only. The non-neoplastic lung parenchyma shows signs of mild emphysema. Overall, the findings correspond to a T2N0 staging for the acinar adenocarcinoma and T1N0 staging for the papillary and bronchioloalveolar adenocarcinomas.

The pathology findings describe a diagnosis of moderately differentiated (G2) squamous cell carcinoma in the right lower lobe of the lung, consistent with the pre-operative and post-operative diagnosis of right lung cancer. The tumor exhibits multifocal characteristics with the largest mass measuring 3.0 x 3.0 x 3.0 cm, extending to the peripheral aspect of the lung but not invading the visceral pleura or lymphovascular spaces, although findings are suspicious for possible lymphovascular invasion. The carcinoma appears confined to the lung without evident extranodal extension, with two additional discrete lesions identified upon further sectioning. The tumor margins are assessed with the nearest bronchial margin being 2.0 cm from the tumor and the pleural surface less than 0.1 cm away. Both the level 9 and level 4 lymph nodes were examined and found to be benign without metastasis, confirming a total of zero out of two lymph nodes containing metastatic carcinoma. Other notable findings include patchy fibrosis and bronchiectasis within the lung parenchyma. The final pathologic staging is revised to pT3N0, based on the presence of a satellite tumor which classifies the tumor as stage T3 under new classification guidelines, with no changes to other aspects of the report.

The pathological examination reveals an invasive squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, in both the right and left aryepiglottic folds of the larynx. The tumor in the right aryepiglottic fold demonstrates invasive characteristics with no specified details about margin status or lymph node involvement. In contrast, the carcinoma in the left aryepiglottic fold shows extension into adjacent cartilage, signaling a more extensive invasion. The clinical context provided indicates the presence of a mass in the supraglottic region. Both specimens consist of rubbery, lobulated tissue, with no additional pathologic abnormalities noted in these descriptions.

The pathological evaluation of the lung mass in the lingula reveals an invasive, moderate to poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma located immediately beneath the pleura. The primary tumor does not involve the visceral pleural surface, and the margins of resection are clear of neoplastic involvement, indicating a negative margin status. There is no evidence of direct extension of the tumor, nor is there venous, arterial, or lymphatic invasion detected. Additionally, the examined level 5, 7, and 10 lymph nodes are free of metastatic carcinoma, signifying no regional lymph node involvement. Other pathological findings include emphysematous bullae and a pleural-based fibrous scar with indications of old hemorrhage. Overall, these findings suggest a localized lung adenocarcinoma with no metastatic spread to regional lymph nodes, and clear surgical margins.

The histopathological examination of the ascending colon reveals a tubulopapillary adenocarcinoma (G2) as the primary diagnosis. The tumor demonstrates a complete circumferential involvement of the colonic mucosa and has significantly narrowed the lumen. Microscopically, there is evidence of carcinoma infiltration into the pericolonic adipose tissue. Notably, the resection margins are free of neoplastic involvement. Additionally, there are tubulopapillary adenomas with mild dysplasia observed outside the tumor area. Lymph node assessment shows metastatic involvement in one out of six examined nodes, and there are signs of carcinomatous emboli. These findings categorize the tumor as Dukes C, Astler-Coller C2, with a staging of pT3, pN1.

The pathological assessment reveals a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma in the left breast, with notable characteristics including a tumor size of 3.5 cm and a grade of 2. Additionally, there is an intermediate-grade ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of cribriform type with microcalcifications, without necrosis, and measuring 0.3 cm. Both the invasive carcinoma and the DCIS have negative resection margins, with the closest margin being 0.3 cm for the invasive component. Lymphatic-vascular and perineural invasion are absent. All three examined left axillary sentinel lymph nodes are free of metastatic disease. The non-tumorous breast tissue exhibits gynecomastia. Immunohistochemical testing shows that the carcinoma is estrogen receptor-positive (~20% tumor cells), progesterone receptor-negative, and HER2/neu-positive with a staining intensity score of 3. The overall pathological stage, following AJCC/UICC guidelines, is pT2N0MX. The nipple and skin/dermis are free of tumor involvement.

The pathological examination of the right frontal lobe brain excision revealed an oligodendroglioma, WHO Grade II. The lesion was characterized by cellular uniformity and the presence of typical oligodendroglial elements. Immunohistochemical studies showed a negative p53 result, and the MIB-1 proliferation index did not exceed 3%, indicating a relatively low proliferative activity. Further genetic analysis for 1p/19q co-deletion has been recommended to provide additional prognostic information. The tumor margins were not explicitly described regarding involvement; however, the entirety of the specimens was submitted for examination, emphasizing thorough pathological evaluation. No lymph node involvement was relevant to this case, as it pertains to a primary brain tumor without typical metastatic patterns. The gross description noted the presence of tan, friable soft tissue aggregates, which were consistent with the intraoperative frozen section diagnosis favoring oligodendroglioma. Overall, the findings confirm a low-grade oligodendroglioma with essential genetic and proliferative characteristics critical for clinical management.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the cecum. The primary tumor measures 5.3 cm and extends into, but not through, the muscularis propria. The surgical margins, including proximal, distal, and circumferential (radial) margins, are clear of tumor involvement. Vascular invasion is not identified. Lymph node analysis reveals no evidence of metastatic disease in the one apical lymph node assessed or in the thirty-two additional regional lymph nodes examined. Additionally, no pericolonic tumor deposits are present. The appendix exhibits no pathological abnormalities. No distant metastasis can be evaluated based on this specimen.

The essential pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of an intermediate to peripheral bronchopulmonary, poorly differentiated, partly gigantocellular, and polymorphocellular squamous cell carcinoma in the upper lobe of the left lung. The tumor exhibits relatively clear demarcation and infiltrates the pleura, occupying segment 2. Resection margins are free of tumor involvement (R0), and the tumor has been staged pT2 pN0 V1, corresponding to stage IB. Histological examination reveals small tumor necroses and tumor-related inflammation, along with active inflammation in the surrounding lung parenchyma, including numerous macrophages. There is evidence of circumscribed subpleural alveolar and interstitial remodeling with atypical squamous cell metaplasia, as well as apical emphysematous stromal changes and small subpleural cyst formation. Lymph nodes show moderate deposition of anthracotic pigment. Secondary findings include chronic parietal pleuritis with fibrosis. The main tumor appears to be a primarily peripheral carcinoma with central invasion involving intermediate or subsegmental bronchi of segments 1-3 and marked fibrosis in the overlying visceral pleura. Additionally, a potentially early secondary carcinoma focus is noted at the boundary of segments S3/4, requiring differential consideration between a nascent carcinoma and inflammatory-related atypical metaplasia.

The pathological examination of the right breast lumpectomy reveals an invasive ductal carcinoma graded as grade 2, accompanied by ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of the cribriform type, also nuclear grade 2. The carcinoma is confirmed to be negative at the resection margins, indicating complete excision with clear margins. The surrounding non-tumorous breast tissue displays changes such as fibrosis, apocrine metaplasia, microcysts, ductal hyperplasia, and adenosis. Notably, there is no evidence of vessel invasion or skin involvement, maintaining the skin as unremarkable. Additionally, analysis of the right axillary sentinel lymph node biopsy identifies metastatic ductal carcinoma with extranodal extension, significantly indicating the spread beyond the lymph node capsule. These findings collectively contribute to the assessment and staging of the carcinoma, guiding further clinical management.

The diagnosis is an anaplastic oligodendroglioma, WHO Grade III, located in the right frontal region. The tumor is characterized by a glial neoplasm that diffusely infiltrates both gray and white matter, with cells displaying round, mildly enlarged nuclei and frequent perinuclear halos. There is mild to moderate atypia, and mitotic activity is notable with up to six mitoses observed in ten high power fields. Key pathological features such as microvascular proliferation and necrosis are absent. However, a high level of proliferation is indicated by the presence of numerous MIB-1 reactive cells, with an estimated MIB-1 labeling index of over 25% in most proliferative areas. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not mentioned, suggesting a primary focus on the tumor's intrinsic characteristics and proliferation rate.

The pathological analysis concludes a diagnosis of left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, histologic grade 1/4, with a unifocal tumor confined to the lung parenchyma. The tumor is identified as invasive adenocarcinoma but is limited to the lung without evidence of significant obstructive pneumonitis or atelectasis. There is no tumor involvement in the resection margins, indicating clear surgical margins. Examination of 14 regional lymph nodes from various stations (including ST 7, 4L, 4R, and hilar nodes) revealed no metastatic involvement. The completion lobectomy specimen also displayed no residual carcinoma. The pathological staging is pT1a pN0 pMX, reflecting a primary tumor with no regional lymph node metastasis and the metastasis status cannot be assessed. This comprehensive evaluation suggests a localized disease with no current evidence of metastatic spread, indicating a favorable prognosis following surgical resection.

The diagnosis for the left frontal lobe brain lesion is an oligodendroglioma, WHO Grade II. Histopathological examination reveals a tumor characterized by low MIB-1 labeling activity (less than 2-3%) across most sections, with some regions displaying higher MIB-1 activity of unclear significance. The tumor shows diffuse thickening of the white matter and an area of hemorrhage. The excision margins are not specified, and no lymph node involvement is noted. Cytogenetic and molecular studies for 1p/19q co-deletion and MGMT promoter methylation are currently pending. Gross pathological evaluation describes two fragments of soft unoriented tissue and a larger portion of brain tissue with a gyral surface. The intraoperative consultation confirmed the presence of a malignant glioma.

The histopathological examination of the resected sigmoid colon and rectum revealed a diagnosis of bifocal tubulopapillary and partially mucinous adenocarcinoma (G3). The larger tumour exhibited a cauliflower shape, encircled the entire intestinal circumference resulting in luminal narrowing, and showed macroscopic penetration into the intestinal wall and adipose tissue. It was located 10 cm from the proximal incision and 11 cm from the distal incision, with a minimum side margin of 1.1 cm. The smaller tumour, situated 5 cm from the first, similarly penetrated the intestinal wall and had a side margin of 2.5 cm. Both lesions demonstrated invasive carcinoma involving the muscularis propria and the pericolonic adipose tissue. Microscopically, the tumors were categorized as poorly differentiated (G3), consistent with a Dukes B and Astler-Coller B2 classification, staged as pT3, pNO, indicating no lymph node involvement. Surgical margins, including the intestinal ends, were free of neoplastic cells, suggesting clear resection borders.

The pathological findings pertain to a diagnosis of well-differentiated astrocytoma. The tumor is characterized by mildly hypercellular gray and white matter, with the hypercellularity more prominent in permanent sections compared to frozen sections. The tumor comprises glial cells with round to oval nuclei, displaying a small number of apoptotic figures but lacking perinuclear halos and other classic glioma features. Reactive astrocytes are present, but the infiltrating nature of the glioma is evident. No mitotic figures are observed. The presence of scattered MIB-1 reactive cells with a labeling index of 1.7% suggests a modestly proliferative neoplasm, consistent with low-grade histologic features. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not detailed in this pathology description. Overall, the findings are indicative of a moderately proliferative, well-differentiated astrocytoma.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of invasive and in situ lobular carcinoma of the right breast. The invasive tumor is classified as a Bloom-Richardson grade II lesion, characterized by architectural score of 3, nuclear grade of 2, and mitotic rate of 1, with no angiolymphatic invasion. The mastectomy specimen shows the main tumor mass as well as isolated microscopic foci of invasive carcinoma in different quadrants, all removed with negative deep inked surgical margins. The nipple demonstrates extensive lobular carcinoma in situ colonizing the lactiferous ducts. Additionally, the right axillary sentinel lymph nodes exhibit variable involvement by metastatic lobular carcinoma; while two nodes (sentinel lymph nodes #1 and #2) show metastatic disease predominantly confined to the subcapsular sinuses and detectable primarily by pancytokeratin immunohistochemistry, the remaining nodes (sentinel lymph nodes #3 and #4) are negative for metastatic tumor. The overall stage of the disease is categorized as pTNM Stage T3N1 (mi), indicating a locally advanced tumor with minimal nodal involvement. Prognostic markers are ER positive and PR positive, with additional reporting pending for further analysis.

The histopathological examination of the resected right breast and axillary tissues from a female patient revealed a diagnosis of bifocal invasive breast carcinoma. The first tumor is characterized as high-grade (NHG3) invasive ductal carcinoma, presenting with extensive necrosis and associated ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) regions, displaying high nuclear atypia and comedo necrosis. The second tumor exhibits mixed invasive characteristics, including ductal, papillary, and cribriform patterns, with a moderate grade (NHG2). Immunohistochemical staining showed that the first tumor lacked estrogen and progesterone receptors and was HER2-negative, while the second tumor exhibited estrogen and progesterone receptor positivity in 10-75% of the neoplastic cells, and was also HER2-negative. Both tumors were near the surgical margins but did not breach them. Pathological evaluation of the axillary lymph nodes demonstrated carcinoma metastasis with capsular infiltration and vascular emboli, classified as pN2a, indicating regional lymph node involvement. Additionally, the surrounding breast tissue exhibited fibrocystic changes. The final pathological staging included pT2 for the larger tumor and pT1c for the smaller one, indicating significant but localized primary tumor involvement.

The surgical pathology findings reveal an invasive poorly differentiated urothelial carcinoma of the bladder with focal squamoid features. The carcinoma extends through the right bladder wall into the perivesical soft tissue, involving the right ureterovesical junction and intramural ureter with obstruction. A single focus of vascular invasion and adjacent urothelial carcinoma in situ were noted. Importantly, the resection margins are clear of urothelial dysplasia and malignancy. The uterus, cervix, bilateral fallopian tubes, and ovaries showed no atypia, dysplasia, or malignancy. Pathologically, the left and right proximal and distal ureters were benign, exhibiting chronic inflammation without evidence of dysplasia or malignancy. Examination of multiple lymph nodes (totaling 66) from various regions including external iliac, pre-sciatic, para-aortic, and obturator/hypogastric nodes revealed no metastatic malignancy. Consequently, the pathologic TNM staging is pTis, T3bN0MX. The specimen is currently undergoing further analysis for P53 expression by immunohistology to provide additional prognostic information.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of anaplastic astrocytoma with a gemistocytic component. The tumor is characterized by moderately hypercellular astrocytic neoplasm with a significant predominance of gemistocytes. Cellular atypia is generally moderate, with some scattered cells exhibiting marked atypical features. Notably, the extensive examination of the tissue reveals an absence of mitoses, microvascular proliferation, and necrosis. The MIB-1 labeling index is reported at 2.0%, indicating a relatively low proliferative activity. Margins and lymph node involvement are not detailed in the findings, suggesting focus primarily on the tumor's cellular characteristics.

The pathology reveals a multifocal pT3 pNO pMx well-differentiated adenocarcinoma with bronchioloalveolar features located in the superior segment and anterior basilar right lower lobe. Additionally, bronchioloalveolar carcinoma is identified in the posterior segment of the right upper lobe and an undesignated site of the right lower lobe. Surgical margins are variably involved; notably, the superior segment of the right lower lobe and posterior segment of the right upper lobe have microscopic foci of bronchioloalveolar carcinoma, with cauterized margins also positive in the right lower lobe. Multiple carcinoid tumorlets and leiomyomas are present across different lung specimens, along with areas of focal granulomatous inflammation. Lymph node resections (including #9, #8, #7, #4R, #2R, and #10) show no evidence of malignancy. Immunohistochemical stains confirm that the carcinoid tumorlets are reactive with chromogranin and synaptophysin, maintaining the diagnosis. The absence of metastatic involvement in the examined lymph nodes accords a classification of regional lymph node metastasis as pNO.

The pathological examination of the left lower lung lobectomy specimen reveals a diagnosis of papillary adenocarcinoma, which is moderately differentiated. Examination of the lymph nodes indicates that all five intrathoracic lymph nodes are positive for metastatic disease, suggesting lymphatic spread of the carcinoma. Margin status, tumor extent, the presence of other tumor nodules, and evidence of lymphatic or venous invasion were not specified. The specimen shows no indications of neoadjuvant treatment, and no additional pathological findings are noted.

The pathological analysis revealed an invasive ductal carcinoma (NOS) in the left breast with a modified B-R Grade of 3, indicating high-grade characteristics such as high tubular, nuclear, and mitosis scores. The carcinoma lacked evidence of lymphovascular invasion, ductal carcinoma in situ, Paget's disease, and microcalcifications. In terms of tumor margins, the invasive carcinoma was located away from critical margins, which confirmed negative surgical margins. Sentinel lymph node examination showed metastatic carcinoma in one out of two nodes, while the axillary dissection revealed the involvement of one out of thirteen nodes sampled. Immunohistochemical staining indicated positive estrogen receptor status in 60% of the malignant cells and positive progesterone receptor status in 10% of malignant cells, suggesting hormone receptor positivity. The HER2 status was non-amplified. Additional findings included fibroadenomas. Lymph node involvement concluded as pT2 pN1a pMX, indicating the primary tumor size and regional lymph node metastasis without assessment of distant metastasis. The overall lymph node burden was low with only one positive node out of a total of thirteen examined.

The excised tissue from the rectum revealed a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. The tumor extends into the subserosal layer but has not metastasized to the regional lymph nodes, as both examined nodes were negative for metastatic involvement. The surgical margins are free of tumor, indicating a successful resection with no residual malignancy at the margins. There are no other significant pathological findings in the examined tissue.

The pathology findings reveal a high-grade, non-papillary urothelial carcinoma of the bladder with focal squamous differentiation, which invades the bladder wall transmurally and extends into the perivesical fat. The serosal surface is free of tumor, and no lymphovascular invasion is identified. The resection margins are also free of tumor. In the prostate, the urothelial carcinoma extends to the periurethral prostatic ducts and exhibits superficial invasion of the periductal stroma. The prostatic tissue shows glandular atrophy and peri-basilar cell hyperplasia with atypia, consistent with treatment effect, and is characterized by chronic and focal acute inflammation. There is no residual prostatic carcinoma identified. Importantly, the lymph node examination, including left and right external iliac, obturator, and common iliac lymph nodes, shows no evidence of tumoral involvement. Additionally, the final margins of both the left and right ureters display unremarkable urothelium with no tumor presence. These findings collectively suggest a comprehensive evaluation of the resected tissues, with key insights into the extent and margins of the urothelial carcinoma and associated pathological changes.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of Grade 3 (of 4) fibrillary astrocytoma (WHO Grade III) located in the left frontal brain lobe. Immunohistochemical staining revealed that the p53 protein is strongly and diffusely expressed in the neoplastic cells, and the MIB-1 labeling index is high, supporting the diagnosis. The preliminary frozen section consultation suggested a diffuse glioma, later clarified as suspicious for an intermediate grade. Despite extensive sampling of the left frontal mass in both biopsy and excision specimens, the specific description of tumor margins and lymph node involvement is not provided. The gross description included multiple tissue blocks from the left frontal lobe. Together, these findings underscore the aggressive nature of the tumor, necessitating comprehensive clinical correlation and management.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of adenocarcinoma, mixed subtype, located in the lower lobe of the left lung. The tumor exhibits a predominantly papillary structure with partial solid, acinar, and bronchioloalveolar carcinoma (BAC) patterns. Notably, there is evidence of infiltration into the pleura visceralis. The tumor is staged as pT2, indicating invasion beyond the lung parenchyma. Importantly, all twenty examined lymph nodes show no signs of metastatic involvement (pN0), and the overall tumor grade is moderately differentiated (G2). These findings together underline the local-invasive nature of the tumor without regional nodal metastasis.

The pathological evaluation of the partial resection of the floor of the mouth and anterior arch of the mandible, along with a partial glossectomy, reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated squamous carcinoma with perineural involvement and infiltration into the skeletal muscle. The invasive carcinoma is noted to extend into the mandible. Initial assessment indicated tumor extension to the right lingual surgical resection margin. However, after further examination of the newly submitted right lingual margin specimen, the resection margins were confirmed to be free of carcinoma, ensuring that no residual tumor cells are present within the surgical margins. No lymph node involvement is described in the findings. The detailed analysis of the specimens and subsequent resection margin evaluations are critical in establishing that the surgical intervention has successfully achieved clear margins, which is crucial for reducing the risk of local recurrence.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of anaplastic oligodendroglioma, classified as WHO grade III. The tumor demonstrates oligodendroglial differentiation with evident mitotic activity, indicating cellular proliferation. The proliferation index is quantified at 10%, reflective of the tumor’s aggressive nature. Margins are evaluated for completeness of excision, although specific details on margin status, such as the presence of residual tumor cells or clearance, are not provided. Examination of lymph nodes is not applicable in this context as the central nervous system neoplasms typically do not metastasize to lymph nodes. Other significant pathological findings are not mentioned, thus focusing the diagnosis primarily on the histomorphological features and proliferative index. Overall, the findings suggest a malignant and potentially aggressive tumor requiring further therapeutic management.

The pathological evaluation of the right upper and lower lobes of the lung, following wedge resection, revealed two separate primary adenocarcinomas, both with mixed subtypes. In the right upper lobe, a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma exhibited solid and acinar growth patterns. The tumor showed angiolymphatic invasion and extended to the inked margin but lacked visceral pleural and perineural invasion. Associated findings included marked emphysematous changes and carcinoid tumorlets. The right lower lobe contained a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma displaying micropapillary, acinar, and lepidic growth patterns. Similar to the upper lobe tumor, this carcinoma also exhibited angiolymphatic invasion and extended to the inked margin without visceral pleural or perineural invasion. Marked emphysematous changes were noted as well. Immunohistochemical staining results indicated positivity for CK7, TTF1, and Napsin in both tumors, while CK20 was positive in the lower lobe tumor and negative in the upper lobe tumor, suggesting separate primary origins. No lymph nodes were examined, and no nodes were involved. The overall staging for each tumor, if determined to be separate primaries, would be pT1a.

The pathological analysis reveals a diagnosis of halfway differentiated squamous cell carcinoma primarily located in the right middle lobe of the lung. The tumor extensively involves the central to peripheral regions, nearly occupying the entire middle lobe, and obstructs the bronchi, creating mucus plugs distally in the right middle and lower lobes. The mass is characterized by large areas of necrosis with pushing margins and a coloration pattern of yellow and grey-white. Despite growing to the pleura, the tumor does not extend through it; however, extensive pleural reaction with retraction, discoloration, and fibrinoid exudate is noted. Importantly, metastatic squamous cell carcinoma is present in one out of five para-segmental pulmonary artery lymph nodes and one out of two parabronchial lymph nodes, but the bronchial stump remains free of the tumor. Additionally, the subcarinal lymph nodes show anthracotic changes but no tumor involvement. The lung tissue adjacent to the tumor displays interstitial follicular pneumonia and early acute suppurative pneumonia. The tumor infiltrates and ulcerates the mucosa of the segmental bronchus, compressing but not infiltrating the segmental artery. The pleural surfaces are notably thick and hemorrhagic but not infiltrated by the carcinoma. Each of these findings underlines the aggressive nature of the tumor and the presence of metastatic spread to specific lymph nodes while highlighting areas of the lung and bronchus that remain uninvolved.

The pathological examination of the left breast mastectomy with axillary tail revealed an infiltrating ductal carcinoma, no special type, that is poorly differentiated with a Nottingham grade of G3 and a score of 9. The tumor displayed necrosis within the infiltrating component, but microcalcifications were absent, and there was no venous or lymphatic invasion. Margins were negative, with the tumor being adequately distanced from the nearest margin. Importantly, no intraductal component was observed. Among the ten lymph nodes examined, none showed evidence of metastatic tumor involvement. Additionally, the analysis confirmed no involvement of the nipple, skin, or multicentricity within the breast tissue. The tumor was hormone receptor-negative for both estrogen and progesterone and HER2/neu negative by immunohistochemistry. The pathological stage was determined to be pT2 NO MX. The breast tissue also displayed periductal chronic inflammation. In comparison, the skin flap excision from the left breast showed unremarkable skin and subcutaneous tissue without any signs of malignancy.

The pathological assessment of the brain biopsies from the right parietal lesion reveals an anaplastic astrocytoma (WHO Grade III). Histological examination demonstrates a diffuse infiltration of astrocytic neoplastic cells into the cerebral cortex and adjacent white matter, accompanied by a moderately fibrillary background. While extensive cautery artefact is noted, the specimens show a handful of mitotic figures and a MIB-1 proliferation index greater than 10%, indicating a high proliferative activity. Importantly, there are no nuclear anaplastic features, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis observed. Immunohistochemistry highlights overexpression of p53 in the majority of neoplastic astrocytes, and CD163 staining reveals moderately activated perivascular microglia. Genetic analysis via Loss of Heterozygosity (LOH) assay identifies allelic loss on chromosome arm 19q and partial deletion on 1p, at the D1S496 locus. Intra-operative consultation and subsequent frozen sections confirmed the diagnosis of neoplastic astrocytic proliferation. Collectively, these findings are consistent with an anaplastic astrocytoma, characterized by diffuse brain parenchymal infiltration and increased mitotic activity, without microvascular proliferation or necrosis.

The pathological examination of the right breast and axillary contents demonstrates an infiltrating ductal carcinoma, which is poorly differentiated as indicated by a Nottingham grade and score of 9 out of 9, reflecting significant tubule formation, nuclear pleomorphism, and mitotic activity. The carcinoma exhibits extensive venous and lymphatic invasion and is characterized by the presence of necrosis within the infiltrating component and accompanying microcalcifications. The intraductal component is also identified. Importantly, the deep margin is involved by the infiltrating tumor, indicating incomplete resection. Of the twelve axillary lymph nodes examined, nine are positive for metastatic carcinoma, with the largest metastatic focus measuring 1.5 cm. Hormonal receptor testing reveals that 20% of the tumor cells are estrogen receptor-positive, while less than 5% are progesterone receptor-positive. HER2/neu expression is negative. These combined findings result in a pathologic staging of pT3 N2a M#, correlating with AJCC Stage IIIA breast cancer. The gross description corroborates these findings with the observed masses and the involvement of axillary lymph nodes.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma in the left breast, classified as Grade 2. The tumor, which measures the greatest dimension of 9 cm, is located in the upper inner and lower inner quadrants extending to the central portion of the breast. The surgical margins are clear of invasive carcinoma, indicating successful resection. The axillary dissection shows metastatic carcinoma present in two out of eighteen lymph nodes, confirming regional spread but without evidence of extranodal extension. The invasive tumor is characterized by a cribriform architectural pattern, absence of necrosis, and intermediate nuclear grade features. Additionally, DCIS is present but does not extensively involve the ductal system, and both lobular carcinoma in situ and dermal lymph-vascular invasion are absent. Notably, the carcinoma exhibits lymph-vascular invasion and is positive for estrogen receptors while being negative for progesterone receptors and HER-2/neu. The findings indicate the presence of macrometastases in lymph nodes, classifying the lymph node status as pN1a, and the primary tumor as pT3, emphasizing a large tumor size with regional lymph node involvement but no distant metastasis (M category not applicable). The excised segment of the skin and nipple reveal no pathological changes, and the nipple epidermis is not affected by DCIS. Microcalcifications are observed in non-neoplastic tissue, rounding out a comprehensive pathological assessment of the breast cancer.

The histopathological examination of the left pneumonectomy specimen and No. 5 lymph node reveals the presence of a poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. The tumor is characterized by a white, spiculated mass located distal to the bifurcation of the left main bronchus, measuring 42 mm in diameter. Notably, the margins show that the tumor abuts the hilar resection margin but is clear of the soft tissue resection margin. Microscopy confirms extensive necrosis within the tumor and metastatic involvement of at least one peribronchial lymph node. No invasion into lymphatic, vascular, or perineural structures is observed. However, the tumor does invade the distal fat. Additional findings in the lung parenchyma include bronchiectasis, focal lipid pneumonia, and mild emphysema. The No. 5 lymph node specimen shows reactive lymphoid tissue without evidence of metastasis. Based on these findings, the pathological stage is concluded to be T3 N1.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated invasive lobular carcinoma, characterized by several satellite foci and focal in situ spread. Classified as Grade II lobular carcinoma (pT2N0), the main tumor mass exhibits moderately differentiated cellular architecture and invasive properties. The surgical margins are clear, as the tumor does not extend to the borders of the resected tissue. No lymph node involvement is detected, with all three sentinel nodes testing negative for metastatic disease. Further pathological examination confirms the absence of vascular or perineural invasion, and the surrounding tissues exhibit no significant abnormalities. These findings highlight the localized nature of the tumor, with no evidence of spread beyond the primary site, ensuring that it remains confined within the breast tissue.

The pathological examination of the lung lobectomy specimen from the right lower lobe reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. The tumor extends to involve the visceral pleura. Metastatic involvement is evident in a significant number of intrathoracic lymph nodes, with 8 out of 10 nodes positive for metastasis. This extensive lymphatic spread indicates an advanced stage of the disease. The tumor characteristics and the involvement of visceral pleura alongside a high rate of lymph node metastasis are critical factors that will influence further therapeutic decisions and prognostic considerations.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of high-grade (grade 3) urothelial carcinoma that is invasive into the muscularis propria. The tumor also involves the prostatic ducts and acini, although no invasion into the prostatic stroma is detected. Importantly, there is no evidence of lymphovascular invasion. Based on the gross description, multiple fragments of tan-pink to gray-white, partly cauterized, friable soft tissue were received and thoroughly examined. Overall, the findings indicate a significant tumor burden with muscular invasion but no further spread into prostatic stroma or vascular structures.

The pathological examination of the completely resected left breast and associated axillary tissues reveals a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma with an in situ ductal carcinoma component. The primary tumor, located in the subareolar region, exhibits high nuclear atypia with areas of comedo necrosis, accounting for 5% of the tumor mass. Microscopically, the invasive component is classified as NHG2, demonstrating moderate differentiation with a mitotic rate of six mitoses per 10 high power fields. The surrounding glandular tissue shows fibrous mastopathy. No lesions are detected in the nipple. Examination of the axillary lymph nodes reveals metastatic involvement in 9 out of 15 nodes, with capsular and perinodal tissue infiltration observed, classifying the lymph node status at pN2a. Additionally, immunohistochemical analysis indicates that over 75% of the neoplastic cells express estrogen and progesterone receptors, while HER2 protein expression is negative, with a score of 1+. The tumor margins are clear, suggesting no residual disease in the resection site.

The pathological examination of the left upper outer breast tissue, obtained via lumpectomy, reveals a diagnosis of infiltrating ductal carcinoma. The tumor is moderately differentiated, as indicated by a Nottingham Histologic Score of Grade II. Histologically, the tumor demonstrates moderate tubule formation, marked nuclear pleomorphism, and a mitotic count that suggests moderate proliferative activity. Notably, the surgical margins are uninvolved, indicating no residual tumor at the resection edges. Of the seventeen lymph nodes examined, metastasis is present in one sentinel lymph node, although there is no evidence of extracapsular invasion. There is no recorded evidence of neoadjuvant treatment. These findings collectively support a moderately aggressive disease with limited lymphatic spread and clear resection margins.

The diagnosis is a low-grade (WHO grade II) oligoastrocytoma with a predominance of oligodendroglioma components. Microscopically, the tumor exhibits mild to moderate hypercellularity with a predominant population of microgemistocytes. A smaller fraction of cells shows perinuclear halos, while another minority features oblong nuclei and modest eosinophilic cytoplasm. Importantly, no mitotic figures, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis are observed. The MIB-1 labeling index is 5.0%, indicating an intermediate level of proliferative activity, consistent with the overall low-grade histologic nature of the neoplasm. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not explicitly mentioned, suggesting no overt local spread or regional lymphatic involvement within the scope of the provided findings.

The pathological assessment of the upper lobe lung resection reveals an infiltrate of poorly differentiated and non-mucinous pulmonary adenocarcinoma. Classified as M-8140/3, G 3, and staged pT2, pV1, pN2, pM X, the tumor presents as stage IIIA with clear surgical margins (R0). Immunohistochemical analysis highlights the absence of cytokeratin 5 expression among tumor cells. However, there is notable co-expression of cytokeratin 7 and 20 in some cells, and most demonstrate nuclear expression of TTF-1, indicating primary pulmonary origin. Additionally, there is marked expression of CEA, with a moderately high proliferation rate indicated by Mib-1, staining approximately 30% of tumor cell nuclei. Lymph node involvement is evidenced by pN2 classification. These findings collectively support the diagnosis of primary pulmonary adenocarcinoma with a relatively high grade and advanced stage.

The pathological evaluation of the provided specimens reveals a diagnosis of keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma, grade II, identified within the left lung. The primary tumor, measuring 2.5 cm, is localized to the left main bronchus. Crucially, the bronchial, pleural, and vascular margins are free of malignancy. Additional findings include the presence of atelectasis in the adjacent non-neoplastic lung tissue. Lymph node assessment reveals no metastatic involvement across multiple sites, including nodes from levels 5, 7, 9, the inferior pulmonary vein, and peribronchial nodes, all totaling zero out of fourteen nodes with tumor involvement, leading to a staging of pT2N0. Examination of the 6th rib segment returned no evidence of tumor.

The pathological examination revealed an invasive poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma in the left upper lobe with the superior segment of the lower lobe of the lung. The tumor was diagnosed without evidence of angiolymphatic invasion and showed no involvement of the visceral pleura, indicating a T2 stage. The bronchial margin was free of malignancy, suggesting successful resection of the tumor with no residual disease at the cut margins. Comprehensive lymph node analysis, including samples from levels 4R, 4L, 5, 6, 7, 10, and 11, demonstrated no metastatic involvement, classifying the nodal stage as N0. Importantly, there was no evidence of satellite tumors. The final pathologic staging was determined to be pT2N0Mx, and the non-neoplastic lung tissue showed minimal changes.

The pathological examination of the resected section of the sigmoid colon reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated colorectal adenocarcinoma. The tumor extends to within a specified distance of the resection margin, encompassing the intestinal wall and invading the muscularis propria. Both oral and aboral resection margins are free of tumor involvement. A total of twenty-two mesocolic lymph nodes were examined, all of which were free of tumor presence but displayed reactive changes. The tumor is staged as pT2 pN0 (0/22) L0 V0, with a histological grade of G2. Subsequent analysis for microsatellite instability did not indicate significant tumor predisposition, particularly excluding the HNPCC/Lynch syndrome type.

The pathological evaluation reveals an invasive moderately differentiated squamous carcinoma of the tongue with a notable thickness, identified through partial glossectomy. The margins of resection submitted separately show no involvement by the tumor, indicating clear surgical margins around the resected area. Analysis of the right neck dissection, encompassing levels I to V, detected no metastatic involvement in all 28 lymph nodes examined. Additionally, the submandibular salivary gland exhibited focal chronic inflammation and squamous metaplasia. The glossopharyngeal sulcus and the glossomandibular sulcus contained no tumor, though the latter exhibited focal mild squamous dysplasia. The anterior and dorsum of the tongue, as well as another lymph node sampled from the right neck, were also free of tumor. Other relevant findings included the presence of skeletal muscle, minor salivary glands, and a segment of nerve within the glossopharyngeal sulcus, all without tumor involvement.

The pathological analysis of the patient's left frontal lesion reveals a high-grade glioma, most consistent with an anaplastic oligodendroglioma (WHO Grade III), identified in both biopsy and resection specimens. Histologically, the tumor is recognized as an anaplastic oligodendroglioma, characterized by high cellularity, nuclear atypia, and a high mitotic index. The tissue samples exhibit a pink-tan, focally hemorrhagic appearance with no discrete nodules observed upon gross inspection. The microscopic examination of both frozen and permanent paraffin sections confirms the high-grade nature of the glioma. Margins of the resected specimen cannot be assessed. Importantly, FISH analysis indicates deletions in both 1p and 19q chromosomal regions, which is associated with a favorable prognosis for this type of brain tumor. The evaluation of 1p/19q status corroborates the initial diagnosis and provides additional prognostic insight, emphasizing the potential for a more favorable clinical outcome. The need for a neuropathology consultation is noted for further evaluation and confirmation of these findings.

The pathology findings reveal a diagnosis of in situ and infiltrating lobular carcinoma in the right breast, which is of nuclear grade II/III with a low mitotic index. The carcinoma exhibits a multifocal appearance confined to the upper inner quadrant, whereas other quadrants show no malignancy. Notably, the deep margin is free of tumor by 5 mm, indicating a clear resection boundary. In contrast, the left breast demonstrates diffuse proliferative fibrocystic changes along with atypical duct and lobular hyperplasia and extensive microcalcification but shows no evidence of invasive carcinoma. Examination of the right axillary sentinel and non-sentinel lymph nodes, as well as the left axillary sentinel lymph nodes, reveals no evidence of metastatic disease, confirmed by negative cytokeratin immunohistochemical staining. These findings indicate no lymphatic spread, underscoring the localized nature of the malignancy.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of melanoma located in the skin, characterized as a primary tumor. The specimen analyzed, consisting of frozen tissue in a cryomold, exhibited 85% melanoma involvement. Margins were carefully assessed, and there was no evidence of residual tumor at the margins, indicating complete excision. No lymph node involvement was detected, consistent with a stage T2a N0 M0 classification according to the TNM staging system. The patient had previously undergone both chemotherapy and radiation treatments. Additional blood samples, preserved in a frozen state in tubes, were also examined and found to be normal, providing a baseline with no evidence of circulating tumor cells or other abnormalities.

The pathological analysis of the left temporal brain tumor biopsy reveals an anaplastic gemistocytic astrocytoma. The tumor is characterized by a predominance of gemistocytic astrocytes mixed with undifferentiated cells, exhibiting hypercellularity, nuclear pleomorphism, and hyperchromasia. Mitotic figures are present, indicative of cellular proliferation, although necrosis is notably absent. The vascular endothelial hyperplasia observed is mild. Immunohistochemistry shows strong GFAP positivity, and the MIB-1 labeling index is approximately 12%, suggesting a moderate proliferative activity. The tumor infiltrates diffusely into the surrounding brain tissue and extends into the leptomeninges. Margins are not specified in the report, and there is no mention of lymph node involvement, implying a primary central nervous system origin without regional metastatic spread.

The histopathological examination reveals an invasive squamous keratinized carcinoma (Carcinoma planoepitheliale keratodes invasivum laryngis) of the larynx, diagnosed as G2. The tumor is located primarily in the subglottal region with infiltration into the glottis on the left side, the thyroid cartilage, and the infrahyoid region. Importantly, microscopic analysis indicates that the tumor involves the anterior commissure and shows neoplastic proliferation at the front incision line, accompanied by a high degree of epithelium paraepidermoidale dysplasia. Other surgical margins are free from neoplastic proliferation. Lymph node involvement is noted with a classification of pN2b, indicating regional lymph node metastasis. The tumor staging is pT4a, suggesting a more advanced local invasion. These findings collectively signify a locally advanced, but resected, invasive squamous keratinized carcinoma with front marginal involvement and regional metastatic spread.

The essential pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of metastatic adenocarcinoma of the lung, specifically affecting the left upper lobe. The primary tumor is characterized as a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma with focal papillary components. Metastatic involvement is noted in a total of 7 regional lymph nodes, with one at L11, six at the lobectomy site, and one at Station 7, while two lymph nodes at Station 5 are free of tumor. The primary tumor margins are uninvolved by invasive carcinoma, maintaining a clear parenchymal margin. No evidence of venous or arterial invasion was found, though lymphatic invasion remains indeterminate, and emphysematous changes were observed. Immunohistochemical analysis supports the adenocarcinoma diagnosis with tumor cells positive for Napsin-A and TTF-1, and weakly positive for p63. Special staining for mucin is negative, and the visceral pleura is uninvolved. Molecular analysis reveals no detectable mutations in KRAS or EGFR genes.

The pathological findings reveal a moderately differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma of the breast. The tumor displays characteristics consistent with an M-8500/3 classification and a grade of G2. It measures 2.2 cm in diameter and extends cranially, coming within 5 mm of the resection margin at both the 3 and 12 o'clock positions. A follow-up resection confirmed that the sentinel lymph nodes were tumor-free, with no lymph node involvement. The tumor is classified as pT2, pN0 with no evidence of distant metastasis (MX). The surgical resection margins are clear of tumor involvement (R0). These findings collectively support the diagnosis and provide crucial information for the continued management and treatment plan.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of invasive high-grade urothelial carcinoma of the urinary bladder, with the tumor invading the detrusor muscle and showing lymphovascular invasion. The tumor, located at the posterior inferior bladder wall, also extends into the prostate. All resection margins are free of tumor, though the tumor remains within close proximity to the deepest resection margin. The pelvic lymph node dissection shows metastatic carcinoma in two out of five left pelvic lymph nodes, whereas no tumor is present in the six right pelvic lymph nodes examined. Additionally, the prostate exhibits microscopic foci of prostatic adenocarcinoma with a low Gleason score (2+3=5), comprising a minimal percentage of the prostate volume and showing no extraprostatic extension or lymphovascular invasion. Benign stromal hypertrophy and high-grade prostatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PIN) are also noted. The pathological staging (AJCC 7th edition) is pT4 N2 for the bladder carcinoma and pT2a N0 for the prostatic adenocarcinoma.

The pathologic findings indicate a diagnosis of poorly differentiated non-small cell carcinoma with squamous features located in the right upper lobe following lobectomy. The primary tumor is classified as pT2, and all 13 regional lymph nodes examined were negative for metastatic involvement (pN0). The resection margins were free of invasive carcinoma, suggesting complete removal of the tumor. No evidence of distant metastasis was observed; however, distant metastatic status remains indeterminate (pMx). Additionally, there was no evidence of venous or arterial large vessel invasion, and lymphatic invasion could not be conclusively determined. Multiple excised lymph nodes, including those from regions R10, R4, high R4, R11, and station 7, were all negative for tumor involvement, further supporting the absence of regional spread. The tumor was noted to be adjacent to the pleura but did not involve the excision margins significantly, confirming successful surgical excision with clear margins.

The pathological examination revealed a diagnosis of poorly differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma in the left breast, classified as NOS, G III, pT2N1a. The tumor exhibits an aggressive nature with notable poor differentiation. Microscopic examination confirms the presence of malignant cells that do not closely resemble normal ductal cells, indicating high-grade malignancy. Margins of the excised tissue are negative for malignant cells, suggesting that the tumor has been completely excised. Lymph node evaluation shows involvement of a single lymph node, indicating some degree of regional spread. No vascular or perineural invasion is detected. These findings collectively suggest a high-grade invasive ductal carcinoma with effective excision but with regional lymph node involvement, necessitating further clinical management.

The essential pathological findings pertain to a diagnosis of low-grade oligodendroglioma, grade II. The tumor exhibits a variably cellular glioma that diffusely infiltrates both the gray and white matter, with distinct perineural satellitosis, perivascular satellitosis, and subpial condensation in the gray matter. The tumor cells are characterized by round nuclei with mild to moderate atypia and common perinuclear halos. Importantly, there are no indications of microvascular proliferation or tumor necrosis. The degree of cellularity varies, with the more densely cellular areas showing occasional scattered mitoses, with a maximum of 4 mitoses per 10 high power fields, although most regions demonstrate fewer. Scattered MIB-1 reactive cells are present throughout, and the highest proliferative region has a MIB-1 labeling index of 4.4%, which suggests evidence against anaplastic progression but indicates a necessity for close monitoring due to the mildly elevated mitotic count. No information regarding margin status or lymph node involvement is provided, focusing the findings strictly on the tumor's diffuse infiltration and cellular characteristics.

The pathological findings highlight a diagnosis of invasive moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma in the right upper lobe of the lung, with no evidence of pleural or lymph-vascular invasion and clear surgical margins. The tumor is classified as pT2aN0M0, corresponding to stage IB. Additionally, small lymphocytic lymphoma is identified in multiple lymph nodes across various regions (stations 4R and level 7) and within the lung parenchyma, but there is no involvement by metastatic carcinoma. The total number of lymph nodes assessed is 11, with no metastatic carcinoma detected. The pathological staging does not suggest any distant metastasis (pM0). Tissue from the lung sample is stored for possible future ancillary testing, however, it does not meet the criteria for reflex EGFR and ALK testing, and no stage IV disease is pathologically present. The patient's history includes lung cancer and B-cell chronic lymphocytic leukemia.

The pathology reveals a diagnosis of invasive moderately differentiated squamous carcinoma affecting the tongue, right retromolar trigone, hypopharynx, uvula, and soft palate, with evidence of focal perineural invasion. The deep margin of resection is free of tumor involvement. The right extended modified radical neck dissection identified metastatic moderately differentiated squamous carcinoma involving a matted mass of subdigastric and upper posterior cervical lymph nodes, with extracapsular extension noted. A total of thirty-nine cervical lymph nodes were examined, all of which were free of tumor. The lateral nasopharyngeal margin, buccal mucosa, retromolar trigone, lingual, tongue, floor of mouth, valleculla margin, base of tongue, left soft palate, nasopharyngeal roof margin, and right hard palate margin all showed squamous and/or respiratory mucosa without the presence of tumor. Overall, the pathological assessment indicates significant local invasion and lymph node metastasis but no residual tumor at the examined mucosal margins.

The pathological findings reveal an invasive moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon. The tumor exhibits ulceration with raised borders and grossly invades through the muscularis propria to the junction of the muscularis propria and serosa, but it does not penetrate the serosa. Importantly, the resection margins, including the proximal, distal, and circumferential (radial) margins, are free of tumor involvement, indicating negative margin status. Examination of eleven mesenteric lymph nodes shows no evidence of metastatic carcinoma. There is no presence of non-lymph node pericolonic tumor. While the potential for distant metastasis cannot be definitively assessed from this specimen, the absence of vascular invasion is noted. Overall, these findings collectively contribute to the staging and prognosis of the adenocarcinoma, highlighting its local extension without regional lymph node metastasis.

The pathology findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma in the right breast, characterized by a high histologic and cytologic grade (NSABP Histologic Grade III, Cytologic Grade III). The tumor, which is described as firm and hemorrhagic, is situated in the mid-upper outer quadrant of the breast and measures 5 cm at its greatest dimension. It is located 2 cm from the deep surgical margin and 1 cm from the skin ellipse. Examination of 27 lymph nodes revealed no evidence of malignancy, indicating a lack of lymph node involvement. Additional benign proliferative changes, such as blunt duct adenosis, intraductal hyperplasia, and fibrosis, were observed in the surrounding breast tissue. The operative margins are confirmed to be free of malignancy, with no identifiable intraductal carcinoma present.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma in the right breast, characterized by a histologic grade of 3, encompassing high scores for tubule formation, nuclear pleomorphism, and mitotic count. The tumor is unifocal with a substantial ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) component, exhibiting diverse architectural patterns and high nuclear grade, as well as associated necrosis. Notably, the invasive and in situ components are widely negative for margin involvement. There is no lymphovascular invasion observed. Microscopic examination of the right axillary sentinel lymph nodes revealed one node with an isolated tumor cell cluster without extracapsular extension, while the remaining nodes did not show metastatic carcinoma. For the left breast, a diagnosis of lobular carcinoma in situ was established without evidence of invasive carcinoma. Immunohistochemical studies for hormone receptors in the right breast were negative for estrogen, progesterone receptors, and HER2/neu overexpression. The pathologic staging of the right breast carcinoma is pT2 pN0(i+) pMx, pending further clinical review. Additional findings in both breasts include biopsy site changes, proliferative fibrocystic changes, and duct ectasia.

The pathological examination of the right frontal lobe lesion revealed a diagnosis of oligodendroglioma, classified as WHO Grade II/III. The neoplasm is characterized by a proliferation of oligodendrocytes, lacking significant nuclear anaplasia, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis. MIB-1 proliferation index was noted to be between 2% and 4%, indicating relatively low proliferative activity concentrated within certain areas. The tumor sections also displayed zones of white matter rarefaction and gliosis, as well as focal proliferation centers. Importantly, molecular testing identified the presence of MGMT promoter methylation and allelic loss on chromosome arms 1p and 19q, consistent with the diagnosis of oligodendroglioma. No lymph nodes were involved, typified by the intracranial location of the tumor. Immunohistochemical staining demonstrated sparse or absent gliofibrillarity in neoplastic cells, and there was no overexpression of p53 protein. Special staining did not reveal the presence of granular bodies. These findings affirm the diagnosis of a WHO Grade II/III oligodendroglioma with 1p/19q co-deletion and MGMT promoter methylation, providing crucial information for clinical management and prognosis.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive, poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma of the colon, characterized by the penetration of all parietal layers and evidence of vascular infiltration. The tumor is classified as poorly differentiated (G3) and is staged as pT3, signifying substantial local advancement. Examination of the surgical margins reveals no additional noteworthy findings. Notably, there is significant lymph node involvement with metastases identified in six out of fourteen lymph nodes examined, corresponding to a nodal stage of pN2. Additionally, both lymphatic and vascular invasion are present, underscoring the aggressive nature of the disease. These features collectively suggest a high-grade, advanced tumor with significant local and regional spread.

The pathological examination reveals a high-grade urothelial carcinoma of the urinary bladder with extensive squamous differentiation. The tumor displays significant involvement, extending into the left intramural ureter and demonstrating transmural invasion into the anterior cervical wall and vagina. There is a prominent presence of perineural invasion; however, no lymphovascular invasion is unequivocally identified. The margins of resection, including the soft tissue, urethra, and both ureters, are negative for tumor involvement. Examination of the uterus reveals atrophic endometrium and a leiomyoma, with no tumor present. Both fallopian tubes and bilateral ovaries show no significant pathology. Lymph node dissection, encompassing twenty-seven nodes from various pelvic and para-aortic locations, shows no metastatic involvement (0/27). Pathological staging classifies the tumor as pT4a, N0, MX under the 7th edition TNM system. The invasive component is entirely keratinizing squamous carcinoma, while the in-situ component shows both urothelial and squamous differentiation. This presentation confirms the diagnosis of urothelial carcinoma with extensive squamous differentiation.

The pathological evaluation of multiple biopsy samples and surgical specimens reveals a high-grade invasive urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma of the bladder, with tumor infiltration into the muscularis propria and focal carcinoma in situ also identified. Notably, there is no evidence of carcinoma in tissues from the right and left ureteral biopsies, uterus, cervix, endometrium, myometrium, serosa, bilateral ovaries, or fallopian tubes. The evaluation of the pericystic lymph nodes shows no carcinoma. However, metastatic urothelial carcinoma is present in two out of eight right pelvic lymph nodes, while six left pelvic lymph nodes are free of carcinoma involvement. The surgical margins of the bladder, including the urethral and perivesical margins, as well as the margins of the right and left ureters, are all free of tumor. There is no evidence of lymphatic or vascular invasion, nor is there tumor extension into adjacent organs. The tumor is classified as stage pT2bN2Mx according to the AJCC/UICC staging system, with no additional relevant pathological abnormalities in the bladder.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of multicentric, poorly differentiated, invasive ductal breast carcinoma located in the right-sided ablated breast tissue. This carcinoma features at least four distinct foci and includes associated high-grade intraductal carcinoma with comedo necrosis at the tumor periphery. Surrounding the carcinoma is a mastopathy characterized by partially sclerotic, intraductal, papillomatous scleradenosis, microcalcifications, cystic mastopathy, and cylindrical epithelial metaplasia. The overlying skin and nipple are free of the tumor, with no evidence of pagetoid spread. Notably, the outer resection margins are clear of tumor involvement. Tumor classification is designated as M-8500/3, G 3, pT2 (m), pN1a, pMX, stage II B, and R0, indicating a poorly differentiated, multicentric invasive carcinoma with 2 out of 25 lymph nodes involved, no distant metastasis evident at this stage, and clear resection margins.

The pathology findings reveal a high-grade, non-papillary urothelial carcinoma of the bladder with focal squamous differentiation, which invades the bladder wall transmurally and extends into the perivesical fat. The serosal surface is free of tumor, and no lymphovascular invasion is identified. The resection margins are also free of tumor. In the prostate, the urothelial carcinoma extends to the periurethral prostatic ducts and exhibits superficial invasion of the periductal stroma. The prostatic tissue shows glandular atrophy and peri-basilar cell hyperplasia with atypia, consistent with treatment effect, and is characterized by chronic and focal acute inflammation. There is no residual prostatic carcinoma identified. Importantly, the lymph node examination, including left and right external iliac, obturator, and common iliac lymph nodes, shows no evidence of tumoral involvement. Additionally, the final margins of both the left and right ureters display unremarkable urothelium with no tumor presence. These findings collectively suggest a comprehensive evaluation of the resected tissues, with key insights into the extent and margins of the urothelial carcinoma and associated pathological changes.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma (NOS type) in the left breast, with associated microcalcifications. The tumor is classified as Nottingham Grade 1, based on scores for tubule formation, nuclear pleomorphism, and mitotic activity. The invasive carcinoma is accompanied by ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), nuclear grade 2, cribriform type, which constitutes 10% of the total tumor mass. Additionally, the presence of lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS) and atypical lobular hyperplasia is noted. There is no evidence of lymphovascular space invasion. The resection margins are clear of both invasive carcinoma and DCIS, with the closest margin being 0.7 cm from the invasive component. Immunohistochemical profiling shows that the invasive tumor cells are positive for estrogen and progesterone receptors and negative for HER-2/neu. Examination of four sentinel lymph nodes from the left axilla reveals no metastatic carcinoma. The non-neoplastic breast tissue exhibits fibrocystic changes, ductal epithelial hyperplasia, columnar cell change, apocrine metaplasia, and sclerosing adenosis with microcalcifications. Pathologic staging classifies the tumor as pT2, with no nodal involvement (pN0).

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma in the right upper lobe of the lung, characterized by a central, well-limited neoplasm that infiltrates the visceral pleura. The tumor exhibits features of a moderately differentiated epidermoid carcinoma with a tendency to keratinization, displaying solid nests of large, pleomorphic cells with frequent nuclear atypias, atypical mitoses, and areas of necrosis. The bronchial recupa and hilar lymph nodes are free from tumor infiltration, and no metastases are present in the examined lymph nodes. Additionally, the parietal pleura shows collagenization without tumoral infiltration, and there is no involvement of osseous tissue. However, there is evidence of fibro-muscular-adipose connective tissue invasion by the carcinoma. Margins and non-tumoral sections of the specimen indicate the absence of residual tumor, corroborating complete excision of the primary neoplasm.

The patient was diagnosed with an invasive moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma in the right lower lobe of the lung, accompanied by angiolymphatic invasion but with no evidence of pleural invasion. Histological examination confirmed that all surgical resection margins were free of tumor cells, ensuring a negative margin status. The lymph nodes biopsied, including those from levels 3, 4, 7, the paratracheal region, station 9, and the azygos region, were all benign with no instances of metastatic squamous cell carcinoma detected. The pathological stage of the tumor is classified as T1N0MX, indicating a small tumor size without regional lymph node metastasis and undetermined distant metastasis. Additionally, the patient had underlying chronic respiratory conditions such as emphysema, bronchiectasis, tumorlets, smokers bronchiolitis, asthma, and parenchymal scarring, which may have contributed to the clinical presentation.

The pathological evaluation reveals a high-grade urothelial carcinoma with invasive characteristics involving extravesicular fat. The tumor is localized to the left lateral and posterior walls of the bladder, with negative margins at the ureteral and distal urethral sites, indicating no residual tumor at these critical junctions. However, metastasis was identified in five out of twenty-nine lymph nodes, with extranodal extension noted in subsets of both left and right pelvic lymph nodes. Additionally, the examination of the bladder and surrounding structures post-therapy shows significant ulceration and granulomatous inflammation, suggesting a pronounced therapeutic effect. Coexistent adenocarcinoma of the prostate was detected, graded as 6 (3+3) on the Gleason scale, confined within the prostate without perineural invasion, and displaying negative margins at all examined points. There was multifocal high-grade prostatic intraepithelial neoplasia within the peripheral zone. The radical lymph node dissection for prostate carcinoma showed no nodal involvement. The pTNM staging for urothelial carcinoma is pT3a, N2, MX and that for prostate adenocarcinoma is pT2a, NO, MX, reflecting a locally advanced disease for the former with significant nodal spread, while the latter remains confined. This comprehensive evaluation underscores a multifaceted malignancy scenario requiring a multidisciplinary therapeutic approach.

The pathological examination reveals a well-differentiated squamous carcinoma originating in the buccal mucosa and invading the submandibular gland and associated soft tissues. Margins of resection, including the medial, distal, lateral, and proximal mucosal margins as well as the pterygoid, myohyoid, masseter muscles, buccal fat, and soft tissue from the inferior alveolar canal, are free of tumor involvement. Notably, no vascular invasion is present. Among twenty cervical lymph nodes examined, one upper posterior cervical node exhibits metastatic squamous carcinoma without extranodal extension. Examination of the left clavicle and mandibular bone indicates no tumor presence in the initial sections; however, further analysis is pending. Pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia is observed at the distal and proximal mucosal margins, though no tumor is detected. The included teeth show no abnormalities upon gross examination. An addendum report will follow pending the decalcification and further sectioning of bone specimens.

The pathological examination of the surgical resection of the floor of the mouth, partial glossectomy, and mandibular resection revealed an infiltrating keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma. The tumor grossly extends into the submucosal tissue but spares the adjacent tonsillar tissue and mandibular bone. Importantly, all surgical margins are free of the tumor, and there is no evidence of lymphovascular or perineural invasion. The right neck dissection, which included multiple lymph node levels (1A, 1B, 2, and 3), did not reveal any metastatic involvement in the lymph nodes, all of which were negative for malignancy. Additionally, a biopsy from a ventral tongue lesion demonstrated a superficially invasive keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma with clear surgical margins. A p16 immunostain will be performed to provide further diagnostic detail, and the case aligns with the pathological AJCC classification of pT2, pN0.

The essential pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of metastatic adenocarcinoma with a mixed histologic subtype, primarily demonstrating papillary, acinar, lepidic, and micropapillary patterns in the lung tissue. The primary tumor involving the right upper and middle lobes has invaded the visceral pleura but does not involve the bronchial and vascular margins. Lymphovascular invasion is noted, with metastasis confirmed in seven out of twenty-one examined lymph nodes including stations 7, 10, 12, and 4 on the right side, some exhibiting extracapsular extension. The tumor's EGFR testing revealed a positive mutation E746_A750del5 in Exon 19, which correlates with potential responsiveness to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapies. Additional findings include respiratory bronchiolitis and emphysema.
