Overall, the tumor is staged as pT2a pN2 pM1a, denoting a primary tumor size within specified limits, regional lymph node involvement, and the presence of pleural nodules or malignant effusion indicative of distant metastasis.

The pathological examination revealed a diagnosis of moderately differentiated infiltrating ductal carcinoma in the right breast, specifically located in the upper inner quadrant. The tumor exhibits nuclear grade III/III with a high mitotic index, predominantly invasive, with minor foci of ductal carcinoma in situ. The invasive carcinoma measures 2.6 cm and extends to the superficial margin, while the medial and deep margins are free of tumor by 5 mm and 1 cm, respectively. Accompanying fibrocystic changes with intraductal epithelial hyperplasia are noted in the non-tumor breast tissue. In the left breast, the findings include diffuse fibrocystic changes, adenosis, and non-atypical intraductal epithelial hyperplasia, with no evidence of malignancy. Examination of the right axillary sentinel lymph nodes shows one reactive lymph node with no metastatic disease (negative cytokeratin staining), and a second node with an isolated tumor cell cluster in a capsular lymphatic space (0.1 mm), visible on H&E staining and confirmed by positive cytokeratin staining, suggesting minimal lymphatic involvement.

The pathology findings indicate a final diagnosis of invasive, well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma, specifically associated with the left composite resection specimen. The tumor demonstrated invasion up to the mandibular bone, but importantly, the surgical margins were negative for malignancy. Examination of numerous lymph nodes from the left neck dissections at levels 1, 2, and 3, as well as from the composite resection, showed no evidence of tumor involvement. Additionally, extensive sampling of the mouth, tongue, buccal mucosa, alveolar ridge, and retromolar trigone all revealed no malignancy. Osteonecrosis was noted in the affected mandibular tissues. All other mandible sections, including proximal and distal parts, showed no evidence of malignancy. Overall, these findings confirm a localized carcinoma with no regional lymph node metastasis and clear resection margins, providing a comprehensive overview of the pathological status.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of low-grade oligoastrocytoma, predominantly oligodendroglioma in nature. The tumor is characterized by a mildly hypercellular glial neoplasm with round to oval nuclei and scant glial processes, but it lacks classic oligodendroglioma differentiation. Scattered moderately to markedly atypical cells are present, however, there are no mitotic figures, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis observed. The immunohistochemical study shows a variable MIB-1 labeling index with the non-enhancing portion displaying minimal proliferation and the enhancing portion showing moderate proliferation. Examination of the surgical margins—posterior, deep, anterior, superior, and inferior—reveals gray matter devoid of hypercellularity or atypical cells, indicating no neoplastic infiltration. Additionally, involved sections of gray and white matter confirm the presence of the neoplasm without significant proliferative activity or signs of high-grade transformation, excluding the classification as Grade III/anaplastic. No lymph node involvement is discussed in the evaluation.

The pathological findings indicate a moderately differentiated mucinous adenocarcinoma in the left upper lobe of the lung, characterized by foci of necrosis and extension to the pleura. The bronchial and vascular margins are free of tumor involvement, suggesting no residual disease at the resection margins. Lymph node analysis reveals metastatic carcinoma in three of the evaluated nodes, including those from the R10 and AP window regions. Additionally, two of the four regional lymph nodes are positive for metastatic disease. The surrounding lung tissue exhibits hemorrhage, extensive remodeling, subpleural scarring, and both acute and chronic inflammation, which may suggest underlying chronic lung pathology or a response to the tumor.

The pathological findings identify a high-grade invasive urothelial carcinoma originating in the urinary bladder, with significant invasion through the muscularis propria and into the perivesical tissue, displaying frequent lymphovascular space invasion. The tumor has metastasized to multiple lymph nodes, with all examined lymph nodes (11/11) testing positive for metastatic urothelial carcinoma, exhibiting extranodal extension. The cancer involved the left pelvic lymph node and all four right pelvic lymph nodes examined, each displaying significant soft tissue invasion. Two pelvic lymph nodes presented with extensive tumorous growth, indicating aggressive metastatic behavior. Both the distal ureteral and soft tissue margins, as well as the serosa, were negative for tumor involvement. The left ovary showed stromal hyperplasia, while the right ovarian specimen lacked identifiable ovarian tissue and the associated fallopian tube was free of tumor. Moreover, microscopic metastatic foci of urothelial carcinoma were identified within lymphatic channels in a peritoneal nodule, alongside benign ovarian epithelial cysts. The overall pathologic stage, according to the TNM classification, is pT3b, N2, M1, denoting advanced disease with regional and distant spread.

The histopathological examination reveals a diagnosis of invasive lobular carcinoma (classical type, partly solid) of the left breast, classified as NHG2. The tumor is multifocal, located centrally within the breast tissue, and is noted to infiltrate the nipple with associated in situ lesions exhibiting pagetoid spread and hyperplasia within areas of adenosis sclerosans. The glandular tissue also displayed fibrocystic changes and sclerosing adenosis. Surgical margins show the tumor to be 0.3 cm from the base and 1.4 cm from the skin. Axillary lymph node assessment reveals metastatic carcinoma in 9 of 11 nodes examined. Immunohistochemical analysis indicates that over 75% of the neoplastic cells possess estrogen and progesterone receptors, with HER2 protein expression scored at 2+; however, further FISH analysis did not confirm HER2 gene amplification. These findings suggest an invasive lobular carcinoma with metastatic spread to axillary lymph nodes, requiring further clinical management tailored to these specific pathological characteristics.

The patient was diagnosed with invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma of the right upper lobe of the lung. The primary tumor exhibited a mixed histologic subtype with acinar (40%), micropapillary (30%), and papillary (30%) components and was classified as moderately differentiated (G2). The greatest dimension of the tumor was noted to be 2.5 cm, and the tumor was free of lymph-vascular invasion. The stapled surgical margin from the wedge resection as well as the bronchial and vascular margins from the completion lobectomy were clear of tumor. Lymph node biopsies from levels 4, 7, and 10 on the right side, as well as nodes examined from the completion lobectomy, showed no evidence of carcinoma; thus, the regional lymph nodes were negative for metastatic disease (pNO). The pathological staging assigned was pT1b, indicating a primary tumor without regional lymph node involvement or distant metastasis. Additional findings included tumor-associated atelectasis extending to the hilar region, squamous metaplasia, organizing pneumonia, and emphysema. Immunohistochemical staining confirmed the adenocarcinoma profile, and molecular analysis revealed a KRAS mutation (p.G12C), while EGFR mutation analysis was negative. The presence of a smoking-related KRAS mutation is noted, which has implications for targeted therapy.

The pathology findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive squamous cell carcinoma, grade 2, originating from the floor of the mouth and extending to the left jaw. The tumor is characterized by the presence of ulceration and notable infiltration into the soft tissues, reaching the muscular layer and involving the osseous tissue. Importantly, there is capsular involvement by metastatic carcinoma observed in four out of ten lymph nodes at Level 2 on the right side, although no metastatic involvement is noted in the left side lymph nodes across Levels 1, 2, and 3. The carcinoma does not exhibit lymphatic, vascular, or perineural invasion. Surgical margin analysis reveals involvement of the posterior margin of the jaw by the carcinoma, while all other examined surgical margins are free from malignancy.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of high-grade urothelial carcinoma of the bladder with extensive squamous differentiation, constituting over 50% of the tumor. The neoplasm shows invasion into the muscularis propria and is positive for lymphovascular invasion, indicating a more aggressive disease. The margins of resection were not mentioned, which leaves the status of completeness of excision unclear. In the prostatic urethra, no evidence of malignancy is observed; however, there is chronic inflammation and reactive changes. Additionally, benign prostatic glands and fibromuscular stroma are present. Lymph node involvement is not addressed in the findings, which may require further investigation for comprehensive staging of the disease. Overall, these findings suggest a high-grade bladder carcinoma with significant invasive and squamous features, alongside benign prostatic changes.

The pathological examination revealed a diagnosis of moderately differentiated invasive squamous cell carcinoma involving the mandibular bone, floor of the mouth, ventral tongue, lower lip, and mental skin, with metastasis to regional lymph nodes. The primary tumor exhibited bone invasion and was found to be ulcerated, extending to multiple adjacent structures. No carcinoma was detected at the surgical margins, indicating complete resection of the primary tumor. Perineural and lymph-vascular invasion were present. Metastatic carcinoma was identified in two lymph nodes, one with extranodal extension. A significant proportion of the examined lymph nodes, however, were benign and free of carcinoma. Additionally, the presence of abundant filamentous organisms consistent with Actinomyces was associated with osteonecrosis in the mandibular bone. Analysis of the inferior alveolar nerves on both sides showed no evidence of carcinoma involvement. Comprehensive dissection of the left and right neck regions revealed metastatic involvement confined to only a few nodes, supporting the staging of pN2b for regional lymph nodes. The pathological findings collectively describe an advanced local disease with regional lymph node metastasis but complete surgical excision as evidenced by negative margins.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive, predominantly nonkeratinizing, poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the right tongue base. The tumor is positive for p16, suggesting a potential association with human papillomavirus (HPV) infection. Tumor infiltration extends to a certain depth, demonstrating significant invasive characteristics. The surgical margin status is not specified, indicating the need for further evaluation to confirm whether the excision was complete. The examination of a lymph node from the right neck at level II reveals skeletal muscle with signs of scar formation, acute and chronic inflammation, and a foreign body giant cell reaction, but no evidence of neoplastic involvement. Confirmatory deeper levels and cytokeratin immunohistochemical staining support the absence of metastatic disease in the lymph node. Overall, despite the absence of tumor in the lymph node, the invasive nature and poor differentiation of the primary tongue carcinoma warrant close clinical follow-up and consideration of adjunctive therapies.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma in the posterior segment of the right lung, identified within a subpleural mass. The tumor demonstrates a bronchoalveolar-type growth pattern and is confirmed as a primary lung neoplasm through strong TTF1 positivity. The resection margins and pleural surfaces are negative for residual tumor, and there is no evidence of vascular or lymphatic invasion. The subsequent lobectomy of the right upper lobe indicates no residual tumor presence, with the lung parenchyma showing mild to moderate emphysematous changes and a nonspecific focus of subpleural scarring. All examined lymph nodes, spanning levels 10, 11, 2R, 4R, and 7, as well as hilar nodes, are negative for metastatic disease, categorizing the nodal status as pN0. There is no distant metastasis observed, though pM status cannot be definitively assessed. The findings are consistent across multiple sections, with histologic examinations confirming the absence of tumor invasion in vascular and bronchial structures, as well as the noted lymph nodes.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated grade III squamous cell carcinoma located in the right middle lobe of the lung. The tumor tissue exhibits characteristics typical of squamous cell carcinoma, including malignant cells with moderately eosinophilic cytoplasm, enlarged and variably shaped nuclei with coarse clumped chromatin, and prominent nucleoli. Additionally, central necrosis of tumor cells and frequent mitotic figures are observed. The carcinoma invades surrounding benign bronchus tissue and is predominantly infiltrated by lymphocytes and fibrous tissue. Despite the aggressive nature of the tumor, both examined lymph nodes (subcarinal and segmental) do not show evidence of metastatic involvement. There is evidence of lymphatic invasion. Importantly, the surgical margins are uninvolved, indicating that the excised tissue borders are free of tumor cells.

The pathological examination of the left modified radical mastectomy specimen reveals a diagnosis of multifocal invasive ductal carcinoma, SBR Grade 3, with the largest tumor focus measuring 6.8 cm. This high-grade malignancy displays extensive necrosis and involves the lateral half of the breast, extending into the superficial dermis. Surgical resection margins are negative for carcinoma, although the tumor is notably close to the deep margin. Lymphovascular and perineural invasions are present. Examination of the left axillary contents (Levels 1 and 2) indicates metastatic carcinoma in two of the three sampled lymph nodes, with extranodal extension, and the largest metastatic focus measuring 2.8 cm. Additional evaluation of resected lymph nodes reveals metastatic involvement in nine out of twelve nodes, also with extranodal extension, with the largest metastatic deposit measuring 3.5 cm. Immunohistochemical staining shows negative results for estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PR), and HER2/neu (negative by FISH). The pathologic stage is classified as pT3 N3a. The non-neoplastic areas of the breast tissue contain fibroadenoma, and there is no evidence of ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS).

The pathological findings pertain to a left parietal brain tumor diagnosed as an oligoastrocytoma, classified as WHO Grade II. The tumor is a well-differentiated glial neoplasm, displaying a mixed population of hyperchromatic and angulated astrocytes along with cells exhibiting smaller, round nuclei with perinuclear clearing, consistent with an oligodendroglial origin. No evidence of mitotic activity, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis was observed. The Ki-67 (MIB-1) labeling index, an indicator of proliferative activity, was notably low at 1-2%, suggesting limited proliferative potential. The tumor's resection margins and lymph node involvement were not evaluated in this summary, emphasizing the primary diagnosis and tissue characteristics.

The pathological evaluation reveals an anaplastic astrocytoma, WHO grade III, originating from the right parietal region. Microscopic examination shows moderately pleomorphic neoplastic astrocytes infiltrating both grey and white matter of the brain. Mitotic figures are present among the tumor cells, indicating a higher proliferative activity. Immunohistochemically, the tumor cells are positive for GFAP, while p53 is negative. Ki67 staining reveals approximately 5% positivity in the tumor cell nuclei, indicative of the proliferative index. No endothelial proliferation or necrosis is observed within the tumor. Congo red stain is negative, and PAS staining highlights blood vessels in the specimen. The margins of resection and lymph node involvement are not discussed, suggesting they were either not evaluated or not relevant for this case. Overall, the findings support a diagnosis of high-grade glioma, specifically an anaplastic astrocytoma.

The pathological findings are consistent with a diagnosis of low grade oligodendroglioma, grade II. Histologically, the tumor is characterized by a mildly to moderately hypercellular infiltrating glial neoplasm composed of neoplastic cells with round, enlarged nuclei and prominent perinuclear halos. Notable features include areas with numerous gemistocytic cells exhibiting eosinophilic cytoplasm and eccentrically placed nuclei, as well as a myxoid to macrocystic background interspersed with thin, delicate branching capillaries. Importantly, there is an absence of both microvascular proliferation and necrosis. A rare mitotic figure is observed within the specimen. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not mentioned, suggesting either they were not evaluated or were not relevant to this specific pathology report. Other significant pathological findings include the lack of high-grade features, confirming the diagnosis of a low grade neoplasm.

The pathological findings reveal an invasive poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma of the right upper lung lobe, extending into the soft tissues of the chest wall. The tumor is characterized as a poorly differentiated non-small cell carcinoma with positive immunohistochemical staining for CEA and TTF-1, confirming its adenocarcinoma nature. The resection margins, including radial, vascular, and bronchial, are free of tumor involvement. Notably, venous invasion is present, though there is no evidence of arterial or lymphatic invasion. Examination of four hilar lymph nodes, as well as other regional lymph nodes (levels IX, X, XI, and others), shows no metastatic carcinoma (pNO). Additionally, all these lymph nodes exhibit nonnecrotizing granulomatous lymphadenitis with special stains being negative for acid-fast and fungal organisms. The ribs at the analyzed margins are negative for malignancy, and the tumor does not metastasize to distant sites (pMx remains unspecified). Other findings include apical emphysematous changes and benign pleural plaques.

The diagnostic findings reveal a bronchopulmonary large-cell polymorphocellular adenocarcinoma of the left lung, characterized by mucin inclusions, and containing giant-cell components. The tumor demonstrates both central and peripheral involvement, specifically infiltrating the upper and lower lobes and inducing obliteration and stenosis of central bronchus segments, with continuous infiltration into parabronchial lymph nodes. The primary tumor is classified as pT4 pN2 pMX V1 R0 at stage IIIB, signifying extensive local growth and regional lymph node involvement, without distant metastasis and with the presence of vascular invasion. Despite the extensive disease, the peripheral manifestation in segment S6 leaves the visceral pleura unaffected. Additionally, metastasis is noted in one of the lymph nodes, but the resection margins of the bronchus, vessels, and the hilar surface, along with lymph nodes from samples 2 and 4 to 6, are free of tumor involvement. The free resection margins indicate a complete surgical removal of the visible tumor.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of glioblastoma (WHO grade IV), characterized by an astrocytic neoplastic proliferation with nuclear anaplasia, significant mitotic activity, a high proliferation index of 33%, and pronounced microvascular proliferation and necrosis. The mass, centrally necrotic and heterogeneously enhancing, involves the right frontal lobe, adjacent to the convexity dura, and extends to the ventricle. Margins are not explicitly stated, but the presence of microscopic neoplastic infiltration within the leptomeningeal space suggests possible spread. Immunohistochemistry confirms glioblastoma with a prominent gliofibrillogenesis by GFAP staining, and approximately 30% of the neoplastic cells overexpressing p53 protein. Lymph node involvement is not discussed, indicating no evidence of extracranial spread. PCR analysis for the EGFR variant III mutation and the methylated MGMT promoter returned negative, indicating the absence of these alterations. This comprehensive assessment underscores the aggressive nature of the glioblastoma, laid out through both macroscopic and microscopic examination points.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of nonsmall cell carcinoma compatible with adenocarcinoma located in the right upper lobe of the lung. The tumor is characterized as poorly differentiated and measures 3.2 cm in its greatest dimension. Notably, there is no evidence of pleural involvement, and the resection margin is negative, suggesting the tumor was completely excised. Examination of lymph nodes at various stations, including subcarinal, right paratracheal, and right upper lobe bronchus, revealed no evidence of malignancy, with a total of eleven lymph nodes assessed and all being negative for metastatic disease. The final pathological staging is pT2aN0M0, corresponding to stage IB, with no regional lymph node metastasis or distant metastasis detected. Ancillary immunohistochemical staining confirmed the diagnosis of pulmonary adenocarcinoma, with TTF-1 showing strong nuclear positivity and p63 being negative. A portion of the tumor has been banked for potential future ancillary studies.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma of the colon, identified in a hemicolectomy specimen. The tumor is characterized by its invasive nature, extending into the pericolonic tissues. The surgical margins are clear of tumor involvement, suggesting complete resection at the studied margins. However, there is significant lymph node involvement, with metastasis evident in multiple regional lymph nodes. Additionally, there is evidence of lymphovascular invasion, further indicating an aggressive tumor behavior. No distant metastasis is noted within the examined tissues. The overall findings point towards an advanced stage of colorectal adenocarcinoma with high-grade histologic features and considerable lymphatic spread.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma with focal intraductal spread. This malignancy is characterized by a high-grade tumor (Grade III) and classified as NOS (Not Otherwise Specified). The primary tumor has been assessed as stage pT2 without any evidence of lymph node involvement (N0). Importantly, the dorsal resection margin is confirmed to be free of tumor involvement, indicating successful clearance in that region. Overall, these findings suggest an aggressive tumor type with no metastatic spread to regional lymph nodes and clear surgical margins on the dorsal side.

The pathological examination confirmed a diagnosis of invasive pleomorphic lobular carcinoma of the left breast, characterized by a mixture of conventional and pleomorphic histologic features, and an overall intermediate grade 2 histology. The primary tumor measured 2.2 cm at its greatest dimension and was oriented closest to the anterior margin at 2 mm, with lobular carcinoma in situ identified within 0.5 mm of the same margin. No ductal carcinoma in situ or vascular invasion was detected. The surgical excision margins were negative. Lymph node evaluation revealed metastatic carcinoma in one of ten examined nodes, specifically the left axillary sentinel lymph node, without evidence of extracapsular extension. The mastectomy specimen from the left nipple was negative for malignancy. Additional lymph node dissection yielded no further metastatic involvement among the nine nodes tested, verified through keratin staining. These findings support a classification of the disease as pT2 pN1a, given the tumor size and single involved node, indicating a localized but nodally metastatic process.

The pathology findings indicate a diagnosis of infiltrating ductal carcinoma, specifically a Nottingham grade III tumor, located in the lower outer quadrant of the right breast. The tumor is well-circumscribed, exhibits extensive necrosis, and has no associated in situ component. Importantly, the surgical resection margins are negative for tumor, with the closest deep margin being free by a small margin. The nipple remains uninvolved, and the adjacent breast tissue exhibits non-proliferative fibrocystic changes. Examination of four right axillary sentinel lymph nodes revealed no evidence of metastatic disease, with nodal staging classified as AJCC pNO(sn). Immunohistochemistry performed on the sentinel lymph nodes confirmed these findings. Additionally, the tumor does not overexpress HER2/neu, scoring 1+ on the HercepTest, and is negative for both estrogen and progesterone receptors, with 0% nuclear staining.

The pathological examination reveals an oligodendroglioma, Grade II (WHO) involving the posterior part of the right frontal brain, anterior portion of the right frontal brain, and the cingulate gyrus of the right frontal lobe. Each lesion exhibits characteristics of a low-grade glioma consistent with oligodendroglioma. Gross examination of the specimens identified infiltrating neoplastic growth in both white and gray matter, submitted in their entirety for histopathological evaluation. Importantly, fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) testing demonstrated a combined loss of chromosomal arms 1p and 19q, consistent with an oligodendroglial lineage. This genetic alteration is significant as it correlates with a potentially more favorable prognosis and responsiveness to adjuvant therapy. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not addressed in the provided sections. These findings are critical for informing the therapeutic approach and prognostic outlook for the patient.

The pathological analysis of tissue from the brain, specifically the hippocampus mass and left temporal lobe, reveals a diagnosis of low-grade diffuse astrocytoma, classified as WHO grade II. Immunohistochemical staining confirms the tumor's glial origin with positive GFAP expression and highlights axonal structures through neurofilament protein staining. The proliferation marker Ki-67 indicates a low proliferative index, suggesting slow tumor growth, and there is no significant mitotic activity observed. IDH1-R132H mutation, which can have prognostic and therapeutic implications, is negative, while p53 shows low to moderate labeling, indicating some degree of tumor suppressor gene alteration. EMA staining is negative, ruling out epithelial differentiation. The lesion displays variable cellularity, but critical features such as a low Ki-67 index and lack of mitotic activity support the classification of this astrocytoma as low grade. Margins and lymph node involvement are not applicable in this intracranial tumor context.

The final pathological diagnosis confirms an invasive keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, located at the right retromolar trigone. The evaluated tumor exhibits an ulcerated gross subtype and histologic grade G2. Surgical margins are free of invasive carcinoma, with a minimal distance from the closest margin. Importantly, both lymph-vascular invasion and perineural invasion are absent. Regional lymph node analysis shows involvement with metastasis in two out of fifteen nodes, specifically within Level 5, accompanied by evidence of extracapsular extension. Pathologic staging indicates a primary tumor classification of pT2 and a regional lymph node classification of pN2b. Other relevant findings include negative malignancy on biopsies from surrounding areas and various margins including the palate, deep pterygoid, retromolar trigone, and different buccal margins. Furthermore, no malignancy was observed in the examined tonsillar tissue, and benign lymph nodes were confirmed in Level 2, Level 3, and Medial Level 5 regions. Additionally, mild chronic inflammation was observed in the medial pharyngeal margin. The findings collectively provide a comprehensive assessment of the malignant state and extent of the disease, aiding in staging and prognostic evaluation.

The essential pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of invasive moderate to poorly differentiated colonic adenocarcinoma located within the sigmoid colon. The tumor has penetrated through the bowel wall into the mesenteric fat, classified as pT3. Examination of the resected specimen demonstrates that the proximal, distal, and circumferential margins are clear of tumor involvement. However, there is the presence of metastatic adenocarcinoma in three out of 41 mesenteric lymph nodes (pN1), including a node at the mesenteric root. Additionally, a soft tissue metastasis is identified within the colonic mesentery, indicating distant metastasis (pM1). An incidental adenomatous polyp was observed, with no signs of malignant invasion. The appendectomy specimen does not show any significant histopathologic abnormalities. Overall, the findings suggest an advanced stage of colonic adenocarcinoma with regional and distant metastatic spread.

The pathological examination of the extended hemicolectomy specimen revealed two invasive, moderately differentiated adenocarcinomas infiltrating the pericolic fatty tissue, alongside evidence of a previously resected adenocarcinoma. Additionally, multiple tubular adenomas with moderate dysplasia were identified. Importantly, the resection margins in the mucosa and the omentum were tumor-free, indicating no residual tumor presence in these areas. The presence of two lymph node metastases out of the 116 examined underscores the metastatic spread. The tumor classification is adenocarcinoma, grade 2 (moderately differentiated), with a pathological stage of pT3 indicating invasion through the muscularis propria into pericolorectal tissues, despite the absence of lymphatic (L0) and vascular (V0) invasion, and confirms a complete resection (R0) with no residual tumor identified.

The pathology analysis revealed that the right paratracheal (4R) and levels 7, 8, and 9 lymph nodes showed no evidence of malignancy. Examination of the right lower lobe wedge resection indicated the presence of a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with bronchioloalveolar features, without visceral pleural involvement or angiolymphatic invasion, and showing mild emphysematous changes. The bronchial margin was free of tumor involvement. Additionally, a separate microscopic focus of adenocarcinoma of the bronchioloalveolar type was identified within the right lower lobe completion lobectomy, again with the bronchial margin and pleural surface clear of tumor and without angiolymphatic invasion. This lobectomy specimen also revealed the presence of mild emphysematous changes and pleural plaque but no further malignancy in the examined lymph nodes. The findings suggest the presence of synchronous primary tumors within the right lower lobe, both being moderately differentiated adenocarcinomas. The pathological staging was determined to be pT1b, N0, Mx based on the examined specimens with no nodal involvement identified in the submitted lymph nodes.

The essential pathological findings reveal a moderately to poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma in the right lower lobe apical segment of the lung, characterized by an extensively necrotic tumor nodule with a homogenous, solid, cream-colored cut surface. The tumor abuts and invades the pleura and demonstrates vascular and lymphatic invasion but lacks perineural invasion. Surgical margins, including the bronchial and inked resection margins, are clear of malignancy. The adjacent lung exhibits mild emphysema with no additional pathological lesions apart from carbon pigmentation. Hilar lymph node analysis shows extensive replacement by metastatic adenocarcinoma that is also focally necrotic. The overall pathological stage is T2 N1, indicating both local and lymph node involvement.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of invasive mucinous adenocarcinoma with poor differentiation in the sigmoid colon. The primary tumor invades through the muscularis propria into the pericolonic fat, consistent with a pT3 classification. Margins are negative for tumor involvement, providing clear resection status. However, metastatic adenocarcinoma is detected in 7 of 20 regional lymph nodes, indicating a pN2 stage, along with extranodal tumor presence. Further examination of the right abdominal wall and omentum shows metastatic mucinous adenocarcinoma, with nodules identified in both locations, marking distant metastasis (pM1). Additional findings include diverticulosis in the resected bowel segment. The overall pathological picture underscores advanced disease with both regional lymph node and distant metastatic involvement.

The pathological evaluation of the bladder tumor reveals an invasive poorly differentiated transitional cell carcinoma. Both submitted specimens, representing the right bladder tumor and its deeper extents, demonstrate the involvement of the muscularis propria, consistent with a histologic grade of III. The tumor does not exhibit lymphovascular invasion, and the presence of muscularis propria allows for the determination of deep muscle involvement. The tissue samples, examined through transurethral resection of bladder tumor (TURBT) procedure, confirm the aggressive nature of the carcinoma with significant infiltration, yet no evidence of spread into the lymphatic or vascular systems. These findings are critical for determining the therapeutic approach and prognosis in the clinical management of bladder cancer.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of malignant melanoma of the skin, characterized by a pigmented, ulcerated lesion. The tumor is situated on the leg and exhibits significant invasion, with a Clark's level of IV and a Breslow tumor thickness of 10 mm. There is evidence of perifocal lymphoid infiltration, suggesting an inflammatory response around the tumor. Venous invasion is absent. The excised specimen's margins are clear, indicating that the lesion was completely excised. No lymph node involvement is noted. These findings collectively indicate a high-risk melanoma due to its ulceration, depth of invasion, and significant tumor thickness without detectable spread to lymph nodes.

The pathological examination of excised liver tissue reveals established fine nodular cirrhosis along with slight fatty degeneration of hepatic cells, suggesting nutritional or ethyl-related toxic damage to the liver parenchyma. In the resected colon, there is a polypoid, moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the colorectal type, infiltrating the tunica submucosa. The margins of the colon resection are tumor-free, and there is no lymph node involvement, with all local lymph nodes being free of tumor. Additionally, the colon specimen includes two tubular adenomas, presenting low-grade and high-grade dysplasia. The overall pathological staging of the tumor is pT1 pN0 (0/22) pMX, grade G2, and there is no evidence of lymphovascular invasion (L0, V0) or residual tumor (R0). These findings collectively indicate a localized early-stage colorectal adenocarcinoma without regional spread, accompanied by chronic liver damage.

The pathological examination of the right upper lobe lobectomy specimen reveals two separate non-contiguous foci of infiltrating moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. The primary tumor measures 3.2 cm, and a smaller nodule, which is surrounded by pneumonia, measures 1.0 cm. Importantly, the tumors do not extend to the overlying pleural surface. The bronchial, vascular, and parenchymal margins are free of tumor involvement. Additionally, all six hilar lymph nodes and lymph nodes from stations R-4 and 7 are negative for metastatic carcinoma, indicating no lymph node involvement. The pathological stage of the tumor is classified as pT2N0Mx, corresponding to Stage I. Associated pathological findings include acute and organizing bronchopneumonia (focal obstructive pneumonitis) in the area surrounding the tumors, as well as emphysematous changes with focal peripheral bulla formation. No other significant lesions are identified within the lung parenchyma, which appears congested and focally cystic in non-affected areas. The findings confirm a diagnosis of non-small cell carcinoma of the lung with no evidence of regional spread or metastasis.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma with focal squamous differentiation in the urinary bladder, identified during a radical cystoprostatectomy. The carcinoma is poorly differentiated and has completely penetrated the muscular wall of the bladder, extending into the paravesical adipose tissue. There is evidence of lymphovascular invasion, and multifocal high-grade urothelial dysplasia/flat carcinoma in situ is present. Surgical margins for the bladder and ureters are uninvolved. Metastatic high-grade urothelial carcinoma is identified in one of two left pelvic lymph nodes, confirming lymphatic spread, with a pathological stage of pT3a, N1. Additionally, the prostate gland harbors adenocarcinoma, accounting for approximately 10% of its volume, with a Gleason score of 6/10, localized to both lobes but without extraprostatic extension, lymphovascular invasion, or perineural invasion. The prostate surgical margins are largely uninvolved except for a single microscopic focus at the prostatic apex. No metastatic adenocarcinoma of the prostate is found in the lymph nodes, designating a pathological stage of pT2c, N0. The findings confirm the presence of metastatic high-grade urothelial carcinoma identical to the bladder primary, without any morphological features resembling prostatic adenocarcinoma. The right and left ureteral margins are negative for malignancy as confirmed by frozen section analysis.

The pathology findings reveal a diagnosis of invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC) of the right breast, graded as SBR Grade 2, with a primary tumor size of 1.5 cm. The specimen exhibits multiple foci of ILC with associated lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS) throughout the submitted sections, confirming multifocal disease. The surgical resection margins are negative for invasive carcinoma; however, LCIS is identified at the medial and lateral margins, and within 1 mm from the inferior margin. An additional excision of the lateral margin contains invasive lobular carcinoma measuring 0.6 cm, with LCIS present within 1 mm of the final lateral margin and invasive tumor 2.5 mm from this margin. No necrosis or vascular/lymphatic invasion is detected. Immunohistochemical analysis indicates the tumor is ER and PR positive, with HER2 negativity confirmed by FISH. All three sentinel lymph nodes from the right axilla are negative for metastatic disease (0/3). The pathological staging is pT1c N0, with no evidence of ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS).

The pathological evaluation of the left frontal brain tumor reveals a diagnosis of oligodendroglioma, classified as WHO grade II. The biopsy and excision specimens display an infiltrating, moderately cellular neoplasm with glial components characterized by round to ovoid nuclei and scant cytoplasm, along with perinuclear clearings. There is evidence of nuclear pleomorphism, mini-gemistocytes, and rare mitotic figures. The immunohistochemical staining shows positive GFAP expression in tumor cells and reactive astrocytes, with a Ki-67 proliferation index ranging from 3-4%, indicating a relatively low proliferative activity. Genetic analysis reveals no loss of chromosomal arms 1p and 19q, with ratios of 1p/1q at 0.81 and 19q/19p at 0.85, suggesting a lack of these common oligodendroglioma genetic alterations. The excised tumor margins are not explicitly mentioned in the summary, and lymph node involvement is not applicable in this central nervous system tumor context. These collective findings are consistent with the histological features of a low-grade oligodendroglioma.

The pathological examination of the right hemicolectomy specimen revealed an invasive adenocarcinoma, moderately differentiated, located in the cecum. The tumor exhibited characteristic invasion into the muscularis propria but did not extend to the inked serosal surface or show macroscopic perforation. All resection margins, including proximal, distal, and mesenteric, were free of tumor involvement. Notably, there was no evidence of lymph-vascular or perineural invasion. The examination of 23 lymph nodes demonstrated no metastatic involvement. Additional findings included a submucosal lipoma and an unremarkable appendix. The tumor did not display any treatment effects, suggesting no prior therapeutic intervention had been administered. Pathologic staging was determined as pT2, pN0, pMx.

The pathological analysis reveals a diagnosis of high-grade urothelial cell carcinoma of the urinary bladder. The tumor is classified as a WHO grade 4 malignancy, notable for its poorly differentiated nature and a high mitotic rate. The tumor invades through the muscularis propria into the perivesical soft tissue, indicating a stage of pT3 N0, without multicentricity, vascular invasion, or perineural invasion. Margins examined, including those of the right and left ureters and the urethra, are negative for malignancy. Adjacent bladder mucosa appears unremarkable. No malignancy is detected in the examined lymph nodes from both left and right pelvic regions. Other associated structures, such as the uterus, bilateral ovaries, uterine tubes, and the vermiform appendix, show no pathological diagnosis except for a benign endocervical polyp in the uterine cervix. Immunohistochemical staining is mostly negative, but the presence of weak MART-1 and equivocal S-100 staining suggests a primary diagnosis of urothelial carcinoma, despite initial considerations of metastatic melanoma.

The pathological findings from a right frontal brain mass biopsy reveal a diagnosis of oligodendroglioma, WHO Grade 2. The tumor is characterized as a low-intermediate grade infiltrating glioma. The histological examination confirms the diagnosis, with a gray-white junction observed in the majority of the specimen, although some areas presented with an obscure differentiation. Real-time PCR analysis indicates a loss at chromosomal arms 1p and 19q, which is consistent with an oligodendroglial lineage and suggests a potentially more favorable response to adjuvant therapy. The specimens examined consisted of pieces of cerebral cortex and underlying white matter, submitted for a combination of histologic examination and frozen section analysis. The detailed examination did not find any focal lesions in the smaller fragments submitted. Margin status and lymph node involvement were not mentioned, indicating the primary focus on the molecular and histological characterization of the tumor.

The histopathological analysis of brain tissue from the left frontal lobe reveals a diagnosis of mixed oligoastrocytoma (diffuse variant), classified as a WHO grade 2 neoplasm. Microscopic examination shows no evidence of mitoses, vascular proliferation, necrosis, or invasion into adjacent structures. The tumor cells exhibit strong positivity for IDH1 and GFAP, with variable staining for Vimentin and S100. The Ki67 proliferation index is low. Margins of resection and overall size of the tumor are not assessable due to the nature of the biopsy. Further genetic testing, specifically for 1p/19q co-deletion, is recommended; however, the current analysis shows no evidence of 1p or 19q loss and no EGFR amplification. These findings suggest a tumor with relatively low proliferative activity and no high-risk genetic alterations normally associated with higher grade gliomas.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma, non-keratinizing type, located in the left lower lobe of the lung. The primary tumor exhibits pleural invasion and lymphatic invasion, with a noted size of 8.5 cm and moderate necrosis. Importantly, one of the two hilar lymph nodes shows metastatic carcinoma, whereas all other examined lymph nodes (including R2, R4, Level 7, and Level 5) are negative for tumor involvement, with a single Level 5 node presenting a calcified granuloma. Bronchial and vascular margins are negative for tumor, suggesting no local spread beyond the primary site. Additionally, non-neoplastic lung tissue displays acute bronchopneumonia. Examination of the excised portion of the 6th rib shows no evidence of tumor. The overall pathological staging is determined to be T2, N1, indicating a tumor size and extent of lymph node involvement consistent with these metrics.

The pathological examination of the specimen localized to the right fronto-temporal region has resulted in a diagnosis of an anaplastic astrocytoma, corresponding to a grade III classification according to the World Health Organization (WHO) criteria. The tumor exhibits high cellularity with significant nuclear atypia, an increased mitotic rate, and evidence of microvascular proliferation, which are characteristic of this grade of malignancy. The resection margins were examined and found to be involved, indicating residual tumor presence. Additionally, no evidence of metastatic involvement in the regional lymph nodes was identified. Further relevant findings include the presence of necrotic areas within the tumor, indicative of aggressive biological behavior. The detailed pathological features support the diagnosis and provide essential information for guiding subsequent therapeutic decisions.

The pathology evaluation reveals a poorly differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma (SBR grade 3) with apocrine features and several foci of microinvasion associated with ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast. The tumor exhibits extensive lymphovascular invasion and measures at least 3 cm in greatest dimension, with the invasive carcinoma located 2 mm from the deep margin. While the overall margins are negative, DCIS is present at the lateral margin and within 1 mm of the anterior/medial and anterior margins, as well as within 2 mm of the deep margin. The axillary dissection uncovered metastatic carcinoma in 2 out of 25 lymph nodes without extracapsular extension, with the largest metastasis measuring 4 cm. Immunohistochemistry results reveal the tumor is estrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR) negative, but HER2 positive with a score of 3+ indicating strong overexpression. The pathological staging corresponds to pT2N1.

The histopathological analysis confirms a diagnosis of malignant melanoma, nodular type, with notable ulceration, and an extensive Breslow thickness. The invasion is characterized by a Clark level V and a high mitotic index, highlighting aggressive tumor behavior. Lymphocytic invasion is minimally present, with observable necrosis within the tumor. The primary tumor borders exhibit tightly packed satellite nodules, and there is evidence of vascular invasion. Surgical margins of the resected lesion are critically close, with both side and base margins being narrowly clear, indicating less than ideal clearance. Lymph node involvement is extensive, categorized as pN3, further underscoring the advanced stage of the melanoma (pT4b). The presence of ulceration, numerous satellite nodules, and the closeness of the margins collectively suggest a challenging prognosis and the need for close clinical follow-up and possibly additional therapeutic interventions.

The final diagnosis confirms right breast cancer as invasive ductal carcinoma of no special type, with two distinct foci identified in the excised tissue. Both tumors are graded as Nottingham Grade 2, indicative of moderate differentiation. One tumor was larger and located at the 4 o' clock position, while the smaller tumor was at the 5 o' clock position. Additionally, there is a presence of ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of nuclear grade 2 with minimal necrosis, comprising a minor portion of the overall tumor mass and situated away from the invasive regions. Lymphovascular space invasion is noted. The surgical margins are clear of carcinoma; however, the invasive carcinoma is quite close to the anterior and posterior margins. Associated findings include atypical ductal hyperplasia, florid ductal epithelial hyperplasia, fibrocystic changes with microcalcifications, and previous biopsy site changes. All examined lymph nodes, including an intramammary lymph node and three sentinel lymph nodes from the right axilla, are negative for metastatic carcinoma. The invasive tumor cells exhibit hormone receptor positivity (estrogen and progesterone) and are HER-2 negative. An additional medial margin excision at the 5 o' clock position reveals no carcinoma, with only benign breast parenchyma and fibrocystic changes observed.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of high-grade (grade 3) urothelial carcinoma that is invasive into the muscularis propria. The tumor also involves the prostatic ducts and acini, although no invasion into the prostatic stroma is detected. Importantly, there is no evidence of lymphovascular invasion. Based on the gross description, multiple fragments of tan-pink to gray-white, partly cauterized, friable soft tissue were received and thoroughly examined. Overall, the findings indicate a significant tumor burden with muscular invasion but no further spread into prostatic stroma or vascular structures.

The pathological examination of the right frontal mass biopsy revealed an anaplastic oligodendroglioma, classified as WHO grade III. Notably, the tumor demonstrates clinical recurrence and progression from a previously diagnosed WHO grade II glioma with 1p/19q codeletion. Immunohistochemical staining for IDH1 (R132H) was negative, suggesting an alternate mutation status. The sections analyzed confirm the presence of a high-grade oligodendroglioma with characteristic histological features such as increased cellularity, nuclear atypia, and mitotic activity indicative of anaplastic transformation. There is no mention of the involvement of surgical margins or lymph nodes in the provided data. The gross pathological assessment included multiple representative sections of the mass, affirming the diagnosis and aiding in the detailed histopathological classification.

The essential pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of non-small cell carcinoma. Examination of the level 5 lymph node reveals the presence of non-small cell carcinoma, confirming metastatic involvement in this lymph node. The left upper lobe lung specimen contains a firm, white-tan-gray tumor focus, which is grossly consistent with malignancy and is contiguous with an umbilication on the pleura. The tumor is situated near the pleural surface and lies at a relatively close distance to the stapled surgical margin. Notably, the tumor approaches but does not invade the margin, indicating a close but negative margin status. No other distinct lesions are observed within the lung parenchyma. The level 7 lymph node fragments also underwent examination, although specific pathological details regarding involvement are not provided here. Overall, the pathological assessment highlights the presence of non-small cell carcinoma with metastatic spread to at least one regional lymph node and a tumor in the lung that is near, but does not invade, the surgical margin.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated invasive squamous cell carcinoma originating in the right side of the tongue. The neoplasm demonstrates significant angiolymphatic and perineural invasion, but reassuringly, the surgical margins are free of tumor cells. Pathologic staging is determined as pT2 N2b. No tumor was identified in the excised sections of the dorsal anterior and posterior tongue, as well as in both anterior and posterior sections of the floor of the mouth. Additionally, the deep margin soft tissue excision showed no evidence of malignancy. A selective dissection of the right neck lymph nodes revealed no tumor presence in the submandibular gland or two level 1A lymph nodes. However, metastasis was detected in two out of thirty-four lymph nodes from levels 2-4, without extracapsular spread, with the largest metastatic lymph node measuring 1.7 cm. Notably, the report includes that ancillary studies will follow to further evaluate these findings.

The pathological analysis reveals a diagnosis of anaplastic astrocytoma, characterized by increased cellularity and mild to moderate pleomorphism. The tumor exhibits microcystic and myxoid areas without evidence of necrosis. Mitoses are focally increased, with up to 4 mitoses per 10 high power fields observed. Notably, the majority of neoplastic cells show intense immunoreactivity for P53, and the MIB-1 labeling index is as elevated as 98.7% in the most proliferative regions of the tumor. The combination of these histologic features, the P53 immunoreactivity, and the significantly elevated MIB-1 labeling index supports the diagnosis of an anaplastic astrocytoma.

The pathological examination of the right breast mass from a female patient revealed a diagnosis of multifocal intraductal and infiltrating ductal carcinoma, classified as grade 2/3 with moderately differentiated nuclear features. The tumor demonstrated significant pathological characteristics, including multifocal presence and moderately differentiated cells. Importantly, the carcinoma was found to extend very close to the surgical margin, within 0.1mm, raising concerns about the completeness of the excision. The proximity to the margin suggests a high risk of residual disease, which may necessitate additional surgical or adjuvant treatment. Other relevant pathological findings, such as lymph node involvement or additional areas of disease, were not detailed in this summary. The provided diagnosis emphasizes the need for careful postoperative management to address any remaining cancerous tissue and to prevent recurrence.

The excision of the tumor from the rectus abdominis revealed an ulcerated adenocarcinoma that is moderately differentiated. The tumor extends into the perirectal tissues, indicating local invasion. Margin status is not specified, suggesting that further assessment may be required to determine clear operational boundaries. All examined lymph nodes (12 out of 12 pararectal lymph nodes) tested positive for metastatic involvement, signifying significant regional lymphatic spread. These findings are critical for staging the disease and planning subsequent therapeutic strategies.

The pathological examination reveals a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the colon or rectum, classified as G2. The tumor demonstrates incipient infiltration into the perimuscular fatty tissue yet notably lacks regional lymph node metastases, with 34 nodes examined and all being negative (pT3 pNO 0/34). Both lymphatic (L0) and vascular (V0) invasion are absent, and the resection margins are free of tumor involvement (RO). These findings suggest a locally advanced malignancy with no evidence of spread to regional lymph nodes or distant sites, as well as a complete surgical resection achieved.

The pathological examination following a left radical mastectomy revealed an infiltrating lobular carcinoma of grade 2 in the left breast. The tumor demonstrated a firm white-gray appearance and was located in the outer quadrants. Importantly, no vascular invasion was observed within the breast tissue. The axillary dissection uncovered fourteen lymph nodes, of which four tested positive for metastatic carcinoma, indicating lymph node involvement. Margins of resection will need further evaluation to ensure complete tumor removal. The overall pathological findings elucidate a locally advanced breast carcinoma with regional lymphatic spread.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated non-small cell carcinoma of the right lower lobe. The tumor is characterized by a high grade (3 out of 3) and a mitotic index of 1 mitosis per 10 high-power fields. The primary tumor is confirmed to be 2.5 cm in size. Importantly, the bronchial and pulmonary resection margins, as well as the pleura, are negative for malignant involvement, and there is no evidence of vascular invasion. Lymph node evaluation shows no evidence of malignancy in all submitted samples, including level 10 (10 pieces), level 7 (12 pieces), level 3 (1 piece), and R4 (16 pieces). Additionally, three attached lymph nodes associated with the lung resection are also free of malignancy. The non-tumorous lung tissue exhibits mild to moderate emphysema. The pathological stage is classified as pT1b N0, indicating no regional lymph node metastasis.

The pathological analysis of the right modified radical mastectomy reveals a diagnosis of infiltrating and intraductal carcinoma with a significant presence of vascular space involvement and an intermediate nuclear grade. Approximately 20% of the tumor mass comprises comedo-type intraductal carcinoma. Microscopic examination identifies foci of the tumor within the vascular spaces across the upper outer, lower inner, and upper inner quadrants. Metastatic carcinoma is noted in six out of thirty-five axillary lymph nodes examined. The nipple is negative for Paget's disease, and the surgical margins are clear of tumor presence in all planes of section observed. Additionally, random sections from the lower outer quadrant exhibit fibrocystic changes and focal severely atypical ductal epithelial hyperplasia. Immunohistochemical staining indicates a high positivity for progesterone receptors (80%), while estrogen receptors are rarely expressed (10%), rendering the progesterone receptor status as positive and estrogen receptor status as negative.

The essential pathological findings comprise a diagnosis of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the right lung. The tumor is characterized by the production of abundant mucin with a small micropapillary component and numerous psammoma bodies. The mass, located in the lower lobe, extends from the bronchus and hilar resection margin and nearly involves the hilar soft tissue margin but has not invaded the pleura definitively. There is prominent lymphatic permeation, particularly at the bronchial margin, though no blood vessel or perineural invasion is observed. Additionally, metastatic adenocarcinoma is extensively present in the hilar, subcarinal, and upper medial sternal lymph nodes. Importantly, the lung shows signs of mild centriacinar emphysema and lymphangitis carcinomatosa pattern. These findings culminate in a staging of T4N2M1 for the malignancy, indicating the presence of lymph node metastases and distant spread.

The primary diagnosis is infiltrating squamous cell carcinoma involving multiple anatomical sites, with notable findings of invasive carcinoma located in the left base of the tongue, the right soft tissue on the vagus nerve, and substantial tumor presence at the base of the tongue, extending into the larynx. The excised tumor is moderately differentiated (G2). Surgical margins include a cauterized area with carcinoma extending to less than 1 mm. Significant lymph node involvement is observed, with metastatic squamous cell carcinoma detected in 45 out of 91 nodes from the larynx and base of the tongue, presenting extensive extranodal extension. Additionally, metastatic involvement is evident in 17 of 41 right neck nodes and 8 of 14 left neck nodes, both with extranodal extension. The largest involved node measures 4 cm. The tumor stage is pT4a with a regional node status of pN2, indicating an advanced stage with extensive regional spread. A retropharyngeal node also reveals metastatic carcinoma with extranodal extension. Other biopsies, including portions from the hypoglossal nerve, right tonsil, right deep base on tongue, and various sections of tongue mucosa and neck, are negative for tumor presence. The pathological findings indicate an aggressive squamous cell carcinoma with extensive lymphatic spread and deep tissue infiltration.

The pathological findings reveal a primary invasive carcinoma of the left breast, characterized as ductal adenocarcinoma, NOS, and tubular carcinoma, with a tumor size of 2.4 cm located centrally. The tumor is of Nottingham grade 2/3, with a tubule formation score of 2, nuclear grade of 2, and mitotic activity score of 1, leading to a total Nottingham score of 5/9. The carcinoma is associated with 40% ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of cribriform and solid types. No lymphovascular invasion is observed, and all surgical margins are free of invasive and in situ carcinoma. However, lobular neoplasia with atypical lobular hyperplasia and lobular carcinoma in situ, as well as atypical ductal hyperplasia and fibrocystic changes, are present in various quadrants of the breast tissue. No tumor involvement is seen in the skin or nipple. Hormone receptor status shows positivity for estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PR), and HER-2/neu.

Sentinel lymph node biopsies from the left axilla reveal metastatic carcinoma in two out of four lymph nodes. The metastatic focus in the first lymph node measures 2.1 mm with no extracapsular extension, while the third lymph node shows a 7 mm metastatic focus with extracapsular extension. The second and fourth lymph nodes are benign with no tumor presence. The overall findings indicate lymph node involvement and metastatic spread, despite the absence of tumor involvement in the resection margins and the skin or nipple.

The essential pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of infiltrating lobular carcinoma in the left breast. The tumor measures approximately 6.5 cm in greatest dimension and exhibits lymphovascular invasion. Histologically, the carcinoma is well-differentiated with a Modified Bloom-Richardson score of 5/9, consisting of a duct formation score of 3, nuclear grade score of 1, and mitotic activity score of 1. In situ carcinoma is present with lobular and antegrade ductal involvement. The tumor infiltrates multiple resection margins including the initial segment, additional segment, inferior, superior, and lateral margins. Lymph node involvement is significant, with metastatic carcinoma identified in three out of five nodes, specifically three out of four sentinel nodes, with no extracapsular invasion noted. Immunohistochemical studies reveal positive estrogen receptors (3+) and trace to 1+ positive progesterone receptors, while HER-2/neu and p53 testing are negative. The tumor exhibits an aneuploid DNA index of 1.6 and an intermediate Ki-67 proliferation marker of 16%. The TNM pathologic stage is classified as pT3 N1a MX.

The pathological evaluation reveals no tumor involvement in the excised lymph nodes from stations 5, R11, and L8, all of which consist of one lymph node and surrounding fibroadipose tissue. The lobectomy specimen from the left upper lobe of the lung has been assessed, though specific characteristics of the tumor within the lung tissue are not described in the provided diagnosis. Importantly, there is no evidence of metastatic spread to the evaluated lymph nodes, suggesting localized disease within the primary lung tissue. All margins and other relevant pathological features must be considered in the context of the complete pathology report to determine the overall prognosis and next steps for treatment.

The pathological examination of the brain tumor reveals a well-differentiated astrocytoma, consistent with WHO Grade II. The tumor exhibits a prominent microcystic component with readily observed microcalcifications, and the presence of round, regular nuclei, some with perinuclear haloes, and mini gemistocytes. There are also focal areas of increased cellularity with elongated, hyperchromatic nuclei indicative of astrocytic differentiation, although some features suggest oligodendroglioma. No unequivocal vascular proliferation, necrosis, or definitive mitotic figures are identified. Immunohistochemically, the Ki-67 proliferation index approaches 5% in focal areas. The tumor's margins and lymph node involvement are not described in the report; however, the presence of hypercellular areas and a mildly elevated MIB-1 labeling index suggests a potential for more aggressive behavior, warranting close clinical follow-up.

The pathological examination reveals an infiltrating glioma in both the posterior right temporal occipital region and right temporal tip, with the resected tumor in the right temporal occipital brain identified as a mixed oligoastrocytoma, WHO Grade II. The tumor exhibits geographically distinct regions of astrocytic and oligodendroglial differentiation, with hypercellularity confined to the oligodendroglial areas. No mitotic figures are present, though a single focus of coagulative tumor necrosis without pseudo-palisading is noted. Microvascular proliferation is absent. Importantly, fluorescence in situ hybridization has confirmed the loss of chromosomes 1p and 19q. Additionally, intraoperative consultations indicated an anaplastic astrocytoma for specimen A and an infiltrating glioma for specimen B. Overall, the mixed oligoastrocytoma displays significant molecular and histological heterogeneity, characteristic of its WHO Grade II classification.

The essential pathological findings include a primary diagnosis of invasive poorly differentiated squamous carcinoma located in the floor of the mouth. The tumor measured 2.4 cm in diameter and 0.7 cm in depth. No vascular invasion was identified, and surgical margins were negative but noted to be close, with the closest margin at 0.1 cm. Notably, there was an associated squamous carcinoma in situ. Lymph node evaluations from multiple neck levels (1A, 1B, 2, 3, and 4) on both sides were all negative for metastatic carcinoma, with no metastases identified in any of the 61 lymph nodes excised. Additionally, biopsy samples from various mucosal and muscular sites, including the tongue base, midline deep margin, right posterior tongue, right floor of the mouth, and mid tongues, showed no evidence of dysplasia or invasive carcinoma. Some samples exhibited benign squamous mucosa and minimal artifacts. The histopathological assessment confirmed these findings, leading to an overall staging of pT2 pN0 pMx according to the AJCC criteria.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive squamous cell carcinoma in the left lung. The tumor is unifocal, located in the left lower lobe, and exhibits a histologic grade of 2-3 out of 3, suggesting moderate to poor differentiation. No treatment effect is observed in the tumor, indicating that it may not have responded to any pre-surgical therapy. Importantly, there is no invasion of the visceral pleura or large vessels, though rare suspicious foci of lymphatic (small vessel) invasion are noted. The resection margins are clear of carcinoma, which is a favorable prognostic factor. A total of 22 lymph nodes were examined, all of which were negative for metastatic carcinoma, confirming no lymph node involvement. The TNM staging is determined to be pT2b, pN0, pMx, corresponding to a moderately advanced primary tumor without regional lymph node metastasis and undetermined distant metastasis. Gross examination revealed a clear surgical margin at the bronchial resection site. The specimen type analyzed was from a left pneumonectomy procedure.

The pathological examination of a right radical mastectomy specimen revealed an invasive ductal carcinoma. The tumor is characterized by a Scarff-Bloom-Richardson score of 8, indicating a high grade with tubular, nuclear, and mitotic scores of 3, 3, and 2 respectively. Importantly, there is no in-situ component identified. Angiolymphatic invasion is present, with involvement extending into the dermis of the skin and nipple. Despite this, microcalcifications were not observed. The tumor margins are clear, with tumor cells located more than 1 centimeter from all margins, and there is no microscopic invasion into muscle or bone. Additionally, all ten examined lymph nodes, including those from level III, show no evidence of metastatic involvement. The tumor is positive for estrogen and progesterone receptors but negative for HER2/neu. The case is staged as pT4B, N0, MX, reflecting the absence of regional lymph node metastasis and undetermined distant metastasis status.

The pathology findings from the right upper lobe biopsy during the thoracotomy reveal an invasive moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. This tumor presents as two nearby nodules connected microscopically, forming a single "dumbbell-shaped" tumor focus with a mixture of clear-type and eosinophilic-type cytoplasm. The tumor shows invasion into scar tissue and the visceral pleura, with a minor extension onto the outer surface of the pleura. Importantly, all margins, including the bronchial and vascular, are negative for tumor involvement. The tumor has not shown evidence of lymph-vascular invasion. Examination of the lymph nodes reveals that the parabronchial, R4, and subcarinal nodes are reactive but free of metastatic disease. Additionally, there is evidence of emphysema within the lung tissue. This pathological assessment classifies the tumor as pT2a PL2 N0 according to the AJCC 7th edition, indicating a localized adenocarcinoma with pleural invasion but no lymph node metastasis.

The pathology analysis reveals a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma (G2) located in the distal rectum. The tumor demonstrates superficial invasion into the lamina muscularis propria but does not extend further. Importantly, both the oral and aboral resection margins are free of tumor involvement, indicating successful surgical clearance. Additionally, there is no evidence of regional lymph node metastasis, as all examined lymph nodes were negative for malignancy (0 out of 17). Other significant pathological findings include the absence of lymphovascular invasion, reflecting a potentially favorable prognosis. Overall, the resected tumor is classified as pT2 pN0 with clear resection margins (R0), suggesting a localized disease without metastatic spread.

The pathological evaluation of the brain tissue reveals the presence of an anaplastic oligoastrocytoma, classified as WHO Grade III. The tumor exhibits notable hypercellularity, mitoses, microvascular changes, and areas of necrosis. Cytological examination shows regions with hyperchromatic cells and increased pleomorphism alongside areas with typical oligodendroglioma features, including cytoplasmic clearing and satellitosis. Immunohistochemical staining indicates that the tumor cells are GFAP positive, and the proliferation index reflected by MIB-1 is approximately 30%. Genetic analysis reveals multiple chromosomal gains, including EGFR and MET, without evidence of EGFR vIII mutation. Immunohistochemistry for various markers shows positivity for MGMT, CA-IX, VEGF, and PDGFr alpha and beta, among others. Molecular testing confirms IDH1 mutation, and MGMT promoter methylation is identified, highlighting potential therapeutic avenues. Overall, the findings support a diagnosis of an aggressive, mixed glial neoplasm with significant proliferative and genetic abnormalities, suggesting a poor prognosis and guiding further clinical management strategies.

The pathological examination of the right frontal brain masses reveals a diagnosis of low-grade oligodendroglioma (WHO grade II) characterized by focal increased cellularity and a mild increase in mitotic activity. Immunohistochemical analysis shows positive IDH1 (R132H) staining, indicating an IDH1 mutation, while p53 is expressed in rare cells and Ki-67 labeling varies from low to focally moderate, supporting the diagnosis. Preliminary frozen section consultation favored oligodendroglioma, which was confirmed on permanent sections. The lesion appears ill-defined grossly and no definitive gross margins can be assessed. Lymph node involvement is not applicable. Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) studies for 1p19q deletion have been ordered to further characterize the tumor. Collectively, these findings support the diagnosis of low-grade oligodendroglioma, consistent with grade II criteria under the WHO classification.

The pathological examination of the left frontal mass reveals a diagnosis of anaplastic oligodendroglioma, Grade III according to the World Health Organization (WHO) classification. This high-grade tumor is characterized by early microvascular proliferation, areas of high cellularity, and brisk mitotic activity. The gross examination describes a cerebral cortex and underlying white matter with a loss of the gray-white junction demarcation and a notable area of gray discoloration. The tumor's microscopic features include infiltrative glioma patterns. Importantly, fluorescence in situ hybridization testing indicates a loss of chromosomal arms 1p and 19q, an alteration associated with the oligodendroglial lineage and potentially indicative of a more favorable response to adjuvant therapy compared to tumors without this chromosomal loss. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not applicable in this context given the intracerebral nature of the mass.

The pathological examination of the right lower lobectomy reveals a moderately differentiated mucinous adenocarcinoma exhibiting visceral pleural and angiolymphatic invasion. The tumor, devoid of satellite gross nodules, demonstrates a mixture of lepidic, papillary, and predominantly acinar architectures in areas of invasion, and measures 7.5 cm at its greatest dimension. Margins are free of tumor involvement. Analysis of fifteen sampled lymph nodes shows reactive changes without evidence of malignancy (N1 and N2 nodes are uninvolved). Additional findings include mild lymphocytic host response and mild emphysematous changes in the lung. Histochemical and immunohistochemical staining confirm strong positivity for mucicarmine, PAS, PAS-D, and visceral pleural invasion, with negative results for p63, cytokeratin 5/6, and TTF-1. Molecular pathology tests indicate a KRAS mutation (p.G12C), while mutations in EGFR and BRAF are absent. The pathologic stage is determined to be T3 N0. Sections of the seventh rib show normal bone histology without evidence of malignancy.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of high-grade invasive papillary urothelial carcinoma in the bladder, which invades the outer half of the muscularis propria and extends into the perivesical tissue microscopically. Lymphovascular and extensive perineural invasion are present. The resection margins are negative for malignancy yet notably close, with carcinoma identified within the lymphovascular space less than 1.0 mm from the left posterior perivesical soft tissue margin. The radical cystoprostatectomy specimen showed no urothelial malignancy in the prostate, although focal high-grade prostatic intraepithelial neoplasia, glandular and stromal hyperplasia with focal chronic inflammation, and glandular atrophy were noted. No malignant involvement was identified in bilateral seminal vesicles. All examined lymph nodes, including those from the left common iliac, left obturator, left external iliac, right common iliac, right obturator, and right external iliac regions, were negative for malignancy. Pathologic staging classifies the tumor as pT3a, indicating microscopic invasion of perivesical tissue, with no regional lymph node metastasis (pN0) and no distant metastasis applicable. Additional findings include a urinary bladder diverticulum and organizing thrombi in pericystic and periprostatic tissues.

The pathological evaluation confirms a diagnosis of poorly differentiated keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma located in the left lung, with a sizeable neoplasm characterized by moderate tumor necrosis. The tumor is indicated to have infiltrated within the lung but has not extended through the pleura, nor has it demonstrated any lymphatic, venous, or arterial invasion. Importantly, the surgical margins are clear of the tumor, suggesting a complete resection. Examination of multiple lymph nodes, including L4, R4, Level 7, Level 8, and Level 10, revealed no metastatic involvement, with all nodes tested showing no signs of cancer. Additionally, non-neoplastic areas of the lung displayed signs of pneumonia with abscess formation. Overall, the cancer staging is determined as T2, N0, indicating a localized tumor without regional lymph node involvement.

The essential pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of Glioblastoma multiforme (WHO Grade IV) for all submitted specimens, which include the right parietal tumor (both specimens A and B) and the posterior brain lesion (specimen C). Tumor characteristics demonstrate high-grade features with prominent vascular endothelial proliferation, nuclear pleomorphism, and areas of necrosis, including focal pseudopalisading necrosis, consistent with the severe and aggressive nature of glioblastoma. Both the right parietal tumor and the posterior brain lesion exhibit these defining traits. Additionally, these findings indicate fully involved surgical margins as the entirety of the submitted tissues are characterized by the tumor process. No lymph node involvement is mentioned, presumably due to the primary intracranial location of this pathology, where lymphatic dissemination is uncommon. This cohesive analysis supports the conclusive identification of an aggressive malignant glioma necessitating immediate and thorough clinical management.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of invasive moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma originating from the oral cavity, with an ulcerating gross appearance. The tumor exhibits infiltrative borders with thick cords of more than four cells and extends with a depth of invasion without perineural or vascular invasion. The composite resection, which included part of the maxilla, glossal tissue, segment of the mandible, and pharyngeal tissue, shows negative margins in all examined squamous mucosa of the lateral buccal, posterior alveolar, soft palate, and maxillary gingival regions. Furthermore, the final assessment indicates no bone or cartilage invasion, as confirmed by additional decalcified sections and analysis of the mandible and medial pterygoid plate. Additionally, lymph node dissections from left neck levels 1, 2A, and 3 yield no evidence of metastatic involvement, with all nodes tested negative for tumor presence. Overall, the resection margins, lymph nodes, and critical structures including bone show no tumor involvement, indicating a localized but invasive carcinoma.

The pathological findings indicate a moderately differentiated lobular carcinoma of the left breast, specifically located in the upper outer quadrant. The tumor is grossly evident, and no metastasis was found in any of the ten axillary lymph nodes examined.
