Importantly, the surgical margins are uninvolved, suggesting that the tumor has been excised completely with no cancerous cells detected at the margins of the removed tissue. The total Nottingham score could not be determined. Overall, these findings suggest a localized lobular carcinoma with no evidence of lymph node involvement, which is crucial for assessing the extent of disease and potential prognosis.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma located in the right upper lobe of the lung. The tumor extends to the visceral pleura but no convincing lymphatic permeation is observed. Importantly, the resection margins are free of tumor involvement. The pathological stage is classified as pT2N0Mx. Examination of the level 7 and level 4 lymph nodes indicates no metastatic tumor presence. Additional findings include segments of benign lung tissue with subpleural and interstitial fibrosis accompanied by chronic inflammation. Intraoperative consultation confirms that the suture line of the wedge resection is negative for tumor cells, with the tumor being sufficiently distant from the staple margin. The remaining lung tissue exhibits no pathological changes.

The diagnosis reveals a poorly differentiated carcinoma conforming to the neuroendocrine type, G3. The tumor demonstrates extensive infiltration into the terminal ileum and serosa, consistent with a pT4 classification. There is notable infiltration of lymphatic and blood vessels, designated as L1 and V1, respectively. Comprehensive evaluation indicates that the resection margins in the mucosal membrane region and the ligature area are free of tumor involvement, ensuring clear margins. Additionally, no metastatic lymph node involvement was detected, corresponding to a pN0 classification. Relevant tumor characteristics are classified under ICDO-DA-M 8140/3. The overall findings highlight the aggressive nature of the carcinoma with significant local invasion but no lymph node metastasis or involvement of margins.

The pathology report reveals a primary diagnosis of poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma (histologic grade G3) located in the supraglottis of the larynx. The tumor invades through the thyroid cartilage, categorizing it as pT4a. Notably, no regional lymph node metastasis is identified (pN0), and there is no evidence of extra-capsular extension or distant metastasis (pMx). Comprehensive resection of adjacent structures, including the right and left pyriform sinus, right and left valleculae, and base of the tongue, shows squamous mucosa with no evidence of tumor involvement. Furthermore, lymph nodes resected from various neck levels, including benign lymphoid hyperplasia, indicate no metastatic involvement. However, the resection margins at the right lateral, right superior, and left superior mucosal edges show tumor infiltration, necessitating further assessment of true margin status. Interestingly, the case also identifies carcinoma in situ and chronic inflammation, with an incidental finding of benign thyroid tissue. No venous, lymphatic, or perineural invasion is observed. This case underscores critical considerations for both the local management of tumor margins and ongoing surveillance for potential recurrences.

The diagnosis is glioblastoma multiforme (GBM), WHO grade IV, confirmed on multiple specimens from the right posterior occipital tumor resection. Tumor cells exhibit positive GFAP immunostaining and a high proliferative index indicated by positive KI-67 immunostain in numerous cells. Notably, extensive necrosis is observed in specimens from both the tumor center and edge. A fragment of white matter adjacent to the tumor demonstrates slight hypercellularity with occasional atypical cells, but no overt malignancy. Margins are not explicitly described, however, the presence of necrosis at the periphery suggests an invasive tumor border. Lymph node involvement is not mentioned in the findings. The assays used for the immunostains were validated with appropriate controls but include analyte-specific reagents not cleared by the FDA.

The pathological examination reveals an invasive squamous cell carcinoma, predominantly non-keratinizing, located in the left tonsil, consistent with a pT2NX staging. Importantly, there is no evidence of angiolymphatic or perineural invasion. Comprehensive assessment confirms that all resection margins are clear of tumor involvement, indicating a complete excision. Further examination of a re-excised left tonsillar fossa shows normal fibroadipose tissue, minor salivary gland, and skeletal muscle with no significant pathological changes, and no residual tumor presence. The right tonsil exhibits hyperplasia with saprophytic bacterial colonization but no malignancy. Ancillary studies performed on a prior biopsy of the primary tumor confirmed positivity for p16 and human papillomavirus. The histological grade of the tumor is moderate (G2), emphasizing a need for continued clinical follow-up due to its malignant nature. Overall, the complete surgical resection and absence of lymphovascular and perineural invasion are favorable prognostic indicators.

The histopathological examination of the left breast and axillary tissue revealed an invasive, multifocal lobular carcinoma. The tumor was classified as NHG2 and displayed characteristics of the histocytoid type. The lesion also included ductal carcinoma in situ with high nuclear atypia and comedo necrosis, comprising 10% of the tumor. The cancer had metastasized to 19 out of 22 examined axillary lymph nodes, indicating a pN3a stage with signs of perilymphatic invasion. The tumor was found centrally located in the breast tissue and was in close proximity to the skin and the upper boundary. Despite these findings, the nipple and remaining glandular tissue showed no pathological lesions, other than fibrocytoid changes consistent with adenosis. Overall, the pathological status indicates a significant and aggressive disease with extensive nodal involvement.

The pathological findings confirm a diagnosis of invasive urothelial carcinoma, originating from the bladder and displaying a high histologic grade. The tumor exhibits both papillary and flat patterns in its non-invasive components, while the invasive portion is characterized by infiltrating growth. Extensive perivesical soft tissue and periurethral stroma invasion into the proximal prostate are noted, with significant vascular invasion but no perineural invasion observed. The surgical margins are free of tumor, although the tumor approaches within 1 mm of the inked soft tissue margins. The non-neoplastic bladder mucosa is unremarkable, with the prostate showing nodular hyperplasia and the seminal vesicles remaining uninvolved. Metastatic involvement is identified in the left pelvic and right pelvic lymph nodes, with extranodal extension present, though other lymphatic regions such as the right common iliac, left common iliac, and pre-sacral lymph nodes evaluated are benign. The presence of additional metastatic carcinoma in two of four perivesical lymph nodes, combined with these findings, classifies the pathological stage as pT3, pN2. Special studies reveal diffuse membranous E-cadherin staining in tumor cells, which lack reactivity for chromogranin and synaptophysin.

The diagnosis reveals a moderately to poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma in the left upper lobe of the lung, confirmed through partial pneumonectomy. The bronchial and vascular resection margins are negative for the tumor, indicating no immediate spread to these surgical boundaries. However, there is extensive lymphatic invasion, with metastatic carcinoma found in one of the lymph nodes within the lung. Additionally, metastatic adenocarcinoma is identified in one anterior mediastinal lymph node, extending into the perinodal soft tissue, and poorly differentiated carcinoma is noticed in the soft tissue of the aortic arch lymph node. These findings suggest a significant spread of the disease via lymphatic pathways, involving multiple nodes and adjacent tissues.

The microscopic examination reveals a moderately cellular glial neoplasm characterized by both astrocytic and oligodendroglial features. The oligodendroglial component is notable for cells with round to oval, moderately pleomorphic nuclei and prominent preinuclear halos, while the astrocytic component displays neoplastic cells with oval to elongate nuclei and moderate cytologic atypia. Scattered microgemistocytes are present, and although mitotic figures are rare, focal areas show elevated mitotic activity up to 3 mitoses per 10 high power fields. Necrosis and microvascular proliferation are absent. The infiltrative nature of the neoplastic cells into the adjacent brain parenchyma supports the diagnosis of oligoastrocytoma. Further assessment with a MIB-1 labeling index reveals an overall low proliferation rate of 2.4-3.7%, with a focal region of increased activity at 6.2%. These pathological findings, including the focally elevated MIB-1 index and mitotic activity, suggest early anaplastic progression in an otherwise low-grade tumor. The final diagnosis of the tumor is oligoastrocytoma, predominantly oligodendroglioma, consistent with early anaplastic progression, classified as WHO grade III. Margin status and lymph node involvement findings are not applicable in this context as the tumor is located within the brain.

The specimen from a right hemicolectomy reveals a high-grade, moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma located in the cecum, characterized by an ulcerative and pushing border. The tumor invades through the muscularis propria into the serosal surface. All resection margins, including proximal, distal, and radial, are negative for tumor. Pathological examination indicates the absence of lymphovascular and extramural venous invasion, though perineural invasion is identified. The peritumoral lymphoid response is mild, and no residual adenoma is noted. Immunohistochemical staining shows a mismatch repair deficiency with the loss of MLH1 and PMS2, while MSH2 and MSH6 remain intact. This deficiency suggests possible sporadic methylation but cannot rule out a germline mutation, necessitating clinical correlation. All 35 regional lymph nodes assessed are negative for metastasis. Based on the AJCC 6th edition classification, the tumor is staged as T4bN0MX. Additionally, a liver biopsy specimen indicates a bile duct adenoma. The findings underscore the importance of further clinical assessment to delineate the implications of the mismatch repair deficiency and to tailor patient management accordingly.

The pathological assessment of the right breast segmental mastectomy revealed an invasive ductal carcinoma, no special type, prominently featuring abundant calcifications. The tumor received a Nottingham grade of 2, evidenced by moderate tubule formation, nuclear pleomorphism, and mitotic activity, culminating in a total score of 6 out of 9. Additionally, ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of nuclear grade 2, cribriform type, with calcifications, was identified, constituting 5% of the tumor and interspersed with the invasive component. Significant lymphovascular invasion was present, though resection margins were clear of carcinoma, with the invasive tumor being in close proximity to the anterior and posterior margins. Examination of the non-neoplastic breast tissue displayed fibrocystic and columnar cell changes, alongside changes consistent with a previous biopsy site. Immunohistochemistry revealed the invasive tumor cells to be positive for estrogen and progesterone receptors, while HER-2 was negative. In the right axilla, one of five sentinel lymph nodes examined showed metastatic involvement, with a metastatic deposit measuring 0.1 cm without extracapsular extension. The T stage was determined to be pT1c, with the N stage classified as pNX and M stage as pMX. Further lymph node dissection is necessary to definitively assign the N stage.

The essential pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of Well-differentiated Astrocytoma, grade II, occurring in the context of cortical dysplasia. The tumor presents as a mildly to moderately hypercellular glial neoplasm, diffusely infiltrating both white and gray matter, with focal perineuronal satellitosis observed within the gray matter. The neoplastic cells predominantly exhibit characteristics of gemistocytes, with some fibrillary astrocytes also present. Nuclear morphology is generally round to oval, without perinuclear halos or features typical of oligodendroglioma differentiation. Notably, the atypia is mild to moderate, and no mitotic figures are identified in 50 high power fields. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not specified,, aligning with the low-grade histologic features evidenced by the presence of only rare MIB-1 positive cells.

The pathological assessment reveals a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma located in the superior segment of the right lower lobe of the lung. The invasive tumor measures 2.0 cm and demonstrates perineural invasion, with focal evidence of angiolymphatic invasion. The bronchovascular margin shows the presence of adenocarcinoma, while the stapled parenchymal margin is free of carcinoma. The tumor exhibits a moderate desmoplastic reaction, with no extrapulmonary extension or significant necrosis observed. Lymph nodes at various levels (4, 7, 8, 10, and 11) are anthracotic and free of carcinoma, indicating no lymph node involvement (pN0). Immunohistochemical staining confirms the adenocarcinoma diagnosis with positive TTF-1 and rare p63 positivity, and negative cytokeratin 5/6. The overall AJCC pathologic staging is pT1a N0 Mx, suggesting an early-stage tumor without metastatic spread. Emphysematous changes are also noted in the lung tissue.

The pathological examination of the left breast lumpectomy specimen revealed an infiltrating ductal carcinoma, moderately differentiated (Nottingham Grade 2), without necrosis or microcalcifications. The tumor is negative for venous or lymphatic invasion and located close to the anterior margin. Ductal carcinoma in situ of intermediate nuclear grade with central necrosis was also identified. Hormone receptor status showed nearly 100% estrogen receptor positivity and 95% progesterone receptor positivity, while HER2 was negative. None of the sentinel lymph nodes (three in total) or non-sentinel lymph nodes (two in total) showed evidence of malignancy. The pathological stage was determined to be pT1c NO (i-) MX. Additionally, the lumpectomy specimen demonstrated fibrocystic changes and evidence of a previous biopsy procedure. Immunohistochemical stains confirmed the presence of invasive carcinoma and its cytokeratin profile. The overall findings suggest a localized breast cancer with features indicating a favorable prognosis, given the absence of lymph node involvement and high hormone receptor positivity.

The pathological evaluation reveals a diagnosis of oligodendroglioma, WHO grade II, characterized by mildly to moderately hypercellular diffuse glioma affecting both the gray and white matter, with a predominance in the gray matter. Tumor cells exhibit enlarged round to oval nuclei with most showing perinuclear halos, accompanied by mild to moderate atypia. Secondary structures in the gray matter include notable perineuronal satellitosis, subpial condensation, and focal perivascular accumulation. Importantly, no mitotic figures are observed. The MIB-1 labeling index indicates that greater than 5% of the tumor nuclei are positive, raising concern despite not justifying an upgrade to anaplastic grade 3. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not applicable to this central nervous system tumor. Given the unusual growth pattern with neurons intermixed among tumor cells, close follow-up is recommended.

The pathological evaluation of the right upper lobe of the lung revealed a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, classified as AJCC stage T1a, NO. The neoplasm did not invade the pleura despite being adjacent to it and measured 1.5 cm in diameter. Following the excision of the right upper lobe, no residual carcinoma was identified in the specimen. The excision margins were analyzed and confirmed to be free of neoplastic cells. The hilar lymph node was found to be reactive but contained no evidence of metastatic neoplasm, indicating no lymph node involvement. The gross examination described a centrally located gritty and white-tan firm mass in the wedge resection specimen, corroborating the microscopic findings of isolated adenocarcinoma without further spread within the tissue. The findings collectively indicate a localized adenocarcinoma with clear resection margins and no nodal metastasis, suggesting a favorable prognosis post-surgery.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of invasive mixed ductal and lobular carcinoma of the left breast, with an SBR grade II. The tumor presents with both solid and cribriform types of ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) exhibiting intermediate nuclear grade. Notably, the tumor measures 3.5 cm and was identified at the inked green medial margin, suggesting proximity to this margin. The resection margins are clear of malignancy. Additionally, reactive changes consistent with fibroadenoma and fibrocystic changes are noted. Crucially, the sentinel lymph node from the left axilla was negative for metastatic carcinoma, and no vascular or lymphatic invasion was identified. The medial margin of the left breast excision also shows fibrocystic changes without malignancy. Regarding hormone receptor status, the tumor is positive for both estrogen and progesterone receptors, but negative for HER2. Pathological staging is consistent with pT2N0, indicating a primary tumor size without lymph node metastasis. The Oncotype DX assay indicates a low recurrence score, suggesting a favorable prognosis with a low risk of distant recurrence.

The pathological examination of the brain tumor, resected from the right frontal lobe, confirms a diagnosis of anaplastic astrocytoma, WHO grade III. The neoplasm exhibits diffuse infiltration into the cerebral cortex and underlying white matter. Histologically, the tumor is characterized by cells with angulated to irregular nuclei displaying moderate to marked nuclear pleomorphism, with a fibrillar to spindled cytoplasm and focal gemistocytic features. Occasional multinucleated tumor cells and scattered mitotic figures are observed, but there is no evidence of necrosis or vascular proliferation. Immunohistochemical studies reveal a high Ki-67 labeling index, reaching approximately 25% in certain areas, indicating high proliferative activity. The tumor cells are positive for mutant IDH-1. There is no mention of specific margin status or lymph node involvement, which is typical given the intracranial location of the tumor. Further molecular analyses, including MGMT gene methylation and 1p/19q chromosomal losses, have been sent for additional characterization. The overall histopathological and immunohistochemical profile suggests an aggressive glial tumor with potential for further progression, though it currently lacks the necrosis and vascular proliferation that would elevate it to a grade IV glioblastoma.

The pathological evaluation reveals metastatic adenocarcinoma of rectal origin in the liver, classified as pN1. Examination of the resected sigmoid rectum shows an invasive rectal carcinoma, graded between G2 and G3, with infiltration into the muscularis layer, consistent with a pT2 stage. There is evidence of vascular (V1) and lymphatic (L1) invasion. The resection margins are free of tumor, and there are three lymph node metastases, indicating regional lymph node involvement (pN1). Notably, the distance from the tumor to the mesorectal fascia is greater than 1 cm, suggesting adequate surgical clearance in this area. The tumor is classified as ICD-O-DA M-8140/3, with an overall staging of G2-3, pT2, L1, V1, pN1, and pM1, reflecting both local invasion and distant metastasis. The anastomosis ring in the surgical specimen is free of tumor, indicating no immediate postoperative tumor spread at the surgical connection site.

The examination of the radical cystectomy specimen with ileal conduit and bilateral pelvic lymph node dissection reveals the presence of high-grade urothelial carcinoma with squamous differentiation, primarily involving the posterior bladder wall and extending into the left ureterovesical junction and left ureter. The tumor extends throughout the entire bladder wall and deeply infiltrates into the perivesical soft tissue, including focal carcinoma in situ associated with the invasive tumor. The tumor approaches within 1 mm of the posterior soft tissue resection margin, but the final soft tissue margin is negative. Other margins, including bilateral ureters (two left and two right), the urethral, and vaginal mucosa margins, are free of tumor. No malignancy is identified in the multiple right and left peri-vesical lymph nodes examined (0/6), nor in the total of 54 lymph nodes sampled from various pelvic and abdominal regions. The left stented ureter shows hydroureter, and the unstented portion exhibits reactive urothelial atypia without evidence of high-grade dysplasia or malignancy. The right and left distal ureters are benign, with no urothelial dysplasia or malignancy. These findings result in a pathological staging of pT3bN0MX for the bladder cancer.

The pathological evaluation revealed bilateral infiltrating ductal carcinoma (IDC) of the breasts. On the right side, the mastectomy specimen showed a Grade II IDC, characterized by a moderate score for tubule formation, pleomorphism, and mitoses. The tumor lacked ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) and lymphovascular invasion, and the margins were tumor-free. Adjacent tissue examination revealed usual ductal hyperplasia and biopsy site changes. On the left side, a Grade II IDC with associated necrosis, DCIS, and focal vascular invasion was identified, with the tumor abutting but not definitively involving the deep margin. Both mastectomy specimens included biopsy site changes and unremarkable skin and nipples. Furthermore, axillary sentinel lymph node biopsies from both sides showed no evidence of malignancy, supported by hematoxylin and eosin staining and immunohistochemical analysis. Additional excised breast tissue and inframammary crease specimens were benign, with no further malignancy observed in associated tissues. The pathological staging for both breast specimens was determined to be pT2N0MX according to the AJCC system.

The essential pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of both infiltrating ductal carcinoma and a papillary squamous cell carcinoma in situ with possible early stromal invasion. The true cut breast biopsy exhibits infiltrating ductal carcinoma with grades 2 out of 3 for nuclear grade and 3 out of 3 for histologic grade, showing lymphatic/vascular invasion. The cervical tumor is characterized as a polypoid papillary squamous cell carcinoma in situ, with a comment noting the architectural resemblance to low-grade papillary transitional cell cancer seen in the bladder, and a potential early microinvasion that is difficult to confirm. The right breast and axillary nodes from the modified radical mastectomy exhibit residual infiltrating ductal carcinoma, multifocal in nature and with an extensive intraductal component, primarily of the cribriform and solid in-situ types with necrosis. The tumor is located in the upper inner quadrant with a size of approximately 4.1 cm for the invasive component and shows lymphatic/vascular invasion. Skin and nipple margins are free of tumor, and the non-neoplastic breast tissue displays fibrosis. Importantly, the surgical margins are negative. Lymph node examination revealed metastatic involvement in eight out of fifteen right axillary nodes, with the largest metastasis measuring 1.5 cm, also indicating extranodal invasion. Estrogen and progesterone receptor assays for the breast tissue returned positive results.

The pathological findings reveal liver metastases originating from a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma of the colon, indicating an advanced stage of the disease. The primary tumor, located in the rectal position, is characterized as an invasive poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma (Grade 3, G3). Extensive lymph vessel dilatation is present, as evidenced by both lymphatic (L1) and vascular (V1) invasion. The tumor exhibits significant lymph node involvement, with a conglomerate of metastases present in more than five lymph nodes, classifying it as pN2. The overall tumor classification is ICDO-DA M-8140/3, with a pathological staging of pT3, pN2, underscoring the aggressive nature of the malignancy.

The pathological analysis reveals a diagnosis of invasive lobular carcinoma, nuclear grade 1, well-differentiated, located in the right breast, accompanied by fibrocystic changes and atypical lobular hyperplasia. The carcinoma is situated 5 mm away from the superficial, lateral, and inferior margins, while all other margins are free of tumor. Examination of three sentinel lymph nodes from the right breast shows no evidence of metastasis, confirmed by negative cytokeratin immunohistochemical staining. Additionally, a separate margin resection of the right breast confirms the presence of fibrocystic changes and atypical lobular hyperplasia, without further invasive tumor. The left breast excisional biopsy indicates lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS), classic type, also associated with fibrocystic changes. Immunohistochemical profiling of the invasive lobular carcinoma reveals the presence of estrogen and progesterone receptors, with 90% and 80% positivity respectively, while HER-2/neu is negative. The proliferation marker Ki-67 is significantly elevated at 65%. These findings suggest a well-differentiated, receptor-positive lobular carcinoma with no lymph node involvement and specific margin clearance, providing crucial insights for subsequent treatment planning.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast, histologic grade III, with a tumor size fitting the pT2 category. The carcinoma is closely approximated to the surgical margins in the main resection specimen, with distances of 1.5 mm from the inferior margin, 1 mm from the posterior margin, and 1 mm from the anterior margin. Additional margin analyses for the superior and posterior walls are negative for carcinoma. The invasion of blood vessels is not identified, but the tumor shows presence of necrosis and calcification. A ductal carcinoma in situ component is also present, characterized by a solid pattern, high nuclear grade, and central necrosis, but the extensive intraductal component is absent. The right retroareolar area reveals a fibroadenoma. All sentinel lymph nodes biopsied from the right axilla (four nodes) are negative for malignancy. These findings support a diagnosis of an advanced-grade invasive carcinoma with a noted proximity to resection margins, no vascular invasion, and no lymph node involvement.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of infiltrative ductal carcinoma in the left breast. The tumor, located at the primary site, is characterized as grade 2, indicating moderate differentiation. The staging includes T2, suggesting a sizable tumor confined to the breast tissue, and N2a, indicating the involvement of multiple regional lymph nodes. However, there is no evidence of distant metastasis, as denoted by M0. The specimen was acquired through a radical mastectomy and preserved in a frozen format within a cryomold. Margin status and additional pathological findings such as lymphovascular invasion or receptor status are not specified. A normal blood sample was collected concurrently via blood draw for comparative purposes.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of grade 3 adenocarcinoma in the left upper lobe of the lung. The tumor forms a subpleural mass and is situated at a safe distance from the bronchial margin. Importantly, the surgical margins are free of tumor involvement. While multiple intrapulmonary peribronchial lymph nodes exhibit metastatic adenocarcinoma, there is no evidence of extranodal extension. Furthermore, the superior and inferior mediastinal lymph nodes, as well as the N1 level lymph nodes, are free of tumor involvement. These findings highlight a localized, high-grade malignancy with regional lymph node metastasis but no distant spread to mediastinal nodes.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma in the left breast, exhibiting a high-grade score (SBR Grade 3) with an extensive tumor size. The tumor is associated with intermediate nuclear grade ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), characterized by solid and cribriform types with central necrosis and microcalcifications. Surgical resection margins are negative, indicating the tumor has been successfully excised without residual involvement at the edges. Extensive lymphovascular invasion is noted. Sentinel lymph node biopsy from the left axilla includes one lymph node positive for metastatic carcinoma out of a total of three examined, with no extranodal extension. Both left axillary dissections reveal no further metastatic involvement in the additional 14 lymph nodes examined. The right breast mastectomy and bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes show no evidence of tumor. The overall pathological staging is pT2 N1, reflecting the tumor size and the extent of lymph node involvement. Estrogen receptor (ER) status is positive, while progesterone receptor (PR) is negative, and HER2 is negative by FISH. The patient’s clinical history includes BRCA2 positivity and pre-operative diagnosis of left breast invasive ductal carcinoma.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of low-grade oligodendroglioma (WHO grade II). The tumor demonstrates moderate hypercellularity and extensive infiltration through both gray and white matter, with notable perineuronal satellitosis in the gray matter. Tumor cells exhibit perinuclear halos and round nuclei with moderate atypia, although no mitotic figures, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis are observed. Furthermore, the MIB-1 labeling index, which reaches up to 5.0% in the most proliferative regions, is borderline for grade II oligodendrogliomas according to some studies. These features support the diagnosis of a low-grade tumor; however, due to the borderline MIB-1 index, vigilant monitoring for anaplastic progression or recurrence is advised. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not described in this examination, implying the primary focus is on the intrinsic characteristics of the neoplasm itself.

The pathological examination of the lesions resected from the left frontal lobe of the brain reveals an anaplastic astrocytoma, classified as WHO Grade III. The tumor is hypercellular, exhibiting moderate pleomorphism and hyperchromasia with scattered mitotic figures. There is no evidence of microvascular proliferation or necrosis. Immunohistochemical staining shows diffuse positivity for glial fibrillary acidic protein and IDH-1, indicating likely mutation, as well as p53 and PTEN. The MIB-1 proliferation index is approximately 20%. The tumor margins cannot be assessed in the provided specimens. Further genetic analysis indicates non-amplification of EGFR and absence of 1p/19q codeletion. Additionally, methylation of the MGMT promoter is detected, which may be prognostically significant for treatment response. The findings confirm the diagnosis of an aggressive glial tumor that would benefit from specialized oncological consultation for potential therapeutic strategies.

The diagnosis is oligodendroglioma grade III, characterized by a mildly to moderately hypercellular glial neoplasm that diffusely infiltrates gray and white matter. The tumor demonstrates perineural satellitosis and features cells with round, mildly atypical nuclei and prominent perinuclear halos, but lacks mitotic figures, microvascular proliferation, and necrosis. The MIB-1 labeling index is elevated at 18.8%, suggesting early anaplastic progression. Margin status and lymph node involvement are not detailed in the findings. The overall pathology indicates a higher-grade glioma with clear markers of aggressive potential.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of moderately to poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma located in the left lower lobe of the lung. The primary tumor is characterized by extensive areas of necrosis, obliterated blood vessels, and moderate-to-poor differentiation, but it does not invade the pleura, vascular structures, or nerves. The visceral pleura and lung parenchyma exhibit notable carbon pigmentation and emphysema, with additional pleural fibrosis in the upper lobe. Metastatic involvement is identified in intraparenchymal lymph nodes and one hilar lymph node, while other sampled nodes show reactive changes, anthracosis, or silicotic nodules without evidence of malignancy. The pathological findings indicate a stage T2 N1 adenocarcinoma, signifying localized invasion with regional lymph node metastasis but no distant spread.

The patient, with a significant smoking history, presents with a diagnosis of invasive squamous cell carcinoma of the epiglottis. The pathological examination reveals moderately-differentiated invasive squamous cell carcinoma accompanied by areas of carcinoma in-situ. The biopsy samples analyzed demonstrate malignant epithelial cells penetrating beyond the basement membrane, indicative of invasive carcinoma. The tumor is moderately differentiated, reflecting an intermediate level of cellular abnormality. Relevant pathological findings include the examination of mucosal fragments from the epiglottis, with no specific margins or lymph node involvement assessed in this biopsy. The clinical presentation of progressively increasing hoarseness, left-sided otalgia, and dysphagia corresponds with the pathological findings, which confirm the aggressive nature of the disease.

The pathological evaluation of the left lung pneumonectomy specimen revealed an invasive grade 3 squamous cell carcinoma forming a centrally located mass, with involvement of the pulmonary artery. Associated findings include post-obstructive pneumonia and pleuritis, without pleural involvement. The bronchial surgical margin is clear of tumor by 3 cm. Examination of multiple intrapulmonary peribronchial, superior mediastinal, inferior mediastinal, and aortic lymph nodes revealed no tumor involvement. Pathological assessment indicates an aggressive carcinoma with no evidence of residual tumor at the surgical margins and no lymph node metastasis.

The pathological examination confirms the diagnosis of an oligodendroglioma, Grade 2, within the cerebrum. The tumor shows typical histologic features of oligodendroglioma with no evidence of discrete nodules upon gross inspection. Margins could not be assessed from the biopsy specimens provided. Fluorescence in-situ hybridization (FISH) analysis indicated deletions of chromosomes 1p and 19q, findings that are associated with a favorable prognosis. No familial syndromes or prior tumor history are noted in this case. Ancillary studies are pending to further validate the diagnosis. Therefore, the initial diagnostic assessment remains unchanged following external consultation.

The specimen reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma, characterized by a 3-cm, superficially ulcerated lesion with dysplasia-free multilayered non-keratinizing squamous epithelium at the margins. The tumor demonstrated varying degrees of keratinization and extensive formation of atypical squamous epithelium, exhibiting both solid and ragged growth patterns. Notably, the tumor cells displayed significant pleomorphism, marked nuclear enlargement, prominent nucleoli, and numerous atypical mitoses. These cellular patterns were accompanied by a varying desmoplastic stromal reaction and a moderately mixed cellular inflammatory infiltrate consisting predominantly of lymphocytes, plasma cells, and occasional neutrophils. Importantly, no angioinvasion or perineural infiltration was observed. The tumor extended up to the lateral and deep soft tissue resection margins marked with black ink, while other resection margins were free of tumor involvement. Additionally, the adjacent mucosa exhibited hyperparakeratosis and signs of low-grade chronic recurrent acute inflammation. There was no lymph node involvement noted in the provided sections.

The pathological evaluation of the bladder specimen reveals a diagnosis of infiltrating urothelial carcinoma, characterized by a low-grade, infiltrative pattern. Microscopically, the tumor exhibits hyperplastic cells arranged in papillary formations, nests, groups, or cords. These tumor cells display moderate basophilic or vacuolated cytoplasm, enlarged nuclei with variability in shape and size, irregular nuclear membranes, prominent nucleoli, and abnormal chromatin patterns. A notable presence of mitotic figures is observed. The carcinoma has invaded the inner muscularis propria, indicating the tumor's propensity to penetrate deeper tissue layers. As the tumor demonstrates a moderately differentiated histologic grade, it suggests a transitional cell carcinoma of papillary type. Critical details regarding lymph node involvement, lymphatic or venous invasion, and surgical margin status are not specified, which are essential for a comprehensive assessment and further treatment planning.

The pathological evaluation of the right frontal brain tumor reveals a diagnosis of anaplastic oligodendroglioma, grade III according to the WHO classification. The tumor exhibits regions characteristic of oligodendroglioma, with particular foci showing anaplasia marked by a significantly elevated mitotic rate, microvascular proliferation, and necrosis. Immunohistochemistry for the Ki67 antigen demonstrates a proliferation index ranging from 12.3% to 18%. Additionally, FISH analysis confirms the presence of 1p and 19q deletions, a molecular signature often observed in oligodendrogliomas, which suggests a favorable response to certain therapies. This tumor is confirmed to be neoplastic upon frozen section analysis, although the final frozen section diagnosis was deferred pending further detailed assessments. Final margin status is not explicitly described in the available data, and no additional lymph node involvement is mentioned, implying the absence or non-assessment of lymph nodes in this context. These findings collectively affirm the diagnosis of an anaplastic oligodendroglioma with confirmed molecular characteristics relevant for therapeutic decision-making.

The pathological examination confirms a diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma, non-keratinizing type, poorly differentiated (Grade III), located in the left upper lobe of the lung. The tumor does not exhibit lymphatic, venous, or arterial invasion, nor is there invasion into the pleura. Moderate tumor necrosis is present. Importantly, all surgical margins are clear of tumor involvement. A total of 19 lymph nodes from various locations, including levels LA, R4, R2, 7, 5, 6, and 11, were biopsied, and all were negative for metastatic disease. Additional findings in the non-neoplastic lung tissue indicate the presence of emphysema. Based on the tumor size and absence of lymph node involvement, the pathological staging is T1, N0.

The final diagnosis revealed squamous cell carcinoma in the left lower lobe of the lung, following a lobectomy that included resection of the 6th and 7th ribs. The tumor was a primary lung malignancy, characterized histologically as a squamous cell carcinoma. Post-surgical evaluation confirmed that the surgical margins were clear of cancerous cells, indicating a successful complete excision of the tumor. Additionally, lymph node involvement was not detected, suggesting no regional metastasis. Other relevant pathological findings, including the extent of parenchymal invasion and any histopathological features of the resected ribs, were meticulously evaluated to ensure comprehensive staging and assessment of the disease.

The pathological examination reveals a high-grade (Grade 3) urothelial carcinoma with extensive squamous differentiation, exhibiting invasive behavior into the muscularis propria. Notably, there is no evidence of lymphovascular invasion. The specimen is composed of multiple fragments of white-tan soft tissue, with approximately 75% of the tissue submitted for detailed histological analysis. These findings are consistent with an aggressive bladder carcinoma, warranting careful clinical management and follow-up to monitor potential progression and response to therapy.

The pathological evaluation of the terminal ileum, right colon with attached small bowel, and right ovary revealed an invasive poorly differentiated colonic adenocarcinoma with focal mucinous differentiation. The tumor extends through the colon wall into the adjacent small intestine and across the ileocecal valve into the terminal ileum, classifying it as pT4. Despite the tumor's extensive local spread, the proximal, distal, and radial resection margins are uninvolved, indicating complete excision. Importantly, all fifteen examined mesenteric lymph nodes were free of metastatic carcinoma (pN0), suggesting no regional lymph node involvement. Vascular invasion is absent, and no non-lymph node pericolonic tumor deposits are identified. The right ovary and fallopian tube are adhesed to the colon but show no evidence of tumor involvement. Distant metastasis could not be assessed (pMX). These findings confirm a locally advanced, but potentially resected, colonic adenocarcinoma with no lymph node involvement.

The pathological findings reveal a poorly differentiated infiltrating ductal carcinoma located in the lower outer quadrant of the right breast. The tumor, classified as T2, does not reveal any evidence of venous invasion and is confined, with uninvolved surgical margins. All ten examined axillary lymph nodes show reactive changes but no metastatic involvement. The microscopic evaluation confirms the absence of tumor cells in the resection margins, suggesting a successful excision of the lesion. Overall, the pathological examination indicates a high-grade, localized carcinoma without lymphatic spread, and clear resection margins.

The pathological findings reveal the presence of basaloid squamous cell carcinoma in both the left upper lobe and the right upper lobe of the lung with tumor sizes of 1.4 cm and 1.1 cm, respectively. The resection margins for both tumors are negative, indicating no residual tumor at the margins. Additionally, all examined lymph nodes, including those from the inferior pulmonary ligament, right TB angle, interlobar, lobar, and peribronchial regions, are negative for malignancy. Similarly, the right upper lobe wedge resection, interlobar lymph node, and bone from the fifth rib show no signs of malignancy, and the left upper lobe lobectomy shows no residual malignancy. The tumors appear to be separate T1N0 primary tumors with no metastatic spread to lymph nodes or other tissues.

The pathological examination of the resected right frontal brain tumor reveals a diffuse astrocytoma, classified as WHO Grade II. The tumor is characterized by moderate hypercellularity with mildly pleomorphic, hyperchromatic nuclei. No mitotic figures, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis are observed. Immunohistochemical analysis shows diffuse positivity for glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) and p53, while MIB-1 labeling index is focally elevated at 20%. The tumor is negative for IDH-1 mutation, suggesting a wild-type status. The PCR-based MGMT methylation assay indicates that gene methylation is not detected, with a score of 1.35, categorizing it as unmethylated. These findings outline a moderately aggressive tumor with a typical appearance of a Grade II astrocytoma, without evidence of aggressive features such as necrosis or high mitotic activity, and an unmethylated MGMT promoter, which may influence therapeutic sensitivity.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of glioblastoma (WHO Grade IV) from biopsies taken from the left cerebral lesion. The tumor is characterized by high cellularity and moderate pleomorphism, with evident mitotic figures, vascular proliferation, and areas of necrosis. Immunohistochemical staining demonstrates that the tumor cells are diffusely positive for GFAP, with synaptophysin highlighting the neuropil background and occasional tumor cells. CD3, CD4, and CD34 stains show rare to occasional positive cells. The Ki-67 labeling index, indicative of the proliferative activity, is approximately 50%. Importantly, MGMT promoter methylation testing is negative, indicating no evidence of methylation in the tumor DNA extracted from paraffin blocks. The samples exhibit well-defined pathological features consistent with high-grade glioma, confirming the diagnosis of glioblastoma. The lesion appears encasing critical structures such as the internal carotid artery and left optic chiasm, contributing to a significant mass effect and resultant herniation identified during the clinical assessment.

The rectal resection specimen revealed fully developed diverticulosis in the proximal segment and an ulcerated, moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma extending into the perirectal fat, classified as grade 2 (G2). Both the oral and aboral margins were tumor-free, and no microscopic evidence of infiltration into the posterior bladder wall was detected. Histologically, the tumor is categorized as pT3. Further analysis of the perirectal lymph nodes revealed that out of 30 lymph nodes examined, 18 contained metastatic infiltrates from the adenocarcinoma. Consequently, the pathological staging of the disease is pT3 pN2 (18/30).

The patient was diagnosed with non-small cell carcinoma of the left upper lobe lung, which upon lobectomy was identified as a poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. The tumor exhibited extensive vascular and perineural invasion, but the bronchial and pleural margins were free of tumor, and no in situ carcinoma was identified. The non-neoplastic lung tissue showed emphysematous changes, respiratory bronchiolitis, and rare small non-necrotizing intrapulmonary granulomas. Immunostaining revealed the tumor cells were positive for CK7 and 34BE12, focally positive for CK20 and 4A4, and negative for TTF-1, PE-10, thyroglobulin, and CD31. Lymph node analysis showed metastatic involvement in peribronchial nodes with extranodal extension and intrapulmonary lymph nodes, whereas level eleven and level seven lymph nodes were benign, and the AP window lymph nodes exhibited metastatic carcinoma. These findings indicate an advanced local spread of the tumor with significant nodal involvement.

The pathological examination of the right frontal brain biopsy revealed a mixed oligoastrocytoma, a diffusely infiltrating glial neoplasm with moderate cellularity. The tumor displays atypical features, including a mix of round and elongated neoplastic cells with slight pleomorphism, as well as the presence of hypercellular areas or "proliferation centers," perinuclear halos, and microcystic change. Mitotic figures are scarce, and the MIB-1 proliferation index is approximately 4% in the most active regions. There is an absence of microvascular proliferation and necrosis, and no overt features of anaplasia were noted. Immunohistochemically, 20% of neoplastic cells overexpress p53 protein, and GFAP staining indicates sparse fibril formation. The absence of MGMT promoter methylation suggests no benefit from certain chemotherapeutic agents. These findings characterize the tumor as an intermediate mixed glioma, predominantly oligodendrocytic, with a potential for aggressive behavior due to the presence of proliferation centers and occasional mitoses.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of well-differentiated invasive squamous carcinoma located in the left partial glossectomy specimen. The tumor is characterized by an exophytic growth pattern with an infiltrative border and a depth of invasion extending into but not beyond the muscular layer of the tongue. Notably, there is no evidence of perineural or vascular invasion, and lymphocytic infiltration is also absent. Both the mucosal and deep margins are free of invasive carcinoma, and no dysplasia is observed at these margins. Extensive lymph node dissection was performed, with a total of forty lymph nodes from multiple levels of the left neck, all of which showed no evidence of metastatic disease. Additionally, salivary tissue from the dissection was free of tumor involvement. These findings collectively suggest a localized tumor with no nodal involvement or perineural and vascular invasion, indicating a potentially favorable prognosis for the patient.

The pathological findings confirm a diagnosis of WHO Grade IV glioblastoma multiforme, as evidenced by biopsies from the left frontal lobe (Parts A and B), which reveal a highly cellular astrocytic neoplasm with mitoses, microvascular proliferation, and necrosis. Immunohistochemistry shows GFAP positive neoplastic astrocytes amidst Neurofilament positive axons. Although no tumor is identified in the resected tissue from the left frontal lobe (Part C), extensive sampling indicates a surgical effect with gray and white matter hemorrhage, but no infiltrating neoplastic glial cells. The MIB-1 labeling index in block B2 is reported at 20.06%, reflecting high proliferative activity. Additionally, a reticulin stain on block B2 is negative for gliosarcoma features. These findings collectively support the aggressive nature of glioblastoma multiforme, with notable tumor characteristics and significant mitotic activity.

The pathological examination of the surgical specimens from a patient with left oral squamous cell carcinoma reveals a complex and detailed profile. A partial glossectomy identified moderately differentiated invasive squamous carcinoma, measuring 3.4 cm at its largest dimension, with an invasion depth of 1.4 cm. The tumor exhibited infiltrative borders with thick cords and moderate perineural invasion, but no vascular invasion was detected. Moderate lymphocyte infiltration was present. Margins were largely negative for invasive carcinoma; however, severe dysplasia was noted at the posterolateral margins. Selective neck dissections at levels I, II, III, and IV, encompassing a total of forty-three lymph nodes, demonstrated no evidence of metastatic involvement. Additionally, squamous mucosa sampled from the posterior gingival and pharyngeal margins showed no tumor presence. Overall, while invasive carcinoma was resected with negative margins, the presence of severe dysplasia at one margin warrants close clinical follow-up and possible further therapeutic intervention.

A female patient has been diagnosed with moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the left upper lobe of the lung. The lobectomy specimen reveals a unifocal tumor without involvement of the pleural surface and with resection margins free of malignancy. Notably, the bronchial and vascular margins are uninvolved by invasive carcinoma. Pathological examination of multiple lymph nodes, including those labeled #5, #6, #7, #8, #9, #10, #11, and #12, reveals no evidence of malignancy, with all nodes and fragments being negative. The tumor is staged as pT1a, indicating a tumor size of 2 cm or less, confined to the lung or visceral pleura, without invasion into the main bronchus. No regional lymph node metastasis (pN0) or distant metastasis (pM not applicable) is observed. The final pathologic stage is 5T1a/NO/Mx.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of basaloid squamous cell carcinoma located in the left upper lobe, characterized as poorly differentiated (G3). The tumor was centrally situated, measuring 3.3 cm at its greatest dimension. Examination revealed that the bronchial margin was negative for tumor involvement, and no satellite tumors were identified. Lymph node analysis showed no evidence of tumor spread, with all sampled nodes, including those from the 4R right distal paratracheal, 4L left distal paratracheal, subcarinal, Level 5, Level 2, and Level 9 regions, testing negative for malignancy (N0). Additionally, there was no angiolymphatic invasion, and visceral pleural involvement was indeterminate. Non-neoplastic lung tissue exhibited emphysematous changes. Immunohistochemical staining further characterized the tumor cells, with positive CD56 (spotty), cytokeratin-HMW, 34BE12, CAM 5.2, and p63, while synaptophysin and chromogranin A were negative. The pathological staging assigned to the tumor is pT2 N0 MX.

The pathological examination reveals a high-grade (Grade 3) urothelial carcinoma with extensive squamous differentiation, exhibiting invasive behavior into the muscularis propria. Notably, there is no evidence of lymphovascular invasion. The specimen is composed of multiple fragments of white-tan soft tissue, with approximately 75% of the tissue submitted for detailed histological analysis. These findings are consistent with an aggressive bladder carcinoma, warranting careful clinical management and follow-up to monitor potential progression and response to therapy.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of invasive squamous cell carcinoma of the right floor of the mouth and right lateral tongue, extending into the anterior tongue. The primary tumor is moderately differentiated and demonstrates an ulcerating growth pattern with a maximum depth of invasion of 1.3 cm. The tumor exhibits an infiltrative border with perineural invasion and mild lymphocyte infiltration, but no vascular invasion. Importantly, the surgical margins are negative for carcinoma, with the closest margin being less than 0.2 cm from the medial edge. Examination of lymph nodes from levels I to IV of the right neck reveals metastatic squamous cell carcinoma involving one lymph node at level I with extranodal extension, while 23 other lymph nodes are negative for carcinoma. Additionally, the specimen identified as "Intra tongue metastasis" does not show squamous cell carcinoma but contains a well to moderately differentiated mucoepidermoid carcinoma, with peripheral margins negative for neoplasm. Other relevant findings include moderate squamous dysplasia in the glossotonsillar sulcus and benign tissues in various resected specimens and lymph nodes.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of poorly differentiated invasive ductal carcinoma with focal intraductal spread. The primary tumor demonstrates extensive ulceration of the skin and is notably in close proximity to the nearest resection margin. A further focus of the same carcinoma type was identified in the ablated breast sample. This additional tumor is situated at a slightly safer distance from the dorsal and lateral resection margins, with all other margins remaining clear. The histopathologic grading assigns the tumor as NOS, Grade III, with a pathologic stage classified as pT1c(m)pN0, indicating no metastatic involvement of the 13 examined lymph nodes. Both lymphovascular and perineural invasion were absent.

The pathology diagnosis for the left frontal brain lesion reveals a high-grade glioma with mixed oligodendrocytic and astrocytic features. Histologically, the tumor exhibits hyperchromatic pleomorphic nuclei, elongated or ovoid nuclear outlines, and a finely fibrillary internuclear space. Marked by frequent mitotic figures, focal tumor necrosis, and notable microvascular proliferation, the neoplastic proliferation underscores its aggressive nature. The presence of clear halos around some neoplastic nuclei and chunky microcalcification further characterizes the tumor. Immunohistochemical staining for GFAP highlights the gliofibrillary background, while a high MIB-1 labeling index at 22% indicates a significantly elevated proliferation rate. These features support the diagnosis of an anaplastic oligodendroglioma. Margin status and specific lymph node involvement are not mentioned, but the overall pathological findings point to a malignant and highly proliferative glial tumor.

The pathological examination confirms a diagnosis of poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma in the right lower lobe of the lung. The primary tumor exhibits invasive characteristics, measuring 2.7 x 1.0 cm, and involves the visceral pleura. Despite this, the bronchial and vascular margins are clear of neoplastic involvement, and no angiolymphatic invasion is observed. Additionally, metastatic carcinoma is absent in all 16 examined lymph nodes, including a small node located at the bronchial margin and two hilar nodes. Microscopic analysis reveals the tumor partially merges with a bronchioloalveolar pattern, and free-floating tumor cells are identified in blood clots external to the visceral pleura. Subpleural nodules consistent with metaplastic epithelial proliferation are also present, displaying both mucinous and nonmucinous features, some with cilia and areas of squamous metaplasia, but lacking nuclear atypia. Immunohistochemical staining finds the malignant cells positive for Napsin A, CK7, and TTF-1, with focal positivity for CK20, corroborating the interpretation of a primary lung adenocarcinoma. The nonneoplastic lung tissue shows signs of emphysema. Overall, the tumor does not show extension into lymphatic or venous spaces, and surgical margins remain uninvolved by the carcinoma.

The pathological examination reveals that the patient has an invasive squamous cell carcinoma located in the left tonsil, which exhibits moderately to poorly differentiated characteristics. The carcinoma extends to the excision margin, indicating that complete surgical resection may not have been achieved, necessitating further treatment consideration. The biopsy of the left base of the tongue shows squamous mucosa with lymphoid tissue, but no tumor is identified in this region. Similarly, the right tonsillectomy specimen reveals a hyperplastic tonsil without evidence of malignancy. In situ hybridization for Human Papilloma Virus (HPV) is positive, suggesting a viral association with the carcinoma. These findings emphasize the localized but aggressive nature of the left tonsillar squamous cell carcinoma, its incomplete excision, and the involvement of HPV in the tumor pathology.

The pathological findings are indicative of a centrally ulcerated, poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma of the colorectal type with extensive local invasion and metastatic involvement. The tumor exhibits broad infiltration into the pericolic fatty tissue and significant lymphangiosis carcinomatosa. There is metastatic involvement of thirty-two regional lymph nodes. Despite these aggressive features, the resection margins of the large intestine and mesenteric tissue are free of tumor cells. The tumor is classified as stage pT3 pN2, with a histological grade of G3, indicating a high degree of malignancy. Lymphovascular invasion is present, while venous invasion is absent. The specific status of distant metastasis remains indeterminate.

The pathological examination of the brain, specifically the left temporal occipital lesion, has led to a diagnosis of low-grade oligoastrocytoma (WHO grade II). The immunohistochemical analysis revealed the tumor cells to be positive for IDH-1 (R132H), with occasional tumor cells staining for GFAP, although a significant portion of the tumor cells lacked GFAP reactivity. P53 showed low to moderate staining, and Ki-67 had focally moderate staining, which is consistent with the diagnosis. The preliminary frozen section consultation of the smear suggested a low-grade glioma, probably oligodendroglioma. Gross examination of the excised tissue resulted in the submission of representative sections for permanent section analysis. Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) for the 1p/19q deletion is currently pending, which may provide further genetic insights into the tumor. There was no mention of the margin status or lymph node involvement in the provided details. Overall, these findings support the diagnosis of a low-grade oligoastrocytoma.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of infiltrating ductal carcinoma in the right upper inner quadrant of the breast. The tumor is poorly differentiated, revealing a significant degree of cellular atypia and structural disorganization. Importantly, the surgical margins are uninvolved, suggesting that no residual tumor is present at the edges of the excised tissue. Examination of the axillary lymph nodes shows no evidence of metastatic involvement, as all 10 lymph nodes evaluated are negative for cancer. Despite the inability to determine the total Nottingham Score, the overall evaluation points toward an aggressive malignancy due to its poor differentiation, though this is potentially mitigated by clear margins and the absence of lymph node metastasis.

The pathological examination of the left breast wide local excision revealed an infiltrating ductal carcinoma, classified as Nottingham grade III. The majority of the tumor is invasive, with less than 10% being in situ, and there is no evidence of vascular invasion, corresponding to AJCC stage pT2. The surgical margins are clear of tumor cells, indicating that the excised tissue edges are free of malignancy. Examination of the left axillary sentinel lymph nodes, confirmed by cytokeratin immunostains, and additional left axillary lymph nodes, revealed no metastatic involvement (AJCC pNO(i-)). These findings indicate that there is no lymph node metastasis, aligning the case with a favorable lymph node status. The absence of tumor in the margins and lymph nodes is a positive indicator for the patient's prognosis.

The pathological examination reveals a diagnosis of low-grade oligoastrocytoma, classified as WHO Grade II. The tumor is characterized by a diffusely infiltrating glioma involving both gray and white matter, exhibiting mild to moderate hypercellularity. The cells display round to somewhat irregular nuclei with scant cytoplasmic processes and frequent perinuclear halos. Mitotic activity is rare, and there are no signs of microvascular proliferation or necrosis. The MIB-1 labeling index, which measures the proliferative activity, is 6.1% in the most active areas, indicating an intermediate level of proliferation. Importantly, this degree of proliferation does not qualify the tumor for reclassification as anaplastic. Details on margin status and lymph node involvement are not applicable in the context of this brain tumor.

The essential pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of low-grade oligodendroglioma, WHO Grade II. The tumor is characterized by mildly to moderately increased cellularity with cells resembling mildly atypical oligodendrocytes. No mitotic figures are observed, and there is an absence of microvascular proliferation and necrosis. The neoplasm diffusely infiltrates both gray and white matter. Immunohistochemical analysis shows MIB-1 positive cells in vague clusters throughout the tumor, with a calculated labeling index of 2.3%, suggesting modest proliferative activity. The tumor margins are not discussed in the findings, and there is no mention of lymph node involvement or additional pathological abnormalities.

The pathological examination reveals two distinct adenocarcinomas in the right lower lobe of the lung. The primary tumor demonstrates high-grade (G3) cellular characteristics and exhibits infiltration through the visceral pleura, categorizing it as stage pT3. Neither of the examined regional lymph nodes shows evidence of metastatic involvement, maintaining a pN0 status. The overall margin status is not explicitly mentioned, but no indications of involvement are described. Other notable findings include the absence of any detectable distant metastasis at this time (pMx). These key pathological attributes are crucial for guiding the clinical management and prognostic assessment of the patient.

The pathological evaluation of the left segmental mastectomy specimen reveals an invasive ductal carcinoma, poorly differentiated, classified as Nottingham grade 3. Immunohistochemical analysis indicates the tumor is negative for estrogen and progesterone receptors, HER2, and androgen receptor, while EGFR is positive. The carcinoma exhibits significant nuclear pleomorphism and a high mitotic rate, consistent with its overall grade. The invasive component measures 3.1 cm at its greatest extension. Notably, the resection margins are clear of invasive cancer, with the closest margin being less than 1 mm from the deep aspect. Skin involvement and lymphatic space invasion are absent. Eight lymph nodes, including sentinel nodes, were examined and all are negative for carcinoma, confirming a pathological staging of pT2pN0M0. Pending tests for keratin and HLA-DR are negative, ruling out additional tumor involvement. The absence of ductal carcinoma in situ further complements the findings. Grossly, the tumor is well-defined, nodular with a central cavity, and is located in close proximity to the deep margin. These findings confirm the comprehensive assessment of a poorly differentiated, high-grade invasive ductal carcinoma, suitably staged and with no evidence of lymphatic or dermal invasion.

The pathological examination following a radical cystectomy, hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, bilateral pelvic lymph node dissection, and associated procedures revealed an invasive papillary urothelial carcinoma, nuclear grade 3/4, that is multifocal and invades deep muscle, urethra, and vagina (without mucosal involvement). Additionally, there were multifocal areas of non-invasive papillary urothelial carcinoma, nuclear grade 3/4, noted within the bladder. All surgical margins were free of tumor, and no vascular or perineural invasion was identified. The uterus showed an atrophic endometrium with chronic cervicitis, while no tumors were found in the bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes. The umbilical hernia sacs consisted of fibroadipose tissue. Examination of lymph nodes revealed no metastasis in all dissected nodes, with a total of 53 lymph nodes being tumor-free. Both the right and left distal ureters, along with proximal ureter margins, displayed no urothelial dysplasia or carcinoma. The omentum and other structures also showed no evidence of carcinoma. The pathological staging is pT4aNOMX, and additional immunohistochemical studies for p53 assay have been ordered.

Pathological examination of the right tongue revealed a well-differentiated keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma, involving a unifocal lesion on the right tongue, with all associated biopsies from various margins showing benign squamous mucosa, occasionally with verruciform hyperplasia or marked chronic inflammation, but consistently negative for high-grade dysplasia, in situ, or invasive carcinoma. The main tumor was non-involtrated by periodic angiolymphatic and perineural invasion and negative for HPV. Surgical margins close to the tumor were observed, with the smallest margin to the deep and medial margin, while the remaining margins were considerably wider. A detailed lymph node dissection revealed that all 24 lymph nodes across various cervical levels were free of metastatic carcinoma. Based on the combined findings, the pathological staging was determined as pT1 pN0. The patient's clinical history includes a diagnosis of stage 2 squamous cell carcinoma of the right lateral oral tongue, with requested HPV testing, which returned negative.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of infiltrating lobular carcinoma of the breast, characterized as multicentric with a poorly differentiated histologic grade (G-3) and the presence of single signet-ring cells. The report details two tumor nodes within the breast tissue, with the largest measuring up to 3 cm in its largest dimension. Immunohistochemical staining shows a strongly positive reaction for estrogen receptors (98%, 3+), a moderately positive reaction for progesterone receptors (50%, 3+), a negative reaction for Her-2/neu (1+), and a low Ki-67 proliferation index (6%). The surgical margins are reported as uninvolved, indicating no residual tumor at the resection edges. Evaluation of ten axillary lymph nodes shows that eight contain metastatic disease. These findings collectively suggest a T2 tumor stage, denoting a tumor size more than 2 cm but not more than 5 cm, with significant lymph node involvement and poor differentiation, guiding therapeutic planning and prognosis.

The pathological examination reveals a poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma measuring 27 mm in maximal dimension located in the right upper lobe, with evidence of both blood vessel and perineural invasion, though lymphatic permeation is not observed. The tumor, situated in the subpleural region near a bronchus with an in situ component, does not show definite pleural invasion and is well clear of the bronchial resection margin. Adjacent lung tissue exhibits emphysema. Examination of the hilar lymph nodes indicates metastases with a poorly differentiated carcinoma deposit observed in the lymph node cortex. Supplementary findings reveal focal neuroendocrine differentiation within the tumor, with immunohistochemical positivity for chromogranin, synaptophysin, and CD56, predominantly within the better differentiated squamous areas. The case is summarized with a staging of T1N1MX, indicating the primary tumor size, lymph node involvement, and an indeterminate metastatic status at this stage.

The histopathological examination reveals an invasive ductal carcinoma of the right breast (NHG2, pT2, pN3a), incorporating signet ring cell carcinoma components. The tumor exhibits high positivity for estrogen receptors in over 75% of neoplastic cell nuclei and moderate positivity for progesterone receptors, detected in 10-75% of the nuclei. HER2 protein assays returned a positive result, with a HercepTestTM score of 3+. Microscopic evaluation describes the infiltrative carcinoma, accompanied by significant parenchymal atrophy in the glandular tissue. Surgical margins indicate the tumor was excised with proximate boundaries; however, metastases are present in axillary lymph nodes (14 out of 16 nodes), including involvement of the capsule and perinodal tissues, along with vascular emboli. The immunophenotype is characterized as CK7-, CK5/6-, GCDFP-15-, E-cadherin+, with PAS positivity. These findings collectively support the diagnosis of a right breast invasive ductal carcinoma with substantial lymph node involvement and specific receptor status, which will guide further therapeutic decisions.

The pathological analysis of the excised specimen from the left back and flank reveals a diagnosis of malignant melanoma, classified as an unclassified type, reaching Clark's level V with a Breslow thickness of 21 mm. The melanoma exhibits satellite lesions and is characterized by a moderate host reaction, low mitotic rate, prominent vertical growth, and the absence of vascular or lymphatic invasion. Ulceration is present on the surface, and no regression is noted. The tumor is believed to have originated from a congenital dysplastic nevus. Two subcutaneous peritumoral lymph nodes tested positive for metastatic melanoma. Additional findings include lentiginous junctional and compound nevi with architectural disorder and mild to moderate melanocytic atypia across nine separate lesions, and the presence of an atypical nevus at the inferior anterior margin. Despite this, the deep and other peripheral margins are negative. Immunohistochemical staining confirmed the melanocytic lineage of the tumor, as it is positive for A103, HMB-45, S-100, and tyrosinase, while negative for SMA and desmin. These findings underline the aggressive nature of the melanoma and its metastatic potential.

The patient presents with a diagnosis of multifocal breast cancer characterized by a grade 2 invasive ductal carcinoma and associated multifocal ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of solid and cribriform patterns. Pathological examination of the left breast mastectomy specimen reveals a primary tumor mass, classified as pT2, with histologic features including moderate differentiation, evidenced by an architectural score of 2, nuclear score of 3, and mitotic score of 1. Notably, the deep surgical margins are free of tumor involvement, indicating the absence of residual invasive carcinoma at the excision boundaries. Microscopic analysis also confirms the absence of vascular invasion and skin or chest wall involvement, with calcifications noted within the tumor. The DCIS component, which maximally measures 1.9 cm at the second biopsy site, contributes to 10% of the total tumor volume and is negative for extensive intraductal components. Sentinel lymph node assessment shows no metastatic involvement (0/1), classified as pNO. Post-biopsy changes are evident in non-tumorous breast tissue, and the nipple and overlying skin are also free from tumorous involvement. The pathologic findings collectively provide crucial data for the ongoing management and prognostication of this patient's breast cancer.

The pathological examination of the urinary bladder following cystectomy reveals high-grade urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma, characterized by a papillary configuration. The tumor is located on both the right and left walls and demonstrates invasion into the inner half of the muscularis propria (detrusor muscle) without direct invasion of adjacent organs or structures. Venous or lymphatic invasion is absent, and no associated epithelial lesions were identified. All surgical margins, including the perivesical soft tissue margin, are free of malignancy. The right and left ureter margins are confirmed to be benign. Lymph node dissections, encompassing a total of 38 nodes from various anatomical regions, are benign with no metastatic involvement detected. Consequently, the staging of the primary tumor is categorized as pT2a, with no regional lymph node metastasis (pNO), and distant metastasis status remains unassessed.

The pathological examination revealed a diagnosis of poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the lung, located in the right upper lobe. The resected mass was characterized by central necrosis and invasion into the hilar region and through the bronchial wall. Notably, the mainstem bronchus, visceral pleura, chest wall, and mediastinum were free of tumor invasion. Furthermore, no additional tumor nodules or panlobar atelectasis were present. The surgical margins, including the bronchial, vascular, parenchymal, and pleural, were negative for malignancy. Importantly, vascular invasion was not observed. Regional lymph node biopsies from levels 7, 10, and 11 were also negative for malignancy, with no evidence of metastatic disease. The pathologic staging, according to the AJCC 6th edition, was determined to be pT2N0M0.
